title: Optimizing Query-by-Example Spoken Term Detection with Audio-to-Token Sequence Clustering and Query-Guided Retrieval
authors: Akanksha Singh, Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen, Vipul Arora
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Query-by-Example Spoken Term Detection (QbE-STD) retrieves relevant audio files corresponding to a spoken query, without relying on explicit word-level textual transcriptions. In low-resource scenarios where annotated data is unavailable, traditional template matching techniques such as Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) are often used. However, these methods are computationally intensive and scale poorly to large audio collections. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a scalable and fully unsupervised framework that combines Audio-to-Token Sequence Clustering (ATSC) with Query-Guided Retrieval (QGR). Audio files are tokenized into discrete acoustic units via self-supervised models and organized into a two-level hierarchical structure: coarse clusters for lexical grouping and fine-grained buckets for intra-term variation, enabling efficient pruning without sacrificing retrieval precision. At query time, hierarchical ranking via Jaccard and TF-IDF-based similarity is followed by fine-grained token alignment using the Smith-Waterman (SW) algorithm, balancing accuracy with computational efficiency. Experiments on multiple QbE-STD benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed ATSC+QGR framework consistently outperforms baselines based on DTW and embeddings, offering substantial improvements in both precision and scalability. The method is fully language-agnostic and requires no supervision, making it well-suited for real-world, low-resource, and multilingual speech retrieval applications.

title: Computational Investigation of Magnetophoretic Separation of Blood-Cell-Inspired Particles in Serpentine Microchannels_supp1-3695600.mp4
authors: Mondal Pranab Kumar
abstract: We computationally investigate magnetophoretic separation characteristics of blood-cell-inspired particles representing white blood cells (WBCs) and red blood cells (RBCs) in serpentine microchannels under saline-water diluted conditions. The flow field inside the microchannel and the induced magnetic field distribution are numerically simulated using a finite-element-based framework, while particle trajectories are obtained through Newtonian particle tracking. The study systematically examines the influence of magnetic configuration, channel geometry, and flow conditions on magnetic field gradients, particle trajectories, recovery, purity, and separation efficiency. The results demonstrate that serpentine microchannels generate stronger and more non-uniform magnetic field gradients compared to planar configurations, thereby enhancing magnetophoretic manipulation and particle separation. The influence of magnet geometry is also analyzed, revealing that square-shaped magnets provide the most favorable separation characteristics among the investigated configurations due to stronger localized magnetic field gradients. Furthermore, the combined effects of Reynolds number, serpentine channel amplitude, and magnet position are shown to govern the balance between magnetophoretic and hydrodynamic drag forces, leading to distinct operating windows for enhanced separation performance. The present work should be interpreted as a first-order computational design study employing simplified particle surrogates under low-Reynolds-number conditions. The findings provide physical insights and computational design guidelines for the development and optimization of magnetophoretic microfluidic separators for blood-cell-inspired particles in lab-on-chip applications.

title: Africa and Tunisia [President’s Message]
authors: Ayman EL-Refaie

title: Surviving (and Thriving) as Organizational Newcomers: Technical Communicators and Proactive Socialization
authors: Kim Sydow Campbell, Samantha Jo Cosgrove, Maia Pacini
abstract: Background: Socialization challenges for employee onboarding are well-established in management studies. This study investigates awareness of proactive socialization tactics for newly hired technical communicators. Literature review: The review examines organizational socialization within management studies and technical communication. Literature reveals the relevance and some application of this theory to technical communicators and encourages further research. Research questions: Do experienced professionals recognize proactive socialization tactics as a means of successful onboarding? What proactive socialization tactics do experienced professionals mention as appropriate for new technical writers? Methodology: Semistructured interviews were conducted with technical writers and their managers (n = 28), and transcripts were coded for instances of proactive socialization tactics (i.e., sensemaking, relationship building, and positive framing) mentioned as challenges faced by newly hired technical writers. Results and conclusion: Participants overwhelmingly recognized proactive behaviors as valuable, mentioning sensemaking most frequently (n = 25) followed by relationship building (n = 10) and positive framing (n = 5). Results offer four actionable insights for new technical writers: 1. be proactive to move more quickly through stages of onboarding; 2. seek information and feedback by independent learning, asking questions, and volunteering; 3. build relationships through social activities; and 4. aim to frame new or less-desirable tasks positively. Research limitations included sample size, interview methodology, deductive coding, and the prominence of remote/hybrid participants. Future research could explicitly address proactive socialization tactics through surveys for a larger sample size and more generalizable findings and explore differences in tactics specific to onsite versus distributed work arrangements.

title: Robust Frequency Regulation in Hybrid Power Systems Under False Data Injection Attacks Using Adaptive Event-Triggered Virtual Inertia Control
authors: Mohamed Soliman, Muhammad Majid Gulzar, Muhammad S. Tolba
abstract: This article presents an advanced virtual inertia (VI) approach for load frequency control (LFC) in networked power systems, formulated within a Takagi–Sugeno (T–S) fuzzy framework. The proposed method enhances stability in renewable-rich grids under random false data injection attacks (FDIAs). The T–S fuzzy model effectively addresses system nonlinearities and uncertainties, providing a tractable design framework. The microgrid LFC integrates an equivalent-input-disturbance-estimator-based state feedback VI auxiliary controller with an adaptive periodic event-triggered mechanism (APETM) for robust, communication-efficient operation. The APETM minimizes data transmission, reduces energy use, and optimizes communication resources. Simulation results demonstrate improved frequency regulation and strong resilience against unknown disturbances and random FDIAs. The APETM reduces the triggering times by about 37% compared with the variable-probabilistic-release-strategy-based event-triggered mechanism, confirming improved communication efficiency in data transmission.

title: An Intention-Driven Lane Change Framework Considering Heterogeneous Dynamic Cooperation in Mixed-Traffic Environments
authors: Xiaoyun Qiu, Haichao Liu, Yue Pan, Jun Ma, Xinhu Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In mixed-traffic environments, autonomous vehicles (AVs) must interact with heterogeneous human-driven vehicles (HVs) whose intentions and driving styles vary across individuals and scenarios. Such variability introduces uncertainty into lane change interactions, where safety and efficiency critically depend on accurately anticipating surrounding drivers’ cooperative responses. Existing methods often oversimplify these interactions by assuming uniform or fixed behavioral patterns. To address this limitation, we propose an intention-driven lane change framework that integrates driving-style recognition with cooperation-aware decision-making and motion-planning. A deep learning-based classifier identifies distinct human driving styles in real time. We then introduce a dual-perspective cooperation score composed of intrinsic style-dependent tendencies and interactive dynamic components, enabling interpretable and adaptive intention prediction and quantitative inference. A decision-making module combines behavior cloning (BC) and inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to determine lane change feasibility. Later, a coordinated motion-planning architecture integrating IRL-based intention inference with model predictive control (MPC) is established to generate collision-free and socially compliant trajectories. Experiments on the NGSIM dataset show that the proposed decision-making model outperforms representative rule-based and learning-based baselines, achieving 96.98% accuracy in lane change classification. Motion-planning evaluations further demonstrate improved maneuver success and execution stability in mixed-traffic environments. These results validate the effectiveness of structured cooperation modeling for intention-driven autonomous lane changes.

title: Leveraging Feature Alignment in Grassmannian Manifold for Multi-output Regression Tasks
authors: Lingping Kong, Jan Zdrazil, Nuria De Diego, Francisco Ignacio Jasso Robles, Václav Snášel, Swagatam Das, Jeng-Shyang Pan
abstract: Despite notable progress in domain adaptation for classification, regression-based domain adaptation remains challenging, particularly in terms of handling complex data structures, ensuring cross-domain generalization, and maintaining the precision and mathematical rigor required to validate model effectiveness. Unlike classification tasks, which are more resilient to variations in feature scaling, regression tasks are notably more sensitive, making their performance more vulnerable in domain adaptation scenarios. In this paper, we propose a generalized regularization technique grounded in the Grassmannian manifold to address the feature alignment problem. This approach leverages the underlying manifold structure of the data while preserving mathematical bounds, thereby enhancing the precision and efficiency of problem-solving. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we apply it to estimate multi-output parameters in two distinct domains: 1) the Arabidopsis thaliana plant dataset, collected from a high-throughput phenotyping platform at Palacký University, and 2) the publicly available dSprites shape recognition with six adaptation tasks. These tasks are critical to advance agricultural research and address generalization challenges in multi-output regression. Accurate predictions provide deeper insights into plant growth and health, thereby supporting more effective crop management strategies. We evaluate the effectiveness of our framework by comparing it with state-of-the-art regression alignment techniques that are independent of the underlying backbone and adaptable to transfer learning tasks. Experimental results show that our framework consistently outperforms existing methods,results description. The source code will be made publicly available upon acceptance at https://github.com/lingping-fuzzy.

title: Development of a Secure Hybrid Deep Learning Model for Honeypot Security and Monitoring System Using Quantum Dilated Convolutional–Fuzzy–Maxout Network
authors: Rajasekar Kaniappan, V. Jamuna
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: Due to the rise of computing techniques and their prevalent applications, cloud computing (CC) has been considered as a significant approach for secure data storage. Network firewalls protect the servers from harmful traffic and unauthorized access. Still, security solutions like intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDPSs) and antivirus (AVs) are complex and consume more time. For solving such issues, a honeypot is considered a popular technique for attaining network security. The discovery of honeypots, which are deliberately created as decoy systems to draw attention to and identify malicious behavior or illegal access, is an essential component of cybersecurity. Honeypots work as bait to help detect security flaws, learn attack patterns, and mitigate possible risks before they have a substantial impact on actual systems. This is essential because honeypots can identify undiscovered dangers, aid in the prevention of future assaults, and enhance network security by giving real-time information on possible intrusions. In CC, identifying honeypots is essential because they provide another line of protection by spotting complex threats that might elude traditional security procedures. Cloud administrators may strengthen intrusion detection, improve data security, and increase the overall resilience of the system by locating and evaluating honeypots. In this research, a novel quantum dilated convolutional–fuzzy–maxout network (QDCFMNet) is developed for honeypot identification in cloud environments. The prime process is the simulation of cloud nodes. The input log files are subjected to data preprocessing using a linear normalization approach. The features from the normalized log files are fused using the deep Kronecker network (DKN) with Kulczynski similarity. The synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) augments the dimension of the log file. The proposed QDCFMNet is employed for honeypot identification. In addition, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) is incorporated to analyze the contribution of input features and to improve the interpretability of the proposed model. Furthermore, the metrics like precision, recall, and F1-score are used to compute the efficiency of the QDCFMNet-based honeypot identification, which attains the finest values of 90.59%, 91.72%, and 91.15%, respectively.

title: Transmission Performance on Digital Communication Signals of Low-Frequency Electromagnetic Waves Under Dynamic Plasma Channel
authors: Bin Sun, Mingxing Wu, Cai Huang, Yusheng Yi, Huiwen Zhang, Ping Ma, Kai Xie
abstract: Utilizing the weakly attenuated magnetic field component of low-frequency (LF) electromagnetic (EM) waves presents a viable method to mitigate radio blackout. This article evaluates the communication performance of multiple phase shift keying (MPSK), multiple frequency-shift keying (MFSK), and frequency-shift chirp modulation (FSCM) under the dynamic plasma channel, employing theoretical, numerical, and experimental validation. Theoretical bit error rate (BER) expressions were established for three modulation schemes: MPSK, MFSK, and FSCM. Simulation results demonstrate that under the BER <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{b}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-6}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and the electron density <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\overline {Ne}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times 10^{12}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, FSCM exhibits superior performance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E_{b}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$N_{0} =6.3$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB), significantly outperforming 4-ary frequency-shift keying (4-FSK) (12.5 dB) and quadrature phase shift keying (QPSK) (20 dB). On a shock-tube experimental apparatus, two shock-tube experiments are established at the low-density (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.58\times 10^{12}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and high-density (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.03\times 10^{13}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) plasma. Frequency-division multiplexing (FDM) was employed to simultaneously transmit the three modulated signals. The experimental results reveal that FSCM achieves zero errors with the performance ranking FSCM > 4-FSK > QPSK, consistent with theoretical predictions. This study demonstrates that FSCM possesses exceptional resistance and robust adaptability under dynamic plasma channels, offering an effective modulation architecture by FSCM and experimental validation for anti-radio-blackout communication.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Hierarchical MHE–EKF for Joint Estimation of Vehicle Lateral Velocity and Road Friction
authors: Jiaxing Lu, Wei Pan, Weiheng Chen, Lin Zhang, Chunlai Zhao, Hong Chen
abstract: Accurate joint estimation of vehicle lateral velocity and tire-road friction coefficient (TRFC) is critical for active safety systems. However, under near-limit conditions, strong nonlinear coupling and mutual error propagation severely degrade estimation accuracy. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel uncertainty-aware hierarchical moving horizon estimation and extended Kalman filter (MHE–EKF) framework. This two-stage architecture employs a dual EKF (DEKF) to provide robust coarse priors, followed by a bidirectionally coupled MHE–EKF for high-precision joint estimation. The key novelty of this MHE–EKF structure lies in an embedded dual-innovation mechanism explicitly designed to manage uncertainty: first, an error-propagation model translates state errors into a high-confidence TRFC constraint interval, strictly bounding the MHE search space; second, a flag-driven adaptive arrival-cost strategy integrates multistage priors to accelerate mutual convergence. Vehicle experiments validate the algorithm. Compared to the conventional DEKF, the proposed framework reduces the lateral velocity mean absolute error to 28.8<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and 42.1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> of the DEKF baseline in dry-asphalt slalom and double-lane-change maneuvers, respectively, while concurrently decreasing the corresponding TRFC estimation errors from 5.73<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> to 0.11<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and from 11.61<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> to 1.38<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Extended evaluations on low-adhesion snow roads further confirm the algorithm's exceptional robustness and consistent accuracy across variable friction conditions.

title: CFR-Net: A Coarse-to-Fine Concatenated Dual Decoder Based on Frequency-Space-Structure Fusion for Robust Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Yuanzhu Wang, Yukun Zhang, Liguo Weng, Haifeng Lin, Min Xia
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Current state-of-the-art change detection methods face a critical dilemma: achieving robustness against non-stationary pseudo-changes (e.g., seasonal shifts and vegetation phenology) while preserving fine-grained boundary details often requires computationally expensive models. Existing lightweight approaches usually sacrifice one aspect for the other, leading to either excessive false alarms or blurred object boundaries. To address this challenge, we propose a novel coarse-to-fine refinement network, termed CFR-Net. CFR-Net adopts a dual-time Mamba-based encoder to suppress non-stationary variations and capture robust spatiotemporal dependencies, followed by a cascaded dual-decoder architecture that progressively refines change boundaries from coarse localization to fine delineation. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks (LEVIR-CD, GZ-CD, and SYSU-CD) demonstrate that CFR-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Specifically, on the LEVIR-CD dataset, our model achieves an F1-score of 97.46% and requires only 6.54 G FLOPs, striking an optimal balance between accuracy and efficiency. These results confirm the effectiveness of CFR-Net for robust and efficient high-resolution remote sensing change detection. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wyyyz-web/CFR-Net.git</uri>

title: Distributed Optimal Control Using Interval-Excited Relative-Output Reinforcement Learning Algorithm
authors: Yong Xu, Wei-Wei Che, Zheng-Guang Wu
abstract: This paper addresses the distributed adaptive optimal output tracking control problem for unknown heterogeneous multi-agent systems. Unlike existing studies that typically assume the leader's state is either globally or distributively available for information exchange, this paper proposes a dynamic state feedback control policy driven by an internal model to achieve output regulation. This policy utilizes only relative output measurements between neighboring agents to achieve output tracking, effectively relaxing the need for leader state information. Second, we design an online adaptive dynamic programming algorithm that operates under interval excitation (IE) conditions, replacing the traditional persistence of excitation (PE) requirement, to learn the optimal control policy. The uniqueness of the solution for the optimal gains of the linear regressor equation is obtained by verifying an invertible matrix under the online-verifiable IE condition. Compared with existing algorithms, our proposed online learning algorithm relaxes the requirements of the PE condition, the full-rank condition, and intelligent data storage. Finally, a numerical example is presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Transient Modulation of the Nucleus Accumbens by Transcranial Temporal Interference Stimulation: An fMRI Study in Healthy Adults_supp1-3696618.docx

title: MC-DiffNet: Mask-Constrained and Cycle-Consistent Diffusion Network for Unpaired Ultrasound Image Enhancement
authors: Nuo Chen, Yongquan Zhang, Guolin Chen, Jinhao Zhao, Changmiao Wang, Amirmehdi Yazdani, Hai Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Ultrasound imaging is a valuable tool in clinical diagnostics due to its real-time feedback and non-invasive nature. Despite these advantages, it often suffers from issues like low contrast, speckle noise, and blurred anatomical boundaries, which can compromise the accuracy of diagnoses. Although diffusion models have proven effective for image restoration, their use in enhancing unpaired ultrasound images is limited. Another limitation is lacking of structural constraints and region specific guidance. To address these challenges, we introduce MC DiffNet, a multi-task, diffusion-based enhancement framework designed specifically for clinical ultrasound images in unpaired settings. Furthermore, the use of lesion masks provides structural guidance that enables the model to focus on pathological char acteristics in ultrasound images. The framework incorporates three principal modules: a Cycle-Consistency Path, a Mask Constrained Module, and a Context-Aware Classification Path. The Cycle-Consistency Path ensures consistency in unpaired training through degradation-reconstruction consistency. The Mask-Constrained Module incorporates lesion-aware structural constraints into the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) loss, aiming to maintain anatomical accuracy. Meanwhile, the Context-Aware Classification Path guides the enhancement process using semantic-level features. Together, these modules enable MC-DiffNet to enhance diagnostically significant regions with precision while preserving anatomical integrity. Experiments conducted on public ultrasound datasets reveal that our method outperforms existing techniques in both peak signal-to-noise ratio and SSIM. Remarkably, when applied to real-world unpaired datasets, MC-DiffNet offers improved structural fidelity and visual clarity. Further evaluation suggests that the enhanced images generated by our framework are beneficial for subsequent segmentation tasks, underscoring the clinical relevance and robustness of our approach. Our code is available at: https://github.com/ichbincn/MC-DiffNet.

title: MO-Enstune++: Multi-Objective End-to-End Ensemble Optimization for Coastal Waste Risk Prediction
authors: Md. Sadik Hossain, Md Atikqur Rahaman, Sadia Zannat Reem, Md Mahbubur Rahman, Chinmay Bepery
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Coastal waste is of increasing concern to the environment and thus accurate and computationally efficient risk prediction models are needed. Current ensemble algorithms sequentially tune hyperparameters and allocate weights, which may not effectively trade-off between predictive performance and computational complexity. To alleviate this restriction, this study proposed a multi-objective ensemble optimization method called MO-EnsTune++, which is a combination of two complementary strategies: (i) MO-EnsTune that sequentially optimizes model hyperparameters with NSGA-II and Optuna before tuning ensemble weights and (ii)MO-EnsTune++ in which an end-to-end joint optimization using NSGA-II for optimizing both model unary parameters and ensemble weights is implemented. Experiments on the Global Plastic Waste 2023 dataset showed that the end-to-end method leads to more attractive Paretooptimal trade-offs with higher accuracy and lower computational complexity than the baseline. These results demonstrated that multi-objective ensemble tuning has great potential in promoting sustainable environmental risk prediction.

title: MAIR: A Modular AI-Ready Framework for Scalable Incident Response and Autonomous Governance in Next-Decade Cybersecurity
authors: Olufunsho Falowo, Jacques Bou Abdo
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This study investigates how artificial intelligence is reshaping the requirements and expectations of modern incident response (IR) frameworks. Drawing on survey data from 194 cybersecurity practitioners and a Delphi-informed expert review process, the research identifies critical limitations in legacy IR models, including lifecycle rigidity, inadequate ethical oversight, and difficulty integrating AI-driven indicators. The study proposes a Modular AI-Ready Incident Response (MAIR) framework designed to enhance scalability, autonomy governance, and decision transparency across the Sense-Decide-Act-Learn cycle. Results show strong practitioner consensus for industrywide framework modernization and the need for adaptive, AIintegrated architectures. The paper concludes with a roadmap for validating MAIR through real-world SOC pilots and iterative refinement, positioning it as a forward-looking foundation for next-decade cybersecurity operations.

title: WIP: AI-Based Dynamic 5G Channel Optimization for TSN Over 5G Using MPNN-TD3
authors: Syed Tasnimul Islam
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The integration of Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) with 5G requires radio links that can fulfil deterministic latency, jitter, and reliability requirements despite time-varying wireless conditions. This paper presents a work-in-progress (WIP) artificial intelligence (AI) driven framework that combines Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNN) and Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) for runtime adaptation of selected 5G radio parameters at the gNB. The framework collects channel quality indicators and quality-of-service (QoS) metrics, represents the current TSN-5G state as a dynamic graph, and uses the learned graph representation to select control actions such as antenna's RF front-end gain and the maximum number of Hybrid Automatic Repeat Request (HARQ) retransmissions. The proposed framework is intended to better support QoS requirements of TSN flows under varying radio conditions.

title: GNSS-Independent and Obstacle Avoidance for UAVs Using a Compact 360° Scanning Head
authors: Md Nafiz Redwan, Jahanoor Islam, Kazi Tauhid Mokbul Hussain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: We develop and evaluate a GNSS-independent guidance, navigation, and collision-avoidance stack for a multicopter that pairs SLAM with an omnidirectional obstacle sensor built from a compact 360° scanning head. The head rotates a single-beam range sensor via a stepper and slip ring with Hall indexing, and its detections are translated into small roll/pitch overrides using a simple distance-to-PWM rule with a 0.70 m safety radius. We test the system in two settings that stress different failure modes: a reflective-wall box arena that exposes specular and multipath effects, and a software-in-theloop scene that exercises drift and loop-closure recovery. In the arena, LiDAR provided tighter polar occupancy and higher repeatability than ultrasound; within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\approx} {1.05 m}$</tex>, LiDAR errors were typically <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<10 \%$</tex> while ultrasound often exceeded 30%. In the city-canyon runs, loop closures at intersections pulled trajectories back to the street grid and kept drift bounded over multi-block paths. Together these results show that a lightweight, compute-efficient sensing and control pipeline can deliver reliable mapping and safe evasive maneuvers without GNSS or a ground station, making it practical for cluttered, denial-prone environments.

title: Energy-Economic-Environmental (3E) Analysis of Rooftop PV Systems for Tropical Universities
authors: Md. Imamul Islam, Siti Nurnadirah Ahmad Latfi, Mohd Shawal Jadin, Ahmed Al Mansur, Azizul Ahmad, Munshi Mahbubur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study presents a 3E (Energy-EconomicEnvironmental) analysis comparing four rooftop PV installation methods for tropical universities. A 1 MW grid-connected system was designed and simulated utilising PVsyst software, assessing fixed-tilt, bifacial, building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV), and single-axis tracking configurations. Technical performance analysis indicates that single-axis tracking attains the maximum yearly energy generation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(1,876,165 \text{kWh} /$</tex> year) and performance ratio <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(84.23 \%)$</tex>, signifying a 39 % enhancement compared to fixed-tilt systems. Economic analysis indicates that BIPV provides the most competitive levelized cost of energy (RM 0.086-0.154/kWh) and the shortest payback period (5-8 years), although fixed-tilt systems yield the highest net present value (RM 5.85 million). The environmental impact assessment indicates that single-axis tracking achieves the greatest decrease in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> emissions (37,522 tonnes over 30 years), followed by BIPV (34,758 tonnes). Sensitivity analysis within Net Energy Metering (NEM) and Self-Consumption (SELCO) policy frameworks suggests that system selection must correspond with institutional priorities: single-axis tracking for energy optimisation, Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) for economic efficiency, and bifacial systems for equitable performance. This paper offers evidence-based decision frameworks for universities adopting renewable energy systems, aiding Malaysia's National Energy Transition Roadmap and carbon neutrality goals by 2050.

title: Towards Single-Channel & Single-Cycle EOG Based Multi-Directional Eye Tracking in Wearable System
authors: Tasnia Nabiha, Orthy Toor, Wakim Sajjad Sakib, Abdullah Bin Shams
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Electrooculography (EOG) is a promising bioelectrical technique for tracking ocular motion, offering substantial potential in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and wearable assistive technologies. However, existing EOGbased interfaces often rely on two-channel configurations, which increase hardware complexity, power consumption, and user discomfort. To overcome these challenges, this study investigates the feasibility of single-channel EOG acquisition for robust direction classification using single-cycle EOG signals, enabling decision-making shorter than human reaction time. EOG data were recorded from multiple participants under controlled laboratory conditions using standard two-channel setup. Horizontal and vertical channel data were isolated and analyzed independently to emulate single-channel operation. Nine distinct eye movement classes were classified using machine learning models trained on single-cycle segments. Feature extraction incorporated both statistical and gradientbased descriptors to enhance the classification performance. Our approach demonstrated accuracy, precision, recall & F1score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 83 \%$</tex> for both the vertical and horizontal configurations, with a latency of 27 ms. Despite using only half the number of electrodes, the model effectively captured the dynamics of diverse eye movements, demonstrating that singlechannel EOG can retain robust performance while substantially simplifying system architecture. This single-cycle, singlechannel paradigm offers fast, low-latency response and minimal electrode usage, making it well suited for next-generation wearable and real-time control interfaces.

title: Harnessing Explainable AI and Biomarker Insights for Robust Breast Cancer Detection
authors: Tahira Mahbub, Rehana Parvin, Tofayel Ahmed, Rafayet Rahman Ridoy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Breast cancer remains one of the most common causes of cancer death worldwide, emphasizing the need to spot it early and accurately. The paper aims to detect early of breast cancer by the predictive performance of 11 models including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest(RF), AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM), XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, K-Nearest Neighbor and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) on Coimbra breast cancer dataset (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n} \bold{=} \mathbf{1 1 6}$</tex>). Data preprocessing dealt with outliers by cross-module treatment and feature scaling. We next performed Boruta-based feature selection, finding that what was most relevant biomarkers but likely just under threshold levels included age, BMI, HOMA, Resistin and glucose. Model evaluation was completed using accuracy, precision, recall, F1score, ROC-AUC, calibration curves and Brier scores. GBM achieved the highest accuracy (96%) with well calibrated probability estimates, while KNN and other boosting methods also performed well. A selective prediction framework with confidence threshold (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\tau$</tex>) was implemented to improve clinical practicality, allowing for 10% automation of case classification and expert review for the remaining 90% with uncertain forecasts. Explainability was dealt with using SHAP, PDP, and ICE analysis, with glucose and Resistin emerging as the major influencing biomarkers all along, consistent with established medical knowledge. The results indicate that the combination of ensemble learning, selective prediction, and interpretability techniques offers a robust method for biomarker-based breast cancer detection which is readily applicable in clinical practice.

title: Scalable multiband-tunable laser enabled by heterogeneously-integrated InP gain regions on a single silicon photonic chip
authors: Takuo Hiratani, Kento Komatsu, Hidenari Fujikata, Naoki Fujiwara, Naoko Inoue, Takehiko Kikuchi, Takuya Mitarai, Shun Kimura, Takuya Okimoto, Yusuke Sawada, Eiichi Banno, Takashi Matsui, Takeshi Fujisawa, Nobuhiko Nishiyama, Hideki Yagi
abstract: We demonstrate the integration of different InP-based gain regions for the C- and L-bands on a single silicon photonic wafer via chip-on-wafer (CoW) bonding, aiming to expand the operating wavelength range of light sources. Firstly, in order to investigate the feasibility of InP/Si heterogeneous integration with different kinds of InP chips, semiconductor optical amplifier (SOA)-integrated tunable lasers operating in the C- and L-bands are fabricated on a single wafer using a single process. From the results of the fabricated tunable lasers, light output powers of 25.5 mW for the C-band and 28.3 mW for the L-band are obtained at a laser bias current of 200 mA and an SOA bias current of 300 mA, and stable single-mode operation with a wavelength tuning range of 56.5 nm for the C-band and 57.5 nm for the L-band is achieved. Furthermore, by using this integration technology with different kinds of InP gain chips, we fabricated a single-chip tunable laser with dual gain regions for the C- and L-bands. This device realizes a wide-wavelength tuning range in a single chip. Stable single-mode operation with a wavelength tuning range of 103.8 nm and a spectral linewidth of less than 50 kHz over the entire wavelength tuning range is also achieved.

title: CaricatureGS: Exaggerating 3D Gaussian Splatting Faces with Gaussian Curvature
authors: Eldad Matmon, Amit Bracha, Noam Rotstein, Ron Kimmel
abstract: A photorealistic and controllable 3D caricaturization framework for faces is introduced. We start with an intrinsic Gaussian curvature-based surface exaggeration technique, which, when coupled with texture, tends to produce oversmoothed renders. To address this, we resort to 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), which has recently been shown to produce realistic free-viewpoint avatars. Given a multiview sequence, we extract a FLAME mesh, solve a curvatureweighted Poisson equation, and obtain its exaggerated form. However, directly deforming the Gaussians yields poor results, necessitating the synthesis of pseudo–groundtruth caricature images by warping each frame to its exaggerated 2D representation using local affine transformations. We then devise a training scheme that alternates real and synthesized supervision, enabling a single Gaussian collection to represent both natural and exaggerated avatars. This scheme improves fidelity, supports local edits, and allows continuous control over the intensity of the caricature. In order to achieve real-time deformations, an efficient interpolation between the original and exaggerated surfaces is introduced. We further analyze and show that it has a bounded deviation from closed-form solutions. In both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, our results outperform prior work, delivering photorealistic, geometry-controlled caricature avatars. Project page: https://c4ricaturegs.github.io

title: 3D Scene Change Modeling With Consistent Multi-View Aggregation
authors: Zirui Zhou, Junfeng Ni, Shujie Zhang, Yixin Chen, Siyuan Huang
abstract: Change detection plays a vital role in scene monitoring, exploration, and continual reconstruction. Existing 3D change detection methods often exhibit spatial inconsistency in the detected changes and fail to explicitly separate pre- and post-change states. To address these limitations, we propose SCAR-3D, a novel 3D scene change detection framework that identifies object-level changes from a dense-view pre-change image sequence and sparse-view post-change images. Our approach consists of a signed-distance-based 2D differencing module followed by multiview aggregation with voting and pruning, leveraging the consistent nature of 3DGS to robustly separate pre- and post-change states. We further develop a continual scene reconstruction strategy that selectively updates dynamic regions while preserving the unchanged areas. We also contribute CCS3D, a challenging synthetic dataset that allows flexible combinations of 3D change types to support controlled evaluations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves both high accuracy and efficiency, outperforming existing methods.

title: ConTiCoM-3D: A Continuous-Time Consistency Model for 3D Point Cloud Generation
authors: Sebastian Eilermann, René Heesch, Oliver Niggemann
abstract: Fast and accurate 3D shape generation from point clouds is essential for real-world applications such as robotics, AR/VR, and digital content creation. We present ConTiCoM-3D, a continuous-time consistency model that generates 3D shapes directly in point space, without relying on discretized diffusion steps, pre-trained teacher models, or latent-space encodings. Our approach combines a TrigFlow-inspired continuous noise schedule with a Chamfer Distance-based geometric loss, providing stable training in high-dimensional point sets while avoiding costly Jacobian-vector products. This enables efficient one- to two-step inference with high geometric fidelity. Unlike previous methods that require iterative denoising or latent decoders, ConTiCoM-3D operates entirely in continuous time with a time-conditioned neural network, achieving fast generation. Extensive experiments on the ShapeNet benchmark demonstrate that our method matches or surpasses leading diffusion and latent consistency models in both quality and efficiency, establishing ConTiCoM-3D as a practical solution for scalable 3D shape generation.

title: PI3DETR: Parametric Instance Detection of 3D Point Cloud Edges with a Geometry-Aware 3DETR
authors: Fabio F. Oberweger, Michael Schwingshackl, Vanessa Staderini
abstract: We present PI3DETR, an end-to-end framework that directly predicts 3D parametric curve instances from raw point clouds, avoiding the intermediate representations and multi-stage processing common in prior work. Extending 3DETR, our model introduces a geometry-aware matching strategy and specialized loss functions that enable unified detection of differently parameterized curve types, including cubic Bézier curves, line segments, circles, and arcs, in a single forward pass. Optional post-processing steps further refine predictions without adding complexity. This streamlined design improves robustness to noise and varying sampling densities, addressing critical challenges in real world LiDAR and 3D sensing scenarios. PISDETR sets a new state-of-the-art on the ABC dataset and generalizes effectively to real sensor data, offering a simple yet powerful solution for 3D edge and curve estimation. Code: https://github.com/fafraob/pi3detr.

title: Tessellation GS: Neural Mesh Gaussians for Robust Monocular Reconstruction of Dynamic Objects
authors: Shuohan Tao, Boyao Zhou, Hanzhang Tu, Yuwang Wang, Yebin Liu
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (GS) enables highly photorealistic scene reconstruction from posed image sequences but struggles with viewpoint extrapolation due to its anisotropic nature, leading to overfitting and poor generalization, particularly in sparse-view and dynamic scene reconstruction. We propose Tessellation GS, a structured 2D GS approach anchored on mesh faces, to reconstruct dynamic scenes from a single continuously moving or static camera. Our method constrains 2D Gaussians to localized regions and infers their attributes via hierarchical neural features on mesh faces. Gaussian subdivision is guided by an adaptive face subdivision strategy driven by a detail-aware loss function. Additionally, we leverage priors from a reconstruction foundation model to initialize Gaussian deformations, enabling robust reconstruction of general dynamic objects from a single static camera, previously extremely challenging for optimization-based methods. Our method outperforms previous SOTA method, reducing LPIPS by 29.1% and Chamfer distance by 49.2% on appearance and mesh reconstruction tasks.

title: EEG-to-Image Synthesis Using Swin Transformer Enhanced with Conditional GANs
authors: Tasnova Islam, Abu Hayat Mohammad Nibras, Arif Istiaque Rupom
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) allows the process for the capture of brain activity with high temporal resolution, which is often affected by noise, variability, and limited datasets. The recent progress in deep learning has made a big change in the study area of synthesizing images from brain signals using Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN). To tackle these challenges, we propose a conditional GAN (cGAN) that incorporates a Swin Transformer encoder to generate character images from EEG signals. The encoder serves to extract EEG embeddings that condition the generator. Our training process combines adversarial loss with pixel-level (L1) and perceptual (VGG) losses to optimize the Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR). On a dataset comprising 10 different EEGcharacter classes, our model achieved a Pixel L1 of approximately 0.24 to 0.27, a VGG perceptual loss of about 3.0 to 3.3, an average PSNR of 14.01 dB, and a Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) of 0.752. These results illustrate the potential of transformer-augmented GANs for synthesizing images from EEG data and their future applications in brain-computer interfaces (BCIs).

title: Cloud-Native, AI-Augmented Fault Correlation and Root Cause Analysis for Multi-Vendor Networks
authors: Sesha Kiran Gonaboyina, Balu Chavan, Nareshkumar Soundarajan
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Arriving at 5G and cloud-native, the telecom industry is quickly onboarding to 5G and cloud-native architectures, which adds enormous complexity to the operation of a network. Today's infrastructures are mainly built with containerized microservices and hardware from many vendors, and the management is fragmented. When faults manifest in such systems, this disaggregation process leads to a flood of alerts which are generally symptomatic and which arise together as “alarm storms”, obfuscating the core root cause incidents. In this paper we present a new AI-derived framework to provide automated fault correlation and Root Cause Analysis (RCA) which is tailored to these heterogeneous environments. Using a synthetic dataset containing 490 individual operating events, this article employs a hybrid ML pipeline of time-series response-based ML and topological graph mapping. Streaming platforms based on logs are taken in as input, processed through Python-based libraries such as Pandas and Scikit-learn for feature extraction, and fed into deep learning training of models via TensorFlow. This methodology can dynamically associate events between disparate vendor protocols without depending on static rule sets. The experimental results show that our approach reduces the number of noise alarms as well as improves the accuracy of root cause analysis compared to traditional methods. This work extends the effectiveness of leveraging AI for reliability in cloud-native networks.

title: A Retrieval-Augmented Framework for Executable Context-Aware Data Quality Assessment
authors: Hadi Fadlallah, Ibrahim Dhaini, Rima Kilany Chamoun
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Context-aware data quality assessment enables the selection of quality checks that are aligned with data usage scenarios. While existing frameworks can model context knowledge and derive context-aware data quality assessment plans, their operationalization into executable validation logic remains largely manual. This paper presents a retrieval-augmented execution framework that bridges this gap by automatically translating validated context-aware assessment plans into executable code. The proposed approach embeds and retrieves dataset context representations, while preserving assessment plans as authoritative knowledge stored in a Neo4j knowledge graph. Retrieved assessment plans are combined with the input dataset context to form a structured augmented prompt that strictly constrains a large language model to act as a translator rather than a reasoning or decision-making component. The model is used solely to generate executable validation logic without introducing new quality dimensions or assumptions. The framework is evaluated using a real radiation wireless sensor dataset collected from a monitoring station. The evaluation demonstrates the feasibility of generating valid and executable Python data quality validation code.

title: Visibility-Aware Language Aggregation for Open-Vocabulary Segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Sen Wang, Kunyi Li, Siyun Liang, Elena Alegret, Jing Ma, Nassir Navab, Stefano Gasperini
abstract: Recently, distilling open-vocabulary language features from 2D images into 3D Gaussians has attracted significant attention. Although existing methods achieve impressive language-based interactions with 3D scenes, we observe two fundamental issues: background Gaussians, which contribute negligibly to a rendered pixel, receive the same feature as the dominant foreground ones, and multi-view inconsistencies due to view-specific noise in language embeddings. We introduce Visibility-Aware Language Aggregation (VALA), a lightweight yet effective method that computes marginal contributions for each ray and applies a visibility-aware gate to retain only visible Gaussians. Moreover, we propose a streaming weighted geometric median in cosine space to merge noisy multi-view features. Our method yields a robust, view-consistent language feature embedding in a fast and memory-efficient manner. VALA improves open-vocabulary localization and segmentation across reference datasets, consistently surpassing existing works. The source code is available on VALA.

title: Pixel-Accurate Epipolar Guided Matching
authors: Oleksii Nasypanyi, Francois Rameau
abstract: Keypoint matching can be slow and unreliable in challenging conditions such as repetitive textures or widebaseline views. In such cases, known geometric relations (e.g., the fundamental matrix) can be used to restrict potential correspondences to a narrow epipolar envelope, thereby reducing the search space and improving robustness. These epipolar-guided matching approaches have proved effective in tasks such as SfM; however, most rely on coarse spatial binning, which introduces approximation errors, requires costly post-processing, and may miss valid correspondences. We address these limitations with an exact formulation that performs candidate selection directly in angular space. In our approach, each keypoint is assigned a tolerance circle which, when viewed from the epipole, defines an angular interval. Matching then becomes a 1D angular interval query, solved efficiently in logarithmic time with a segment tree. This guarantees pixel-level tolerance, supports per-keypoint control, and removes unnecessary descriptor comparisons. Extensive evaluation on ETH3D demonstrates noticeable speedups over existing approaches while recovering exact correspondence sets. The project page is available here.

title: Impact of Motion Strategies on the Net Energy Gain of Solar Energy System
authors: Jiahao Qiu, Jun Wu, Yanling Tian
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Solar tracker is an important component of a solar energy system to improve the efficiency of solar harvesting. However, due to factors such as friction and gravity, the tracker consumes significant amounts of energy while tracking the sun. Intermittent motion can reduce the tracker's energy consumption, but this also reduces its solar harvesting efficiency. To investigate the differences in power consumption and solar energy collection between continuous and intermittent motion, a simulation model of a solar tracker based on an RR-R5RRR parallel mechanism was established for simulation experiments. Four experimental conditions were set: continuous motion and posture adjustment every 2,5 or 10 minutes. The results showed that intermittent motion with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2}$</tex>-minute interval collected almost the same amount of energy as continuous motion at only half the energy consumption, demonstrating that intermittent motion with a short interval is a superior choice for sun trackers.

title: The Bearing of Planetary Roller Screw Based on Pitch Error and Roller Deviation Specificity Analysis
authors: Yujia Bian, Zetai Zhang, Dongjuan Jia, Hao Wang, Shangjun Ma
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This study focuses on the load-bearing characteristics of PRSM, establishing a load distribution model that takes into pitch error and roller deviation. A sensitivity analysis method based on the orthogonal experimental design is proposed, where an orthogonal test table is designed. Sensitivity functions are employed to quantitatively evaluate the influence of pitch errors in the nut, screw, and rollers, as well as flank clearance, on the thread load distribution. The results indicate that the sensitivity to pitch error in the rollers is significantly higher than that in the nut and screw. In the design, manufacturing, and assembly of planetary roller screw, the compensation and control of pitch errors are more critical than the adjustment of tooth side clearance. The pitch accuracy of the rollers is a key control parameter that determines the system's load- bearing performance and transmission reliability.

title: Development of the Saponification System Using Recycled Oil Implementing Temperature and pH Fuzzy Control
authors: Nicolas Gabriel Garcia Espichán, Aaron Pichilingue Cedillo, Renzo Francisco Albatrino Aza, Luighi Guiseppe De Francesch Saavedra, Joel Jesús Figueroa Vilcarromero, Ayrton Krickst Nieves Acosta
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Current saponification systems lack coordinated automatic control of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> and temperature, which leads to unstable operation and inconsistent product quality. Small-scale soap production often relies on manual saponification, where the operator heats and mixes while adding base by feel and occasional pH checks. In practice, heat and alkalinity are adjusted separately, and the reaction drifts as feedstock and ambient conditions change. This routine leaves no simultaneous control of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> and temperature, which makes the process unstable, raises safety concerns with caustics, and yields inconsistent product. To address this gap, we design and implement an automated mechatronic plant for used cooking oil (UCO) that applies Mamdani fuzzy logic control (FLC) to regulate pH and temperature in concert. The rule base captures operator intuition while adapting online to disturbances and the nonlinear coupling between loops. The system was validated in three production batches, maintaining temperature at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0} \pm \mathbf{2}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> within 911.

title: Polarization-Insensitive Silicon-on-Insulator Arrayed Waveguide Grating Router with Bidirectional I/O
authors: Siyang Zhou, Jun Zou, Zikang Xu, Shuojian Zhang, Zhuping Fan, Jian-Jun He
abstract: Polarization sensitivity of silicon-based wavelength division multiplexing (WDM) devices complicates the deployment in randomly polarized fiber communication systems. Here we present a polarization-insensitive arrayed waveguide grating router (AWGR) on conventional silicon-on-insulator (SOI) platforms using a 2D-grating based polarization diversity scheme and bidirectional transmission in a single symmetric AWGR. The device shows 0.32 dB polarization-dependent loss (PDL) and 3.29 dB average excess loss, with 1.2 nm channel spacing. It has a compact size of only 2.3×1.0 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. It also features bidirectional input/output ports, which supports simplified single fiber array coupling and greatly facilitates the wavelength routing in dense wavelength division multiplexing (DWDM) systems employing bidirectional transceivers with a reduced number of fibers.

title: Multistatic Radar Joint Range-Velocity Anti-Deceptive Jamming Methods Based on Information Geometry
authors: Siyi Yao, Yi Liao, Yanwen Su, Yusheng Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a joint range-velocity anti deceptive jamming method based on information geometry for multistatic radar scenarios and adapts to both measurement information and spoofing parameter approaches. The location information of physical targets can be obtained from the radar's angle and range measurements, while velocity can be derived from the radar's velocity and angle measurements, both of which contain correlated components. This paper maps normalized samples of location and velocity information obtained from multiple measurements onto a power-spectrum manifold to discriminate between physical targets and active false targets. Compared with existing methods, the proposed distance-velocity joint anti-deceptive jamming algorithm not only theoretically leverages information from both distance and velocity dimensions more effectively but also demonstrates its feasibility and effectiveness by simulation results.

title: A 3-bit Digitally-Reconfigurable Driving-Capability nW-Powered Three-Stage Fully-Synthesizable Inverter-Based OTA for Multi-Load/Applications
authors: Riccardo Della Sala, Marco Privitera, Giuseppe Scotti, Alfio Dario Grasso, Massimo Alioto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a fully synthesizable three-stage inverter-based operational transconductance amplifier (OTA) featuring a 3-bit digitally reconfigurable output stage for robust operation across a wide range of capacitive loads in ultra-low-voltage (ULV) and ultra-low-power (ULP) environments. Implemented entirely with standard-cell inverters and inverting-tristate (ITS) buffers, the proposed architecture operates from a 0.3-V supply while consuming less than 2.7 nW. The digital tuning mechanism enables near-constant gain-bandwidth product (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$GBW$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and average slew rate (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${SR}_{av}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) across a 10-110 pF load range, making the OTA suitable for both on-chip and off-chip applications in battery-less and energy-harvested systems. Fabricated in 180-nm CMOS, the OTA achieves > 65-dB DC gain, 3.5-kHz <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$GBW$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, 0.16-V/ms <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${SR}_{av}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and 94% rail-to-rail output swing, while demonstrating strong resilience to PVT variations. Measurements confirm > 2.1X improvement in small-signal efficiency establishing a new state-of-the-art for < 0.5-V digital-based OTAs.

title: Coherent Radar Networks: Terminology, Synchronization, and Applications
authors: David Werbunat, Lukas Paulus, Julian Aguilar, Vinzenz Janoudi, Julian Kanz, Max Basler, Martin Vossiek, Christian Waldschmidt
abstract: In coherent radar networks, multiple radar sensors not only operate cooperatively but also facilitate coherent evaluation of signals from other spacially distant radar units. This overcomes the limitations of single radars, enabling extended apertures and new multistatic sensing geometries. Applications are manifold, ranging from network-based direction of arrival (DoA) estimation to bi-/multistatic synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and joint communication and sensing (JCAS). In recent years, this has led to significant research interest and the design of a wide range of radar networks and synchronization concepts. This paper introduces a unified terminology and a general signal model applicable to radar networks and bistatic radars. Building on this model, this work provides a detailed review of the main sources of synchronization errors and their impact on radar signal processing. The paper further surveys and categorizes synchronization strategies proposed in the literature to address these errors. It thereby enables both a structured comparison of current radar networks and the design of novel network and synchronization concepts for emerging applications. To illustrate the potential of these approaches, three applications are presented in detail: a novel DoA estimation strategy for distributed radar networks, UAV-borne bistatic SAR, and JCAS.

title: Temperature-Based Hybrid Modeling for Heating Power Prediction and Fault Detection in Power Transformers
authors: Fanghui Yin, Siyu Nie, Shaowei Rao, Guotao Feng, Tao Liu
abstract: Accurate estimation of internal heating power is essential for transformer thermal assessment and fault diagnosis. However, the multi-physics coupling and nonlinear heat transfer in oil-immersed transformers make it difficult to infer heating power directly from external temperatures using purely physical models. This paper proposes a hybrid temperature-based framework that integrates physical understanding with data-driven learning. The physical thermal–electrical model is used to reveal the nonlinear temperature–power relationships, guiding feature selection via mutual information (MI) analysis. Selected temperature features are then applied to a random forest (RF) regressor for heating power prediction. Experiments on a thermally equivalent scaled prototype of a 500 kV power transformer, involving 28 operating cases under both normal and fault conditions, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high prediction accuracy, with an average test <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> of 0.9956 and a test mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 2.27%. Moreover, deviations in predicted heating power under constant load effectively indicate localized thermal anomalies. The results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid approach offers a practical and interpretable solution for transformer condition monitoring using only external temperature measurements.

title: Comprehensive Analysis and Noise Suppression of Real-World Mud Pulse Telemetry Using an Adaptive Signal Decomposition Method for Downhole Sensing
authors: Nimra Irshad, Mingdong Xu, Nan Xie, Henry Leung
abstract: Reliable signal extraction in mud pulse telemetry (MPT) is critical for accurate pressure-based downhole sensing and safe drilling operations. To enhance sensing reliability and suppress severe noise in harsh drilling environments, this article presents an enhanced approach to mud pulse signal processing using an Adaptive Trend Variational Mode Decomposition (ATVMD) algorithm. Traditional signal decomposition methods, such as Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD), face notable limitations in practical MPT scenarios due to their sensitivity to the predefined number of modes and their inability to handle strong global signal trends induced by poorly synchronized mud pumps. Moreover, spectral overlaps between non-harmonic pump noise and the useful signal, especially in the presence of strong low-frequency noise affecting the signal’s trend, leads to severe mode mixing, which in turn lowers decoding accuracy and sensor system reliability. However, the proposed ATVMD framework dynamically determines the number of modes (k) and explicitly extracts dominant signal trends. An Otsu-based thresholding strategy applied to Power Spectral Density of signals is employed to identify and suppress the strongest noise components. Additionally, an adaptive filtering technique is used to extract the strong signal’s trends that enhance VMD’s ability to accurately decompose the remaining signal into its true modes. The approach is validated through both synthetic datasets and real-field measurements collected from drilling operations in diverse geological environments. Experimental results demonstrate a significant improvement in mud pulse signal recovery under harsh noise conditions, achieving a pulse detection accuracy of 97.65% with a 95% confidence interval of [97.01%, 98.28%] across 31 detected pulses.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A

title: ClustRecNet: A Novel End-to-End Deep Learning Framework for Clustering Algorithm Recommendation
authors: Mohammadreza Bakhtyari, Bogdan Mazoure, Renato Cordeiro De Amorim, Guillaume Rabusseau, Vladimir Makarenkov
abstract: Identifying an effective clustering algorithm for a given dataset remains a fundamental unsupervised learning issue. We introduce ClustRecNet, a novel end-to-end deep learning framework that recommends suitable clustering algorithm(s) by directly learning high-order representations of raw tabular data. To facilitate robust meta-learning, we first construct a comprehensive repository of 34, 000 synthetic datasets encompassing a large variety of clustering scenarios, run 10 popular clustering algorithms, and use Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) to establish ground-truth labels. ClustRecNet’s architecture integrates a convolutional, two residual, and an attention block to capture both local and global structural patterns, effectively bypassing the knowledge bottleneck associated with manual feature engineering. Extensive evaluation on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrates that ClustRecNet consistently outperforms traditional internal cluster validity indices such as Silhouette, Calinski-Harabasz, Davies–Bouldin, and Dunn as well as state-of-the-art Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) approaches such as ML2DAC, AutoCluster, and AutoML4Clust. For example, our framework achieves an average 0.497 ARI gain over the Calinski–Harabasz cluster validity index on synthetic data and an average 44.16% ARI improvement over the leading AutoML approach (ML2DAC) on real-world benchmarks.

title: Enhancing Bangla Spam SMS Detection with BERT: A Deep Learning Perspective
authors: Jobaida Abedin, Yasha Fatema, Farzana Tasnim, Fariba Tasnia Khan, Tanjim Mahmud, Rakhimjon Rajapboyevich Rakhimov, Valisher Sapayev Odilbek Uglu, Abubokor Hanip, Mohammad Shahadat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Spam messages pose a persistent problem in digital communication, particularly for low-resource languages like Bangla. The complex grammatical structure of Bangla, frequent code-mixing with English, and informal writing styles make spam detection far more challenging than in high-resource languages such as English. This study explores a comparative deep learning approach for classifying Bangla spam SMS messages. The models evaluated include Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT), BanglaBERT, XLM-BERT, and a hybrid BiLSTM + CNN model. A balanced dataset of 2,004 Bangla SMS messages- 1,030 spam and 971 ham-has been prepared for training and evaluation. Among the tested models, BanglaBERT has achieved the highest accuracy of 98.5 %. This outcome has demonstrated the strength of language-specific transformer models in capturing the contextual and structural nuances of Bangla text. Explainable AI (XAI) methods, including LIME and SHAP, have been applied to interpret the model's internal decision-making process and ensure transparency. The analysis has revealed that the words “ফ্রি”, “অফার”, and “জয়” are the most influential indicators for identifying spam messages. The proposed framework has achieved an effective balance between performance and interpretability, setting a new benchmark for Bangla spam SMS detection. This work contributes to the growing field of Bangla Natural Language Processing (NLP) and illustrates that combining sequential learning with contextual modeling can effectively address the challenges of spam detection in low-resource languages.

title: Noninvasive Assessment of Lung Fluid Accumulation Using Tetrapolar Impedance Measurement
authors: Mahjabin Mobarak, Habiba Begum, Md. Moniruzzaman, Ruhul Amin, Abdullah Al Mahfazur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Electrical impedance technique is a noninvasive and low-cost technique which plays a very significant role in bioimpedance measurement. Lung being dynamic organ, where the impedance changes during respiration, and the changes of impedance is an indicator of lungs condition. This study investigates the performance of a six-electrode Tetrapolar Impedance Measurement (TPIM) configuration in isolating and monitoring the left and right lungs individually, with a focus on the impact of fluid accumulation in the lower part of the right lung. Simulations were conducted using a phantom with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$663 \text{cm}^{3}$</tex> of fluid introduced into the lower segment of the right lung. 6electrode TPIM configurations were implemented by placing electrode on the right side (RS) and left side (LS) of the thorax to assess sensitivity to each lung. Results indicated that in the absence of fluid, percentage changes in total impedance during respiration were nearly identical for RS and LS placements (∼ 34%). However, with fluid in the right lung, the RS placement showed a significant drop in percentage change (from 34.65% to 9.76%), while the LS placement remained largely unaffected (35.24%). This validates the TPIM's ability to distinguish between lung conditions. Additionally, simulations at various vertical electrode placements showed that the percentage change increased gradually with elevation, reaching a peak about 15 cm, indicating sensitivity to fluid location.

title: Fundamental-Frequency Switching Strategy for a Five-Level Cascaded H-Bridge Inverter Using Selective Harmonic Elimination
authors: Sadikur Rahman, Fahim Shaharia, Md Zunaid Hossen, Samia Sultana Tuly, Ziam Chowdhury Noun
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, and experimental validation of a single-phase five-level Cascaded Hbridge (CHB) inverter employing Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE) with Newton-Raphson-based switching-angle computation. The topology comprises two H-bridge cells fed by isolated DC sources, synthesizing a five-level stepped output <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left\{-2 V_{d c},-V_{d c}, 0, V_{d c}, 2 V_{d c}\right\}$</tex>. A Fourier model is derived and SHE equations with two switching angles per quarter-cycle <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2})$</tex> are formulated to set the fundamental while canceling dominant low-order harmonics <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(5^{{th }}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7^{{th }})$</tex>. Angles are solved iteratively via Newton-Raphson and executed on a controller that drives the CHB power stage at fundamental frequency. A MATLAB/Simulink model is used to verify waveform synthesis, RMS voltage, and harmonic spectra. Hardware measurements using a Fluke power analyzer show close agreement with theory; for a representative case with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha_{1} \approx 17.9^{\circ}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha_{2} \approx 57.4^{\circ}$</tex>, the analyzer reported <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{rms}} \approx 16.87 \mathbf{V}$</tex> at 49.2 Hz and Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 20.4 \%$</tex>. The results demonstrate that five-level SHE with fundamental-frequency switching achieves effective harmonic control without high-frequency PWM, making the approach well-suited to cost-sensitive UPS, renewable energy interfaces, and low-frequency motor drives.

title: Bengali Fake Shield: Developing an Efficient Transformer-based Fake News Detection System for Bengali Language
authors: Ranjana Karmaker, Fariya Islam, Tasnia Ishrat Khan, Promi Madhu, Sayma Alam Suha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Fake news is a huge threat to social trust and cohesion whichhave little resources,particularly in languages such as Bengali. But fake news is hard to find in these languages: There are not many annotated datasets and the grammar structurescan be complicated. In this work we introduce Bengali FakeShield, a strong detecting system offake news in Bengali based on transformer models. We used theBanFakeNews dataset with 48,000 real news and 1,000 fake news. It also addresses class imbalanced problemby applying SMOTE and upsampling. A exhaustive analysis of diverse models (including traditional machine learning and deep-learning approaches) was carriedout. Bangla-BERT performed the best among them, interms of accuracy by 99.97% and recall by 100 %. The final model was then integrated intoa Flutter app optimised for real-time inference using TensorFlow Lite. This work provides a scalable method for addressing misinformation in Bengali and demonstrates the transformers effectiveness for low-resourcelanguage settings.

title: Fabrication Defect Detection and Characterization of Semiconductor Devices from SEM Images Using Morphological Image Processing
authors: Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Tazin Hossain, Muhammad Towfiqur Rahman, Md Shafiul Alam, Md. Shofiqul Islam, Tasnia Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents defect detection and characterization methods of semiconductor devices using morphological image processing. The proposed algorithm can detect various processing and fabrication defects, such as cracks, delamination, EOS, and ESD damage, from SEM images of the devices. It can also characterize the defects based on properties of the defected region. Like other image processing computational methods of defect detection; this algorithm does not need any reference image to compare with the inspected image. The algorithm involves different steps like filtering, global thresholding, segmentation, edge detection, feature extraction and defect characterization. The algorithm is implemented using MATLAB R2018b Software. The proposed method is cost-effective and less time-consuming for defect detection of semiconductor devices.

title: Optimized Digital Filtering of EEG Signals for ERP Preservation and Noise Suppression
authors: Md. Mostafa Goni, Amir KhosruTalha, Yaseen Rahat, Razia Sultana Rimu, Rakayet Rafi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate filtering of EEG signals is essential for reliable brain activity interpretation, particularly in Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) and neurodiagnostic applications where noise can distort event-related potentials (ERPs). This study compares the performance of four widely used digital filters applied to real EEG data from a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) task (PhysioNet, 2048 Hz sampling rate): FIR bandpass filters with Hamming, Hanning, and Blackman windows, IIR Butterworth, IIR Chebyshev Type II, and FIR Parks-McClellan notch filters. Filter performance was assessed using SNR, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle$</tex> SNR, MSE, RMSE, PSNR, and frequency-domain metrics including stopband attenuation, passband ripple, and group delay.
