Leveraging the EfficientNet convolutional neural network architecture, the proposed model effectively extracts key features from drawing tests, capturing subtle motor impairments characteristic of PD. Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets demonstrates that the EfficientNet-based classifier achieves high accuracy, precision, and F1-score in distinguishing Parkinson's patients from healthy controls.

title: Pattern Recognition of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from ECG Signals using LabVIEW and Artificial Neural Network
authors: Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Muhammad Towfiqur Rahman, A. M. A. Zaidi, Md Shafiul Alam, Tasnim Tamanna Rafa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study proposes a unique method of pattern recognition and analysis of various types of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from Electrocardiogram (ECG) signals. The analyzed features of the ECG signals are the PR-intervals, heart rates, QRS-width, QT-intervals, and the RR-intervals. Any eccentricity found in these classified features indicates the presence of a heart problem known as cardiovascular arrhythmia. The signal processing is accomplished using LabVIEW, and the extracted features were used to design a Multi-Layer Perceptron Artificial Neural Network (MLP-ANN) for the purpose of recognizing and distinguishing patterns between normal sinus rhythm and cardiovascular arrhythmia, which are Slow Term Atrial Fibrillation (STAF), Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation (PAF), and the Supraventricular Tachycardia (ST). The data required to train ANN was collected from the MIT-BIH database available on PhysioNet Database. This proposed method detects any early sign of classified cardiovascular arrhythmia from ECG signals by recognizing and classifying patterns of signals using state-of-the-art ANN algorithms, which can be life-saving in crucial conditions.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Hybrid Passive-Active Wristband for Efficient Parkinsonian Tremor Reduction
authors: Adam Koubbi, Badr Boughrara, Salma Sebbar, Yassine Salih-Alj
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Hybrid wrist-worn devices that combine passive damping with active feedback control offer a promising approach to suppressing Parkinsonian tremor while maintaining energy efficiency. Although several designs have been proposed, the relationship between system parameters and overall performance remains insufficiently characterized. This paper presents an evaluation of a hybrid wristband architecture in which a tuned-mass damper and a PID-controlled actuator operate in parallel. A two-degree-of-freedom forearm model is subjected to tremor excitation, and the system is assessed across varied damping and control configurations. Performance is quantified using three key metrics: root-mean-square (RMS) tremor attenuation, actuator control effort, and total energy consumption. The evaluation framework enables systematic analysis of design trade-offs and supports the development of wearable tremor-suppression systems that balance responsiveness with power efficiency.

title: Parametric Gaussian Human Model: Generalizable Prior for Efficient and Realistic Human Avatar Modeling
authors: Cheng Peng, Jingxiang Sun, Yushuo Chen, Zhaoqi Su, Zhuo Su, Yebin Liu
abstract: Photorealistic and animatable human avatars are a key enabler for virtual/augmented reality, telepresence, and digital entertainment. While recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have greatly improved rendering quality and efficiency, existing methods still face fundamental challenges, including time-consuming per-subject optimization and poor generalization under sparse monocular inputs. In this work, we present the Parametric Gaussian Human Model (PGHM), a generalizable and efficient framework that integrates human priors into 3DGS for fast and high-fidelity avatar reconstruction from monocular videos. PGHM introduces two core components: (1) a UV-aligned latent identity map that compactly encodes subject-specific geometry and appearance into a learnable feature tensor; and (2) a disentangled Multi-Head U-Net that predicts Gaussian attributes by decomposing static, pose-dependent, and view-dependent components via conditioned decoders. This design enables robust rendering quality under challenging poses and viewpoints, while allowing efficient subject adaptation without requiring multiview capture or long optimization time. Experiments show that PGHM is significantly more efficient than optimization-from-scratch methods, requiring only approximately 20 minutes per subject to produce avatars with comparable visual quality, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability for real-world monocular avatar creation.

title: Unified Geometry and Color Compression Framework for Point Clouds via Generative Diffusion Priors
authors: Tianxin Huang, Gim Hee Lee
abstract: With the growth of 3D applications and the rapid increase in sensor-collected 3D point cloud data, there is a rising demand for efficient compression algorithms. Most existing learning-based compression methods handle geometry and color attributes separately, treating them as distinct tasks, making these methods challenging to apply directly to point clouds with colors. Besides, the limited capacities of training datasets also limit their generalizability across points with different distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time unified geometry and color compression framework for 3D point clouds. Instead of training a compression model based on specific datasets, we adapt a pre-trained generative diffusion model to compress original colored point clouds into sparse sets, termed 'seeds', using prompt tuning. Decompression is then achieved through multiple denoising steps with separate sampling processes. Experiments on objects and indoor scenes demonstrate that our method has superior performances compared to existing baselines for the compression of geometry and color. Our codes would be released at https://github.com/Tianxinhuang/DiffCom.git.

title: DINO in the Room: Leveraging 2D Foundation Models for 3D Segmentation
authors: Karim Knaebel, Kadir Yilmaz, Daan De Geus, Alexander Hermans, David Adrian, Timm Linder, Bastian Leibe
abstract: Vision foundation models (VFMs) trained on large-scale image datasets provide high-quality features that have significantly advanced <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> visual recognition. However, their potential in 3D scene segmentation remains largely untapped, despite the common availability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> images alongside 3D point cloud datasets. While significant research has been dedicated to 2D-3D fusion, recent state-of-the-art 3D methods predominantly focus on 3D data, leaving the integration of VFMs into 3D models underexplored. In this work, we challenge this trend by introducing DITR, a generally applicable approach that extracts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> foundation model features, projects them to 3D, and finally injects them into a 3D point cloud segmentation model. DITR achieves state-of-the-art results on both indoor and outdoor 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks. To enable the use of VFMs even when images are unavailable during inference, we additionally propose to pretrain 3D models by distilling 2D foundation models. By initializing the 3D backbone with knowledge distilled from 2D VFMs, we create a strong basis for downstream 3D segmentation tasks, ultimately boosting performance across various datasets.

title: Enhancing 3D Object Tracking via Dual-Context Propagation and Temporal Context Fusion
authors: Peijing Jiang, Yuanping Zhang, Jinlong Pang, Zhongjun Lin
abstract: Point cloud-based 3D single object tracking (3D SOT) plays a pivotal role in applications such as autonomous driving and robotic vision. Despite recent progress, most existing approaches rely solely on current-frame features for target localization. This approach overlooks temporal information that is crucial for robust tracking under occlusion, appearance variations, and sparse point clouds. In addition, the effectiveness of 3D SOT largely depends on the quality of feature fusion between the target template and the search region. Traditional fusion strategies often suffer from limited interaction capacity and weak discriminative representation. To address these challenges, we propose DT-Tracker, which performs multi-layer bidirectional feature interaction and temporal cue propagation to improve tracking robustness and feature discrimination capability. Specifically, we introduce a Dual-Context Propagation Network that applies bidirectional cross-attention across multiple layers between the template and search region, enabling deep semantic alignment and progressive feature refinement. Furthermore, we design a Temporal Context Fusion module that adaptively incorporates temporal cues from historical fusion features into the current frame, effectively improving resilience to occlusion and appearance drift. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate that DT-Tracker achieves competitive results compared to existing representative methods.

title: Robotic Welding with AI in the Loop
authors: Hamid Reza Saadati, Wojciech Kowalczyk, Sebastian Feldmann
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive overview of developing and implementing an AI-enabled intelligent robotic welding system. The primary objective of the project is to design and realize a fully automated welding solution that combines CAD-based path planning, real-time seam tracking, cloud-based data streaming, and AI-driven defect detection. The proposed system is capable of performing high-precision and consistent welding operations, thereby improving process efficiency, enhancing weld quality, and minimizing the need for manual intervention or conventional robot programming. Key components of the system include dynamic vision-based seam tracking integrated with arc sensing feedback, CAD file processing for optimized vision-based seam tracking, and cloud connectivity for remote monitoring and data analysis. Additionally, an AI model is deployed for real-time defect detection and classification, contributing to a closed-loop, intelligent welding process.

title: Joint Deployment, User Association, and Power Allocation for Data Collection in UAV-Assisted Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Yanping Liu, Kunkun Zhang, Xuming Fang, Ming Xiao, Fuhong Song, Qing Xue, Yaping Cui, Changfeng Ding
abstract: In recent years, uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly prevalent for collecting environmental data from various wireless sensors. However, existing research on employing UAVs to collect data from wireless sensors has often ignored the heterogeneous requirements of sensors. In this paper, we investigate joint deployment, user association, and power allocation for data collection in the UAV-assisted wireless sensor network to accommodate the heterogeneous requirements of sensors, where a novel satisfaction function is designed for three types of sensors, including sensors with delay requirements, sensors with energy consumption requirements, and sensors with both delay and energy consumption requirements. Leveraging the satisfaction function, we formulate the optimization problem aimed at jointly optimizing the positions of UAVs, the association between sensors and UAVs, and the power allocation of sensors to maximize overall satisfaction of sensors. In order to effectively address the considered problem, we decompose it into two subproblems, i.e., joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and transmission power allocation subproblem. An enhanced human evolutionary algorithm is developed to tackle the joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and the Lagrange dual method and gradient descent method are employed to solve the transmission power allocation subproblem. The suboptimal solution is achieved by iteratively addressing the two subproblems until convergence of the proposed enhanced Lagrange and gradient descent-based human evolutionary optimization algorithm is attained. Extensive simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm in enhancing overall satisfaction of sensors, underscoring its significant advantages in managing heterogeneous network environments.

title: Classical Trajectory Planning for Dual-Camera Visual Servoing on Edge Systems
authors: Abilash Philip Madavath, Chandra Yuvesh Aubeeluck, Augustin Raju, Felix Hackelöer, Florian Zwanzig, Nicolas Pyschny
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Grasping moving objects in industrial environments requires precise synchronization between perception and robot actuation. While deep learning has advanced object detection, the latency introduced by Edge AI computation can degrade interception accuracy in high-speed conveyor applications. This paper presents a comparative analysis of trajectory prediction and interception algorithms implemented on an edge platform (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano). The proposed system utilizes a dual-camera architecture, handing over control from a conveyor overhead predictor to a local wrist-mounted visual servo. We study two prediction algorithms (RANSAC-based linear extrapolation versus Kalman filtering) and five interception strategies (Analytical Linear, Fixed-Point Iteration, Newton-Raphson, Secant, and Bisection methods) against ground-truth data obtained via an OptiTrack motion capture system. Results indicate that while RANSAC offers superior fitting accuracy, the Kalman filter provides the low-latency performance required for real-time control. Among interception solvers, the Newton-Raphson method demonstrated the optimal balance between computational speed and stability. Furthermore, we analyze the impact of adding lead time on the interception success rate. The results quantify these trade-offs, establishing design guidelines for algorithm selection in real-time dynamic picking.

title: Learning Compact 3D Gaussians via Feed-Forward Point Fusion
authors: Brandon Smart, Chuanxia Zheng, Iro Laina, Victor Adrian Prisacariu
abstract: We present Splatt3RFusion, a feed-forward neural network that, given a set of unposed and uncalibrated images, directly reconstructs a compact and high-quality 3D Gaussian Splat representation of a scene. Unlike prior pixel-aligned feed-forward methods that typically predict one 3D Gaussian primitive per pixel in each image - producing severe redundancy, duplication, and ghosting on one physical surface - our approach efficiently fuses points in 3D space through a multi-scale octree structure, yielding a compact and coherent representation. Built upon VGGT, a foundation model for pose-free 3D geometry prediction, Splatt3RFusion introduces a Gaussian prediction branch that infers primitive parameters using only photometric supervision. We also introduce the ability to control the number of 3D Gaussians generated at test-time, allowing for a controllable tradeoff between PSNR and the number of 3D Gaussian primitives used. The model is efficient, reducing both memory usage and rendering cost, while achieving state-of-the-art results on RealEstate10k and ScanNet++.

title: Current-Sensor Fault-Tolerant Control for PMSM Drives via Offset-Free Phase Extended Generalized Integrator
authors: Zifeng Chen, Ruiqi Xu, Xijian Lin, Shun Cai, Dianxun Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: Conventional current sensor fault-tolerant control methods in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) drive systems face dual challenges: measurement drift due to current sampling errors and complex nonlinear observer structures. These issues result in inaccurate phase current reconstruction and ambiguous stability characteristics under faulty conditions. This letter proposes an offset-free fault-tolerant control strategy using a single-phase current sensor based on the Offset-free Phase Extended Generalized Integrator (OPEGI) to address these issues. By employing the OPEGI algorithm to reconstruct three-phase currents, the core innovations of this letter include: 1) achieving current reconstruction in the abc axis during current sensor signal loss faults and remedying the stability defects of conventional schemes; 2) real-time suppression of offset errors in current measurement, enhancing the accuracy of current signal measurement. The effectiveness of this method has been validated through experiments conducted on a three-phase 4 kW PMSM setup.

title: A New SCAN Method for Unsupervised Learning: Developing an Auto-Labeling Framework in Semiconductor Manufacturing_supp1-3692570.pdf
authors: Shu-Kai S. Fan
abstract: This paper proposes a novel self-supervised clustering framework for automatic defect annotation in semiconductor manufacturing, aiming to reduce the heavy reliance on expert-labeled data. Building upon the Semantic Clustering by Adopting Nearest-neighbors (SCAN) model, we introduce a new SCAN methodology that integrates three key innovations: a redesigned pretext task, an enhanced SCAN model with neighbor loss, and a self-labeling mechanism. The new pretext task replaces the traditional ResNet18 backbone with MobileNetV2 and incorporates focal loss to address class imbalance, enabling more effective feature extraction from unlabeled wafer maps. The SCAN model is further refined through a neighbor loss function that leverages high-confidence samples to iteratively correct pseudo-labels, improving clustering accuracy. Additionally, we apply strong augmentation techniques and a confidence-based thresholding strategy to enhance model robustness. Experimental evaluations on the WM-811K and MixedWM38 datasets demonstrate that the proposed unsupervised clustering framework achieves a notable accuracy improvement (from 36.5% to 57.7% and 36.09% to 57.69%, respectively) compared to the original SCAN, with further gains up to 66.20% and 64.80% using high-confidence sample filtering. The results confirm that our approach significantly enhances classification performance in unsupervised settings and offers a practical solution for scalable, automated defect labeling in intelligent manufacturing environments. Note to Practitioners—This study introduces a method for automated data grouping, or pseudo-labeling, which reduces reliance on manual labeling in unsupervised learning. Using the new SCAN model, the model learns meaningful features, enabling more accurate clustering. High-confidence samples help correct pseudo-labels of nearby data points, enhancing clustering quality without expert labels. This approach shows promise in improving classification accuracy in self-supervised learning, particularly for semiconductor manufacturing, by minimizing manual annotation efforts and refining model performance in unsupervised scenarios.

title: Frequency Domain Mixing and Boundary Modulation Network for SAR Small Target Detection
authors: Wenbin Gao, Zhi Yuan, Donglin Jing
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: As SAR imaging resolution gradually improves to the sub-meter level, the fine scattering structures of both targets and background clutter in the scene are simultaneously restored with high fidelity. The degree of feature aliasing between small targets (such as small ships) and speckle noise or nearshore clutter is significantly intensified, and the difficulty of stable feature extraction is greatly increased. On the one hand, traditional multi-scale methods (feature pyramids, fixed-scale convolutions) use discrete hierarchical designs, which fail to adapt to the continuous scale variations of SAR small objects and lead to inefficient feature extraction. On the other hand, SAR small objects have significant local radar scattering differences; even different regions of the same ship show drastic feature changes, which severely reduces intra-class feature similarity and consistency. To address these issues, this paper proposes a SAR ship detection method based on frequency-domain mixing and part-aware representation. First, a frequency-domain differential decoupling module is designed, which realizes adaptive frequency band division through multi-scale Gaussian filtering and filters key ship frequency features combined with a dynamic masking mechanism. This module can not only suppress speckle noise but also construct a continuous-scale feature mapping space. Second, a part-aware dynamic convolution module is developed to fuse local morphological features and global contextual information for generating dynamic dilation rate convolution kernels, so as to accurately depict target boundary contours in dense scenes and suppress feature aliasing between targets. Comparative experiments on two mainstream datasets, HRSID and MSAR-1.0, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in small target detection, dense scene adaptability, and robustness to strong noise.

title: Finite Element Framework for Electrostatics and Optical Simulations in IGZO TFTs—Part 2: Application
authors: Pietro Rinaudo, Adrian Chasin, Ben Kaczer, Ying Zhao, Harold Dekkers, Subhali Subhechha, Attilio Belmonte, Valeri Afanas'ev, Ingrid De Wolf, Jacopo Franco
abstract: We apply our finite element-based framework, introduced in Part 1, to understand the effects of channel thickness and doping on the degradation of indium–gallium–zinc oxide (IGZO)-based thin-film transistors (TFTs) with different architectures. By taking into account only experimentally extracted band alignments and amorphous semiconductor-specific physics, it is possible to explain apparent discrepancies in previously reported positive bias temperature instabilities (PBTI) trends. Our analysis shows that common PBTI benchmarks used in standard inversion-mode bulk Si devices cannot be employed in thin-film accumulation-mode IGZO devices. Finally, based on these insights, we propose device optimization strategies to reduce the electron trapping component of PBTI in amorphous IGZO TFTs.

title: Neural Wave Propagation for Surgical Video Action Recognition: A New Dataset and Baseline
authors: Tianxiang Chen, Weibin Wang, Zhentao Tan, Ru Zhou, Yue Wu, Ziyang Wang, Le Lu, Mingliang Wang, Zi Ye
abstract: Accurate and efficient recognition of surgical actions in videos is critical for advancing AI-driven surgical robotics. However, current surgical video action recognition (SVAR) datasets suffer from limitations such as small scale, low resolution, inconsistent annotations, and insufficient action coverage. Most latest video recognition models are trained on large-scale common datasets and underperform in SVAR due to architectures that suppress high-frequency visual details (crucial for recognizing surgical tools and motions) and lack a strong spatial inductive bias, requiring extensive training data for good convergence. This is particularly challenging in the surgical domain, where data access is limited. Therefore, a new baseline is required. To address these issues, we introduce LapSurg-230K, an SVAR dataset of 7,569 high-resolution laparoscopic surgical video clips with 230,246 frames, well-annotated for 11 key actions across 9 surgery types. It supports both full and progressive data volume evaluation settings. We further propose WaveR, an attention-free baseline based on physical wave propagation. WaveR embeds an innate physical inductive bias: each video patch acts as a wave source that propagates waves toward action-critical regions (e.g., instrument tips), adaptively aggregating spatial-temporal context while preserving high-frequency surgical cues. This mechanism eliminates dependency on massive training data. Experiments demonstrate WaveR’s robustness under extreme data scarcity (≤ 30% training samples), achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on both surgical video action recognition and phase recognition tasks. The complete dataset, licensed under CC-BY 4.0, is available at [data link]. Our code is available at [code link].

title: MINE 2025–2026: Strong Global Engagement in Phase 1 and Exciting Opportunities Ahead [Education News]
authors: Nishad Mendis, David Celeita Rodriguez
abstract: Provides society information that may include news, reviews or technical notes that should be of interest to practitioners and researchers.

title: The First Desktop Computer: The History of the Olivetti Programma 101 [History]
authors: Aldo Boglietti
abstract: The Olivetti Programma 101 designed in the mid-60s and conceived by Pier Giorgio Perotto engineer of Olivetti Company in Ivrea Italy, was the first desktop personal computer in the world, ahead of its time compared to the computer revolution of the 70s. The Olivetti Programma 101 was a programmable, elegant and compact computer used even by NASA for the Apollo 11 mission. His history, summarized in this contribution, has represented a missed opportunity for Italian industry, which failed to fully exploit its technological primacy in the transition from office automation based on electromechanical systems to mass computing.

title:
A Highly Integrated Primary-Side Zero-Voltage Switching Active Clamp Flyback Control IC for a 5-cm
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    5-V/1-A AC–DC Converter With Adaptive Frequency and Deadtime Control
authors: Akiyoshi Tanaka, Shan He, Reza Mounesi, Xinjian Liu, Omar Faruqe, Nugaira Gahan Mim, Daniel S. Truesdell, Adel Nasiri, Benton H. Calhoun
abstract: This article presents a miniaturized 5-W output AC–DC converter for Internet of Things (IoT) and smart building powered by wall power. The implemented control IC for an active clamp flyback (ACF) AC–DC converter achieves up to 500-kHz switching operation to reduce passive elements compared to conventional passive clamp flyback and secondary-side regulation flyback converters. Toward further miniaturization of the system, the implemented IC can support adaptive frequency control and zero-voltage switching (ZVS) without high-voltage (HV) components. The chip can adaptively operate from 300- to over 500-kHz switching frequency with dynamic deadtime control techniques. This work reduces the magnetizing inductance of the transformer by 60% compared to previous works, down <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$85~{\mathrm {\mu \text {H} }}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. The full AC–DC system volume occupies 4.99 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, which is 50% smaller than prior works, and achieves a power density of 1.00 W/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at 5 V/1 A from 85- to 265-Vac input with a peak efficiency of 81.0%.

title: Direct Cardiac T1 Mapping with Subspace Modeling and Free-breathing Data Acquisition_supp1-3696845.pdf
authors: Chao Ma
abstract: Objective: Cardiac T1 mapping is an emerging magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) tool in the quantitative assessment of cardiomyopathies. Conventional techniques necessitate breath-holding during each scan, which can be challenging for certain patients. This work presents a novel T1 estimation framework based on subspace modeling and direct T1 estimation for cardiac T1 mapping with free-breathing data acquisition. Methods: Sparse (k,t)-space data is collected along a random radial trajectory using a free-breathing, ECG-gated, inversion-recovery fast low-angle shot (FLASH) sequence. The T1 relaxation model is incorporated into a direct reconstruction framework to perform end-to-end T1 mapping directly from the undersampled (k,t)-space data. The resultant image reconstruction problem is solved using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm, which decomposes the optimization problem into subproblems, such as image reconstruction with a low-rank constraint, parametric fitting with a sparsity constraint, and total variation (TV) based image denoising problems. Results: The performance of the proposed method is evaluated by comparisons with the two-step, indirect approaches through numerical simulations and in-vivo experiments. Results demonstrate the benefits of the proposed direct approach in terms of T1 estimation bias and variance Conclusions: Direct cardiac T1 mapping with subspace modeling enables free-breathing cardiac T1 mapping with improved accuracy. Significance: This study demonstrates the potential utility of incorporating MR physics knowledge and subspace modeling into a direct estimation framework for improved cardiac T1 mapping with free-breathing acquisition.

title: Anchor-Driven Compact Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction_supp2-3695021.mp4
authors: Yiling Xu
abstract: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods typically rely on per-Gaussian deformation from a canonical space to target frames, which overlooks the strong redundancy among spatially and temporally adjacent Gaussian primitives and leads to suboptimal efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose ADC-GS++, an anchor-driven compact Gaussian splatting frame work for efficient and high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. Specifically, ADC-GS++ organizes Gaussian primitives into an anchor-based canonical representation, enabling attribute sharing across local regions. To efficiently model dynamic scenes, we introduce a static–dynamic decomposition mechanism and further employ a coarse-to-fine deformation strategy driven by dynamic anchors at multiple granularities. In addition, a unified rate-distortion optimization is adopted to achieve a balanced trade-off between storage efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Furthermore, a temporal significance-based anchor refinement strategy is employed to dynamically grow and prune anchors, allowing robust adaptation to complex and large-scale motions. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world dynamic scene datasets demonstrate that ADC-GS++ significantly improves rendering speed over deformation-based approaches by 300% 700%, while maintaining competitive rendering quality. More over, ADC-GS++ achieves a more favorable rate-distortion trade off, resulting in substantially reduced storage consumption across different bitrate settings.

title: Diffusion Feature Completion–Driven Textual Dual Distillation for Multi-modal Remote Sensing Classification with Missing Modality
authors: Dan Xu, Wenqian Dong, Song Xiao, Jiahui Qu, Yunsong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, research on joint classification of multi-modal remote sensing data has achieved remarkable progress. However, constrained by imaging conditions or sensor issues, missing modality frequently occurs in practice. Knowledge distillation, as one of the mainstream strategies to deal with missing modality, is widely used due to its excellent transfer ability. However, most existing knowledge distillation methods focus on directly transferring available modal information through feature alignment or predictive constraints, without explicitly modeling or refining the latent semantic structure of missing modality. Furthermore, the heterogeneity between different modalities makes effective alignment difficult through direct transfer, thus limiting the utilization of cross-modal complementary information and consequently hindering model performance improvement in remote sensing scenarios with missing modality. Therefore, in this paper, we propose a diffusion feature completion-driven textual dual distillation model (DFC-TD<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) for multi-modal remote sensing image classification with missing modality. This model explicitly models the missing modality, introduces textual semantic information to guide cross-modal feature transfer, and achieves multi-constraint collaborative optimization under the supervision of classification labels, thereby effectively improving the overall performance of multi-modal classification tasks with missing modality. Specifically, a shared-constrained diffusion feature completion network (SCFC) is designed, which uses shared features extracted and frozen from multi-modal data as constraints to guide the diffusion model to effectively model and complete missing modal features, thereby generating semantically consistent and more discriminative modal representations. Furthermore, a text-guided dual distillation network (TGD<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) is designed to guide cross-modal feature alignment using text semantics and combine it with classification label supervision to construct a feature-label dual constraint, which effectively improves feature discriminativeness and supervision efficiency, thereby mitigating cross-modal representation heterogeneity and improving the stability and effectiveness of knowledge transfer. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of this method in dealing with missing modality.

title: NOVA: A Self-Supervised Graph Framework for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Internet of Vehicles_supp1-3696324.docx
authors: Arash Heidari
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) enables cooperative driving and real-time Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication but remains vulnerable to behavioral and structural anomalies due to its dynamic, decentralized nature. Existing deep learning methods either overlook topological inconsistencies or ignore communication feature fidelity, while random-walk sampling introduces contextual noise. In this paper, we propose Network Observation for Vehicular Anomalies (NOVA), a self-supervised graph-based framework that detects both behavioral and structural anomalies in IoV networks without labeled data. NOVA models vehicular communications as attributed graphs and employs intimacy-guided subgraph sampling to extract meaningful neighborhoods. A Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)–based generative module reconstructs node attributes to reveal behavioral deviations, while a contrastive module validates structural coherence through embedding comparisons of real and perturbed contexts. Their hybrid anomaly score enables accurate, scalable, and real-time detection of compromised nodes. Performance results show that NOVA achieves state-of-the-art performance (98.7% accuracy, 98.1% F1), real-time throughput (~4.7k events/s at 5k msg/s), and strong robustness (AUROC 0.99, AUPRC 0.98, FAR 0.05) with near-linear scalability (≤40 ms latency for 50k vehicles). By integrating generative and contrastive self-supervised learning with context-aware sampling, NOVA significantly enhances IoV security, reliability, and adaptability.

title: Quantum Depth-First Search-Based Reliability Assessment of Composite Generation and Transmission Systems
authors: Hejun Yang, Yue Liu, Yiwen Jiang, Dabo Zhang
abstract: The acceleration of classical composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment is mainly achieved through algorithmic innovations at the state sampling stage. Typical approaches include combining machine learning techniques or scenario reduction methods with Monte Carlo simulation. The former improves computational efficiency at the expense of partial accuracy, while the latter relies on a large number of samples and thus reduces the effective number of reliability samples. In contrast, quantum computing possesses a theoretical speed advantage over classical computing owing to its unique superposition and entanglement properties, and it has been demonstrated to achieve quantum supremacy. Motivated by this potential, this paper proposes a composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment method based on quantum-inspired algorithms. First, a quantum depth-first search (DFS) algorithm considering path connectivity is developed to construct a quantum model of the initial node and transmission paths. This model remains invariant with respect to changes in system fault states and enables the direct identification of subsystems connected to the initial node. Second, a quantum-inspired reliability assessment framework for composite generation and transmission systems is proposed, in which a quantum depth-first search model and a quantum power flow calculation model are established. By alternately integrating quantum-inspired computation with classical computation, the reliability indices of the composite generation and transmission system can be efficiently evaluated. Finally, two designed test systems and the RBTS system are analyzed. The results demonstrate the effectiveness and correctness of the proposed quantum depth-first search algorithm and the quantum-inspired composite generation and transmission system reliability assessment model.

title: Proposal and Experimental Evaluation of a Wire-Driven Flap-Type Non-Return Valve for Sampling in Small Lunar and Planetary Rovers
authors: Ryo Oyori, Shotaro Date, Kojiro Iizuka
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: In subsurface sampling using coring during lunar and planetary exploration, a non-return valve is essential for reliably retaining collected samples. Conventional mechanisms such as core catchers and flexible tubes have encountered issues including sand leakage and structural complexity. To minimize penetration resistance and achieve sample collection with a simple mechanism, this study proposes a wire-driven flap-type nonreturn valve that operates using only vertical motion. Because the actuation wire has a very small cross-sectional area, its influence on penetration resistance is expected to be minimal. The sampling performance of the proposed mechanism was experimentally evaluated in terms of sampling success rate, collected mass, and sampling efficiency. The sampler successfully retained regolith in all 25 trials, demonstrating stable and reliable operation in a granular environment. These results confirm the feasibility of using a wire-driven flap mechanism as a simple and lightweight non-return valve for coring systems. Future work will focus on integrating the mechanism with a vibratory penetration system to realize a lightweight, high-retention subsurface sampling device.

title: Modeling and Verification of Reconfigurable Distributed Control Systems
authors: Friederike Bruns, Bianca Wiesmayr, Felix Schmid, Lisa Wünderlich, Andreas Rauh
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Distributed control systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous, mixed-criticality communication that must meet diverse real-time requirements, adapt to dynamic modes, and ensure safety and correctness. Traditional design workflows often treat communication and control logic separately, limiting early verification and complicating system integration. The proposed design flow supports offline verification of reconfigurable communication architectures while ensuring predictable execution under strict timing constraints. Communication is modeled explicitly through logical messages and physical channels in a contract-based design flow, allowing integration of real-time or quality-of-service properties into the application model. This enables the specification of assumption-guarantee contracts, supports early syntactic and semantic checks, and incorporates dynamic validation of time traces. A battery production case study demonstrates communication heterogeneity and dynamic operating modes in a tool-supported modeling environment.

title: RGS-DR: Deferred Reflections and Residual Shading in 2D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Georgios Kouros, Minye Wu, Tinne Tuytelaars
abstract: In this work, we address specular appearance in inverse rendering using 2D Gaussian splatting with deferred shading and argue for a refinement stage to improve specular detail, thereby bridging the gap with reconstruction-only methods. Our pipeline estimates editable material properties and environment illumination while employing a directional residual pass that captures leftover view-dependent effects for further refining novel view synthesis. In contrast to per-Gaussian shading with shortest-axis normals and normal residuals, which tends to result in more noisy geometry and specular appearance, a pixel-deferred surfel formulation with specular residuals yields sharper highlights, cleaner materials, and improved editability. We evaluate our approach on rendering and reconstruction quality on three popular datasets featuring glossy objects, and also demonstrate high-quality relighting and material editing. The source code is available at https://github.com/gkouros/RGS-DR.

title: Shape Classification of Consecutive Obstacles for Water-Driven Soft Robot Using Reservoir Computing
authors: Junya Mieno, Hidenobu Sumioka, Takashi Takuma
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Soft robots are highly adaptable but difficult to sense because of their deformable structures. This study addresses shape recognition of a consecutively shape-changed pathway using water pressure of a front chamber from a developed waterdriven soft robot. To estimate the path shape through which the robot moved, we used reservoir computing (RC). We then propose two processes for accurately estimating path shapes. The first step is to reset RC to its internal state. Cycle-wise classification with internal state reset, “batch classification” assumes that the path shape does not suddenly change abruptly for one cycle. The second step involves dividing each cycle into smaller segments, performing sequential classification, and determining the final estimate through majority voting in each cycle. Experiments showed that the robot moved through a path of consecutively changing between two shapes, and the proposed system achieved 93% accuracy in the first half, 90% in the second half, and 91% overall with 200 reservoir units, demonstrating the effectiveness of RC for shape estimation for a consecutively shape-changed pathway.

title: LegacyAvatars: Volumetric Face Avatars for Traditional Graphics Pipelines
authors: Safa C. Medin, Gengyan Li, Ziqian Bai, Ruofei Du, Leonhard Helminger, Yinda Zhang, Stephan J. Garbin, Philip L. Davidson, Gregory W. Wornell, Thabo Beeler, Abhimitra Meka
abstract: We introduce a novel representation for efficient classical rendering of photorealistic 3D face avatars. Leveraging recent advances in radiance fields anchored to parametric face models, our approach achieves controllable volumetric rendering of complex facial features, including hair, skin, and eyes. At enrollment time, we learn a set of radiance manifolds in 3D space to extract an explicit layered mesh, along with appearance and warp textures. During deployment, this allows us to control and animate the face through simple linear blending and alpha compositing of textures over a static mesh. This explicit representation also enables the generated avatar to be efficiently streamed online and then rendered using classical mesh and shader-based rendering on legacy graphics platforms, eliminating the need for any custom engineering or integration. https://syntec-research.github.io/LegacyAvatars/

title: EgoMDM: Diffusion-Based Human Motion Synthesis from Sparse Egocentric Sensors
authors: Soyong Shin, Anuj Pahuja, Alexander Richard, Kris Kitani, Jason Saragih, Yuhua Chen, Weipeng Xu, Eni Halilaj, Timur Bagautdinov
abstract: Accurate three-dimensional (3D) human motion tracking is essential for immersive augmented reality <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(A R)$</tex> and virtual reality (VR) applications, allowing users to engage with virtual environments through realistic full-body avatars. Achieving this level of detail, however, is challenging when the driving signals are sparse, typically coming only from upper-body sensors, such as head-mounted devices and hand controllers. To address this challenge, we propose EgoMDM (Egocentric Motion Diffusion Model), an end-to-end diffusion-based framework designed to reconstruct full-body motion from sparse tracking signals. EgoMDM models human motion in a conditional autoregressive manner using a unidirectional recurrent neural network, making it well-suited for real-time applications. By embedding local-to-global translation, forward and inverse kinematics, and foot-contact detection within the diffusion framework, EgoMDM achieves seamless, end-to-end motion synthesis, effectively reducing artifacts like foot sliding and ground penetration. Additionally, EgoMDM is conditioned on the user's body scale, allowing it to generalize across a diverse population and produce consistent avatar shapes over time. In our extensive experiments on the AMASS motion capture dataset, EgoMDM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both motion tracking accuracy and synthesis quality, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability across various human motion scenarios. Furthermore, EgoMDM significantly outperforms the existing models when tested on real signal inputs, highlighting its robustness and applicability to the real-world data. See the project page at: https://yohanshin.github.io/egomdm.github.io/

title: Reflect3r: Single-View 3D Stereo Reconstruction Aided by Mirror Reflections
authors: Jing Wu, Zirui Wang, Iro Laina, Victor Adrian Prisacariu
abstract: Mirror reflections are common in everyday environments and can provide stereo information within a single capture, as the real and reflected virtual views are visible simultaneously. We exploit this property by treating the reflection as an auxiliary view and designing a transformation that constructs a physically valid virtual camera, allowing direct pixel-domain generation of the virtual view while adhering to the real-world imaging process. This enables a multi-view stereo setup from a single image, simplifying the imaging process, making it compatible with powerful feedforward reconstruction models for generalizable and robust 3D reconstruction. To further exploit the geometric symmetry introduced by mirrors, we propose a symmetric-aware loss to refine pose estimation. Our framework also naturally extends to dynamic scenes, where each frame contains a mirror reflection, enabling efficient per-frame geometry recovery. For quantitative evaluation, we provide a fully customizable synthetic dataset of 16 Blender scenes, each with ground-truth point clouds and camera poses. Extensive experiments on real-world data and synthetic data are conducted to illustrate the effectiveness of our method. Code and Blender scenes are available in: https://github.com/jingwu2121/reflect3r.git.

title: Integrative Analysis of Shared Molecular Signatures in Temporal Lobe Epilepsy and Alzheimer's Disease with Ligands Discovery Targeting SOD1
authors: Md. Mehedi Hasan Khan Siam, Ram Prosad Sarker, Md. Mehedi Hassan, Abanti Bhattacharjya, Bikash Kumar Paul, Touhid Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Temporal Lobe Epilepsy (TLE) and Alzheimer's Disease (AD) are prevalent neurological disorders with shared molecular mechanisms that remain poorly understood. This study integrates gene expression datasets from GEO (GSE256068, GSE48350) and NCBI gene information to identify candidate genes implicated in both TLE and AD. Sixteen key genes were identified and analyzed for differential expression, proteinprotein interactions, and pathway enrichment to uncover potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets. One of these was SOD1, which showed contrary direction of expression change in the two brain disorders (up-regulation in TLE and down regulation in AD) and is a crucial mediator of oxidative stress activity and thus for ligand-based analysis toward its disturbed expression pattern associated to TLE. Ligand mining and molecular docking were conducted using a Python-based Jupyter Notebook workflow, with data from RCSB Protein Data Bank. Candidate ligands were screened for drug-likeness and ADMET properties before being further ranked by their potential to affect the activity of SOD1. While the focus here has been on targeting SOD1 overexpression in TLE, the ligands may also have indirect benefits for AD by replenishing redox Bbalance and buffering oxidative stress, a shared pathological feature of these maladies. This integrative bioinformatics and cheminformatics approach provides insights into shared molecular mechanisms underlying TLE and AD and offers a framework for identifying candidate biomarkers and therapeutic strategies.

title: Tunable Optical and Electronic Properties of Ca-Doped $\varepsilon$ - $\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$: A First-Principles Study Toward Achieving $p$-Type Transparent Oxide Semiconductors
authors: Md. Billal Hossain, David Jevers Gourob Roy, Md. Nibir Islam, Syeda Rukaiya Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Epsilon gallium oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>) has drawn a lot of interest as a semiconductor material with a broad band-gap that may support future high-power electronic devices and ultraviolet photodetectors. The difficulty of achieving p-type conduction in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>, however, continues to be a technological obstacle. Here, we present a thorough computational analysis of the structural, electronic, and optical characteristics of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> and Ca-doped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> from first principles. Several studies using density functional theory demonstrates that Ca atoms can introduce acceptor states close to the valence band edge, raising the bandgap and indicating the possibility of p-type conduction. The hybridized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ca} 3 p$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{O} 2 p$</tex> orbitals make up the majority of the acceptor levels. The examination of the optical properties revealed that the system's optical activity changed as a result of doping, as evidenced by the increased dielectric function, absorption, reflectivity, and refractive index in the UV-visible region. The enhanced absorption and its adjustable band structure offer significant potential for the use of Ca-doped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon$</tex> - <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> as a candidate for transparent electronic devices and in optoelectronic applications that call for p-type oxide semiconductors, despite the decreased transparency.

title: A Low-Cost, Ultra Low-Power, and Scalable LoRa-Based Weather Balloon for Atmospheric Monitoring: A Bangladesh Perspective
authors: Md. Tanvir Hassan, Md. Samiul Islam Mobin, Sourav Karmokar, Tama Fouzder
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: To address the scarcity of accessible upper atmosphere data in regions such as Bangladesh, this research work presents the design and validation of a low-cost, easily scalable weather balloon. Our system uses a smart power-saving approach: an ESP32 microcontroller goes into deep sleep, waking up every ten minutes just long enough to grab data from its environmental and Global Positioning System (GPS) sensors and send it on its way. This approach allows a small 500 mAh battery to last an incredible 248 hours, which is much longer than any single flight. To make sure we do not lose any data, the readings are sent wirelessly via a LoRa module to a ground station and, at the same time, are saved to a microSD card right on the balloon. We tested the system in a 10-day ground test, and it performed perfectly, showing that our design is a reliable and affordable alternative to expensive commercial radiosondes. We believe that this new platform can really help expand opportunities for atmospheric research and education in places that have not had the opportunity before.

title: Machine Learning-Based Classification of Phishing Websites: Leveraging Website Behavior and Structure Features
authors: Ramij Wasith Rahat, Ashim Dey, Annesha Das
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Phishing is a prevalent cybercrime where attackers trick users into installing malicious programs or disclosing private information by using deceptive digital communication. Phishing attacks have become an alarming problem in recent days. An efficient and automated method of identifying phishing websites is necessary to mitigate the threat caused by scammers and other cyber fraudsters in the virtual world. Effective detection of phishing websites is still challenging with current technologies. To resolve the drawbacks of existing phishing attack detection techniques, we propose a machine learning-based method to identify websites as either phishing or legitimate. This method increases the accuracy of phishing website identification by using important features that are extracted from the dataset. For this research, a publicly accessible webpage phishing detection dataset is used that contains multiple features characterizing website behavior and structure. The dataset includes both phishing and legitimate website samples, providing sufficient diversity for comprehensive model training as well as validation. Different classification models, including XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, Decision Tree, etc., are assessed using 5-Fold Cross Validation, and achieved the highest accuracy score of 96.91% with the XGBoost Classifier model. Ensemble-based models regularly outperformed Non-Ensemble models. This approach improves user protection against cyber threats by minimizing false negatives, maximizing phishing detection accuracy and precision, and offering scalability for real-time implementation in websites or security systems. We anticipate that this work will aid robust phishing-website detection and enhance user security.

title: Self-Reconfiguration of Modular Robotic Systems and Programmable Matter Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Khodor Ghayad, Jad Bassil, Abdallah Makhoul, Roger Achkar, Jad Nassar
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Modular self-reconfigurable robots adapt their morphology to diverse tasks. Finding the sequences of movements for each module to reach a target configuration is computationally difficult due to exponential state-action spaces, especially in large configurations. While existing algorithmic methods are scalable, they often require manual, task-specific design; meanwhile, learning-based approaches have struggled to scale. This paper applies Q-learning to a system of 2D hexagonal lattice-based modular robots. By utilizing a position-independent state representation and connectivity-aware learning, we demonstrate that the system can successfully self-reconfigure and generalize to untrained initial configurations. Evaluation results from simulation show that with 100 training episodes, the learned policy achieves 100% success on the trained configuration and successfully generalizes to three distinct untrained initial configurations, including a spatially inverted scenario.

title: Learning-Assisted Proactive Reattachment in Tree-Structured VLC-VANETs
authors: Rim Touhami, Maha Sliti
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Vehicular visible light communication (VLC) can complement V2X, but links are sensitive to LoS/FoV alignment, mobility, and ambient-light noise. In treestructured VLC-VANETs, these effects lead to parentlink degradation and reactive reattachments that increase tail delay and packet loss. We propose a learning-assisted proactive reattachment trigger in which each vehicle runs a lightweight local predictor to anticipate imminent QoS violations from observable features (parent-link SNR, short-term SNR trend, relative motion, and basic topology/QoS indicators). When the predicted risk exceeds a threshold, the vehicle proactively switches to a feasible neighbor using the same QoS/SNR parent-selection score as the baseline, while dwell-time, hysteresis, and cooldown safeguards bound ping-pong and signaling overhead. Simulations across vehicle densities and illumination levels show higher packet delivery and lower high-percentile delay than reactive baselines, with reattachment churn kept bounded.

title: Would Learning Help? Adaptive CRC-QC-LDPC Selection for Integrity in 5G-NR V2X
authors: Sarah Al-Shareeda, Gulcihan Ozdemir, Arouj Fatima, Mădălin-Dorin Pop, Bander A. Jabr, Yasser Bin Salamah, Jacques Demerjian
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications impose stringent physical-layer integrity requirements, particularly under short-packet transmission and mobility-induced channel variation. This paper studies whether standard-compliant online selection of Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) polynomials and Quasi-Cyclic Low-Density Parity-Check (QC-LDPC) coding rates can reduce silent (undetected) errors in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$mathbf{5 G}$</tex> New Radio (5GNR) V2X links. The joint configuration problem is formulated as a lightweight Contextual Bandit (CB) with a small, discrete action space, and a discounted LinUCB policy is evaluated against greedy online adaptation and a conservative fixed baseline. A 5G-NR-compliant physical-layer simulation is developed using Sionna, modeling mobility through time-correlated Rayleigh fading, where vehicle speed governs channel correlation, and non-stationary interference via a two-state Markov process. The learning agent operates on coarse receiver feedback, including a noisy Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) estimate and indicators of burst interference and deep fades, and targets minimization of the Undetected Error Probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{U E}$</tex>) while accounting for the Detected Error Probability (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{D E}$</tex>). Overall, our objective is to delineate the mobility regimes in which learning-assisted CRC-QC-LDPC configuration improves physical-layer integrity in 5G-NR V2X systems. Our results indicate that learningassisted adaptation is most effective at low to moderate mobility, reducing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P_{U E}$</tex> by up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 - 7 0 \%}$</tex> relative to greedy selection in the low-SNR regime (-5 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex>) and approaching the best fixed configuration at higher <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{b} / N_{0}$</tex>. At high mobility (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 180 \mathbf{~ k m} / \mathbf{h}$</tex>), fast channel decorrelation weakens temporal predictability, limiting the effectiveness of online learning and reducing performance differences across policies.

title: Frequency Diversity 3D Printed DRA for Wireless Applications
authors: Jana M. AlNatour, Layan T. AbuRayyan, Raneem F. Abdelrahim, Yanal S. Faouri
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper presents and investigates a 3D-printed multiband frequency-reconfigurable dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) for wireless applications. The proposed antenna design comprises four non-identical boxes separated by a plusshaped patch. The main reason for the variation between the boxes is the different PLA infill percentages of each box (20%, 60%, 70%, and 100%,respectively); hence, distinct electric permittivity and loss tangent values were achieved for each box. The capability of frequency tuning is obtained by changing the state of the four switches implemented using PIN diodes installed between the center of each box and the plus patch, resulting in different resonant frequencies in each state.

title: Interpretable Cross-Corner Timing Prediction Based on XGBoost-SHAP Solution
authors: Keqing Ouyang, Qi Wei, Weiran Gou, Chenfei Wu, GuoHua Zhou
publish: ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems
abstract: None

title: Modular Synthesis of Coupled-Resonator Filters Using Divide-and-Conquer Paradigms
authors: Qiuyi Wu, Feng Tang, Xiaoqi Yang, Zihan Wang, Yimin Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a one-step method for decomposing a network into cascaded independent subnetworks, contrasting with the conventional “gather-in” approach and following the divide-and-conquer (D&C) paradigm. By modeling the network with ABCD matrices and leveraging the invariance of external Y-parameters, the decomposition reduces to a simple system of linear equations, requiring minimal numerical effort. The method applies to all typical configurations—including fully canonical or nonfully canonical networks, with or without frequency-dependent couplings (FDCs)—and can be extended recursively to handle complex high-order networks with arbitrary orders and transmission-zero allocations. Its effectiveness is demonstrated through various examples, including a dual-band filter with a hybrid cascaded–parallel topology, a structure that challenges traditional analytical techniques. Simulation and measurement results confirm the robustness of the algorithm and its potential for practical engineering applications.

title: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
authors: N/A

title: Event-Triggered Impulsive Control for Nonlinear Systems: A Q-Learning-Enhanced Memory-Based Method
authors: Zidong Ai, Bao-Lin Zhang, Qing-Long Han, Lianghong Peng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: An intelligent memory-based event-triggered impulsive control (METIC) scheme is proposed to address the stabilization problem for a class of nonlinear systems while accounting for exponential convergence, dynamic performance, and control frequency. The contribution of the scheme is the incorporation of weighted historical data into the triggering condition, using both fixed thresholds and adaptive thresholds based on Q-learning. By utilizing the system states at the two most recent triggering instants to construct the triggering condition, several exponential stability criteria are first established via an iterative approach. Then, in the general case in which additional historical states are included, a comparison system approach is employed to derive new stability conditions. Furthermore, to adaptively tune the event-triggering thresholds and improve system performance, a Q-learning-based optimization algorithm is developed, and a set of easily verifiable stability conditions is derived within the framework of switched system theory. For both fixed-threshold and adaptive-threshold cases, Zeno behavior is rigorously excluded through theoretical analysis. Finally, comparative simulation results are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Random Dense Knowledge Distillation for Continual Learning
authors: Jie Chu, Pei Liu, Tong Su, Yunpeng Wu, Yu Song, Zenglin Shi
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: Compensation of Frequency Shift Induced by the Electron Layer in Relativistic Magnetron
authors: Dong-Hyun Oh, Woo-Sang Lee, Jung-Hoon Han
abstract: The relativistic magnetron (RM) has attracted significant attention as a high-power microwave (HPM) source. Due to its narrowband characteristics, achieving a precise target frequency is a critical requirement in the initial design process. However, a frequency shift phenomenon observed in eigenmode and particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations complicates accurate frequency tuning. This shift is primarily attributed to the formation of a dense electron layer around the cathode during operation, which acts as a virtual conductor and effectively expands the cathode radius. To address this discrepancy, we propose two geometric compensation techniques: a posteriori and a priori methods. The a posteriori approach determines the effective extended boundary by explicitly analyzing the simulated space-charge distribution. In contrast, the priori method analytically predicts this effective expansion by utilizing the double-stream characteristics of the space-charge density, thereby eliminating the need for preliminary PIC simulations. The proposed compensation methods were verified using four magnetron models with distinct structural parameters. While the uncompensated resonant frequencies exhibited significant discrepancies ranging from 28 to 85MHz compared to the actual oscillation frequencies, the application of the proposed methods successfully reduced this frequency difference to within 25MHz across all models.

title: Conference Calendar
authors: N/A

title: Enhanced Circle Method for Discriminating Incipient Faults in Power Transformers Using Dissolved Gas Analysis
authors: Abdelmoumene Hechifa, Chouaib Labiod, Abdelaziz Lakehal, Redha Meneceur, Ali Bebboukha, Said Djaballah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Power transformers are essential components in electrical power systems, ensuring the continuous transmission and distribution of electricity. Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) has become a widely accepted method for monitoring the condition of transformers and detecting incipient faults. Among the various DGA methods, graphical techniques have gained significant popularity in recent years. In this study, an Improved Circle Method is proposed as an enhanced extension of the Circle 1 and Circle 2 methods to address the limitations of traditional DGA methods in distinguishing overlapping fault types. The method employs a sigmoid activation function to control the convergence and divergence distances between fault samples, significantly improving the separation of electrical and thermal faults. By utilizing Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO), optimal angles are determined for the best fault separation. Experimental validation using the IEC TC10 database demonstrated that the Improved Circle Method achieves an overall accuracy of 79.49%, which can be considered an improvement over both Circle 1 and Circle 2 methods. The results confirm that the proposed method serves as a reliable alternative to other conventional graphical techniques.

title: Reconstruction of curing temperature field evolution in epoxy composite insulators based on sparse monitoring data and reduced order modeling_supp1-3696940.docx
authors: Ying Lin
abstract: Non-uniform temperature distribution during epoxy resin curing is a primary source of internal stress development. However, existing experimental techniques cannot capture the global evolution of internal temperature fields, while conventional numerical simulations are challenged by strong coupling among material properties, temperature, and degree of cure. In this work, a global reconstruction model for curing temperature fields is developed for cycloaliphatic epoxy insulators based on sparse monitoring data and reduced-order modeling. The reconstruction accuracy and generalization capability of the proposed model are systematically evaluated. The results confirm that the proposed model enables accurate reconstruction of the curing temperature field evolution, with a reconstruction time of less than 0.05 ms. Reconstruction accuracy improves with increasing numbers of monitoring points and snapshots, although the improvement gradually saturates. Further improvements are achieved through optimized snapshot selection and reduced monitoring errors. The model developed at 130 °C successfully predicts temperature fields at lower curing temperatures with a maximum error below 1.6 °C, and maintains errors within 2.6 °C for epoxy systems exhibiting different curing kinetics. Higher curing temperatures yield more diverse thermal field characteristics, resulting in improved generalization performance. This work establishes a global curing temperature field reconstruction strategy, providing a quantitative foundation for enhancing the reliability of epoxy composite manufacturing.

title: Dynamic Escaping Population Driven Evolutionary Algorithm for Multi-objective Optimization Problems with Deceptive Constraints
authors: Xinyu Zhou, Weifeng Lin, KunJie Yu, Kangjia Qiao
abstract: Deceptive constrained multi-objective optimization problems (DCMOPs) pose severe challenges to constrained multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (CMOEAs), as constraint violation (CV) values become unreliable. In this paper, a dynamic escaping population-driven CMOEA, named DEPEA, is proposed by maintaining three cooperative populations: an escaping population (EP), an auxiliary population (AP), and a main population (MP). The EP employs slow and rapid escape modes to regulate its escaping behavior at different evolutionary stages, the AP aims to converge toward the unconstrained Pareto front (UPF), and the MP focuses on approximating the constrained Pareto front (CPF). To avoid mutual interference between the AP and EP while controlling the escaping behavior of the EP, a one-way fine-grained cooperation mechanism is designed. In addition, a boundary diversity enhancement strategy is proposed to strengthen exploration along boundary regions. Experimental studies conducted on four benchmark suites and six real-world constrained optimization problems demonstrate that DEPEA achieves superior performance compared with thirteen state-of-the-art CMOEAs.

title: Knee Point-Guided Online Cooperative Path Planning for UAVs in Dynamic Multi-Objective Environments_supp1-3694877.pdf
authors: YUE XU
abstract: Online path planning for unmanned aerial vehicles requires dynamic trade-offs between path length and safety risks in complex and dynamically changing threat environments. However, most of recent researches either do not consider multi-UAV cooperative operations or do not incorporate online responses to dynamic threats. To address these limitations, a knee point-guided dynamic multi-objective path planning method is proposed in this paper. The main contributions are as follows: 1). A dual-UAV cooperative jamming mechanism is proposed to actively respond to threat risks and improve mission success rates; 2). A dynamic role-switching mechanism is developed under a leader-follower framework to enhance the system’s flexibility in responding to threats; 3). A knee point-guided change response mechanism is constructed, which integrates neighborhood sampling and imbalanced transfer learning to adaptively adjust the response strategy based on the degree of environmental change. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed mothed can effectively and rapidly respond to environmental changes, enabling UAVs to generate feasible and efficient online flight paths under complex dynamic threat conditions.

title: Communication-Constrained Remote Power Grid Resilience Enhancement: Coordinated Planning of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Mobile Energy Storage Vehicles
authors: Hongzhen Wang, Boyu Qin, Shidong Hong, Fan Li, Tao Ding
abstract: The underdeveloped power and communication infrastructures in remote areas are prone to simultaneous failure during extreme disasters, exacerbating the difficulty of power system recovery. Mobile energy storage vehicles (MESVs) and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can achieve the coordination of power and communication, providing precise and effective power support for remote power grid. However, the coordinated planning of MESVs and UAVs is constrainted by factors such as endurance, coverage, and connection. The relationship between these constraints and the performance of dispatching flexibility and resilience support has not been well investigated. This paper proposes a deployment-dispatching two-stage coordinated planning framework of UAVs and MESVs to enhance the remote power grid resilience. The topology-based UAV deployment model and the power flow based MESV deployment mode is proposed. The coordinated dispatching model for UAVs and MESVs considering communication availability is proposed. The test case on IEEE 33 node system and application case on a remote power grid in Northwest China is conducted. The results show that the spatial dispatching flexibility in exchange for communication and traffic constraints significantly improves the resilience support performance of MESVs.

title: A Compact Design of Branch-Line Coupler Based Reactively Compensated Parallel Coupled-Lines
authors: Niwat Angkawisittpan, Somkuan Srisawat, Somchat Sonasang, Ravee Phromloungsri
abstract: A planar 3-dB quadrature branch-line coupler (BLC) employing simultaneous inductive and capacitive compensation of parallel coupled microstrip lines is proposed for size-constrained RF front-ends. Closed-form synthesis equations derived from even/odd-mode analysis enable a first-pass design without electromagnetic optimization. Fabricated on Rogers AD260, the 3.0 GHz prototype occupies only 0.54 λ2g , corresponding to a 46% size reduction compared to the conventional λ/4 implementation, while achieving |S11| < −23 dB, isolation > 25 dB, insertion loss of 3.25 ± 0.25 dB, amplitude imbalance ≤ 0.4 dB, and phase error < 1.2° over a 27% fractional bandwidth. Sensitivity analysis confirms stable operation under ±10% component tolerances, and the measured results deviate from theory by less than 3%. Compared with recent compact BLC designs, this work achieves the highest bandwidth-to-area ratio using only two chip inductors and two planar capacitors, while remaining fully compatible with single-layer PCB fabrication and extensible to multiband or reconfigurable hybrid circuits.

title: Accelerating Time-Evolving Multiphysics Uncertainty Quantification of Millimeter Wave Components Using Reusable Preconditioning
authors: Hao-Xuan Zhang, Shi-Wei Guo, Lin-Peng Yu, Yi-Yao Wang, Hao Xie, Liang Zhou, Wen-Yan Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Transient electromagnetic (EM)–thermal– mechanical uncertainty quantification (UQ) is computationally demanding because it requires repeated solutions of many closely related EM systems across time steps, coupling iterations, and random samples. This article presents an efficient finite-element multiphysics framework that couples the frequency-domain Helmholtz equationwith time-domain heat conduction and evaluates thermomechanical responses through small-deformation thermoelasticity. To avoid repeated remeshing in large-sample studies, thin metallization layers are modeled by a reduced-dimensional thermal formulation on a fixed mesh. To accelerate the repeated EM solving process, a reusable LU-based preconditioning strategy is incorporated into the coupling loop. UQ is then performed by nonintrusive polynomial chaos expansion (PCE), from which confidence bands and Sobol sensitivity indices are extracted for both time- and frequency-domain quantities of interest (QoI). Numerical results on a substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) filter and a silicon-based D-band diplexer confirm that reusable preconditioning and fixed-mesh thin-layer modeling can substantially reduce the computational cost of large-sample transient UQ. This makes the proposed framework suitable for reliability-oriented analysis and design of millimeter-wave components operating under high-power and high-temperature conditions.

title: Shadow Distance Analysis of Agrivoltaic System in Bangladesh: From Geometry to Simulation
authors: Zihan Rejwana, Mohammad Tasnimul Hasan, Md. Mosaddequr Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study compares two methods for calculating shadow distance in agrivoltaic systems: geometrical analysis and 3D modeling. Simulations were conducted for an agricultural site during the extended shading period of winter to assess model performance. A MATLAB program was developed using modified equations for geometrical calculations, while SketchUp was employed for 3D simulation and validation. The results indicate that the geometrical model maintains an average error of less than 6 % compared to the 3D approach. These findings suggest that geometrical modeling can serve as a reliable tool for shadow assessment in agrivoltaic system design.

title: MawaNet: A Custom Deep Classifier for Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: Md Nuruzzaman, Afsana Nadia, Md Masum Billal, Khan Md Shibli Nomani, Md Tarikul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Credit card fraud detection is still challenging in the financial industry. It indicates the necessity of modern machine learning techniques to detect abnormal transactions without erroneously classifying normal account activities. The existing studies heavily rely on traditional machine learning and deep learning approaches, with a lack of optimization and regularization for tabular data. We propose a deep learning architecture called MawaNet for the detection of credit card fraudulent activity with proper regularization to work effectively on tabular data. The extensive experiments have been applied to ten different machine learning algorithms.
