The framework consists of three subnetworks for surface-normal estimation, depth reconstruction, and current refinement, together with an intermediate physics-guided module for coarse current initialization based on physical optics (PO). By progressively embedding geometric cues and electromagnetic priors, the proposed method effectively alleviates the ill-posed geometry-to-current mapping and enforces physical consistency. Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed model accurately reconstructs high-fidelity surface currents and radar cross sections (RCS) at 1GHz, achieving agreement comparable to the method of moments (MoM) results while requiring only a single image as input. Experimental validations using 3D-printed objects further confirm the robustness of the framework under real-world measurement uncertainties, including illumination variations and sensor noise. The results show that the proposed method eliminates the need for full geometric modeling and offers strong potential for real-time radar signature prediction, EM simulation acceleration, and autonomous perception.

title: Generalized Direct Grid Current Control for Voltage Sensorless Grid-connected Inverter with Output LCL Filter
authors: Jinli Zhu, Bokang Zhou, Yuan Li, Fang Z. Peng, Brad Lehman
abstract: This paper presents a novel grid voltage sensorless strategy that enables the rapid synchronization and robust control of an LCL-filtered grid-connected inverter. By exclusively measuring the grid current in combination with internal modulation voltage feedforward, the need for AC voltage sensing and PLLs is eliminated, and the inverter control performance under weak grid conditions is significantly improved. The corresponding real-time implementation involves zero-crossing phase detection and fundamental voltage feedforward generation, where the feedforward decoupling effectively enhances the control stability. The performance of the proposed strategy is compared with that of a classical PI control scheme requiring both grid voltage and current sensors. The low-voltage ride-through capability and the robustness to grid impedance variations are verified through simulation. Experimental results effectively validate the performance and reliability of the proposed strategy under normal operation, weak grid, and unbalanced grid conditions.

title: R BD: A Reconstruction-Based Method for Generalizable and Efficient Detection of Fake Images_supp1-3692527.pdf
authors: Zhongjie Ba
abstract: Recently, reconstruction-based methods have gained attention for AIGC image detection. These methods leverage pre-trained diffusion models to reconstruct inputs and measure residuals for distinguishing real from fake images. Their key advantage lies in reducing reliance on dataset-specific artifacts and improving generalization under distribution shifts. However, they are limited by significant inefficiency due to multi-step inversion and reconstruction, and their reliance on diffusion backbones further limits generalization to other generative paradigms such as GANs. In this paper, we propose a novel fake image detection framework, called R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD, built upon two key designs: (1) G-LDM, a unified reconstruction model that simulates the generation behaviors of VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models, thereby broadening the detection scope beyond prior diffusion-only approaches; and (2) a residual bias calculation module that distinguishes real and fake images in a single inference step, which is a significant efficiency improvement over existing methods that typically require 20<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$+$</tex-math></inline-formula> steps. Extensive experiments on the benchmark from 10 public datasets demonstrate that R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD is over 22× faster than existing reconstruction-based methods while achieving superior detection accuracy. In cross-dataset evaluations, it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by an average of 13.87%, showing strong efficiency and generalization across diverse generative methods. The code and dataset used for evaluation are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/QingyuLiu/RRBD</uri>.

title: Lightweight Multi-Channel Generative Adversarial Network for Synthesizing Brain Perfusion CT Series from Healthy Subjects: Toward Radiation Dose Reduction in Clinical Imaging_supp1-3696320.docx
authors: Yuanyuan Yang

title: T-LEAP Posit: A Truncated Logarithmic Enhanced Arithmetic Posit Multiplier for Area–Power Efficient and Accuracy-Robust Deep Neural Networks.
authors: Thalla Narasimha Swetha, Vignav Ramkumar, Syed Ershad Ahmed
abstract: Posit arithmetic offers improved numerical accuracy and dynamic range compared to IEEE 754 floating-point representation. However, hardware implementation of posit multiplication remains complex due to costly decoding and mantissa computation stages, limiting its suitability for resource-constrained systems. This limitation becomes critical in applications such as deep learning inference,where low delay and reduced chip area are often more critical than very high arithmetic precision. In many deep neural networks, increasing multiplier precision beyond a certain level does not lead to maintaining the model accuracy. As a result, implementing fully precise arithmetic circuits can increase hardware cost without providing improvement in inference accuracy. To address this challenge, an efficient approximate posit multiplier is designed to reduce hardware overhead while preserving computational accuracy for deep neural networks. The proposed architecture integrates a posit decoder that replaces conventional regime extraction by eliminating adder, multiplexer and complement operation, with a compact XOR-driven approach with leading-bit removal of regime bits, thereby reducing hardware overhead and improving energy efficiency. A new logarithmic-truncated mantissa multiplier that uses truncated shifter-based operations for logarithmic multiplication is proposed, significantly reducing hardware complexity. In the input decoding stage, a streamlined XOR-based approach achieves a reduction in power–delay product of 50-61% and area reduction of 3-20% compared to existing decoders. The proposed T-LEAP multiplier demonstrates a notable reduction in hardware cost, achieving up to 20-46% lower area, 21-66% lower power consumption, 11-19% delay reduction, and 31-73% improvement in power-delay product (PDP) compared to existing posit multiplier architectures. Experimental results show that the deep neural networks using T-LEAP multiplier maintain over 90% classification accuracy on LeNet-5 and ResNet-20 across different benchmark datasets such as MNIST and CIFAR-10. These findings indicate that T-LEAP multiplier offers an effective trade-off between hardware efficiency and computational accuracy, making it a promising solution for deploying deep neural networks.

title: Stable Learning for Trustworthy Modeling in Industrial Processes With Small Samples
authors: Liang Cao, Youqing Wang, Yankai Cao, Fan Yang, Jicong Fan, Yan Qin, Bhushan Gopaluni, Richard D. Braatz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Developing trustworthy models for industrial processes is challenging due to changing operating conditions and limited data. In this work, we present an innovative and effective framework for establishing trustworthy process models from limited data. Our approach introduces the uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) algorithm to uncover the essential low-dimensional structure in real industrial data, which allows high-quality virtual sample generation (VSG) that captures the underlying process dynamics. Furthermore, we propose a new algorithm for stable learning that uses sample reweighting to effectively mitigate spurious correlations that can undermine the stability and reliability of the model. Two case studies, the Tennessee Eastman process (TEP) and a commercial fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. In the Tennessee Eastman benchmark, the proposed method reduces the average error by 12.5% compared with VSG alone and by 47.8% compared with the OLS baseline. In the FCC case study, it further achieves a 41.5% RMSE reduction compared with the strongest competing UMAP-augmented model. Furthermore, the proposed framework maintains robust performance under changing operating conditions. Our framework enables the development of trustworthy industrial process models from limited data, which offers a powerful method to improve operational safety and efficiency in real-world applications.

title: Anchor-Driven Compact Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction_supp1-3695021.mp4
authors: Yiling Xu
abstract: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods typically rely on per-Gaussian deformation from a canonical space to target frames, which overlooks the strong redundancy among spatially and temporally adjacent Gaussian primitives and leads to suboptimal efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose ADC-GS++, an anchor-driven compact Gaussian splatting frame work for efficient and high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. Specifically, ADC-GS++ organizes Gaussian primitives into an anchor-based canonical representation, enabling attribute sharing across local regions. To efficiently model dynamic scenes, we introduce a static–dynamic decomposition mechanism and further employ a coarse-to-fine deformation strategy driven by dynamic anchors at multiple granularities. In addition, a unified rate-distortion optimization is adopted to achieve a balanced trade-off between storage efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Furthermore, a temporal significance-based anchor refinement strategy is employed to dynamically grow and prune anchors, allowing robust adaptation to complex and large-scale motions. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world dynamic scene datasets demonstrate that ADC-GS++ significantly improves rendering speed over deformation-based approaches by 300% 700%, while maintaining competitive rendering quality. More over, ADC-GS++ achieves a more favorable rate-distortion trade off, resulting in substantially reduced storage consumption across different bitrate settings.

title: Toward Explainable Trust: Polynomial-Driven Bidomain Memory-Gain-Scheduling of Active Suspension Systems via Mode-Affected Physical-Layer Authentication
authors: Xingchen Shao, Xiang-Peng Xie
abstract: The evolution of 6G networks empowers the integration of sensing, communication, computing, and control, driving mining vehicles toward unprecedented mobility. Although this deep wireless integration enhances operational efficiency, it comes at the cost of breaking down the physical isolation of vehicular control loops. Such architectural openness invalidates the conventional trust-by-default assumption, directly exposing active suspension systems to mode-affected cyber-physical threats that secretly manipulate operational data. To reconstruct systemic reliability and shift the overall paradigm toward explainable trust, this article proposes a resilience-oriented co-design scheme. Guided by the principle of building trusted sensing through authentication, we establish a mode-affected signature of hardware emanation (MA-SHE) model. This model quantitatively characterizes the residual risk, explicitly explaining the necessary tradeoff between security stringency and real-time control availability. To execute the concept of using control mechanisms to mitigate authentication inaccuracies, we synthesize a polynomial-driven memory-gain-scheduling strategy. This strategy dynamically compensates for the quantified vulnerabilities, guaranteeing suspension stability even when the authentication tolerates a subset of spoofed signals. Furthermore, to handle the complex transition probabilities inherent in this stochastic environment, we develop a bidomain framework. It reconstructs the incomplete probability matrix via polytopic formulation in the structural domain and utilizes homogeneous-polynomial theory in the design domain, substantially enlarging the feasible solution space. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments validate that the proposed framework drastically reduces the conservative attenuation index and improves vibration suppression by approximately 20%, embedding explainable trust into the closed-loop architecture to ensure dynamic resilience.

title: Long-Reach, High-Capacity Non-Orthogonal PAM4–OFDM Optical Access Transmission Enabled by Advanced Chromatic Dispersion Pre-compensation
authors: Joungmoon Lee, Jinwoo Park, Sang-Kook Han
abstract: We propose an advanced non-orthogonal transmission framework for converged wired (four-level pulse amplitude modulation, PAM4) and wireless (radio-over-fiber orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing) services in long-reach passive optical networks. In contrast to conventional approaches that focus primarily on signal restoration, the proposed method re-engineers chromatic dispersion pre-compensation to suppress mutual interference, thereby enlarging the interference-free overlapping bandwidth available to heterogeneous traffic. By leveraging the distance-dependent power-fading nulls in C-band optical transmission, spectral coexistence is achieved without conventional orthogonal resource allocation. Experimental validation over a 70-km single-mode fiber link demonstrates that both services maintain bit error rates below their respective forward error correction (FEC) thresholds despite stringent hardware constraints. Specifically, the 12.5-Gbaud PAM4 signal exhibits a negligible power penalty of less than 1 dB at the soft-decision FEC threshold relative to the optical back-to-back reference. These results confirm that the proposed scheme provides a robust solution for high-capacity converged optical access networks.

title: Strong electric field measurement method based on Rydberg atom Stark effect
authors: Sheng Yan, Dongping Xiao, Huaiqing Zhang, Dongyang Wen
abstract: Quantum electric field measurement based on the Stark effect of Rydberg atoms has the advantages of high precision, traceability, and self-calibration. However, the current perturbation-based modeling method for the relationship between field strength and energy level shift only applies to weak fields. In applications such as power systems, electric fields can reach the kV/m level. Under such electric fields, the Rydberg state cannot ignore the strong coupling effect between adjacent high l states, and the relationship model established by perturbation approximation is no longer accurate. This paper introduces the Landau–Zener (L-Z) tunneling model to address this limitation and describes the coupling between energy levels. It is used to construct the Rydberg atomic Hamiltonian, which includes high l state coupling. We calculate the eigenenergies of Rydberg atoms at various electric-field strengths via matrix diagonalization, thereby obtaining a discrete mapping between field strength and energy-level shift in the strong-field regime. Subsequently, the hybrid Levenberg–Marquardt (LM) and Bayesian polynomial regression (BPR) neural network (LM-BPR) was constructed to train the discrete mapping into a continuous prediction model. In addition, the dynamic programming (DP) algorithm performs global ridge tracking on the electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) spectrum and extracts frequency shift information from the complex EIT spectrum. Combining the above methods, strong electric field inversion based on Rydberg atoms was achieved. Experiments using the 28D5/2 Rydberg state demonstrate electric-field measurements up to 7 kV/m, with a relative error of 2.9%.

title: Long-Term Optimal Incentives for Differential-Privacy Federated Learning: A Multi-Stage Game Approach
authors: Liang Xie, Yuntao Wang, Hengzhi Wang, Laizhong Cui
abstract: Differential-privacy federated learning (DP-FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm capable of mitigating the inherent threat of traditional FL architectures that are vulnerable to inferential attacks due to the frequent exchange and updating of model parameters. However, existing DP-FL frameworks often assume that the client's perturbations remain constant throughout the FL process, while ignoring the varying influence of the client's perturbations in distinct communication rounds on the model performance. Besides, existing DP-FL frameworks posit the FL server as a fully rational actor, thereby neglecting the bounded rationality that the FL server may exhibit in the face of risk and uncertainty. In this paper, we propose a novel long-term (i.e., throughout the FL process) privacy-preserving FL framework to address the optimal incentive design, in the presence of the bounded rationality inherent in the FL server and the dynamic influence of perturbations on model performance. Specifically, we first investigate the impact of local perturbations of the client on the model's convergence performance in different communication rounds, elucidating the trade-off between learning performance and privacy loss. Then, to reconcile learning performance with privacy loss, we design a long-term privacy-preserving incentive scheme, where the interactions between clients and the FL server throughout the FL process are modeled as a multi-stage privacy-preserving game. Furthermore, by applying prospect theory (PT) to formulate the risk-aware behavior of the bounded rationality FL server, we employ contract theory to derive the equilibrium of the game, thereby ensuring optimality and fairness. Finally, extensive simulations illustrate that our scheme can motivate clients to provide high-quality models and improve the accuracy of the global model, compared with benchmarks.

title: Broadband Microwave Signal Processing via Low-Frequency-Detected Photonic Correlation in Parallel Bidirectional Frequency-Shifting Loops
authors: Di Peng, Zewen Zhu, Jingyi Zhang, Shuoyang Qiu, Lingjie Zhang, Songnian Fu, Yuwen Qin
abstract: We propose and demonstrate a photonic correlator based on parallel optical bidirectional frequency-shifting loops (BFSLs) and low-frequency detection, which enables broadband microwave signal processing with an extended time window. By mapping correlation results to low-frequency spectral envelopes, the system avoids the need for high-speed electronics. Precise control of relative delays in each BFSL ensures high temporal resolution and broad operating bandwidth. Moreover, the overall time window is significantly expanded via sequentially stitching spectra from multiple BFSLs. Both numerical simulation and experimental verification based on two parallel BFSLs confirm the feasibility of the scheme. Experiments demonstrate autocorrelation of sinusoidal signals, band-limited white noise, linear frequency-modulated (LFM) signals, and fourth-order raised-cosine shaped nonlinear frequency-modulated signals, achieving a doubled time window with temporal resolution ranging from 20 ps to 1 ns. In a radar ranging scenario using LFM signals centered at 4-10 GHz and with 1.5-4 GHz bandwidth, the system achieves a ranging error of 0.88 cm, a range resolution of 0.375 cm, and a time-window extension factor of 2. The scheme offers a practical solution for high-resolution broadband correlation with an extended time window, showing great potential for advanced radar and communication systems.

title: Full Index Mapping Multi-Domain AFDM-IM for High-Mobility Communications
authors: Shunyan Huang, Wenjing Zheng, Taojie Zhu, Ting Qi, Lei Wang
abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) is an emerging multicarrier modulation technique for high-mobility communications. However, existing AFDM index modulation (IM) schemes suffer from two critical limitations: unreliable index detection caused by subcarrier activation and limited index-space expansion due to single-domain indexing. To overcome both issues simultaneously, this letter proposes an enhanced multi-domain (EMD) IM scheme for AFDM, termed EMD-AFDM-IM. Unlike existing AFDM-IM schemes, EMD-AFDM-IM adopts fully active subcarriers and conveys additional index bits through a novel joint multi-domain indexing mechanism. Furthermore, a dedicated low-complexity progressive hybrid detection algorithm (PHDA) is developed, and the average bit error probability (ABEP) of the proposed scheme is analyzed. Simulation results validate the analysis and demonstrate that EMD-AFDM-IM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art benchmark schemes over doubly dispersive channels, achieving notable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains.

title: Flexbee: A Grasping and Perching UAV Based on Soft Vector-Propulsion Nozzle
authors: Yue Wang, Lixian Zhang, Yangguang Liu, Xuwei Yang, Yimin Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: To improve environmental adaptability maneuverability, and structural reuse, for cooperative tasks in existing grasping/perching uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), we propose a soft vector-propulsion nozzle (SVPN) and develop Flexbee, a grasping and perching UAV built upon this concept, and it enables fully-actuated flight as well as grasping and perching. By redesigning both the mechanism and the system architecture, the SVPN consolidates multiple functions into a single modular unit, thereby expanding the achievable grasping range and improving perching versatility compared with add-on, functionally independent mechanisms. Moreover, fully-actuated flight enhances controllability and operational robustness during aerial interaction. This study covers the complete system design, including mechanical design, dynamic modeling, and controller design, which demonstrates the novelty and feasibility of the proposed platform through real-world experiments.

title: Adaptive Neural In-Loop Filtering via Boundary-Aware Skipping and Time-Distortion Optimization
authors: Hyukmin Kwon, Juyeon Seo, Donghyun Kim, Sung-Chang Lim, Hyunsuk Ko
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: “Let me try again” – Leveraging Social Interfaces to Improve Trust in Automation for Teleoperation
authors: Pouya Pournasir, Daniel J. Rea
abstract: None

title: SplatFusion: Training-Free 3D Scene Completion From Sparse Views Using Temporal Diffusion Priors and Gaussian Splatting
authors: Tanveer Younis, Dawar Khan, Zhanglin Cheng
abstract: Reconstructing complete 3D scenes from extremely sparse viewpoints (e.g., 2-3 wide-baseline images) remains a core yet unsolved challenge. Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and neural rendering methods degrade severely when view overlap is limited, often producing incomplete or geometrically distorted results. We introduce SplatFusion, a reconstruction framework that requires no training or fine-tuning of diffusion models, instead relying solely on pretrained video diffusion priors to synthesize missing scene content plausibly. Our core idea is a Scene-Consistent Temporal Guidance (SCTG) mechanism that tightly couples 3D structure with generative diffusion models. Specifically, SCTG conditions video diffusion on sequences rendered from the evolving 3DGS representation, enforcing both spatial alignment with geometry and temporal coherence across synthesized frames. These refined views are back-projected to densify and correct the 3D scene iteratively. Extensive experiments on diverse realworld datasets demonstrate that SplatFusion consistently outperforms existing sparse-view reconstruction methods. Evaluations using VLM-based perceptual scores and the MEt3R metric for geometric consistency show clear gains in visual fidelity and temporal coherence, even in scenarios where previous approaches fail. Our training-free framework opens new possibilities for practical 3D reconstruction applications where dense view acquisition is impractical.

title: CamC2V: Context-aware Controllable Video Generation
authors: Luis Denninger, Sina Mokhtarzadeh Azar, Juergen Gall
abstract: Recently, image-to-video (I2V) diffusion models have demonstrated impressive scene understanding and generative quality, incorporating image conditions to guide generation. However, these models primarily animate static images without extending beyond their provided context. Introducing additional constraints, such as camera trajectories, can enhance diversity but often degrade visual quality, limiting their applicability for tasks requiring faithful scene representation. We propose CamC2V, a context-to-video <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(C 2 V)$</tex> model that integrates multiple image conditions as context with 3D constraints alongside camera control to enrich both global semantics and fine-grained visual details. This enables more coherent and context-aware video generation. Moreover, we motivate the necessity of temporal awareness for an effective context representation. Our comprehensive study on the RealEstate10K dataset demonstrates a 24.09 % (FVD) improvement in visual quality and camera controllability. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/LDenninger/CamC2V.

title: Improving Multi-View Reconstruction via Texture-Guided Gaussian-Mesh Joint Optimization
authors: Zhejia Cai, Puhua Jiang, Shiwei Mao, Hongkun Cao, Ruqi Huang
abstract: Reconstructing real-world objects from multi-view images is essential for applications in 3D editing, AR/VR, and digital content creation. Existing methods typically prioritize either geometric accuracy (Multi-View Stereo) or photorealistic rendering (Novel View Synthesis), often decoupling geometry and appearance optimization, which hinders downstream editing tasks. This paper advocates an unified treatment on geometry and appearance optimization for seamless Gaussian-mesh joint optimization. More specifically, we propose a novel framework that simultaneously optimizes mesh geometry (vertex positions and faces) and vertex colors via Gaussian-guided mesh differentiable rendering, leveraging photometric consistency from input images and geometric regularization from normal and depth maps. The obtained high-quality 3D reconstruction can be further exploit in down-stream editing tasks, such as relighting and shape deformation. Our code will be released in https://github.com/zhejia01/TexGuided-GS2Mesh

title: NeuralFur: Animal Fur Reconstruction from Multi-View Images
authors: Vanessa Sklyarova, Berna Kabadayi, Anastasios Yiannakidis, Giorgio Becherini, Michael J. Black, Justus Thies
abstract: Reconstructing realistic animal fur geometry from images is a challenging task due to the fine-scale details, selfocclusion, and view-dependent appearance of fur. In contrast to human hairstyle reconstruction, there are also no datasets that can be leveraged to learn a fur prior for different animals. In this work, we present a first multi-viewbased method for high-fidelity 3D fur modeling of animals using a strand-based representation, leveraging the general knowledge of a vision language model. Given multiview RGB images, we first reconstruct a coarse surface geometry using traditional multi-view stereo techniques. We then use a vision language model (VLM) system to retrieve information about the realistic length structure of the fur for each part of the body. We use this knowledge to construct the animal's furless geometry and grow strands atop it. The fur reconstruction is supervised with both geometric and photometric losses computed from multi-view images. To mitigate orientation ambiguities stemming from the Gabor filters that are applied to the input images, we additionally utilize the VLM to guide the strands' growth direction and their relation to the gravity vector that we incorporate as a loss. With this new schema of using a VLM to guide 3D reconstruction from multi-view inputs, we show generalization across a variety of animals with different fur types. For additional results and code, please refer to https://neuralfur.is.tue.mpg.de.

title: Autonomous Security Framework for AI-Native 6G Networks: Architecture and Threat Taxonomy
authors: Faten Hasni, Arij Salha Nefzi, Akram Hakiri, Abderrazak Jemai
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Sixth-Generation (6 G) networks are envisioned as AI-native systems, where large language models (LLMs) and generative AI orchestrate intent-based networking, autonomous service management, and dynamic slicing. While these capabilities promise unprecedented efficiency and flexibility, they also introduce novel security challenges, i.e., LLM-driven controllers are vulnerable to intent injection, model inversion, and adversarial manipulation, potentially impacting core, edge, and userplane functions. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the security landscape for LLM-driven 6 G networks, mapping known LLM vulnerabilities to telecom network functions, and analyzing approaches for autonomous detection, mitigation, and self-healing. We examine multi-agent red teaming, zero-touch security frameworks, and AI-powered orchestration (SMO), and discuss alignment with O-RAN and ETSI ZSM (Zero-Touch Service Management) standards. Our review identifies open challenges, research trends, and practical considerations for building resilient, self-defending 6 G networks.

title: Explainable Kidney Stone Detection from CT Scans: Integrating Grad-CAM with YOLO Model
authors: Fatma Safi, Fadi Kacem, Walid Jallouli, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in medical imaging has transformed diagnostic practices. However, the opacity of many AI models, particularly deep learning systems, poses significant challenges in clinical settings. Explainable AI (XAI) emerges as a cutting-edge solution, aiming to enhance the interpretability and trustworthiness of AI outputs in healthcare. In this study, we provide a comparative analysis of multiple XAI methods to assess their effectiveness in improving interpretability. Additionally, we propose an explainable framework that integrates YOLO11 with Grad-CAM to both detect kidney stones and provide visual explanations of the model's decision-making process. Quantitative evaluation demonstrated high segmentation performance with a precision of 96.6 % and recall of 97.8 %. Qualitative assessment showed that Grad-CAM effectively highlighted clinically relevant areas, increasing model transparency in real-world diagnosis. The proposed approach bridges the gap between accurate automated kidney stone segmentation and clinical explainability, providing a robust tool for computerassisted diagnosis.

title: Enhanced Stacking Ensemble Using Multi-Level Meta-Learning for Student Course Evaluations Sentiment Analysis
authors: Ramzi El Habbal, Ziad Doughan, Sobhi Abou Chahine
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Student voice is an important element in the quality assurance and enhancement of Universities; however, when it comes to assessing student course evaluations, the most common way has been through basic statistical analysis and/or manual review procedures that have trouble in capturing the complex and subtle emotional nuances rooted in student responses to course evaluations. In this study, we propose an enhanced stacking ensemble framework using seven different base models - Logistic regression with both L1 and L2 regularization, Naïve Bayes, Random forest, Extra trees, gradient boosting, calibrated support vector machine (SVM), and fine-tuned RoBERTA transformer as well as sophisticated multi-level meta learning. Advanced feature extraction techniques are employed to extract 64 meta features from course evaluations, including entropy, confidence margins, probabilities as well as VADER sentiment scoring and linguistic metrics. Class imbalance is addressed by utilizing intelligent sampling with SMOTE-Tomek linkages, and the final prediction is made by an ensemble of meta learner models (XGBoost, Light GBM, CatBoost, and logistic regression with weighted voting) that optimize the final model's predictions. Results obtained on a dataset of 5563 course evaluations comments, where each feedback was labeled with a sentiment, indicate excellent performance (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=96.77 \%$</tex>, F1 macro <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$= 96.39 \%$</tex>, Precision <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=95.94 \%$</tex>, Recall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=96.88 \%$</tex>), which represents a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 9 8 \%}$</tex> increase compared to the best baseline stacking approach for evaluating the sentiment in student comments. Therefore, the proposed framework can automatically analyze large amounts of comments and provide a scalable and timely solution for enhancing educational programs and improving teaching quality at universities.

title: Pretrained Biomedical Foundation Models: Comparative Analysis and Practical Applications in Healthcare
authors: Mounira Zouaghi, Fadi Kacem, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of pretrained biomedical foundation models has transformed healthcare AI, yet researchers and clinicians face persistent challenges in selecting the most appropriate model for a given application, as architectures, pretraining strategies, and computational requirements vary substantially across systems. This review provides a structured comparative synthesis of representative pretrained biomedical foundation models to support informed model selection for clinical and research applications. Seven models spanning encoderonly, decoder-only, and multimodal architectures and three pretraining strategies, continued pretraining, from-scratch pretraining, and instruction tuning, are compared across biomedical NLP benchmarks covering named entity recognition, question answering, and clinical reasoning. A multi-criteria framework evaluates each model across architecture, strategy, vocabulary type, task suitability, scale, and open-weight accessibility. Pretraining strategy and vocabulary alignment appear to be more influential factors in downstream performance than parameter count alone: from-scratch domain pretraining tends to outperform continued pretraining in several reported benchmarks, and instruction tuning has been shown to enable expert-level performance on specific clinical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., USMLE). These findings suggest that model selection should be guided by corpus-task alignment rather than scale, and that openweight alternatives can recover a meaningful fraction of closedmodel performance at lower deployment cost. Practical selection guidelines, current limitations including bias, hallucination, and computational accessibility, and future research directions are discussed.

title: Numerical Analysis and Performance Optimization of $\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$-Based Perovskite Solar Cells Under Non-Ideal Conditions
authors: Tapos Chandra Saha, Md. Abul Bashar, Ashikur Rahaman Siam, Sadikatul Islam Sara, Md Shahidul Islam, Md. Shafayet Hossen, Sk Minhajul Abedin, Arifuzzaman Rajib
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have become the cell of the future in terms of photovoltaic because of their adjustable bandgap, good optoelectronic characteristics, and low cost of manufacture. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$</tex> perovskite solar cell was numerically studied through SCAPS-1D simulation software in the present work to determine the influence of the non-ideal conditions and optimize the performance of the device. The suggested design is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F T O} / \mathbf{Z n S e} / \mathbf{R b G e B r}_{\mathbf{3}} / \mathbf{C u}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O} / \mathbf{N i}$</tex>. Systematic analysis was done to understand the effect of different non-ideal parameters such as optical reflectance, series shunt resistance, bulk defect density, and interface recombination. The impact of the absorber layer which is doped with density and thickness variation. Firstly, analyzed to determine the best absorber characteristics through different ETLs and HTLs layers. Then shows the effect of ZnSe as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{E T L}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C u}_{2} \mathbf{O}$</tex> as a HTL layer. The analysis of temperature dependence of the device performance has been conducted between temperatures of 278 K and 338 K, and the results indicate a decrease in Voc and PCE with temperature. Lastly, there was a performance comparison of a non-ideal and ideal scenario. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) was increased by 16.25 % in smaller non-ideal situations to 26.94 % with increasing Open-circuit voltage (Voc) 1.20 V and Fill Factor (FF) 85.93% in ideal situations, primarily caused by elimination of reflectance, series-shunt resistance and interface defects. The findings prove that the performance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$</tex>-based PSCs can be substantially increased with the help of careful interface and defect engineering.

title: Satellite-Derived Chlorophyll-a and Deep Learning for Enhanced Potential Fishing Zone Advisory in the Bay of Bengal
authors: Rafiul Awal, Nabila Rahman, Md. Maruf Rusafi Arnob, Md Abrar Nawar, Protiva Roy, Hafsa Binte Rashid
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bay of Bengal resources support Bangladesh marine fisheries, but Potential Fishing Zones (PFZs) identification remains difficult because of oceanographic variability at a seasonal scale. Existing threshold-based advisories created using sea surface temperature and chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) concentration are ineffective for spatio-temporal dynamics, particularly at monsoon transitions. In this work, we employ MODIS-Aqua Chl-a data (July–December 2023) and develop deep learning models—U-Net, ConvLSTM, DeepLabv3+, HRNetv2-w18, and SigFormer—to identify PFZ. The proposed framework allows seasonal PFZ pattern mapping, distinguishes monsoon–post-monsoon contrast, and provides high-resolution hotspot maps. Experimental results show that deep learning techniques are more accurate than traditional methods in boundary detection and spatial acuteness and demonstrate temporal generalization by forecasting PFZs in December from models learned using July–November data. The present work provides a foundation for developing dynamic PFZ advisory systems that can support sustainable marine fisheries in Bangladesh.

title: Attention-Based CNN Architectures for Automated Binary Classification of Salmon Fish Diseases
authors: Jasmin Jahan Puspo, Diya Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Fish health is important in aquaculture, and early disease detection can mitigate large-scale losses. In this study, an image dataset, SalmonScan, is utilized, which is divided into two classes: fresh salmon and infected salmon. A novel model has been proposed based on CNN architectures (InceptionV3, ResNet50, DenseNet-121) with convolutional block attention module (CBAM) mechanism. Experimental results show that InceptionV3 provides a superior classification accuracy of 96.50 % on training, and 92.40 % on testing. These findings demonstrate that attention-based deep learning methods can provide an effective tool for fish health diagnosis, potentially assist farmers make timely decisions, and improve aquaculture productivity.

title: Chaotic Dynamics Forced to Evolve Outside Sphere with Positive Divergence
authors: Jiri Petrzela
abstract: This paper contributes to discovering a new autonomous third-order deterministic dynamical system that is chaotic and exhibits some predefined unique properties. Mentioned uniqueness belongs to feature that stands hidden from the viewpoint of external observations: specific shaping of vector field divergence. Positive divergence inside a sphere with predefined radius forces strange attractor to evolve mostly outside these places. For novel mathematical model derived, it will be demonstrated that chaos is neither numerical artifact nor long transient motion. To prove robustness and correctness of numerical analysis, the dynamical system will be designed as a lumped electronic circuit and practically constructed. Finally, experimental measurement concludes a good correspondence between theory and practice.

title: Chirped-FBG-assisted weak-FBG demodulation method and its application in quasi-distributed temperature sensing and battery monitoring_supp1-3695147.docx
authors: Chunliu Zhao
abstract: Thermal runaway of the power battery can pose serious security risks. Therefore, it is imperative to monitor and control battery temperature in real time. To address the shortcomings of existing fiber Bragg grating (FBG) based temperature demodulation methods on efficiency, accuracy, and cost, a chirped-FBG (CFBG) slope-assisted weak-FBG (wFBG) demodulation method is proposed in this paper. The core mechanism of this method involves using the rising edge of the CFBG transmission spectrum as a wavelength-selective filter for wavelength-addressing series-connected identical wFBGs. In the proposed system, all optical components are fiber-coupled, thereby providing advantages such as low cost and compact size. The experimental results reveal that the system can operate stably at a demodulation rate of 1 Hz within 33°C to 107°C, with a demodulation standard error of 0.0831°C. The temperature demodulation resolution is 1.73×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-4</sup> °C, equivalent to a spectrometer demodulation resolution of 1.6 fm. By deploying the system on battery cells, online monitoring of global temperature in power batteries with high sensing density can be realized, providing a feasible technical solution to enhance monitoring precision, real-time performance, economic efficiency, and implementation convenience for the temperature monitoring requirements of battery management systems and thermal management systems.

title: A Full-Digital Heterogeneous Computing-in-Memory With Dual-Bit Serialized Weight Stream and Ping-Pong Computing for Efficient Transformer Acceleration
authors: Dengfeng Wang, Liukai Xu, Shuai Yuan, Weifeng He, Guanghui He, Fengbin Tu, Xueqing Li, Yanan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across various computer vision tasks. However, the self-attention mechanism in transformers incurs high processing loads and costly off-chip data movement in conventional von Neumann architectures. This paper proposes a full-digital heterogeneous Computing-in-Memory (CIM) in-situ hybridizing differential RRAM and SRAM to enable efficient on-chip weight storage and flexible row-column dual-directional access for efficient transformer acceleration. A Ping-Pong computing dataflow is introduced to maximize the throughput density by allocating static and dynamic computations across CIM macros. A low-toggle weight tuning algorithm is proposed to minimize the dynamic power under the dual-bit-serial CIM scheme by introducing only small weight rounding distance. Besides, a patch-channel skip mechanism is used to reduce the redundant computing for dynamic matrix with high sparsity. Experimental results on different transformer models show that the proposed DBS-CIM architecture reduces layer-wise computing energy by up to 59.9% compared to conventional digital CIM. Compared to prior homogeneous CIM designs, it achieves $2.0\times $ to $3.3\times $ higher end-to-end energy efficiency, and outperforms existing heterogeneous CIM designs by $2.13\times $ in computational density.

title: An IEEE802.15.4a/z/ab compatible IR-UWB 2TRX with full-duplex radar sensing and aliasing suppressing semi-synchronous TX
authors: Anoop Narayan Bhat, Erwin Allebes, Elbert Bechthum, Zule Xu, Stan Van Der Ven, Mohieddine El Soussi, Johan Van Den Heuvel, Paul Van Zeijl, Paul Mateman, Murat Eskiyerli, Stefano Traferro, Amirashkan Farsaei, Evgenii Tiurin, Sahan Gamage, Pepijn Boer, Igor Petrov, Jochem Govers, Huib Visser, Martijn Hijdra, Sharada Prasad Shantharam, Ivo Marques, Gururaja Kasanadi Ramachandra, Kaijie Ding, Ran Li, Aasish Boora, Peter Vis, Mario Konijnenburg, Arjan Breeschoten, Peng Zhang, Yao-Hong Liu, Christian Bachmann
abstract: This letter presents an 802.15.4ab/a/z compatible IR-UWB 2TRX highlighting a full-duplex-based radar, a semi-synchronous TX and TRX’s digital baseband. A capacitive tuning technique proposed in the electrical balance duplexer (EBD)-based duplex RF front-end (RF-FE) improves TX-antenna insertion loss by 1.4dB and the sensitivity of TX-RX isolation by 3.4dB. The TRX achieves >30dB isolation while transmitting 500MHz pulses centered at 8GHz over 2VSWR antenna variation. The proposed asynchronous first-order-hold (FoH) switched-capacitor power amplifier (SCPA) with a delay-locked-loop (DLL)-based calibration in TX achieves <−50dBr alias sidelobes, 5dB lower compared to the state of the art (SoTA). The digital baseband supports next generation 802.15.4ab’s sensing packets and high data rate (HDR) mode. The 2TRX is implemented on a 22nm CMOS process.

title: The accurate co-estimation of state of charge and state of energy for lithium-ion battery based on model-data fused framework_supp1-3697112.docx
authors: Bin Duan
abstract: Accurate estimation of the state of charge (SOC) and state of energy (SOE) is extremely difficult due to their sensitivity to operating conditions and complex coupling relationships. To address the problems, this paper proposes a co-estimation of SOC and SOE using model-data fusion. First, adaptive resampling and the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm are used to overcome the two core problems of particle filtering: particle degradation and sample depletion, achieving robust and accurate SOC estimation. Subsequently, a deep learning framework for SOE estimation is proposed, which includes feature extraction using two-dimensional convolutional neural network, importance enhancement using squeeze-and-excitation network attention mechanism, dependency capture using bidirectional long short-term memory neural network, and online correction using Gaussian filtering. Validation results at different temperatures demonstrate that the proposed method achieves maximum mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.806% and 1.144%, respectively, for SOC estimation, and converges to the true SOC value in just one time step. The SOE estimation shows a MAE of less than 0.827%, an RMSE of less than 1.005%, and a maximum error of less than 2.486%. The proposed hybrid model-data-driven co-estimation decouples the complex mapping relationship between parameters and improves accuracy by more than twice compared with the advanced methods in the field.

title: Real-Time Seat-Based Sitting Action Recognition Using Soft Pressure Sensors and Embedded Machine Learning_supp1-3693752.mp4
authors: Chi Cuong Vu
abstract: Seat-based sitting behavior monitoring is an important research area due to its impact on spinal health, work performance, and long-term comfort. However, challenges remain in accuracy, stability, cost, deployment flexibility, and data privacy. This paper proposes an integrated system combining soft pressure sensors, embedded machine learning, and a flexible communication architecture. The pressure sensor, fabricated from conductive textile and Au film, achieves a sensitivity of up to 0.46 kPa⁻¹ with response/recovery times of approximately 60/50 ms. The compact machine learning model (4.34 KB) achieves 99.7% accuracy in recognizing six sitting actions, including trunk tilting and sitting transitions. All processing and inference are performed locally on the embedded board, with only classification results transmitted to the cloud to enhance data security. The system focuses on trunk tilt and sitting transition recognition rather than full-body ergonomic posture assessment. These results demonstrate the feasibility of real-time, resource-efficient monitoring of sitting actions on edge IoT devices. This pilot study used data from one participant, with a fixed chair and sensor setup in a single recording session. Therefore, the results show feasibility under controlled conditions, not generalizability across subjects.

title: Longitudinal Feature Disentanglement With Cross-Time Contrastive Learning for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis
authors: Dong Dai, Na Zhang, Hao Zhang, Yan Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Longitudinal structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (sMRI) is pivotal for capturing dynamic brain atrophy patterns associated with the progression of Alzheimer's Disease (AD). However, effectively leveraging longitudinal data remains challenging due to the difficulty in modeling complex inter-temporal correlations and the presence of inherent noise and redundancy. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Longitudinal Feature Disentanglement Network for AD diagnosis and progression prediction. Specifically, we design a lightweight 3D convolutional encoder with a weight-sharing strategy to extract spatial representations from dual-time-point images. A Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-based mechanism is then introduced to disentangle these representations into stable features that encapsulate disease-specific identity and marginal features that account for noise and irrelevant variations. To further enhance the disentanglement capability, we incorporate a cross-time-point contrastive learning mechanism to enforce the temporal consistency of stable features, alongside a longitudinal feature reconstruction module that ensures the preservation of comprehensive information by recombining decoupled features. Extensive experiments conducted on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both AD diagnosis and MCI-to-AD conversion prediction tasks. Furthermore, visualization analysis validates the model's effectiveness in isolating discriminative brain regions and interpreting the decision-making process.

title: Challenges of the DTT ECH Launchers and Options Under Study
authors: Alessandro Bruschi, Massimiliano Bonesso, Daniele Busi, Carlo Cesari, Roman Chomicz, Razvan Dima, Francesco Fanale, Pierluigi Fanelli, Giacomo Favero, Eleonora Gajetti, Saul Garavaglia, Gustavo Granucci, Alfredo Pagliaro, Adriano Pepato, Paola Platania, Natale Rispoli, Afra Romano, Alessandra Salvitti, Laura Savoldi, Alessandro Simonetto, Matteo Turcato, Fabio Zanon
abstract: The electron cyclotron heating (ECH) launchers for the Divertor Tokamak Test (DTT) facility are currently being designed. The aim is to inject power in different plasma locations with high flexibility to allow plasma current ramp-up and ramp-down, central heating with tailored deposition profiles, and neoclassical tearing modes (NTMs) stabilization. Two antennas are developed, one for the equatorial port with six beam lines and one for the upper port with two beam lines, with similar front-steerable mirror modules. Each beam-line module is composed of a cooled corrugated waveguide launching a beam toward a fixed shaping mirror (M1) and then to the plasma-facing plane mirror (M2), movable around two axes, both cooled to sustain the high heat load due to the ohmic losses of the incident 1 MW beam and the plasma radiation. Thermal loads also include plasma and microwave stray radiation. Main structural loads on the mirrors and their supports, including the launcher supporting baseplate, are forces and torques due to the induced electro-magnetic (EM) currents, during normal operation and disruptions, interacting with the high magnetic field in which the mirrors and supports are immersed. Thermal and structural challenges, in particular on the M1 and M2 mirrors, have opposite solutions that require a delicate trade-off: optimal cooling requires high-thermal-conductivity materials such as copper alloys, while low-electrically-conductive materials are needed to have lower induced currents during disruptions. Solutions under study for the mirror cooling for minimal deformations and stresses, and for drive mechanisms, are presented. The possible reduction of induced currents during disruptions using different materials, supporting structures, and layouts is discussed.

title: Multichannel Reflectometer for Core-Localized Electron Density Fluctuation Measurement on the HL-3 Tokamak
authors: P. W. Shi, Z. C. Yang, W. Chen, L. W. Hu, A. S. Liang, M. Jiang, Z. B. Shi
abstract: A novel multichannel microwave reflectometer has been developed for core-localized density fluctuation measurement on the HL-3 tokamak. The microwave diagnostic is characterized by having 16 independent microwave sources, achieving beam separation or combination through a multiplexer technique, and optimizing signal quality via remote control of amplifiers. Sixteen working points are arranged in a wide frequency region of 50–75 GHz, so that the microwave diagnostic has a large measurement range. Depending on detail requirement of tokamak operation, the reflectometer can work with ordinary or extraordinary waves. It is sensitive to density fluctuation and the corresponding maximum measurable wavenumber reaches <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.16~\rm cm^{-1}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Recently, the microwave reflectometer has been installed on HL-3 tokamak and utilized to measure the spatial location of pressure gradient-driven kinetic ballooning modes.

title: DSF-DETR: End-to-End SAR Ship Detection via Spatial-Frequency State Space Modeling and Dynamic Attention
authors: Yunqi Zhang, Lin Bai, Wenqing Zhou, Danni Xue, Wentao Liu, Meng Hui
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship detection sup ports maritime surveillance, but remains difficult due to multiplicative speckle, complex coastal clutter, and large-scale variation in ship size. CNN-based detectors often trade noise suppression for detail and typically rely on post-processing such as non-maximum suppression (NMS). To overcome these limitations, we propose DSF-DETR, an end-to-end Transformer detector that decouples spatial and frequency processing to explicitly disentangle noise-sensitive fluctuations from target structures. Its Decoupled Spatial-Frequency Backbone (DSF-Net) extracts local geometry in a spatial branch, while a frequency branch combines a Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) with State Space Models (SSMs) to aggregate global context and suppress noise sensitive components. To avoid redundant computation over homogeneous background, Content-Aware Dynamic Attention (CADA) sparsifies the encoder, and an Efficient Re-parameterized Feature Pyramid (ERFP) strengthens multi-scale fusion and is re-parameterized into a single-branch structure for inference. Experiments on SSDD, HRSID and the SAR-Ship-Dataset show that DSF-DETR improves AP50 over RT-DETR by 2.0, 2.3, and 1.4 points, respectively, while reducing the parameters by 44.5% in SSDD. On the NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, the re parameterized model delivers a 53.3% FPS gain. Our code will be available at https://github.com/CHD-IPAC/DSF-DETR.

title: Complementary Standard and Inverted Power Normalized Gammachirp Cepstral Approach for Noise-Robust Speaker Identification
authors: Youssef Zouhir, Mohamed Zarka, Lilia El Amraoui, Kais Ouni
abstract: Despite the natural ability of humans to recognize speakers in noisy environments, automatic speaker recognition systems still face significant performance decrease under such conditions. This paper introduces a complementary feature extraction approach that concatenates the “inverted Power Normalized Gammachirp Cepstral” (invPNGC) coefficients with the original PNGC coefficients in order to enhance speaker recognition robustness in challenging acoustic environments. The invPNGC approach integrates two key innovations: (1) an “Inverted Gammachirp FilterBank” (invGcFB) using an “inverted Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth” (invERB) rate scale to provide high resolution at high frequencies, effectively capturing speaker-specific consonantal information often de-emphasized by traditional methods, and (2) a “Noise Suppression Module” (NSM) inspired by human auditory masking. Furthermore, the concatenation of the two coefficients yields the cmpPNGC to capture a comprehensive spectral representation. Comprehensive experiments on the TIMIT database mixed with AURORA noise types demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline techniques (PNGC, PNCC, MFCC, FDLP, and FDLP-Mel) using “Gaussian Mixture Model-Universal Background Model” (GMM-UBM) for speaker modeling. For instance, in Exhibition noise, cmpPNGC, invPNGC and PNGC achieved an average recognition accuracy of 77.38%, 73.87% and 66.75% (0-30dB SNR) respectively, compared to 50.35% for PNCC, 48.43% for MFCC, 49.96% for FDLP, and 36.45% for FDLP-Mel. These results confirm the efficacy of prioritizing high-frequency spectral cues for robust speaker identification. These findings suggest that the cmpPNGC feature offers a reliable solution for biometric authentication and forensic voice analysis systems deployed in challenging noise conditions.

title: Generative AI for Electromagnetic Metamaterials and Metasurfaces: A Comprehensive Review of Deep Learning, Evolutionary, and Physics-Informed Approaches
authors: Sumaia Jahan Mishu, Yaser Mike Banad, Sarah Sharif
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative learning has transformed the inverse design of electromagnetic (EM) metamaterials and metasurfaces by enabling more efficient, adaptive, and high-performance design workflows. However, existing surveys often discuss data-driven EM design in broad terms, while a unified review focused specifically on generative design pipelines across deep generative models, search-based and physics-driven optimization methods, and hybrid solver-in-the-loop frameworks remains limited. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of generative-learning-based methodologies for EM metamaterials and metasurfaces.We organize the field into three major groups: deep generative models, including generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, autoregressive models, diffusion models, and normalizing-flow-based approaches; search-based and physics-driven optimization methods, including genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning, and topology optimization; and hybrid pipelines that combine these paradigms to improve physical fidelity, constraint handling, and deployability. Beyond surveying these method families, we examine how practical considerations such as dataset requirements, computational cost, validation strategy, and fabrication feasibility shape their real-world applicability. Finally, we discuss emerging trends, open challenges, and future directions for generative inverse design in next-generation EM metamaterials and metasurfaces.

title: AdPrST:An Adversarial Graph Deep Learning Pre-clustering Framework for Deciphering Spatiotemporal Structures in Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics
authors: Haoyuan Ma, Shensi Huang, Haiyun Wang, Jianping Zhao, Junfeng Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Spatially Resolved Transcriptomics (SRT) has revolutionized our understanding of gene expression within tissue microenvironments, yet accurately deciphering spatiotemporal structures—encompassing spatial domain identification, trajectory inference, and pseudo-spatiotemporal map construction—in complex tissues remains a formidable challenge. AdPrST begins with a pre-clustering process on gene expression data to establish initial domain groupings. It then constructs dual-view graph structures using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for local similarities and r-radius for broader spatial contexts. Through adversarial self-supervised contrast, leveraging Wasserstein distance-based GANs and contrastive learning, AdPrST generates robust low dimensional embeddings for each view. These embeddings are fused via a dot-product attention mechanism, guided by pre clustering labels, to achieve accurate spatial domain identification. Benchmarking across multiple datasets demonstrated AdPrST's superior performance over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its potential to advance spatial transcriptomics research by elucidating spatial functional patterns and developmental trajectories. In particular, AdPrST excels in infer encing spatiotemporal structures, reconstructing developmental sequences and temporal features in complex tissues.

title: Programmable In-Network Aggregation for Communication-Aware Federated Learning in 5G RANs
authors: Emilio Paolini, Andrea Pinto, Luca Valcarenghi, Flavio Esposito
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, making it attractive for privacy-preserving applications at the wireless edge. However, when executed over real 5G networks, FL performance degrades due to uplink congestion, heterogeneous client capabilities, and intermittent connectivity. Most existing approaches attempt to mitigate these issues indirectly by optimizing clients (through adaptive participation, local training, or selection strategies) or by optimizing models (via pruning, quantization, or compression), but they ignore potential network bottlenecks. This paper introduces FLAG, an FL architecture that embeds innetwork aggregation directly into 5G gNodeBs, transforming the network into an active participant in the learning process. In particular, FLAG performs parameter aggregation at line rate within the 5G Service Data Adaptation Protocol layer and incorporates three mechanisms: Partial-Contribution Correction for loss-tolerant averaging, a timer-driven pipeline for real-time scheduling, and a deadline-based grouping strategy to mitigate stragglers. Experiments with realistic wireless emulation show that FLAG achieves up to 5.1× faster time-to-accuracy and maintains accuracy within 0.8% of a loss-free baseline, while reducing gNB-to-server bandwidth by aggregating pergNB rather than per-client. FLAG requires no modifications to clients or the parameter server, demonstrating how 5G-aware system design can make federated learning scalable, efficient, and resilient under real-world wireless conditions.

title: Image Segmentation With Large Language Models: A Survey With Perspectives for Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Sanjeda Akter, Ibne Farabi Shihab, Anuj Sharma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with computer vision is profoundly transforming perception tasks like image segmentation. For intelligent transportation systems (ITS), where accurate scene understanding is critical for safety and efficiency, this new paradigm offers unprecedented capabilities. This survey systematically reviews the emerging field of LLM-augmented image segmentation, focusing on its applications, challenges, and future directions within ITS. We provide a taxonomy of current approaches based on their prompting mechanisms and core architectures, and we highlight how these innovations can enhance road scene understanding for autonomous driving, traffic monitoring, and infrastructure maintenance. Finally, we identify key challenges, including real-time performance and safety-critical reliability, and outline a perspective centered on explainable, human-centric AI as a prerequisite for the successful deployment of this technology in next-generation transportation systems.

title: Additively Manufactured X-Band Diplexer using Laser Beam Powder Bed Fusion Technique
authors: Aleksandr Voronov, Vicente Boria, Lluc Sempere, Mirko Kunowsky, Carmen Bachiller
abstract: This work presents the additive manufacturing of an X-Band diplexer composed of two filters: one centered at 9.5 GHz and another at 10.5 GHz, both with a bandwidth of 250 MHz, plus a power divider. The whole set was implemented in rectangular waveguide technology, manufactured in a single block. Good agreement between simulated and measured results is achieved, with return losses (RL) lower than 20 dB, insertion losses (IL) around 0.3 dB in both filters, and isolation of 30 dB between them. For demonstration purposes, the set was used for the characterization in anechoic chamber of the directivity, gain and radiation efficiency of an additive manufactured horn antenna. The diplexer was implemented using Powder Bed Fusion Laser Beam, (PBF-L/M) technology on a metal alloy (AlSi10Mg). The horn antenna was manufactured via Vat Photopolymerization by exposure to Masked UV light (VPP-UVM) technology, using a polymeric resin (Gray from Applylabwork), subsequently metallized. Mechanical optimization for the additive manufacturing of the design allows the development of an ergonomic single-part device, enabling perfectly squared inner cavities. This reduces weight, volume, and manufacturing waste, while maintaining the electromagnetic response of the diplexer.

title: ISAC Channel Measurements and Closed-Loop Modeling with Bidirectional Parameter Coupling in Industrial IoT Scenarios
authors: Zengyao Bi, Xi Liao, Li Wang, Xiangquan Zheng, Yang Wang, Jie Zhang
abstract: With the advent of 6G networks, integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is a key enabling technology for native perception in complex industrial Internet of Things (IoT) environments, which demands accurate ISAC channel models. However, most existing models mainly capture correlation at the cluster level, making it difficult to characterize finer-grained parameter relationships and intra-cluster variations, and lack an effective mechanism for bidirectional parameter coupling between communication and sensing channels in industrial IoT scenarios. This paper proposes a shared-cluster geometry-based stochastic ISAC (SC-GBSM-ISAC) channel model for industrial IoT. The model is parameterized using channel measurements at 38 GHz and 132 GHz in a high-clutter indoor factory (InF (A)) scenario. Compared with existing ISAC channel models, the proposed model has the following features. First, a closed-loop “sensing–communication–sensing” channel modeling framework is constructed based on 3GPP TR 38.901, which integrates bidirectional parameter interaction between communication and sensing channels. Second, by selectively reusing sensing-target clusters to construct shared clusters for communication-channel modeling, the model introduces a physically consistent shared-scattering structure while preserving the statistical characteristics specified in 3GPP TR 38.901. Third, by combining scatterer information inferred from the communication channel with a scenario-dependent sensing probability model, the sensing background component is reconstructed. Finally, measurement-simulation comparisons in an independent low-clutter InF (B) scenario demonstrate that the proposed model effectively reproduces bidirectional parameter coupling and primary spatio-temporal statistical metrics at 38/132 GHz, validating its accuracy and cross-scenario consistency in the measured scenarios.

title: Systematic Use Case Modeling and Specification Analysis of Transportation Systems in the Era of Software-Defined Vehicles
authors: Sebastian Siegl
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Transportation systems are built up with the integration of numerous mechatronic systems that collectively enable the desired functionalities. The term software-defined vehicle (SDV) represents a paradigm shift in engineering. While the physical hardware components-such as sensors, actuators, and electronic control units-are modified in relatively slower development cycles due to manufacturing constraints and validation requirements, the software changes evolve at a higher iteration frequency. This accelerated software development enables faster deployment of new features to continuously optimize the performance and usability, or to remediate security vulnerabilities and perform maintenance updates. Various approaches have been published, adressing the rising system complexity with hierarchical decomposition, to gain insight, analyse, and maintain the system's performance characteristics from a system's approach to modelling. Various load situations, hereinafter referred to as use cases, can be analysed in detail. An open question is often how structured all use cases have been covered. For this purpose, expert knowledge or Monte Carlo simulation is often used. However, both approaches have the disadvantage that they have not built and structured the use cases through a clear methodology. In this paper, we present a modeling method to systematically describe the use cases for a transportation system using a structured modeling process. In this context, modeling already focuses on the fact that all relevant and possible use cases are considered during modeling, so that statements can also be made about completeness and significance in the analysis of the use cases of the system. We present the application and results of the method using an example of a use case model of an exemplary vehicle's themal energy system and a safety feature, as it could be part of autonomous mobility.

title: InterPose: Learning to Generate Human-Object Interactions from Large-Scale Web Videos
authors: Yangsong Zhang, Abdul Ahad Butt, Gül Varol, Ivan Laptev
abstract: Human motion generation has shown great advances powered by recent diffusion models and large-scale motion capture data. Most of existing works, however, currently target animation of isolated people in empty scenes. Meanwhile, synthesizing realistic human-object interactions in complex 3D scenes remains a critical challenge in computer graphics and robotics. One obstacle towards generating versatile high-fidelity humanobject interactions is the lack of large-scale datasets with diverse object manipulations. Indeed, existing motion capture data is typically restricted to single people and manipulations of limited sets of objects. To address this issue, we propose an automatic motion extraction pipeline and use it to collect interaction-rich human motions. Our new dataset InterPose contains 73.8 K sequences of 3D human motions and corresponding text captions automatically obtained from 45.8 K videos with human-object interactions. We perform extensive experiments and demonstrate InterPose to bring significant improvements to state-of-the-art methods for human motion generation. Moreover, using InterPose we develop an LLM-based agent enabling zero-shot animation of people interacting with diverse objects and scenes.

title: C3PO: Canonicalization of 3D Pose from Partial Views With Generalizable Correspondence Features
authors: Yu Chi, Leonhard Sommer, Olaf Dünkel, Dominik Muhle, Daniel Cremers, Christian Theobalt, Adam Kortylewski
abstract: Progress in 3D object understanding relies on the category-level canonicalization of 3D objects, i.e., bringing 3D instances into a consistent position and orientation. Most related works assume complete 3D representations, while real-world applications often require solving the more challenging task of canonicalizing from partial views, i.e., short videos that cover only a part of the object. We introduce C3PO, a method capable of canonicalizing partial views from arbitrary object categories by enforcing geometric and feature-level appearance consistency of overlapping views. We represent partial views as 3D point clouds obtained via structure-from-motion, where each point carries a feature vector that is extracted from 2 D images using a novel feature extractor capable of estimating generalizable correspondence features. Notably, our correspondence features are learned on a large dataset and generalize to object categories not seen during training. On top of this, we introduce an efficient pairwise-registration framework that aligns partial object representations into a globally consistent canonical frame. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that C3PO significantly outperforms existing methods.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Transmission Characteristic Analysis of Integrated Linear Joints
authors: Shengbei Wang, Yan Huo, Huijun Wang, Ning Dong, Shangjun Ma
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: To analyze the coupled behavior of integrated linear joints in precision robotics, this study develops a multibody dynamics framework to perform dynamic modeling and transmission characteristic analysis. The proposed model integrates the inverted planetary roller screw (IPRS) with a frameless motor, taking into account the centripetal excitations caused by rotor eccentricity and utilizing a continuous friction model. A set of global dynamic equations is established to comprehensively describe the radial, axial, and torsional interactions within the system. The results characterize the non-uniformity in internal load distribution and the coupling effects between radial and axial motions. Furthermore, a comprehensive analysis across time-domain, frequency-domain, and phase-space indicates that the joint maintains stability with quasi-periodic motion under complex excitation. This work establishes the fundamental connections between internal mechanical excitations and motion accuracy, providing a theoretical basis for refining the structure and precision control of integrated robotic actuators.

title: Vibrations of a Delta Robot in the Perspective of Energy Harvesting
authors: Michele Tonan, Matteo Bottin, Alberto Doria, Giulio Rosati
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Delta robots are characterized by large accelerations and vibrations, hence they are a benchmark case for the application of Vibration Energy Harvesting (VEH) technologies in the field of robotics. Experimental modal analysis is used to find the natural frequencies of the first modes of vibration of a Delta robot. The comparison with the frequency spectrum of the acceleration due to the planned motion shows that resonance phenomena are not present, and the robot experiences low frequency forced vibrations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(f<20 ~\text{Hz}$</tex>) with harmonics that depend on the shape of the planned trajectory. A piezoelectric vibration harvester is designed to scavenge energy from such low-frequency forced vibrations. Experimental tests showed that the harvester is able to generate large voltages <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\approx 20 \mathbf{V}$</tex>) that by means of a proper converter will make it possible to feed sensor nodes.

title: Time-Optimal L1 Guidance for Straight-Line Path Following in 3D
authors: Pradyumn Mahajan, Sayantan Pal, Sikha Hota
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This work presents a modified L1 guidance law that generates smooth and time-efficient trajectories for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to converge to straight-line paths in three-dimensional (3D) space. This approach employs the L1 guidance law to converge to the straight-line segment of the time-optimal but curvature-discontinuous 3D Dubins path before transitioning to the desired straight line path. In this way, this law significantly reduces the convergence time of L1 guidance while maintaining its curvature continuity (C2-continuous path) and respecting the curvature bounds defined by the minimum turn radius of the UAV. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed guidance law through comparisons with classical L1 guidance and Dubins paths.

title: Insight Into the Insight Toolkit
authors: Terry S. Yoo
abstract: As a federally funded initiative, the Insight Toolkit (ITK) is intended to make a lasting software investment for biomedical image computing, enabling our communities with open-source algorithms for image segmentation and registration. Its 25-year success has been the result of a combination of carefully integrated factors: executive support, project management expertise, user involvement and skilled developers, the adoption of agile processes and tools, and maintaining clear business objectives in our execution. This history of ITK covers these factors, from the inception of the toolkit through its agile implementation and continuous testing and integration. The story includes motivations and fortunate enabling opportunities and ends with summary advice on how to succeed in creating a successful project of open-source software.

title: FreeFix: Boosting 3D Gaussian Splatting via Fine-Tuning-Free Diffusion Models
authors: Hongyu Zhou, Zisen Shao, Sheng Miao, Pan Wang, Dongfeng Bai, Bingbing Liu, Yiyi Liao
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields and 3D Gaussian Splatting have advanced novel view synthesis, yet still rely on dense inputs and often degrade at extrapolated views. Recent approaches leverage generative models, such as diffusion models, to provide additional supervision, but face a tradeoff between generalization and fidelity: fine-tuning diffusion models for artifact removal improves fidelity but risks overfitting, while fine-tuning-free methods preserve generalization but often yield lower fidelity. We introduce FreeFix, a fine-tuning-free approach that pushes the boundary of this trade-off by enhancing extrapolated rendering with pretrained image diffusion models.
