We present an interleaved 2D-3D refinement strategy, showing that image diffusion models can be leveraged for consistent refinement without relying on costly video diffusion models. Furthermore, we take a closer look at the guidance signal for 2D refinement and propose a per-pixel confidence mask to identify uncertain regions for targeted improvement. Experiments across multiple datasets show that FreeFix improves multiframe consistency and achieves performance comparable to or surpassing fine-tuning-based methods, while retaining strong generalization ability. Our project page is at https://xdimlab.github.io/freefix.

title: Frequency-Aware Gaussian Splatting Decomposition
authors: Yishai Lavi, Leo Segre, Shai Avidan
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) enables efficient novel view synthesis, but treats all frequencies uniformly, making it difficult to separate coarse structure from fine detail. Recent works have started to exploit frequency signals, but lack explicit frequency decomposition of the 3D representation itself. We propose a frequency-aware decomposition that organizes 3D Gaussians into groups corresponding to Laplacian-pyramid subbands of the input images. Each group is trained with spatial frequency regularization to confine it to its target frequency, while higher-frequency bands use signed residual colors to capture fine details that may be missed by lower-frequency reconstructions. A progressive coarse-to-fine training schedule stabilizes the decomposition. Our method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and rendering speed among all LOD-capable methods. In addition to improved interpretability, our method enables dynamic level-of-detail rendering, progressive streaming, foveated rendering, promptable 3D focus, and artistic filtering. Our code will be made publicly available.

title: Making Minimal Solvers Inverse-Free Using Null Space Computation
authors: Hassan Bozorgmanesh, Janne Heikkilä
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel resultant-based method for solving polynomial systems of equations that are commonly encountered in computer vision, particularly as minimal problems. Unlike traditional algorithms that rely on matrix inversion, the primary merits of the proposed method are the numerical stability of its formulation and the lack of need to compute the inverse of a matrix by leveraging null space computations. Additionally, its formulation paves the way for more computations to be performed in the offline stage by using the sparsity of coefficient matrices, thereby reducing the computational load in the online stage. This inverse-free formulation is especially suited for sparse systems and offers improved robustness in scenarios where matrix inversion is unstable or infeasible. Experimental results demonstrate better accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods such as SRBM and GAPS across a variety of camera geometry problems.

title: Fillerbuster: Unified Generative Scene Completion Model for Casual Captures
authors: Ethan Weber, Norman Müller, Yash Kant, Vasu Agrawal, Michael Zollhöfer, Angjoo Kanazawa, Christian Richardt
abstract: We present Fillerbuster, a unified model that completes unknown regions of a 3D scene with a multi-view latent diffusion transformer. Casual captures are often sparse and miss surrounding content behind objects or above the scene. Existing methods are not suitable for this challenge as they focus on making known pixels look good with sparse-view priors, or on creating missing sides of objects from just one or two photos. In reality, we often have hundreds of input frames and want to complete areas that are missing and unobserved from the input frames. Our solution is to train a generative model that can consume a large context of input frames while generating unknown target views and recovering image poses when camera parameters are unknown. We show results where we complete partial captures on two existing datasets. We also present an uncalibrated scene completion task where our unified model predicts both poses and creates new content. We open-source our framework for integration into popular reconstruction platforms like Nerfstudio or Gsplat. We present a flexible, unified inpainting framework to predict many images and poses together, where all inputs are jointly inpainted, and it could be extended to predict more modalities such as depth.

title: Syntheto-PCOS: A Fairness-Aware Generative Learning Framework for Robust Assessment of Metabolic Dysfunction in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
authors: Syeda Zaina Rohana Sneha, Mumtahina Mozumder, Farida Akter Banu, Rafshia Mahbuba Ayshe
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) diagnosis using machine learning is often hindered by limited and imbalanced clinical data, leading to biased and unreliable models. This paper introduces Syntheto-PCOS, a fairness-aware framework that addresses this challenge through generative data augmentation. We employ a Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN) to create high-fidelity synthetic data, effectively doubling our dataset and balancing the class distribution. A suite of five machine learning models was trained on this augmented data, with RandomForest and LightGBM optimized via Bayesian techniques. Our results show that the ensemble models achieved superior performance, with the optimized LightGBM model attaining an AUC of 0.893 and a clinically significant diagnostic recall of 68.8%. Feature importance and SHAP analysis confirmed that the model's predictions are driven by established clinical markers (e.g., follicle counts, AMH), ensuring both interpretability and clinical relevance. The Syntheto-PCOS framework demonstrates a robust methodology for developing accurate and trustworthy diagnostic tools in the face of common medical data limitations.

title: Identifying Patterns in Bangla Fake News Using Word2Vec and Attention Based CNN
authors: Rakibul Hasan Nirob, Jeba Wasima, Francis Rudra D Cruze, Fizar Ahmed, Md. Nasimul Kader
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The rapid dissemination of misinformation poses a significant challenge to the integrity of online information, especially for low resource languages such as Bangla. Despite being spoken by millions, Bangla still lacks reliable systems for automated fake news detection. This work presents a novel framework that combines multiple feature extraction methods with deep learning (Dl) architectures to address this gap. We employ Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TFIDF), CountVectorizer(CV), Global Vectors for Word Representation(GloVe) and Word2Vec embeddings for text encoding and utilize an attention based Convolutional Neural Network (AttCNN) to enhance contextual understanding and capture subtle linguistic patterns. The proposed method demonstrates scalability for large scale datasets while maintaining strong classification performance. Through extensive experimentation, our model has been tested and proven robust with the Att-CNN using Word2Vec embeddings achieving state of the art accuracy of 98.42 % in Bangla fake news detection.

title: A Reputation Based Consensus Mechanism Inspired by Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)
authors: Takia Nawrin, Md. Yusuf, Maftun Ahommed, Fahmeda Hossain Orna, Iffat Jahan Kona, Nafees Mansoor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Enhanced and transparent data exchange is crucial for enterprises when it comes to averting fraud and ensuring operational dependability. Efficient collaboration is usually compromised with centralized systems that are likely to be manipulated, susceptible to unauthorized access, and lacking in trust. Controlled, auditable transactions have been shown to offer a powerful solution to permissioned blockchains, with frameworks such as Hyperledger Fabric adding support to modular consensus and privacy preserving designs. Nevertheless, conventional consensus protocols, like Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) and Proof of Authority (PoA) are restricted in terms of scalability, centralization threats, and fixed selection of the leaders. The article presents a Reputation-Based Consensus Mechanism (RBCM), which is based on the ant colony optimization inspired clustering and dynamically changing reputation scoring. Blended with Hyperledger Fabric, RBCM evaluates node trustworthiness, dynamically chooses trustworthy leaders and alleviates malicious behavior. According to theoretical analysis, RBCM offers a higher level of security, scalability and fairness, which make it very suitable in enterprise applications.

title: Hybrid CNN Framework Leveraging VGG16 for Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification
authors: MD. Emtiaz Ahmed, Md Sifat Ullah Sayem, Amjad Hossain Piash, Afroza Akter
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Brain tumors are a critical health issue with a significant impact worldwide. Their detection and classification are crucial for effective treatment. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) serves as a reliable diagnostic tool for visualizing brain anomalies. To enhance diagnostic efficiency and reduce human error, automated computer-assisted approaches are essential. In this research, we introduce a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model that combines features from a pre-trained VGG16 model and a custom CNN architecture to classify brain tumors using MRI images. The custom CNN is composed of several convolutional layers with increasing filter sizes. Two publicly available datasets were utilized: one from Kaggle, comprising 6,217 images across four classes, and another from Figshare, containing 3,064 images across three classes. Once the data was preprocessed and divided into training and testing sets, our proposed model was trained using a multi-input fusion approach. We obtained an accuracy, precision, recall, and f1-score of 95.12%, 93%, 94%, and 93%, respectively, from our proposed model using the Kaggle dataset and 95.31%, 95%, 97%, and 96% by using Figshare dataset. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed model for reliable brain tumor diagnosis using MRI images.

title: Tariff-Sensitive and Seasonally Adaptive Sizing of an On-Grid Solar-Wind EV Charging Station in Coastal Bangladesh
authors: Taseen Rahman, Nadman Ahmed Chowdhury, Zannatul Musta, Murshida Rahman, Hamza Mubarak, A.S. Nazmul Huda, Shameem Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents a tariff-sensitive and seasonally adaptive techno-economic analysis of an on-grid solar-wind electric vehicle (EV) charging station for the coastal region of Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh, using the HOMER Pro simulation tool. The study integrates Bangladesh's net-metering framework with two distinct EV-load profiles, representing the high tourist season and the low off-season, to assess how tariff variations and seasonal demand influence system design and cost. Multiple grid sellback rates ranging from 0.034 to 0.067 $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$/ \text{kWh}$</tex>, were analysed under a fixed grid import rate of 0.087 $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$/ \text{kWh}$</tex> to determine the least-cost configurations. Results indicate that both tariff and seasonal dynamics significantly impact system performance and the renewable fraction. During the optimal setup of 458 kW photovoltaic (PV) capacity with EO20 wind turbines achieved a levelized cost of energy (LCOE) of around $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.041 / \text{kWh}$</tex> and a renewable fraction of 69 %, whereas the off-season condition favored a 344 kW PV system only with an LCOE of $ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.066 / \text{kWh}$</tex>. The findings emphasize the need for tariff-aware and seasonally responsive renewable energy sizing strategies to support cost-effective EV-charging infrastructure expansion in Bangladesh's coastal zones.

title: The Role of Model Architecture in Breast Cancer Classification: A Comparison of CNN and Transformer Paradigms on Ultrasound Data
authors: Siam Tahsin Bhuiyan, Halima Khatun, Samiul Karim Mazumder, Fatin Israq, Riyadul Islam, Rashedur Rahman, Ashraful Islam, Saadia Binte Alam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Breast cancer is the leading malignancy in women globally, and early detection is critical for improving survival. Deep learning has shown promise in medical image analysis, yet the effects of model architecture remain underexplored. This study conducts a comparative evaluation of four models: DenseNet121 (pure CNN), ConvNeXt (modernized CNN with transformer-inspired design), ViT-B/16 (pure Vision Transformer), and Swin-B (hierarchical transformer with CNN-like inductive biases) for breast ultrasound classification using the public BUSI dataset of 780 images. Results demonstrate that DenseNet121 exhibits limitations due to its reliance on local receptive fields, which restricts its ability to capture distributed pathological features. ConvNeXt outperforms DenseNet121 across all metrics, with 83% accuracy and 91% AUROC, highlighting the benefits of transformer-inspired refinements within CNNs. ViT-B/16 achieves the highest AUROC of 94%, underscoring the strength of global self-attention for capturing distributed pathological cues. Swin-B attains the most balanced performance, with 86% accuracy, 85% recall, and 0.92 AUROC, reflecting the effectiveness of combining global attention with hierarchical locality. These findings suggest a clear performance hierarchy, where hybrid transformer architectures such as Swin-B offer the most clinically reliable balance between sensitivity and specificity. Future research should explore hybrid models that integrate CNN inductive priors with transformer-based global reasoning to further enhance robustness in real-world diagnostic applications.

title: Vision Meets Convolution: A Hybrid Approach for Brain Tumor Classification from MRI Scans
authors: Abdur Rab Dhruba, Tasnim Jannat Yeamoni
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Due to the high diagnosis mortality, brain tumors represent a significant challenge in medical imaging. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has become a widespread method for recognizing and monitoring brain tumors, but interpreting these scans remains a task that relies heavily on the expertise of a radiologist. This article presents a hybrid model that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Vision Transformers (ViT) to improve the accuracy of classification of brain tumors from MRI scans. Unlike conventional hybrids that simply concatenate CNN and transformer features, the proposed hybrid model shows that high-level feature embeddings restructured by EfficientNetV2B0, enabling a lightweight global attention mechanism without the patch extraction step used in standard ViT Models. Train and evaluate the model using data records of 2622 MRI images, including glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumors, and no tumor categories. The hybrid approach achieved an impressive accuracy of 97.71 %, surpassing the traditional CNN model (96.80 %) and ViT model (85.58 %). Furthermore, the hybrid model showed a reduction in loss (0.15) compared to the other methods. This study highlights the effectiveness of a combination of local characterization extraction and global attentional mechanisms to improve the accuracy of tumor detection and provides a reliable tool for automated classification of brain tumors.

title: Agentic AI in Distributed SDN: Synchronization and Design Framework
authors: Rawan Alsheikh, Etimad Fadel, Nadine Akkari
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Large-scale Software-Defined Networking (SDN) deployments are increasingly adopting Physically Distributed but Logically Centralized (PDLC) control planes, in which multiple controllers must coordinate to maintain a consistent global view of the network. In practice, this creates a persistent trade-off between state consistency and synchronization overhead under limited control-plane budgets. Existing AI-based synchronization solutions often rely on centralized learning and decision components, which can reduce scalability and introduce single-point-of-failure-like dependence. This paper presents a design framework for adaptive synchronization in PDLC SDN from an agentic AI perspective. The objective is to express synchronization as a budget-aware decision process that can be learned by controller-resident agents while enabling cooperative learning without centralized run-time action selection. We model synchronization as a budget-aware Markov Decision Process (MDP) with explicit state, action, and reward definitions that capture staleness, constrained neighbor selection, and benefit-cost tradeoffs. Based on this formulation, we adopt Centralized Training and Decentralized Execution (CTDE): decentralized Actors make local synchronization decisions at run time, while a trainingtime Critic provides coordination signals to stabilize multi-agent learning. The framework is instantiated with Communication Multi-Agent Actor-Critic (C-MAAC) and extended with an Attention-Based Critic (ABC-MAAC), where attention suppresses low-impact inter-controller relations and emphasizes influential ones before value estimation. Complexity verification shows linear per-update scaling for C-MAAC and an additional quadratic interaction term for ABC-MAAC due to pairwise attention, while preserving decentralized, budget-aware run-time execution.

title: Evaluating Deep Learning and Ensemble Techniques for Cyberbullying Detection in Bengali Social Media with Feature Fusion
authors: Tasnim Begum Srity, Humaira Saida Tasnima, Ummul Humaira, Sayma Alam Suha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Cyberbullying, broadly defined as harassment facilitated by digital and information communication technologies, has been identified as a prevalent and significant societal problem in the digital age. The spread of social media sites has eased online communication, but little has been done to detect cyberbullying in non English speaking languages especially Bengali language compared to English. Thus, this study investigates the detection of offensive Bengali text through a comparative analysis of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and ensemble models, including logistic regression, random forest, long shortterm memory (LSTM), and bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM). An ensemble voting system is proposed here to label content as offensive or non-offensive in Bengali language to increase the general performance. The dataset comprises 43,998 comments from Bengali social media, sourced from the Cyberbullying Bangla dataset, which were subsequently preprocessed through cleaning, tokenization, stemming, and noise removal. Additionally, the data are transformed using ensemble feature extraction techniques, including TF-IDF, one-hot encoding, and Word2Vec. The models are then trained and tested on binary classification scenarios and LSTM and Bi-LSTM performed with the highest accuracy of 87.6 % with the ensemble model performing at 79 %. Comparisons were made of the measures of precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC area, which showed greater efficacy of deep learning strategies on large datasets. The contribution of this work is automated detection of cyberbullying in Bengali, which can be used to safeguard vulnerable users in local online communities.

title: Satellite-IoT Access Technologies and Simulation Frameworks in 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks: Limitations and Modeling Challenges
authors: Khaoula Kacem, Nawel Zangar, Soumaya Hamouda
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs) are expected to play a key role in sixth-generation (6G) systems by extending Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity beyond terrestrial coverage. Satellite-based IoT enables large-scale deployments in remote and underserved regions; however, it introduces structural challenges related to large coverage footprints, time-varying propagation conditions, and predominantly ALOHA-based uncoordinated random access under massive device density. This paper presents a structured survey of NTN-IoT access technologies and their associated simulation frameworks, focusing on scalability limitations and modeling constraints, particularly those induced by large-scale random access contention in satellite beams. Cellular and non-cellular solutions, including LoRaWAN Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) and Long Range Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (LR-FHSS), are comparatively analyzed in terms of access robustness and density-dependent performance trade-offs. Existing simulation tools are further examined with respect to their ability to represent NTN geometry, dynamic propagation behavior, large-scale random access contention, and waveform-specific mechanisms. The study highlights persistent fragmentation in current evaluation approaches and emphasizes the need for unified and reproducible modeling methodologies to enable fair performance comparison of future NTN-IoT systems.

title: Analysis on the Potential Key Feature of FSO in 5G
authors: Nayeema Rouf Nobonita, Faria Tarannum Rutba, Sabbir Ahmed Nahian, Ehtesham Al Shahriar Adnan, Bobby Barua
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Free-Space Optical (FSO) communication appears as a promising solution to fulfill high data rate, high bandwidth and low cost 5 G network secure transmission as it's free from Electromagnetic Interference (EI). This study examines the performance of different modulation schemes OOK, BPSK, QPSK, 8-PSK and M-ary PPM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(M=2,4,8,16)$</tex> for link distance of 3000 m under moderate atmospheric turbulence condition of Bangladesh. A system model for each modulation form has been developed, accounting for fluctuations of intensity due to turbulence and additive white Gaussian noise. The atmospheric channel is designed using Gamma-Gamma distribution, both capturing small scales and large scale disturbance effects, while Rytov variance is used to determine the strength of disruption. Simulation results show that better efficiency and low bit failure speed of 16-PPM than other modulation schemes. These findings provide insight into the choice of 16-PPM for optimal modulation plans of reliable FSO connections at high speed 5G networks under moderate weather conditions.

title: Freshness Assessment of Fruits and Vegetables Using Dual Attention Vision Transformer
authors: Rupa Rani Paul, Rajkumar Biswas, Chinmoy Modak Turjo, Tonmoy Ghosh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate assessment of produce freshness plays a critical role in maintaining food safety standards, ensuring quality assurance, and minimizing post-harvest economic losses. This research presents an automated classification framework utilizing a Dual Attention Vision Transformer (DaViT) architecture to tell the difference for identifying whether fruits and vegetables are fresh or decayed. The methodology leverages a comprehensive dataset comprising 28,189 images across 14 distinct fruit and vegetable categories, with each category containing of fresh and rotten classes, making it a total of 28 classes. The dataset distribution follows a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 1 \% - 9 \% - 2 0 \%$</tex> divided for training, testing, and validation phases, respectively. The DaViT model undergoes fine-tuning procedures using pre-trained ImageNet weights as initialization parameters. Performance evaluation demonstrates exceptional classification capabilities with 99.04% accuracy, alongside 99% recall and 99% precision metrics. These results surpass the present advanced techniques methodologies in the domain. The proposed automated system reduces reliance on manual inspection processes while minimizing human-induced errors, offering a robust and scalable solution for integration into modern agricultural monitoring systems and intelligent food supply chain management frameworks.

title: Motor Imagery Spatial-Temporal Cross-Fusion Classification Based on fNIRS–informed EEG Source Space and Brain-Atlas Constraints
authors: Lufeng Feng, Baomin Xu, Bihai Lin, Jun Bai, Li Duan, Shifan Jia, Ziyu Jia, Wei Ni
abstract: Due to the inherent volume conduction effect, EEG-based motor imagery (MI) decoding is limited by the low spatial resolution of scalp recordings. To improve MI decoding, we propose a brain atlas-constrained EEG source-space spatial-temporal cross-fusion framework assisted by functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS). In the source-imaging stage, task-relevant spatial priors derived from fNIRS are used to regularize EEG source imaging, yielding more reliable source-space signals. In the spatial-temporal fusion stage, a lightweight temporal branch is constructed to capture multi-scale temporal dynamics. Spatially, ROI nodes are defined under a brain-atlas constraint, and a correlation-based functional connectivity graph is built among nodes. To better model MI-related spatial laterality and inter-regional cooperation, a spatial branch that combines approximate personalized propagation (APPNP) and a graph attention network (GAT) is employed to learn long-range dependencies and adaptive connection weights. Finally, a cross-attention fusion module is used to enable dynamic interactions between temporal features and spatial node representations. The proposed method achieves an accuracy of 72.81% ± 7.23 on Dataset 1 and 34.97% ± 5.39 on Dataset 2, outperforming representative EEG–fNIRS multimodal baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/useflf/MI-ESI.

title: EEG Ensemble Learning Framework for Prognostic Prediction of Post-rTMS Motor Recovery in Stroke
authors: Tianyu Jia, Haiyang Long, Diantong Xie, Yixu Zhao, Yifan Wu, Yifeng Li, Linhong Mo, Xinyu Guan, Dario Farina
abstract: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a promising neuromodulatory therapy for post-stroke rehabilitation, offering the potential to modulate brain network dynamics. However, its clinical effectiveness remains debated due to the high inter-individual variability in patient responses, largely attributed to heterogeneous patterns of neural reorganization after stroke. In this study, we proposed an EEG ensemble learning framework (EEG-ELF) to predict motor recovery outcomes following rTMS therapy in stroke patients. The framework was evaluated on EEG recordings acquired during a motor imagery task from 20 individuals with subcortical stroke, collected both before and after a four-week rTMS intervention, along with the upper-limb Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA) scores. EEG-ELF's prediction performance was assessed through both regression and classification tasks using leave-one-subject-out cross-validation. The results demonstrated a strong correlation between predicted and actual recovery rates (Pearson's R = 0.92, p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.001), and the framework achieved 95% accuracy in classifying patients into good and poor prognosis groups. These findings suggest that EEG-ELF can accurately predict individual motor outcomes from pre-treatment EEG features, highlighting its potential to guide personalized rTMS interventions through data-driven stratification of stroke patients.

title: IEEE Robotics and Automation Society Publication Information
authors: N/A

title:
3D NAND Scaling Performance Enhancement using 2D MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Channel
authors: Mohd Ashraf Lone, Dibyadrasta Sahoo, Sanjeev Kumar Manhas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: The polysilicon channel in conventional 3D NAND flash memory shows degraded subthreshold slope and low electrostatic control over the channel upon scaling gate pitch. 3D NAND with MoS2 channel material can potentially overcome these challenges, as it shows higher mobility with a channel thickness of a few atoms. In this work, using TCAD simulations calibrated to experimental data, the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> material is incorporated as the channel in 3D NAND to investigate scaling performance. We demonstrate that the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> channel NAND performs better, even at a scaled pitch of 20nm with 10nm gate length, exhibiting a good cell current value, subthreshold slope, and improved memory performance. We investigate neighboring cell interference by analyzing the effect of barrier lowering by side transistors on the focus cell at a gate length of 10nm. This shows the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> channel NAND has a higher energy barrier than the polysilicon channel NAND due to better electrostatic gate control over the channel. We also demonstrate that the trapped charge during program operation is higher in MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> 3D NAND at 10nm gate length, making it suitable for multi-bit operation. We analyze the retention property of the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> channel NAND and observe almost similar charge loss at a scaled 20nm gate pitch compared to 50nm, indicating reliable operation with gate pitch scaling. Therefore, 20 nm gate-pitch MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> channel NAND, with a 9 V memory window, enables up to 3× higher storage density by allowing more cells to be stacked within the limited physical height of the 3D NAND stack.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Accurate Lowpass-to-Bandpass Frequency Transformation for Distributed-Element Resonator Filter Design
authors: Juseop Lee, Keebaek Lee
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, we introduce an accurate lowpass-to-bandpass (LP–BP) frequency transformation tailored for transmission-line wideband bandpass filter design. The conventional LP–BP transformation is entirely accurate for shunt-series lumped-element resonator filters. When applied to wideband transmission-line filters, it introduces discrepancies between the specified target bandwidth and the bandwidth of the filter topologies, primarily due to approximations related to the transformation. On the contrary, the proposed frequency transformation achieves the desired target bandwidth directly, without requiring any preadjustment or iterative correction.

title: A 26–42-GHz Broadband 2.8-dB NF LNA With Multidiode Thermo-Adaptive Biasing for SATCOM Applications
authors: Zhongliang Li, Qiangji Wang, Yan Wang, Guixiang Jin, Wenbin Wang, Jiangyuan Liang, Wensong Wang, Jiawei Xu, Jiawen Wang, Na Yan, Hao Huang, Weifeng Sun, Zhongyuan Fang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a broadband low-noise amplifier (LNA) with a wide operating temperature range, suitable for satellite communications (SATCOMs). An on-chip multidiode thermo-adaptive biasing circuit has been presented. The biasing circuit is composed of a series diode and a resistor. The variable gain is realized through a variable-transconductance topology. The proposed LNA employs pole-tuning technology to extend the bandwidth. Besides, a stability network and an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$RC$</tex-math> </inline-formula> feedback network are designed to increase gain flatness and enhance stability. The circuit is implemented using a 0.15-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m GaAs pHEMT process with a chip area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.50\times 1.00$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Measurement results demonstrate that the proposed LNA achieves an average gain of 22 dB and a noise figure (NF) of 2.8 dB across the operating frequency range from 26 to 42 GHz. It maintains stable performance over a temperature range from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$- 55~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$85~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, with a gain variation of less than 0.0186 dB/°C and an NF variation of less than 0.002 dB/°C. In addition, the measured output 1-dB compression points (OP1dB) are 12 dBm.

title: Prescribed-Time ESO-Based Robust Repetitive Learning Control for Cable-Driven Rehabilitation Exoskeleton Robots
authors: Chunjie Xiao, Qiang Chen, Yun Cheng, Guanbin Gao, Xiongxiong He
abstract: This article proposes a prescribed-time extended state observer (PTESO) based robust repetitive learning control (RLC) scheme for cable-driven rehabilitation exoskeleton robots (CDRER). Based on the repetitive characteristics of rehabilitation training, the uncertain dynamics in CDRER are divided into two separated parts, i.e., periodic part and nonperiodic part. The periodic part is involved in an unknown bounded desired input, and a fully saturated repetitive learning law with a continuous function is proposed, such that the estimation of the desired input is strictly confined within a predefined region and its continuity is guaranteed. Then, by constructing a bounded and continuous gain function in the ESO design, the observation error of the nonperiodic part converges into a small adjustable domain at a preset time and maintains it in the later time without switching, such that the demand of rapid disturbance rejection in the exoskeleton is satisfied. The proposed control scheme enables the CDRER to provide satisfactory steady-state tracking performance as specified by the therapist during repetitive rehabilitation tasks, and comparative experiments on a cable-driven knee exoskeleton validate its effectiveness.

title: APTQ+: Attention-FFN-aware Post-Training Quantization for a Layer-wise LLM Accelerator on FPGA
authors: Ziyi Guan, Zhengfei Chen, Dongkuan Wu, Ao Shen, Yifan Guo, Yupeng Su, Graziano Chesi, Mingqiang Huang, Ngai Wong, Hao Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) deliver impressive performance in NLP, but their enormous parameter counts and computational costs pose severe challenges for deployment on resource-limited or specialized hardware. In this work, we propose APTQ+, an Attention-FFN-aware Post-Training Quantization framework that leverages second-order Hessian information to optimize both the self-attention and feed-forward network (FFN) sub-layers of transformers, including those with Softmax and SwiGLU activation. APTQ+ enables accurate, hardware-friendly 4-bit quantization via a symmetric INT4 scheme (with per-tensor scaling), and is co-designed with an end-to-end compiler for seamless FPGA deployment. Additionally, we introduce an integrated toolchain that combines algorithms, evaluation, compilation, and deployment. We demonstrate 4-bit inference on real LLMs (LLaMA-2 7B, LLaMA-3 8B, Qwen2, etc.) with negligible accuracy loss: for example, APTQ+ yields a WikiText-2 perplexity of 5.66 on LLaMA-2 7B (vs. 5.53 in FP16) and a mean zero-shot accuracy of 72.27% on a LLaMA-3 8B model (vs. 72.83% FP16). Moreover, APTQ+ outperforms prior 4-bit methods (e.g., GPTQ and AWQ) in both perplexity and zero-shot evaluations. Our FPGA-based accelerator delivers up to ×3.29 higher energy efficiency than an NVIDIA A100 GPU while achieving comparable throughput and latency. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first-of-its-kind end-to-end INT4 FPGA realization of an LLM, establishing a new state-of-the-art for low-bit deployment. A reference implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/EasonGZY/AutoAPTQ.

title: Gaussian Wardrobe: Compositional 3D Gaussian Avatars for Free-Form Virtual Try-On
authors: Zhiyi Chen, Jie Song, Hsuan-I Ho, Manuel Kaufmann, Tianjian Jiang, Chen Guo
abstract: We introduce Gaussian Wardrobe, a novel framework to digitalize compositional 3D neural avatars from multi-view videos. Existing methods for 3D neural avatars typically treat the human body and clothing as an inseparable entity. However, this paradigm fails to capture the dynamics of complex free-form garments and limits the reuse of clothing across different individuals. To overcome these problems, we develop a novel, compositional 3D Gaussian representation to build avatars from multiple layers of free-form garments. The core of our method is decomposing neural avatars into bodies and layers of shape-agnostic neural garments. To achieve this, our framework learns to disentangle each garment layer from multi-view videos and canonicalizes it into a shape-independent space. In experiments, our method models photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity dynamics, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on novel pose synthesis benchmarks. In addition, we demonstrate that the learned compositional garments contribute to a versatile digital wardrobe, enabling a practical virtual try-on application where clothing can be freely transferred to new subjects.

title: Latency and Joint Synchronization Analysis by Quantifying Usb Transport Bottlenecks in a Ros-Based Distributed Modular Robot
authors: Diogo F. Gomes, Paulo Costa, José Gonçalves, Vitor H. Pinto
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The pursuit of bio-inspired locomotion requires control architectures that are computationally efficient, deterministically synchronized, and modular. While centralized controllers offer simplicity, they suffer from wiring complexity. Consequently, distributed systems reduce cabling but introduce challenges in time synchronization due to communication latency. This paper presents an evaluation of a distributed control system for a quadrupedal robot utilizing the Raspberry Pi Pico 2W. An analysis was performed on a decentralized topology in which independent microcontrollers control specific parts of the robot rather than the entire system or a limb, and are synchronized via Micro-ROS over a serial transport layer. Leveraging the RP2350's dual-core symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) capabilities and FreeRTOS, the system maintains a 1 kHz motor control loop on Core 1, employing a “Hybrid SpinWait” strategy to minimize scheduling jitter. To systematically test the architecture, the system underwent five stress tests: Core Isolation Jitter Analysis, Dual-Board Step Response Latency, Safety Disconnect validation, and Time-based execution. Experimental data reveal a transport-induced bottleneck capping telemetry at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx$</tex> 400 Hz, despite internal generation >500 Hz, confirming that the USB-Serial interface is the primary limiter for dynamic gait synchronization, requiring a future transition to UDP-based Ethernet.

title: Static and Dynamic Dispatch in Robotics Control Loops: A Practical Performance Study in Rust
authors: David Mostoslavski, David Mejia, Xuan Wu, Kamilo Melo
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Robotic control loops require tight timing guarantees while remaining maintainable as hardware and platforms evolve. In Rust, trait-object dispatch enables open-world composition of heterogeneous components, whereas static dispatch (e.g., generics or enum-based polymorphism) can reduce call overhead but often introduces closed-world designs and boilerplate. We benchmark representative dispatch mechanisms in Rust and contextualize them against end-to-end actuator communication latency on a real robotic platform. In our setup, blocking device communication dominates loop time (hundreds of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu \mathrm{s}$</tex> to a few ms), while dispatch overhead remains in the tens of ns—an orders-of-magnitude separation that renders dispatch choice irrelevant to end-to-end timing in I/O-bound loops. In microbenchmarks without I/O, the fastest static mechanisms exhibit lower latency (approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5 0 \%}$</tex> in our measurements, corresponding to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \text{1. 5 ~ n s}$</tex>) and more predictable execution than trait-object dispatch, making them preferable in compute-bound hot paths and extremely high-rate inner loops. As actuator and bus latencies decrease and communication is moved off the critical path (e.g., via asynchronous I/O), such software-level costs can consume a larger fraction of the real-time budget. Overall, our results support using dynamic dispatch at hardware abstraction and composition boundaries today, while motivating static dispatch in performance-critical kernels as robotic systems become increasingly compute-bound.

title: Reconstructing Hand-Held Objects in 3D from Images and Videos
authors: Jane Wu, Georgios Pavlakos, Georgia Gkioxari, Jitendra Malik
abstract: Objects manipulated by the hand (i.e., manipulanda) are particularly challenging to reconstruct from Internet videos. Not only does the hand occlude much of the object, but also the object is often only visible in a small number of image pixels. At the same time, two strong anchors emerge in this setting: (1) estimated 3D hands help disambiguate the location and scale of the object, and (2) the set of manipulanda is small relative to all possible objects. With these insights in mind, we present a scalable paradigm for hand-held object reconstruction that builds on recent breakthroughs in large language/vision models and 3D object datasets. Given a monocular RGB video, we aim to reconstruct hand-held object geometry in 3D, over time. In order to obtain the best performing single frame model, we first present MCC-Hand-Object (MCCHO), which jointly reconstructs hand and object geometry given a single RGB image and inferred 3D hand as inputs. Subsequently, we prompt a text-to-3D generative model using a VLM to retrieve a 3D object model that matches the object in the image(s); we call this alignment Retrieval-Augmented Reconstruction (RAR). RAR provides unified object geometry across all frames, and the result is rigidly aligned with both the input images and 3D MCCHO observations in a temporally consistent manner. Experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-theart performance on lab and Internet image/video datasets. We make our code and models available on the project website: https://janehwu.github.io/mcc-ho

title: TexAvatars: Hybrid Texel-3D Representations for Stable Rigging of Photorealistic Gaussian Head Avatars
authors: Jaeseong Lee, Junyeong Ahn, Taewoong Kang, Jaegul Choo
abstract: Constructing drivable and photorealistic 3D head avatars has become a central task in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A R / X R$</tex>, enabling immersive and expressive user experiences. With the emergence of high-fidelity, efficient representations such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 D$</tex> Gaussians, recent works have pushed toward ultra-detailed head avatars. Existing approaches typically fall into two categories: rule-based analytic rigging or neural networkbased deformation fields. While effective in constrained settings, both approaches often fail to generalize to unseen expressions and poses-particularly in extreme reenactment scenarios. Other methods constrain Gaussians to the global texel space of 3DMMs to reduce rendering complexity. However, these texel-based avatars tend to underutilize the underlying mesh structure. They apply minimal analytic deformation and rely heavily on neural regressors and heuristic regularization in UV space, which weakens geometric consistency and limits extrapolation to complex, out-of-distribution deformations. To address these limitations, we introduce TexAvatars, a hybrid avatar representation that combines the explicit geometric grounding of analytic rigging with the spatial continuity of texel space. Our approach predicts local geometric attributes in UV space via CNNs, but drives 3D deformation through mesh-aware Jacobians, enabling smooth and semantically meaningful transitions across triangle boundaries. This hybrid design separates semantic modeling from geometric control, resulting in improved generalization, interpretability, and stability. Furthermore, TexAvatars captures fine-grained expression effects-including muscle-induced wrinkles, glabellar lines, and realistic mouth cavity geom-etry-with high fidelity. Our method achieves state-of-theart performance under extreme pose and expression vari-ations, demonstrating strong generalization in challenging head reenactment settings.

title: Supervising 3D Talking Head Avatars with Analysis-by-Audio-Synthesis
authors: Radek Dančeěcek, Carolin Schmitt, Senya Polikovsky, Michael J. Black
abstract: In order to be widely applicable, speech-driven 3D head avatars must articulate their lips in accordance with speech, while also conveying the appropriate emotions with dynamically changing facial expressions. The key problem is that deterministic models produce high-quality lip-sync but without rich expressions, whereas stochastic models generate diverse expressions but with lower lip-sync quality. To get the best of both, we seek a stochastic model with accurate lipsync. To that end, we develop a new approach based on the following observation: if a method generates realistic 3D lip motions, it should be possible to infer the spoken audio from the lip motion. The inferred speech should match the original input audio, and erroneous predictions create a novel supervision signal for training 3D talking head avatars with accurate lip-sync. To demonstrate this effect, we propose THUNDER (Talking Heads Under Neural Differentiable Elocution Reconstruction), a 3D talking head avatar framework that introduces a novel supervision mechanism via differentiable sound production. First, we train a novel mesh-tospeech model that regresses audio from facial animation. Then, we incorporate this model into a diffusion-based talking avatar framework. During training, the mesh-to-speech model takes the generated animation and produces a sound that is compared to the input speech, creating a differentiable analysis-by-audio-synthesis supervision loop. Our extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that THUNDER significantly improves the quality of the lip-sync of talking head avatars while still allowing for generation of diverse, high-quality, expressive facial animations.

title: Design of a Narrowband Low-Noise Amplifier with Optimized Input Matching for GPS Receiver at 1.575 GHz
authors: Nafisa Anjum Promi, Tasnim Mehedy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Global Positioning System (GPS) technology is an indispensable part of navigation, transportation, precision agriculture, and many other countless location-based services in daily life. However, GPS signals arriving at Earth are inherently weak and highly susceptible to noise and interference. The Low Noise Amplifier (LNA) ensures these signals are amplified while introducing minimal noise. In this paper we introduced a low noise amplifier (LNA) for a GPS receiver operating at 1.575 GHz, designed and simulated in Cadence Virtuoso with the gpdk090 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(90 \text{nm})$</tex> CMOS process. Efficient input matching is done using gate inductance along with an inductive degenerated commonsource (CS) amplifier, which increases the gain of the circuit, maintaining the low noise figure (NF). The output part of the LNA incorporates an LC matching circuit, including an LC tank, serving as a bandpass filter that allows the desired frequency while attenuating spurious signals. Simulation achieved a gain of 18.4 dB with excellent input matching of −44.2 dB and a very low noise figure of 0.502 dB. The design is intended to enhance the GPS receiver's energy efficiency, as the system consumes 6 mW DC power at 1.8 V, increasing reliability and environmental sustainability by reducing component count and hardware size.

title: Deep Learning-Based Leaf Diseases Detection in Indoor Ornamental Plants: A Comparative Study of YOLOv8, YOLOv9, and YOLOv11
authors: Nishad Mahmud Opu, Md Saiful Islam, Ruma Akter, Kazi Maisha Jannath, Shahriar Ahmed, Rakibul Islam Rafi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate leaf disease detection in indoor ornamental plants remains a critical challenge due to reliance on subjective visual inspection and the high cost of molecular diagnostics. This work addresses the need for an efficient and lightweight system to monitor plant health and prevent disease spread in controlled environments. The main objective is to develop and evaluate a comprehensive end-to-end detection model, based on modern YOLO architectures, to identify and localize nine disease and healthy classes across three species: Money Plant, Snake Plant, and Spider Plant. A custom dataset of 787 highresolution images was collected under variable lighting and backgrounds, annotated and expanded to 3,935 images via systematic augmentation. Three variants-YOLOv8s, YOLOv9s, and the YOLOv11s were trained on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$640 \times 640$</tex> inputs with AdamW optimization and evaluated using Precision, Recall, mAP@50, and mAP@50-95. YOLOv11s achieved superior performance, recording 97.4% mAP @ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50,89.3 \% \text{mAP}$</tex> @ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50-95,93.7 \%$</tex> Precision, and 96.7% Recall on the held-out test set. Class-wise analysis revealed consistently high recall, with minor confusions among visually similar disease symptoms. To enhance interpretability, occlusion sensitivity and RISE (Randomized Input Sampling for Explanation) analyses were employed, confirming that the model focuses on disease-relevant leaf regions while suppressing background features. The YOLOv11s framework delivers an accurate and interpretable solution for indoor plant disease monitoring, enabling early diagnosis and timely intervention in ornamental plant care.

title: Effect of Augmentations on CNN Based Models for Congenital Heart Disease Detection from Chest X-Rays
authors: Sauharda Sarkar, Farha Yesmin, Md. Abrar Sayed Tasin, Fatin Israq, Siam Tahsin Bhuiyan, Rashedur Rahman, Saadia Binte Alam, Ashraful Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Congenital heart disease is one of the most common cardiovascular diseases, and its early detection is critical for effective treatment. Although chest X-ray imaging is affordable and accessible in both rural and urban health centers, manual interpretation for the diagnosis of CHD is challenging and prone to incorrect diagnoses. In this study, we classify chest X-ray images into four classes: normal, atrial septal defect (ASD), ventricular septal defect (VSD), and patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) based on deep learning models. A publicly available Kaggle competition dataset was used in our study, which consists of 828 X-ray images. Four Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models are trained and evaluated. Experimental results show that EfficientNetB1 with moderate augmentation achieved the highest test accuracy of 69.51 %, followed by DenseNet121 with 66.46 %, while MobileNetV2 yielded the worst performance at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 0. 9 8 \%}$</tex>. These findings show the augmentation effect on deep learning models for automated CHD detection from chest X-ray images.

title: OvaSignal AI: An Explainable and Real-Time Machine Learning Framework for Early Prediction of Ovarian Cancer
authors: Esrath Kanon, Md Sadi Al Huda, Md Shahidul Khan Pappo, Md Shamsul Alam, Kazi Tanvir, Md Asraf Ali, Syamimi Mardiah Shaharum
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Ovarian cancer remains among the most fatal gynecological cancers, largely because it is often diagnosed at advanced stages. To tackle this problem, we introduce OvaSignal AI, a real-time framework for early prediction using routine blood tests, biochemical indicators, and tumor-specific markers. The data were carefully preprocessed with class balancing and recursive feature elimination, which reduced noise and selecting the 20 most informative attributes. We evaluated seven machine learning models, such as Random Forest, Logistic Regression, KNN, AdaBoost, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and SVM alongside two deep learning models, ANN and LSTM. Among these, SVM and LR achieved the highest accuracy of 94.3 %, outperforming other approaches. To ensure trust and usability, we applied explainable AI methods such as SHAP and LIME, which provided clear global and local insights into model predictions. The system was deployed through a Streamlit application, which provides privacy, rapid predictions, clinician-friendly interfaces, and downloadable reports. Compared to earlier investigations, our technique demonstrated more accuracy and therapeutic relevance. This makes OvaSignal AI both interpretable and applicable to healthcare use. In conclusion, the study proposes a dependable, transparent, and real-time strategy for enhancing early ovarian cancer prediction.

title: Quantum Multiple Rotation Averaging
authors: Shuteng Wang, Natacha Kuete Meli, Michael Moller, Vladislav Golyanik
abstract: Multiple rotation averaging (MRA) is a fundamental optimization problem in 3D vision and robotics that aims to recover globally consistent absolute rotations from noisy relative measurements Established classical methods, such as L1-IRLS and Shonan, face limitations including local minima susceptibility and reliance on convex relaxations that fail to preserve the exact manifold geometry, leading to reduced accuracy in high-noise scenarios. We introduce IQARS (Iterative Quantum Annealing for Rotation Synchronization), the first algorithm that reformulates MRA as a sequence of local quadratic non-convex sub-problems executable on quantum annealers after binarization, to leverage inherent hardware advantages. IQARS removes convex relaxation dependence and better preserves non-Euclidean rotation manifold geometry while leveraging quantum tunneling and parallelism for efficient solution space exploration. We evaluate IQARS's performance on synthetic and real-world datasets. While current annealers remain in their nascent phase and only support solving problems of limited scale with constrained performance, we observed that IQARS on D-Wave annealers can already achieve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 12 {\%}$</tex> higher accuracy than Shonan-the bestperforming classical method evaluated empirically. Project page: https://4dqv.mpi-inf.mpg.de/QMRA/.

title: SocialGen: Modeling Multi-Human Social Interaction with Language Models
authors: Heng Yu, Juze Zhang, Changan Chen, Tiange Xiang, Yusu Fang, Juan Carlos Niebles, Ehsan Adeli
abstract: Human interactions in everyday life are inherently social, involving engagements with diverse individuals across various contexts. Modeling these social interactions is fundamental to a wide range of real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce SocialGen, the first unified motionlanguage model capable of modeling interaction behaviors among varying numbers of individuals, to address this crucial yet challenging problem. Unlike prior methods that are limited to two-person interactions, we propose a novel social motion representation that supports tokenizing the motions of an arbitrary number of individuals and aligning them with the language space. This alignment enables the model to leverage rich, pretrained linguistic knowledge to better understand and reason about human social behaviors. To tackle the challenges of data scarcity, we curate a comprehensive multi-human interaction dataset, SocialX, enriched with textual annotations. Leveraging this dataset, we establish the first comprehensive benchmark for multihuman interaction tasks. Our method achieves state-of-theart performance across motion-language tasks, setting a new standard for multi-human interaction modeling. Our dataset and source code will be made publicly available.

title: STen: Productive and Efficient Sparsity in PyTorch
authors: Andrei Ivanov, Nikoli Dryden, Tal Ben-Nun, Timo Schneider, Saleh Ashkboos, Torsten Hoefler
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: A 28-nm Fully-Integrated Reconfigurable CMOS Simulated Coherent Ising Machine With Dynamic Pump-Rate Control and Spin Freezing
authors: Qiaoyi Fu, Yifeng Zhou, Qinchao Cai, Wenzhen An, Renlong Li, Lei Liao, Zhuojun Chen
abstract: Combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) play a critical role in various domains but are computationally challenging. Although coherent Ising machines (CIMs) exploit quantum-inspired dynamics to solve COPs, their optical implementations suffer from bulky components and high system cost. In this work, we present the first fully-integrated CMOS-based simulated CIM (SCIM). The proposed design integrates a Booth-optimized multiplier–accumulator and piecewise linear activation circuit, thereby reducing power consumption and area. A reconfigurable fully-connected topology enables direct mapping of a wide range of COPs, supporting variable spin counts and coupling bit-widths. We further introduce a dynamic pump-rate control that accelerates escape from high-energy states and enables fine-grained search near the bifurcation point, along with a spin freezing (SF) technique that stabilizes converged spins and eliminates redundant updates. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS technology, SCIM occupies 0.354 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and consumes only 2.952 mW. For MAX-CUT benchmarks, it achieves time-to-solution (TTS) ranging from 0.15 to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$30~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s, corresponding to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1653\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup over state-of-the-art hardware implementations in terms of normalized TTS. Furthermore, the number partitioning problem (NPP) and multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) detection are performed using the proposed SCIM, which achieves high-performance solving, thereby demonstrating a reconfigurable, power-efficient, and high-speed approach for physics-inspired combinatorial optimization.

title: A Method for Determining the DC Capacitance of Solid Dielectric Capacitors: A Case Study
authors: A Hemavathi, Thomas John, P Anagha, Priyanka Jain, R.P. Aloysius
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper presents a method for estimating the DC capacitance of solid dielectric capacitors. Using a high-precision capacitance bridge, we measured the capacitance-frequency profiles of polypropylene capacitors (ranging from 100 pF to 100 nF) from 10 kHz down to 50 Hz. The measurements demonstrate a clear frequency dependence, where capacitance decreases as frequency increases. We observed that the capacitance-frequency profile can be approximated by a sum of decaying exponentials. Consequently, we derived an empirical formula to extrapolate the measured data down to millihertz frequencies. The values calculated using this formula align closely with measured data in the overlapping frequency range, providing a smooth extrapolation from 10 kHz down to DC. Notably, the DC capacitance of the tested polypropylene capacitors was found to be approximately 0.1% higher than their calibrated values at 1 kHz. Establishing accurate DC values is critical for the application of these capacitors in precision DC metrology.

title: A Thermally Controlled Multifunctional Polarization-Independent Metasurface for 6G Applications
authors: Ajeet Singh Verma, Somak Bhattacharyya
abstract: This article presents a multifunctional metasurface (MS) structure maintaining subwavelength thickness and periodicity with compact geometry. The proposed structure can simultaneously function as a transmittive, reflective, and absorptive type MS in distinct frequency bands in the terahertz (THz) region, utilizing a single metalayer design. The metalayer consists of three concentric rings, where one of them is made of vanadium dioxide (VO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), while the other two are made up of gold. The metalayer is sandwiched between two distinct silicon dioxide (SiO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) layers, where the bottom SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> layer is VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> backed. The structure offers functional switchability to act as a dual stopband (0.74 THz each), separated by a narrow transmission band (0.2 THz) to dual absorption bands (0.11 and 1.11 THz) separated by a reflection band (1.25 THz), attributed to the thermal properties of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. The proposed MS offers wide angular stability up to 60° incident angles for both TE and TM polarizations, while the fourfold symmetry of the meta-atom makes the structure polarization-insensitive in nature. Furthermore, the performance of the proposed structure is validated by incorporating an in-house developed equivalent circuit model (ECM). Exhibiting significantly superior features in comparison to the state-of-the-art literature, the proposed structure can be utilized for sensing and communication for selective signal modulation in the next generation 6G communication.

title: Time-Frequency and Multi-Scale Learning Network for Long-Term Cloud Workload Prediction
authors: Weijian Zheng, Weiping Zheng, Yiqi Chen, Zichen Wang, Yimin Mao
abstract: Accurate long-term workload prediction (LTWP) is crucial for effective resource management in cloud environments, where workload dynamics are characterized by high variability, noise, and long-range temporal dependencies. Existing methods, such as linear models and PatchTST-based models, ofen struggle with capturing long-range dependencies, handling noise, and adapting to diverse workload patterns. To address these issues, this paper proposes TFMSNet, a novel time-frequency and multi-scale learning network for LTWP. TFMSNet integrates low-frequency wavelet features to capture global workload trends and high-frequency components to model local variations and change points, enabling more comprehensive temporal representation. Its multi-branch architecture allows for efficient capture of multi-scale dependencies, enhancing adaptability to diverse and dynamic workloads. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets—Azure-2017, Alibaba-2018, Google-2019, Alibaba-2020 and Alibaba-2021—demonstrate that TFMSNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models in performance metrics of MAE, MSE, RMSE and MAPE across multiple prediction horizons. Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of frequency-enhanced attention and multi-branch architecture in improving accuracy. Moreover, the model's robustness under noisy conditions and its efficiency with shorter input sequences further highlight its practical applicability for real time, resource-constrained cloud environments. Code is available at: https://github.com/jiange6daobao/TFMSNet-Workload Forecasting

title: Front Cover Page: Welcome Message
authors: N/A
