title: Emo-gestor: 3D Co-speech Body Generation Based on Multimodal Emotion-Driven
authors: Qiyun Yang, Daowu Yang, Ruihui Li, Yunchuan Qin, Xu Zhou, Zhuo Tang
abstract: The task of generating vivid and diverse 3D co-speech body movements relies on the coordination of linguistic and non-linguistic cues. A key challenge lies in maintaining rhythmic consistency while effectively integrating semantic and emotional information. In this paper, we propose Emo-gestor, a multimodal emotion-driven framework for 3D co-speech body generation, which can synthesize personalized and emotionally consistent full-body gestures in a unified multimodal setting. Considering that emotional signals are often entangled within audio, text, and facial modalities, we separately extract and encode emotional features from each modality, and construct a unified multimodal emotional representation through an emotion embedding mechanism to guide the generation of rhythmically synchronized and emotionally expressive gestures. Then we introduce a conditional diffusion model with a Transformer to ensure the alignment of gesture content with emotional cues. To enhance the model’s capability of capturing long-term dependencies and mitigate emotional non-smoothness caused by selfattention mechanisms, we propose a Multimodal Emotion Body Reconstruction Module, which integrates Emotion linear convolution into the self-attention mechanism to linearly capture longrange dependencies while preserving short-term detail modeling. Experimental results demonstrate that Emo-gestor significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of emotional consistency, naturalness, and personalization.

title:
R
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    TT: High-Accuracy 3D Localization Using Ray-Tracing-Based Wi-Fi RTT Virtual Fingerprints for Challenging Scenarios
authors: Xin Wang, Xikun Liu, Zhen Lyu, Ruijie Xu, Weisong Wen
abstract: Wi-Fi fingerprinting based on signal strength adapts well to complex indoor signal distributions but is limited by low positioning accuracy and the high cost of fingerprint database construction. Wi-Fi round-trip time (RTT) positioning can achieve meter-level accuracy in open environments but suffers from significant signal reflection effects in complex indoor spaces. This paper proposes R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>TT (Ray-Tracing Round-Trip Time), a high-precision 3D indoor positioning system that combines the advantages of fingerprint-based and ranging-based methods by leveraging virtual RTT ranging fingerprints. More importantly, virtual fingerprint generation eliminates the need for labor-intensive data collection, enabling efficient 3D fingerprint construction. The proposed method consists of two stages: (1) constructing a virtual RTT fingerprint database using low-cost light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and ray tracing techniques and (2) employing deep learning to capture signal reflection characteristics for robust and high-accuracy localization. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that in complex, reflective environments, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>TT achieves a 3D positioning error of approximately 1.3 meters, improving 3D accuracy by over 65% compared to traditional least squares.

title: Active Security Resilient Optimal Strategy for Safe and Stable Autonomous Driving under CAN Bus Attacks
authors: Guoqiang Li, Zhenyang Li, Yu Lu, Hongru Zhang
abstract: The Controller Area Network (CAN) is the core component of in-vehicle network communications, which is highly vulnerable to malicious cyberattacks due to the lack of authentication and encryption, leading to serious threat to the safety of autonomous driving. To solve this issue, this paper proposes an innovative active security resilient optimal strategy for distributed drive electric vehicles (DDEVs) to defend against various CAN bus attacks for safe and stable autonomous driving. First, a data-driven system modeling approach for DDEVs is designed to describe the nonlinear dynamics accurately under three typical CAN attacks on vehicle position and heading states. Then, a novel multi-sensor-information-based detection method with sliding window cumulative residual is developed to detect the attack timely. When the CAN attack is detected, the abnormal signals are removed and replaced with image-based state estimates before being fed into the controller. Once the attack defense is activated, a novel integrated optimal control method is proposed, jointly optimizing the steering and four-wheel torques to achieve precise path tracking and stable vehicle motion control. Extensive simulations on a MATLAB/Simulink and CarSim co-simulation platform validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach on the attack detection, the secure state estimation, and the optimal control, and hardware-in-the-loop experiments further confirm the real-time feasibility of the proposed control algorithm.

title: A Discrete Space Vector Modulation-based Model-Free Predictive Control with Improved Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Observer for Permanent Magnet Linear Synchronous Machine
authors: Chengwei Luo, Ying Zou, Zhimeng Rao, Ming Li
abstract: Due to its simple implementation and excellent performance, model predictive control (MPC) is applied to permanent magnet linear synchronous machine (PMLSM) drive systems. However, the selected voltage vector (VV) in conventional MPC has large error and thrust fluctuation. In addition, it also exhibits strong parameter dependency, and mismatched parameters can deteriorate control performance. Thereof, to further improve control performance, a discrete space vector modulation based MPC (DSVM-MPC) strategy is proposed, which redefined the action region of the VV and redesigned the optimal VV search algorithm based on the action region. Compared with conventional methods, the proposed method can obtain more accurate VV under similar computational burden. Moreover, a model free predictive control (MFPC) strategy based on an improved super-twist sliding mode observer (ST-SMO) is designed, which introduces an exponential term in the convergence rate function, not only has higher control accuracy, but also improves convergence speed. To filter out the dc-offset signals in the sampling currents, a two-degree of-freedom (TDF) filter is also designed for DSVM-MFPC. Finally, the superiority of the proposed method is verified through simulations and experiments on the prototype.

title: Learning Policy-Dependent Traversability for Terrain-Aware Quadruped Navigation_supp1-3696520.mp4
authors: Jiahu Qin
abstract: Quadruped robots have exhibited highly adaptive locomotion capabilities, yet reliable navigation on complex mixed unstructured terrain remains a major challenge. A key difficulty is that existing navigation frameworks typically rely on geometric costmaps, which fail to capture the fact that terrain traversability is inherently policy-dependent, as different locomotion controllers exhibit distinct contact strategies, dynamic limits, and behavioral capabilities. To address this issue, we present a unified framework that learns and exploits policy-dependent traversability for large-scale autonomous navigation with quadrupedal robots. We first develop a data-driven traversability estimator that predicts the traversability of each terrain region under a given locomotion policy, trained from a kernel-based traversability sample map aggregated from simulated policy rollouts. This estimator is used to construct a terrain-aware costmap with policy-dependent feasibility constraints. Then, a two-stage planning pipeline with grid-based search and trajectory optimization is proposed to generate smooth, policy-feasible global trajectories for execution by the corresponding locomotion policy. Finally, we extend the framework to multiple locomotion policies, enabling automatic policy switching based on predicted traversability and enhancing adaptability to diverse terrain conditions. Experiments across large and challenging environments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation reliability, terrain adaptability, and long-horizon autonomy for quadrupedal robots.

title: A Configurable RISC-V Co-Processor with Instruction-Controlled Stream-Based Accelerators
authors: Rohan Krishna Vijayaraghavan, Ahmed Kamaleldin, Matthias Nickel, Lester Kalms, Diana Göhringer
publish: ACM Transactions on Reconfigurable Technology and Systems
abstract: None

title: Teaming Up with Artificial Agents in Non-routine Analytical Tasks
authors: Lorenzo Cominelli, Federico Andrea Galatolo, Caterina Giannetti, Felice Dell'Orletta, Cristiano Ciaccio, Philipp Chapkovski, Giulia Venturi
abstract: None

title: Expressing Emotion Through Heartbeat: A Soft Robotic Heart Integrated With a Soft Sensor for Arts in Robotics_supp2-3687384.mp4
authors: Jian ZHU
abstract: The heartbeat is both a physiological signal and a universal expression of emotion. This work presents a pneumatically actuated soft robotic heart for interactive art. A fully 3D-printed, shielded soft capacitive bending sensor is integrated onto the heart’s surface, providing stable, real-time displacement feedback without affecting its motion. A lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) model estimates deformation from the sensor signal and enables closed loop control. Emotional states are selected via a graphical interface or inferred from ambient light to modulate target heart rates (0.5–3 Hz) for displacement control. Physiologically inspired trajectories derived from left ventricular volume data (Wiggers diagram) are reproduced at 0.5 Hz and 1 Hz. A state-switched proportional–derivative (PD) controller drives the heart to track these targets with good accuracy and robustness. In public exhibitions, the heart was paired with the poetic narrative “To My Love. ” The work shows how soft actuation, soft sensing, machine learning, and feedback control can be combined with physiological modeling and artistic expression in a single soft robotic platform.

title: TRIM: Tactile Road Interactive Mapping for Real-Time Haptic Feedback
authors: Huimin Jiao, Yaoyu Duan, Haodong Zhang, Lizhi Song, Mingjin Xu, Xingwei Guo
abstract: Accurate tactile perception of ground surfaces is crucial for non-visual navigation and terrain awareness in various assistive applications. However, existing tactile interfaces often fail to provide high-resolution, low-latency, and cost-effective feedback across diverse terrains. This study presents an interactive tactile map that integrates Digital Elevation Model (DEM) processing, reconstructed tactile input via a piezoresistive sensor array, and vibration feedback based on the 2D funneling illusion. Through an algorithmic rendering pipeline, the system transforms multidimensional DEM attributes—elevation, slope, and roughness—into dynamic tactile signals with fine spatial detail. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed rendering algorithm achieves sub-millisecond latency and significantly enhances environmental perception, confirming its effectiveness for real-time tactile terrain representation.

title: Vibration Reduction of Nonswitching High Frequency Band in FSCW IPMSMs With Harmonic-Oriented Slotted Rotor
authors: Zhuting Zhao, Renxin Xiao, Wenxiang Zhao, Yuhua Sun, Yunhan Zhou
abstract: In this article, a harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is proposed to significantly reduce vibration without sacrificing the torque performance in the fractional-slot concentrated-winding (FSCW) interior permanent magnet synchronous machines (IPMSMs). First, the generation mechanism of nonswitching high frequency band vibration is revealed. Unlike conventional theory, nonswitching high frequency band vibration dominates instead of the low frequency band vibration in the 10-slot/8-pole FSCW IPMSMs. Second, a harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is designed by constructing the compensation function. The required air-gap permeance phase angle is determined based on the permanent magnet flux density distribution. In this way, the specific permanent magnet flux density harmonics can be directly reduced. Finally, the FSCW IPMSM with harmonic-oriented slotted rotor is manufactured and tested. The experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: IEEE Quantum Week
authors: N/A

title: GSFix3D: Diffusion-Guided Repair of Novel Views in Gaussian Splatting
authors: Jiaxin Wei, Stefan Leutenegger, Simon Schaefer
abstract: Recent developments in 3D Gaussian Splatting have significantly enhanced novel view synthesis, yet generating high-quality renderings from extreme novel viewpoints or partially observed regions remains challenging. Meanwhile, diffusion models exhibit strong generative capabilities, but their lack of awareness of specific scene information hinders accurate 3D reconstruction tasks. To address these limitations, we introduce GSFix3D, a novel framework that improves the visual fidelity in under-constrained regions by distilling prior knowledge from diffusion models into 3D representations, while preserving consistency with observed scene details. At its core is GSFixer, a latent diffusion model obtained via our customized finetuning protocol that can leverage both mesh and 3D Gaussians to adapt pretrained generative models to a variety of environments and artifact types from different reconstruction methods, enabling robust novel view repair for unseen camera poses. Moreover, we propose a random mask augmentation strategy that empowers GSFixer to plausibly inpaint missing regions. Experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that our GSFix3D and GSFixer achieve state-of-the-art performance, requiring only minimal scene-specific fine-tuning on captured data. Realworld test further confirms its resilience to potential pose errors. Our code and data are publicly available: https://github.com/GSFix3D/GSFix3D.

title: Evaluation of Convolutional Neural Network-Based Computer Vision Architectures for the Detection of Maize Foliar Diseases
authors: Pablo Sari, Hugo Torres
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The study presents a comparative evaluation of six computer vision architectures applied to the automatic detection of maize foliar conditions, including Healthy Leaf, Northern Leaf Blight, Common Rust, and Gray Leaf Spot. For this purpose, the models Faster R-CNN, SSD, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8n, MobileNetV3-Large, and EfficientNetV2-RW were trained and analyzed using a dataset collected in the field and manually annotated. The methodology included data augmentation techniques, standardized training for 20 epochs, and evaluation using widely used object detection metrics such as mAP@0.5, mAP@0.5:0.95, average recall, performance by object size, and inference speed. The results show that YOLOv8-based architectures achieve the best overall performance, with YOLOv8m reaching an mAP@0.5 of 0.72 and an mAP@0.5:0.95 of 0.41, while YOLOv8n achieves 32 FPS with a reduced model size of 8.7 MB, making it suitable for embedded implementations on drones. In contrast, Faster R-CNN and SSD present limitations in detecting small lesions, while lightweight models such as MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNetV2-RW offer lower precision but high speed. These findings confirm the potential of the YOLOv8 family for real agricultural applications aimed at early monitoring of diseases and foliar damage in maize crops.

title: PromptVFX: Text-Driven Fields for Open-World 3D Gaussian Animation
authors: Mert Kiray, Paul Uhlenbruck, Nassir Navab, Benjamin Busam
abstract: Visual effects (VFX) are key to immersion in modern films, games, and AR/VR. Creating 3D effects requires specialized expertise and training in 3D animation software and can be time consuming. Generative solutions typically rely on computationally intense methods such as diffusion models which can be slow at 4D inference. We reformulate 3D animation as a field prediction task and introduce a text-driven framework that infers a time-varying 4D flow field acting on 3D Gaussians. By leveraging large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) for function generation, our approach interprets arbitrary prompts (e.g., “make the vase glow orange, then explode”) and instantly updates color, opacity, and positions of 3D Gaussians in real time. This design avoids overheads such as mesh extraction, manual or physics-based simulations and allows both novice and expert users to animate volumetric scenes with minimal effort on a consumer device even in a web browser<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>Web Demo: http://promptvfx.duckdns.org. Experimental results show that simple textual instructions suffice to generate compelling time-varying VFX, reducing the manual effort typically required for rigging or advanced modeling. We thus present a fast and accessible pathway to language-driven 3D content creation that can pave the way to democratize VFX further. Code available at https://obsphera.github.io/promptvfx/.

title: Control-Oriented Modeling of Electrode Material for Mechanical Notching and Stacking Processes of Battery Manufacturing
authors: Andreas Rauh, Lisa Wünderlich
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The processes of notching and stacking represent two crucial stages of battery cell manufacturing that have a significant influence on the quality of the produced cells from the perspective of lifetime. In addition, these stages are also crucial concerning safety, especially, when the production of cells with high energy density is concerned. This is due to the fact that imperfect cutting edge quality in notching (due to burr) may lead to sharp edges that cut through separator foils when stacking is executed. This may produce internal short circuits in produced cells that are possibly not detected during the formation stage but only show up during operation when internal pressure and temperature variations in battery cells lead to a gradual extension of this defect. The same is also true for mechanical deformations of electrodes during their transport within the stacking process which may lead to wrinkles as well as cracks. Therefore, this paper presents a control-oriented modeling procedure for flexible electrodes that can be used for forecasting deflections in elastic and plastic regimes during notching and stacking and therefore is inevitable for a model-based design of control strategies for both handling stages. The core of this modeling approach is the replacement of the governing partial differential equation for the material deformation by a model that is discretized in space and time. For that purpose, the Crank-Nicolson approach is employed due to its favorable property of preserving the stability properties when discretizing the underlying infinite-dimensional model.

title: Accuracy and Precision Tests of the Neuromorphic Robotic Arm ED-Scorbot
authors: Diogenes E. Trejos-Hernandez, Elena Cerezuela-Escudero, Alejandro Linares-Barranco
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: In this research, we analyse the Accuracy and Precision (P-ACC) performance of the ED-Scorbot robotic arm, which is controlled by SPID (Spiking Proportional-IntegralDerivative) controllers inspired by neuroscience and implemented on FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Arrays). This implementation offers benefits in terms of energy consumption, hardware utilization, and timing (latency) efficiency. However, it also presents certain constraints that affect the precision and accuracy of the robot. The main goal of this study is to identify which parameter variations-specifically, the Command Transmission Interval (CTI) to the robot and the distance traveled by the Tool Center Point (TCP) between successive points along a reference trajectory-lead to the lowest error in P-ACC tests. Accuracy and repeatability were assessed according to the definitions set out in ISO 9283, which consider the repetition of the movement towards a target for the calculation of the mean deviation of the TCP. Initially, tests were conducted independently on each joint, and subsequently, multi-repetition trajectories were executed, resulting in a minimum error of 13.17 mm for a CTI of 1 second and a Point-To-Point Distance (PTPD) of 11.8 mm.

title: An Event-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Autonomous Air Combat Tactical Decision-Making
authors: Chen Zhang, Minchi Kuang, Heng Shi, Jihong Zhu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: To address the challenges of low training efficiency and the difficult credit assignment problem in applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to high-fidelity, continuous-time air combat simulation-caused by the fundamental timescale mismatch between high-frequency simulation steps and lowfrequency tactical decisions-this paper proposes a novel framework named Event-Driven Decision Sampling (EDS). This framework precisely aligns the agent's decision-making tempo with the tactical tempo by asynchronously triggering decisions based on pre-defined tactical events. It is combined with PPO and LSTM to construct an intelligent agent capable of handling longterm dependencies and partial observability. Validation was performed on a self-developed, high-fidelity air combat simulation platform. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to traditional uniform sampling methods, the proposed approach significantly improves training efficiency, with the agent converging to a mission success rate of over 80 % after approximately 50,000 training episodes and exhibiting various advanced, intelligent tactical behaviors.

title: Design, Theoretical, and Experimental Validation of a Geometrically-Optimized Electromagnetic Air Valve for Linear Velocity Output
authors: Kawthar U. Witwit, Hussein Firas, Mohammed Sadiq, Ibrahim Nabeel, Ibrahim AL-Tameemi
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Achieving linear airflow velocity in pneumatic systems remains a complex challenge, as most existing valves function in a binary on/off mode. This study introduces an electrically actuated air valve driven by a voice coil mechanism designed to deliver continuous linear airflow. An analytical model was developed to incorporate the relevant geometric and operational parameters of the valve and relate them to the air velocity output. A 3D-printed prototype was fabricated and experimentally evaluated, demonstrating linear plunger displacement across a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 c m}$</tex> stroke and a corresponding linear output air velocity ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0. 6}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 3. 4 ~ m} / \mathrm{s} \pm \text{0. 1}$</tex>. Additionally, a numerical model was implemented to evaluate the velocity distribution in the fluid field within the valve. The performance of the valve exhibited a high degree of linearity, with a coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.895$</tex>), confirming the potential of the proposed design for precise pneumatic control and automation applications.

title: GMT: Goal-Conditioned Multimodal Transformer for 6-DOF Object Trajectory Synthesis in 3D Scenes
authors: Huajian Zeng, Abhishek Saroha, Daniel Cremers, Xi Wang
abstract: Synthesizing controllable 6-DOF object manipulation trajectories in 3D environments is essential for enabling robots to interact with complex scenes, yet remains challenging due to the need for accurate spatial reasoning, physical feasibility, and multimodal scene understanding. Existing approaches often rely on 2D or partial 3D representations, limiting their ability to capture full scene geometry and constraining trajectory precision. We present GMT, a multimodal transformer framework that generates realistic and goal-directed object trajectories by jointly leveraging 3D bounding box geometry, point cloud context, semantic object categories, and target end poses. The model represents trajectories as continuous 6-DOF pose sequences and employs a tailored conditioning strategy that fuses geometric, semantic, contextual, and goal-oriented information. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that GMT outperforms state-of-the-art human motion and human-object interaction baselines, such as CHOIS and GIMO, achieving substantial gains in spatial accuracy and orientation control. Our method establishes a new benchmark for learning-based manipulation planning and shows strong generalization to diverse objects and cluttered 3D environments.

title: KaoLRM: Repurposing Pre-Trained Large Reconstruction Models for Parametric 3D Face Reconstruction
authors: Qingtian Zhu, Xu Cao, Zhixiang Wang, Yinqiang Zheng, Takafumi Taketomi
abstract: We propose KaoLRM to re-target the learned prior of the Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) for parametric 3D face reconstruction from single-view images. Parametric 3D Morphable Models (3DMMs) have been widely used for facial reconstruction due to their compact and interpretable parameterization, yet existing 3DMM regressors often exhibit poor consistency across varying viewpoints. To address this, we harness the pre-trained 3D prior of LRM and incorporate FLAME-based 2D Gaussian Splatting into LRM's rendering pipeline. Specifically, KaoLRM projects LRM's pre-trained triplane features into the FLAME parameter space to recover geometry, and models appearance via 2D Gaussian primitives that are tightly coupled to the FLAME mesh. The rich prior enables the FLAME regressor to be aware of the 3D structure, leading to accu-rate and robust reconstructions under self-occlusions and diverse viewpoints. Experiments on both controlled and in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that KaoLRM achieves superior reconstruction accuracy and cross-view consistency, while existing methods remain sensitive to viewpoint variations. The code is released at https://github.com/CyberAgentAILab/KaoLRM.

title: X-LRM: X-Ray Large Reconstruction Model for Extremely Sparse-View Computed Tomography Recovery in One Second
authors: Guofeng Zhang, Ruyi Zha, Hao He, Yixun Liang, Alan Yuille, Hongdong Li, Yuanhao Cai
abstract: Sparse-view 3D CT reconstruction aims to recover volumetric structures from a limited number of 2D X-ray projections. Existing feedforward methods are constrained by the scarcity of large-scale training datasets and the absence of direct and consistent 3D representations. In this paper, we propose an X-ray Large Reconstruction Model (X-LRM) for extremely sparse-view (<10 views) CT reconstruction. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex>-LRM consists of two key components: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex>-former and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex> triplane. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\quad X$</tex>-former can handle an arbitrary number of input views using an MLP-based image tokenizer and a Transformer-based encoder. The output tokens are then upsampled into our X-triplane representation, which models the 3D radiodensity as an implicit neural field. To support the training of X-LRM, we introduce Torso-16K, a largescale dataset comprising over 16 K volume-projection pairs of various torso organs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that X-LRM outperforms the state-of-the-art method by 1.5 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d B$</tex> and achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$27 \times$</tex> faster speed with better flexibility. Furthermore, the evaluation of lung segmentation tasks also suggests the practical value of our approach. Our code and dataset will be released at https://github.com/Richard-Guofeng-Zhang/X-LRM.

title: Zero-Shot ECG Arrhythmia Classification from Waveform Images: An Experimental Study with Vision-Language Models
authors: Joseph Azar, Raphaël Couturier, Ke Du, Jiaxing Yan
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Deploying deep learning for ECG classification requires large labeled datasets and specialized expertise-resources unavailable in most clinical settings. We investigate whether general-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can classify ECG windows as normal or abnormal in a fully zero-shot setting, using only plotted waveform images and natural language prompts. We evaluate two VLMs-GPT-4.1 (proprietary) and Qwen3-VL-8B (open-source)-on 355 windows from the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database across six configurations, comparing direct scoring, Visual Question Answering (VQA), and hybrid approaches augmented with physiological features. Through iterative prompt engineering, we identify a key strategy: separating visual perception from clinical reasoning by having the VLM answer factual morphological questions while a lightweight language model performs classification. This VQA approach achieves 74.08 % accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1=0.755$</tex>), with strong ventricular beat detection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(89 \%)$</tex> but poor supraventricular detection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(62.5 \%)$</tex>. Rather than claiming state-of-the-art results, this paper documents the experimental journey-strategies that failed, prompt engineering pitfalls, and practical lessons for applying VLMs to medical time-series classification. We find that prompt design choices matter more than model selection or feature engineering, and that any prompt language providing “reasons to classify as normal” systematically degrades abnormal detection.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of a Quantum-Illumination LiDAR System
authors: Hawraa Slaiman, Sara Abou Chakra, Hassanein Rabah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Modern optical sensing systems, particularly LiDAR, play a central role in remote sensing and precision measurement. However, they face fundamental limits when operating in noisy and highly lossy environments. Quantum illumination has emerged as a promising approach to overcome these challenges by exploiting quantum correlations. This work presents the system design and performance analysis of a quantum illumination based LiDAR architecture operating at optical frequencies. The proposed system exploits entangled photon pairs generated through spontaneous parametric down conversion to enhance detection sensitivity and robustness under weak return conditions. A complete hardware oriented framework is developed, including the entanglement source, nonlinear crystal configuration, pump laser requirements, optical propagation path, quantum memory implementation, and a detection architecture based on quantum erasure Hong-Ou-Mandel interference. Practical realization using superconducting nanowire single photon detectors is discussed to enable high efficiency and low noise photon measurement. In addition, a system level link budget and noise analysis evaluates the impact of optical losses, atmospheric attenuation, turbulence, and detector imperfections on overall performance. The study highlights how quantum correlations can improve signal to noise performance compared with classical illumination strategies, particularly in low reflectivity and high background scenarios. The presented architecture provides a realistic framework for implementing quantum LiDAR systems and supports the development of next generation long range sensing technologies.

title: Unlocking Student Autonomy: The Indirect Positive Effect of Generative AI Use on Academic Performance via Self-Regulated Learning
authors: Suryateja Jonathan Gandha, Jeklin Harefa
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The present study tries to determine the effect of GenAI projects in higher education as a facilitator for students' learning autonomy and even as a contributor to their better academic results. The interest of the study is on planning and monitoring that represent the core processes of self-regulated learning (SRL) to understand if GenAI use interferes positively or negatively with the development of these metacognitive strategies and, then, with learning results. The sample comprised 158 undergraduate students enrolled in different universities across Indonesia who independently filled out an online questionnaire. The regression and PROCESS macro analyses showed that using GenAI has a positive and significant effect on planning and monitoring, respectively, refuting Hypothesis H1 on its negative relation with these factors. In addition, planning and monitoring were observed as strong predictors of academic performance (GPA), whereas GenAI showed no statistically significant direct effect on GPA. It is an indirect effect that is mediated by SRL strategies, the more powerful of these being self-regulation. The findings suggest that GenAI tools, if they are successfully integrated, can actually elevate the level of metacognitive skills in students; hence, educational policies and guidelines concerning responsible AI usage should be put in place to promote critical learning and academic independence. Beyond this empirical contribution, the scope of this research is further extended by a component of a prototype: the development of a supportive application architecture that implements the empirical findings to reinforce students' metacognitive skills.

title: Generating Synthetic Tabular Data Using Large and Small Language Models
authors: Uendi Muça, Hadi Koubeissy, Abdallah Makhoul
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Synthetic data generation has become a key solution for enabling data sharing, accounting for privacy constraints, and addressing the limited availability of real-world datasets, particularly in tabular format. Conventional statistical models and generative adversarial networks are widely used, but they require dataset-specific preprocessing and sometimes struggle to capture complex semantic relationships among features accurately. Recent advances in large and small language models(LLMs and SLMs) have improved the development of synthetic tabular data by introducing transformer-based architectures and textual representations of tables. This paper reviews and analyzes in detail language model-based methods for generating synthetic tabular data, focusing on state-of-the-art approaches such as GReaT, TabuLa, and other small language models, and highlighting their training strategies and conditioning mechanisms. The performance of each model across multiple datasets is evaluated based on 3 main criteria: downstream machine learning utility, data representativeness, and privacy preservation. Furthermore, we examine the potential of smaller language models to achieve competitive performance with significantly reduced computational cost. Experimental results show necessary trade-offs among model size, data quality, and efficiency, underscoring that larger models are not necessarily optimal.

title: IoT-Enabled Deep Learning System for Strawberry Cultivation Viability and Disease Detection in Senegal: Ka-Nguessa
authors: Fatoumata BALDE, Mamadou Korka DIALLO, Alla LO
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Agriculture is an important part of Senegal's economy, but it continues to face challenges related to climate variability and traditional practices that limit productivity. According to the ANSD report in July 2024 (https://www.ansd.sn), agriculture accounts for 15% of GDP and employs nearly 70 % of the working population. Although the distribution of agricultural households by crop type shows that 67.4 % practice rain-fed farming, strawberry cultivation allows producers to diversify their income, reducing dependence on traditional rain-fed crops (millet, corn, peanuts). Strawberry cultivation has strong economic potential, but it suffers from poor early disease detection, limited access to modern technologies, and a lack of knowledge about optimal growing conditions. This work proposes Ka-Nguessa, an intelligent agricultural system combining the Internet of Things (IoT) and deep learning to improve the sustainability and health management of strawberry cultivation in Senegal. The system integrates three complementary modules: early disease detection through image analysis, with 98.3% accuracy; automatic provision of agronomic recommendations; assessment of the viability of soil and climate conditions with an accuracy of 99.2 % for all crops and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 1. 5 \%}$</tex> for strawberries, and an ROC-AUC of 99.8% for all crops and 89.2 % for strawberries; and real-time environmental monitoring using 7-in-1 NPK sensors and an ESP32 microcontroller. Accessible on mid-range smartphones and operating partially offline, this solution is a comprehensive decision-making tool for small-scale producers, combining disease diagnosis, feasibility assessment, and agronomic monitoring.

title: Advancing Brain Tumor MRI Classification with Interpretable Attention-Enhanced Deep Learning
authors: Md. Shakhauat Hossan Sumon, Sabbir Sarder Munna, Umme Habiba, Md Kamrul Siam, Ahoteshanul Haque Shah, Md. Ehsanul Haque, Amit Kirti Mondal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Neuro-oncology relies on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain tumor classification since automated methods can greatly improve diagnosis. Despite advances in deep learning, long-range spatial interactions, model interpretability, and computational efficiency for realtime deployment remain obstacles. This paper introduces an attentionenhanced DenseNet201 network with Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) to solve these constraints. DenseNet201 emphasizes dense feature reuse, and MHSA focuses on concentrated areas surrounding the image's spatial frame and improves discrimination. MRI images were prepared by rescaling, intensity normalization, and contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. Data augmentation via rotations, translations, horizontal and vertical flips, and zoom operations strengthened model generalization. Gradient-Weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), an explainable artificial intelligence method, produced class-specific saliency maps that visualized tumoral locations and gave clinical interpretability. Test accuracy of 0.9966, macro-average F1-score of 0.9966, Cohen <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\kappa$</tex> value of 1.0000, and consistent five-fold cross-validation accuracy of 0.9734 demonstrate superior performance. Individual MRI scan inference takes less than 24 milliseconds per image, making it suitable for real-time use. Comparative assessments of contemporary convolutional neural networks and transformer-based architectures indicate that the DenseNet201 + MHSA framework effectively classifies brain tumors with commendable accuracy and interpretability, establishing it as a viable therapeutic tool.

title: Secure Connected $k$-Coverage in Spatial Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Dakshanya Maddala, Habib M. Ammari
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Coverage in spatial wireless sensor networks (WSNs) is an open problem. In particular, the problem of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage in spatial (or three-dimensional, 3D for short) WSNs, where every point in a spatial field of interest (FoI) is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-covered (i.e., covered by at least <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> sensors simultaneously), is more challenging. In this paper, we investigate the problem of connected <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> coverage in spatial WSNs, where a FoI is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex>-covered while all participating sensors are connected to each other. This study leverages convex polyhedral space-fillers, which helps produce an irregular hexagonal prism-based tessellation, named Irregular Honeycomb Network. The latter serves as a medium to achieve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage in spatial WSNs. First, we propose a sensor placement strategy to ensure full <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-coverage of a spatial FoI. Second, we suggest a secure communication protocol using RSA public key cryptography to achieve secure spatial connected <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> coverage. We corroborate our analysis with various simulation results.

title: LeafGuard: An Improved CNN-Based Model for Tea Leaf Disease Early Detection
authors: Jannatul Fardus Armin, Md. Abu Saleh, Aunik Hasan Mridul, Jannat Rosul Nisha, G.M. Shakil, Erani Effat Sarna
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Tea leaf diseases drastically lower crop output and quality, despite the fact that tea farming is essential to world agriculture. For efficient administration and the avoidance of financial losses, early diagnosis and identification of tea leaf illnesses are essential. This paper offers a deep learning-based method for accurately identifying and categorizing tea leaf illnesses using convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The suggested technique automatically detects sickness from photos of tea leaves by utilizing CNNs' sophisticated image processing and feature extraction capabilities. A large dataset of both healthy and sick leaf samples was used to train and validate the system, yielding encouraging accuracy, precision, and recall results. For agronomists and tea growers, this method offers a scalable and affordable option that facilitates prompt intervention and sustainable crop management techniques. In this study, we use deep learning models, such as the InceptionV3 Model, VGG19, VGG16, and EfficientNetB4, to automatically detect tea leaf illness. The datasets that are based on characteristics of tea leaf disease add to the thorough analysis. By automating the detection of possible symptoms of tea leaf illness, the study seeks to expedite the diagnostic process and enable early intervention. The models' accuracies differ when compared, however our suggested model performs exceptionally well in identifying tea leaf disease, with an accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 8 8 \%}$</tex>. These findings highlight how deep learning methods may improve the accuracy and effectiveness of diagnosing tea leaf disease. The incorporation of cutting-edge technology to enhance current diagnostic techniques presents a viable path toward the early diagnosis of tea leaf disease.

title: Efficiency Enhancement of Crystalline Silicon Solar Cells via Parameter Optimization Using PC1D Simulation
authors: Tariqul Islam, Tama Fouzder, Sudipto Pramanik, Himangshu Ranjan Ghosh, Muhammad Moshiur Rahman Tarafder
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study investigates the optimization of key structural and doping parameters to enhance the performance of crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar cells. Using the PC1D simulation platform, we systematically analyzed the impact of varying the emitter thickness, base layer thickness, back surface field (BSF) layer thickness, and corresponding doping concentrations on critical output metrics such as open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current (Isc), fill factor (FF), and overall cell efficiency. The simulation results demonstrate that optimal values for emitter thickness (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.1 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>), absorber thickness (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$176 \mu \mathbf{m}$</tex>), and BSF thickness (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$33 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>), combined with doping concentrations of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 10^{20} \text{~ c m}^{-3}$</tex> (emitter), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times 10^{16} \text{~ c m}^{-3}$</tex> (base), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times 10^{17} \text{~ c m}^{-3}$</tex> (BSF), significantly improve carrier collection and reduce recombination losses. The corresponding efficiency was increased from an initial 18.5 % to 21 %, marking a substantial enhancement for conventional c-Si architectures. These findings were further supported by the analysis of diffusion length, minority carrier lifetime, and conductivity trends, all of which contributed to the identification of performance-limiting mechanisms. This work reinforces the value of simulation-driven design in photovoltaic engineering and offers practical guidelines for improving silicon-based solar cell efficiency using accessible fabrication strategies.

title: Towards Label-Efficient Breast Cancer Detection in Histology with Self and Semi-Supervised Learning
authors: Ishra Naznin, Tanjim Mahmud, Abubokor Hanip, Mohammad Shahadat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate detection of breast cancer using histopathological images is critical for early diagnosis and therapy. However, the scarcity of labeled medical data is a substantial hindrance to supervised deep learning systems. In this paper, we offer a label-efficient approach that uses both self-supervised and semi-supervised learning to enhance breast cancer classification using the BreakHis 400X histology dataset. We improve the SimCLR contrastive learning system by incorporating a multi-pair contrastive loss that uses multiple augmented views per image, resulting in richer and more robust feature representations from unlabeled data. The pretrained encoder is then fine-tuned with FixMatch, a semisupervised learning technique that combines pseudo-labeling with consistency regularization to make the most of limited labeled data and copious unlabeled samples. To highlight the benefits of our combined method, we experiment with linear evaluations on the pretrained representations and compare performance to FixMatch-only baselines. Experimental results show that our multi-pair SimCLR pretraining followed by FixMatch fine-tuning increases classification accuracy while greatly lowering the requirement for extensive annotations. This study demonstrates the feasibility of merging advanced self- and semi-supervised learning approaches for scalable and reliable breast cancer diagnosis in histopathology, paving the way for practical clinical implementation with limited expert labeling.

title: A Deep Learning Approach for Spot and Rot Disease Detection in Betel Leaves
authors: Sharmin Sultana Nimu, Suchona Akter, Mithila Akter Mim, Umme Salma, Asib Hasan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In Southeast Asia, betel leaf (Piper betel L.) is a significant plant for both cultural and medical uses. However, despite being important, the crop is extremely subject to diseases like spot leaf and rot leaf, which can drastically lower productivity and result in big losses for farmers. Moreover, manual inspection is slow and unreliable, so automated detection is required. To address this issue, we generated a dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0, 1 7 0}$</tex> photos using pre-processing approaches like scaling, normalization, and augmentation from both selfcollected samples and public sources. Then in order to identify whether leaves were healthy, rotted, or spotted, six deep learning models, such as a custom Light CNN, ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet-B0, VGG16, and MobileNetV3Large, were tested. Pretrained networks significantly beat the baseline, with EfficientNet-B0 achieving the highest accuracy of 99.48 %. Furthermore, we applied Grad-CAM on the EfficientNet-B0 model to visually interpret the learned features, and we expanded the explanation to improve clarity and interpretability. These results indicate that current pretrained CNNs are capable of accurately detecting betel leaf diseases, improving early identification, cutting crop losses and helping farmers in improving crop management.

title: Ontology-Based Knowledge Graphs for Detecting Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) With Neurosymbolic AI
authors: Md. Awinul Hoque Utsha, Farshid Rafiq, Soheli Tangila Richi, Md. Mahin Khan, Yeasir Ramim, Fabiha Nawal Aurna
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: APTs are usually well-crafted multi-staged attacks that are low-signal and context dependant thereforethey can succeed in avoiding traditional intrusion detection system. In this paper, we propose a novel neurosymbolic AI framework that is composed of ontology-based KG and GNN toenhance the detection of APTs in enterprise networks. We create a cyber securityontology that represents APT tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs) using notions from MITRE ATTCK and the Cyber Kill Chain, and we include in this representation explicit causal and temporal relationships between entities such as malicious infrastructure, hosts, processes, attack phases. Variety: Heterogeneous sources of data such as network logs and open-source threat intelligence (e.g., STIX/TAXII) are fed into a dynamic KG which facilitates context-awarereasoning. A heterogeneous GNN is further used to propagate node and edge level latent representations within this KG,but the symbolic rules learned from the ontology are leveraged to better refine and re-weight neural anomaly scores across plausible attack evolutions (e.g., “reconnaissance → C2 communication'). This neurosymbolic fusion aims to solve the challenges over APT detection,i.e., scarcity of labeled data for zero-day attackers and no interpretability in black-box models. Our method obtains in both DARPA TRACE and MITRE ATTCK Evaluations the higher precision, recall, F1-score comparedto baselines (more than XGBoost and LSTM-based IDSs) generating interpretable alerts thanks to cause-and-effects chains. The framework is capable of near real-time operation and can be operationalised in security operations centres using widely-used large-scale graphanalytics libraries such as Neo4j and PyTorch Geometric, allowing users to scale out explainable APT detection as well as proactive threat hunting.

title: Design of a Dual-Band Heptagonal-Slotted Microstrip Patch Array Antenna for Ku-/Ka-Band 5G and Satellite Applications
authors: Md. Sakib-Ul-Hasan, Anik Paul, Farhin Sultana, Md. Azad Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: A dual-band circular microstrip patch array antenna with a central heptagonal slot is proposed for Ku-band and Ka-band applications. Designed on Rogers RT/Duroid 5880 substrate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\epsilon_{R}\right)=2.2$</tex>, thickness 0.8 mm), the antenna targets 5G millimeter-wave and satellite communication systems. The initial single-patch design offered compactness but limited gain and directivity; these limitations were overcome by developing a two-element corporate-fed array. The heptagonal slot facilitates dual-band resonance, while the optimized feed network ensures equal power division and phase matching between elements. Simulated results show operation at 17 GHz and 28.5 GHz with return loss better than 10 dB, VSWR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$< 2$</tex>, and high radiation efficiency. The array configuration significantly enhances gain and bandwidth compared to a single element, maintaining stable radiation patterns across both bands. Owing to its compact size, high performance, and dual-band capability, the proposed antenna is well suited for integration into modern high-frequency wireless and satellite communication systems.

title: LightSim: A Flexible, Fast Modeling Framework for Holistic Photonic Computing Systems
authors: Yuxuan Lin, Guohao Dai, Shanshi Huang, Yuzhe Ma
publish: ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems
abstract: None

title: Minimum Description Length-Driven Fragment Mining for Pretraining Molecule Property Prediction Model_supp1-3697250.pdf
authors: Zhen Cui
abstract: Molecular fragments play a crucial role in molecular property prediction. However, most existing deep learning approaches rely heavily on expert-defined substructural patterns, limiting their ability to identify novel or latent fragments. This constraint reduces the generalizability and applicability of molecular fragments in molecular representation learning. In this study, we propose the Molecular Multi-view Pre-training model with Adaptive Fragment Mining (MMP-AFM), a unified framework that facilitates the seamless integration of molecular structural information. MMP-AFM formulates fragment discovery as a combinatorial optimization problem, using description length as the objective to enable adaptive extraction of molecular fragments and dynamic construction of a fragment library. Additionally, we design a molecular multi-view self-supervised pretraining framework that aligns features from the fragment, global, and data augmentation views, ensuring a comprehensive integration of molecular substructural information. Finally, the MMP-AFM is applied to both molecular classification and regression tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that MMP-AFM consistently outperforms existing methods across multiple tasks, highlighting its broad applicability and efficiency.

title: Tactile-Sensor-Embedded Treadmill for Deep Learning-Based Step-Length Measurement_supp1-3696640.docx
authors: HOSU LEE

title: Agentic Continual Adaptation: Enabling Lifelong Learning in Wireless Channel Estimation
authors: Ahsan Bilal, Muhammad Ahmed Mohsin, Muhammad Umer, Muhammad Ali Jamshed, Ayesha Mohsin, John M. Cioffi, Dean F. Hougen
abstract: As wireless networks evolve toward 6G and beyond, deep learning-based channel estimators face a fundamental challenge: their performance degrades when operating conditions deviate from training distributions. This phenomenon, known as distribution shift, manifests through temporal non-stationarity due to user mobility and operational regime changes across varying signal-to-noise ratios (SNR). Traditional sequential training approaches suffer from catastrophic forgetting, where adaptation to new conditions erases previously learned knowledge. This article introduces Agentic Continual Adaptation (ACA), a framework that formulates continual learning as reinforcement-based sequential decision-making to enable continuous adaptation without forgetting. ACA integrates three synergistic mechanisms: an anchor memory that detects forgetting in real-time through continuous performance monitoring on representative samples, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent that dynamically selects from seven adaptation strategies balancing plasticity and stability, and a forgetting-aware rollback mechanism that rejects parameter updates exceeding adaptive degradation thresholds. Experimental evaluation across ten SNR regimes demonstrates 4.3% average improvement in channel estimation accuracy with peak gains of 13.6% at challenging low-SNR conditions, while triggering rollback on only 7.5% of parameter updates. This work provides a practical framework for continual adaptation in next-generation wireless networks, addressing an important part of the gap between laboratory performance and real-world deployment.

title: Evaluating and Mitigating LLM-as-a-Judge Bias in Communication Systems
authors: Jiaxin Gao, Chen Chen, Yanwen Jia, Xueluan Gong, Kwok-Yan Lam, Qian Wang
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to autonomously evaluate the quality of content in communication systems, e.g., to assess responses in telecom customer support chatbots. However, the impartiality of these AI “judges” is not guaranteed, and any biases in their evaluation criteria could skew outcomes and undermine user trust. In this paper, we systematically investigate judgment biases across 6 LLM-as-a-judge models spanning both prompt-based and fine-tuned judges under the pointwise scoring setting, encompassing 11 types of biases that cover both implicit and explicit forms. We observed that state-of-the-art LLM judges demonstrate robustness to biased inputs, generally assigning them lower scores than the corresponding clean samples. We further found that fine-tuning an LLM on high-scoring yet biased responses can significantly degrade its performance, highlighting the risk of training on biased data. We also discovered that the judged scores correlate with task difficulty: a challenging dataset like GPQA yields lower average scores, whereas an open-ended reasoning dataset (e.g., JudgeLM-val) sees higher average scores. Finally, we proposed four potential mitigation strategies to ensure fair and reliable AI judging in practical communication scenarios.

title: A 40k fps 99.5 dB Wide Dynamic Range PWM Image Sensor Featuring a Low-Power Pixel Array
authors: Xiaopei Shi, Ruoyu Ji, Kaiming Nie, Zhiyuan Gao, Jing Gao, Yu Xin, Jiangtao Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$500\times 1000$</tex-math> </inline-formula> array pulse-width modulation (PWM) sensor with wide dynamic range, low power consumption fabricated in 110-nm CIS FSI process. The prototype demonstrates key performance metrics with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$17\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m pixel pitch, achieving 40k fps full-resolution imaging while maintaining 21.5% fill factor and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20.40\times 11.08$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> die size. The chip achieves 99.5 dB dynamic range while maintaining 37.5 pJ/pixel<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>frame energy efficiency under dark conditions. Dynamic power management scheme implements adaptive row activation achieving 0.75W dark-current power consumption. By encoding scene information in compressed bit-stream format, the sensor provides high spatiotemporal resolution while eliminating redundancy in raw data transmission. Quantizing light intensity directly in the temporal domain, instead of the voltage domain, enables a wide dynamic range to be achieved at a substantially lower power cost. This parallelized architecture shows strong potential for intelligent vision systems requiring real-time high-speed processing capabilities.

title: BSFL: Secure and Efficient Blockchain-Based Split Federated Learning for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Zixu Jiang, Cheng Chi, Yizhong Liu, Haohua Du, Zixiao Jia, Tairan Ding, Qianhong Wu, Zhenyu Guan, Dawei Li, Willy Susilo
abstract: The rapid development of the automotive industry and the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) has led to an exponential growth of distributed vehicular data, driving the need for secure and efficient collaborative machine learning solutions. However, existing distributed collaborative machine learning (DCML) approaches, such as federated learning and split learning, face significant challenges in IoV scenarios, including limited training efficiency, centralized aggregation vulnerabilities, and constrained privacy and model protection. To address these issues, we propose a blockchain-based split federated learning (BSFL) scheme for IoV applications. BSFL non-trivially combines federated learning and split learning to enable vehicles with low computational power to participate in parallel training, improving both model accuracy and training efficiency. By utilizing blockchain as a decentralized infrastructure, BSFL eliminates the risks of single points of failure and ensures model consistency through Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus. Furthermore, we design a noise addition mechanism based on differential privacy to safeguard client data privacy and model security. Formal security analysis and extensive experiments demonstrate that BSFL achieves enhanced privacy, security, and training performance. Comparing to related DCML schemes, BSFL reduces computational overhead by up to 88.84% and client training time by up to 29.49% while maintaining comparable accuracy. When training on ResNet-50 based on CIFAR10, BSFL achieved an accuracy of 93.15%. And the verification process for each model’s training results on the blockchain requires 1.49 ms.

title: Secure RIS-Aided Multicasting: Globally Optimal Beam Management and Discrete-Phase RIS Configuration
authors: Luis F. Abanto-Leon, Setareh Maghsudi
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) are poised to revolutionize wireless multicasting by enabling extended coverage and reliable operation in obstructed environments. These benefits, however, can be undermined by security vulnerabilities arising from practical deployment factors. This work addresses three such critical factors, (i) the discrete nature of RIS phase shifts, (ii) the presence of colluding eavesdroppers, and (iii) the inefficiency of static illumination beams, each threatening security if not properly accounted for in system design. To mitigate these issues, we formulate a joint resource allocation problem that minimizes the wiretap signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) across all eavesdroppers by co-optimizing the RIS configuration and the base station (BS) beam management. This yields a complex, nonconvex mixed-integer nonlinear program (MINLP), which we equivalently reformulate into a tractable mixed-integer quadratically constrained program (MIQCP) solvable to global optimality. Numerical results confirm that the proposed scheme significantly bolsters security, suppressing the wiretap SNR by up to 58% compared to existing baselines.

title: Force Control of an Underactuated Tendon-Driven Manipulator_supp2-3688030.mp4
authors: Nicolas Jacques Serge Testard
abstract: This article investigates the application of force controls on a tendon-driven underactuated robotic arm. A direct use of classical hybrid force/motion control based on computed torque control can lead to instability for this type of system. Reformulating the hybrid force/motion control using the pseudo-computed torque control (PCTC) framework leads to a stable control strategy. Finally, it is shown that applying admittance control in combination with PCTC also results in stable behavior when exerting a force against a wall or in a cobotics context. These results are then validated through experimentation.

title: Deconvolution method using B-splines
authors: Zdeněk Kopecký, Věra Mazánková, David Trunec
abstract: In this paper, we present a new algorithm to solve a deconvolution problem, which is mathematically described by the Fredholm integral equation of the first kind. This problem has to be solved in many measurements. The solution of this problem with experimental data is ill-conditioned and therefore difficult to solve numerically. It was assumed that the measured data have a Poisson distribution. The maximum likelihood method was used for the solution and the function sought was assumed to be a spline represented in a B-spline basis. The algorithm was tested using known data and it was found that the errors are smaller than those of the usually used methods. The results of the developed algorithm for the deconvolution of the neutron spectroscopy measurement with organic scintillators are presented.

title: Transformer Based GAN Model for Speech Emotion Conversion Using Melspectrograms
authors: Humaira Fairooz Moohy, Arif Istiaque, Md. Azad Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Speech emotion conversion enhances human computer interaction by enabling machines to modify the emotional tone of speech while preserving speaker identity and linguistic content. This work proposes a justified transformerbased generative adversarial network, Speech Emotion Transformer GAN (SE-TransformerGAN), for effective emotion conversion using Mel-spectrogram features. Leveraging the parallel structure of the RAVDESS dataset, Melspectrograms are paired by speaker, sentence, and repetition to form aligned training samples. The generator employs transformer encoder-decoder layers conditioned on target emotion embeddings, while the discriminator evaluates both authenticity and emotion class. The model is trained using a combination of adversarial, classification, and reconstruction losses to ensure emotionally accurate and natural conversions. Experimental results demonstrate that SE-TransformerGAN successfully transforms speech emotions while retaining speaker and linguistic consistency. Both quantitative metrics and qualitative analyses (e.g., spectrogram visualization and listening tests) validate the model's effectiveness. This work contributes toward emotionally intelligent speech systems for more natural and expressive human-computer interactions.

title: RAPID: Robotic Arm Platform for IoT-Driven Disabilities Support
authors: Ashiqur Rahman Saron, Foysal Ahmed Neloy, Soumodip Madhu, Tasmeem Jannat Prome, Tanjina Akter Rima
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Persons with Disabilities (PWDs) commonly face significant challenges in finding professional employment due to low educational attainment, lack of training, and lack of access to assistive technology. These issues are often linked with poverty and exclusion. State-of-the-art technology solutions are mostly focused on gesture identification systems for controlling vehicles, whereas combined gesture recognition systems for mobility as well as object manipulation are difficult to implement using current approaches. This creates a hindrance for Persons with Disabilities (PWDs) to effectively participate in industrial as well as other service-related tasks that require manual dexterity and coordination. In this paper, we introduce an innovative gesture recognition solution using sign languages for remote vehicle as well as robotic arm manipulation for tasks like object retrieval and manipulation. Our design takes advantage of cutting-edge models for real-time detection as well as features of the Internet of Things (IoT) technology. This invention has immense potential for enabling Persons with Disabilities to take part in manufacturing, logistics, and supply chain activities, thereby providing a safer and more enabling way of getting employed. Experimental results confirm that our approach provides enhanced precision and realtime performance, which promotes a significant improvement over other gesture-control solutions.

title: Bangla Sign Language Recognition Using Deformable Convolution Neural Network
authors: Atik Al Wadud, Md. Ferdous Wahid, Md. Rifat Hoshen, Tuhin Shuvra Mondal, Md. Mahruf Alam, Tamanna Mustarin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bangla Sign Language (BdSL) is the primary means of communication for individuals with hearing and speech impairments in Bangladesh. BdSL recognition is therefore essential to improve communication accessibility, foster social inclusion, and enable active participation of this community. However, technological advancements in BdSL recognition remain limited. This research addresses this gap by designing and evaluating two convolutional neural network architectures: a baseline Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Deformable CNN using a prepared balanced dataset comprising 36 BdSL signs. Both CNN architectures consist of five layers, each layer comprising a convolutional block, batch normalization, max pooling, and dropout to enhance feature extraction and generalization. The baseline model employs standard convolutional layers throughout, whereas the Deformable CNN model replaces the first convolutional block with a deformable convolutional block, leaving the remaining layers unchanged. Training was conducted under identical conditions for each model, yet the Deformable CNN consistently achieved superior results, with a blind test accuracy of 97.03 % compared to 87.31 % for the baseline CNN. Furthermore, to interpret the decision making process of the Deformable CNN, Grad-CAM was applied, revealing that the Deformable CNN effectively focuses on key regions such as fingertips and palm contours, improving BdSL recognition by adaptively modeling spatial variations and complex hand gesture patterns. These findings underscore the effectiveness of deformable convolutions in improving recognition performance and suggest their potential applicability to other computer vision tasks with high spatial variability.

title: Design, Modeling and Fabrication of a 400 Hz Multi Level Inverter with Optimized Filter for Aircraft Applications
authors: Bayazid Khan, Wahid Tausif Arzu, Taj Mihir Islam Teethi, Afzal Hossain, Faruk Hossain, Mohammad Mostafizur Rahman Biswas
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In this paper, a single-phase, 5-level multilevel inverter (MLI) is designed for aircraft electrical systems operating at 400 Hz. The proposed inverter topology is developed with only six IGBT switches and two diodes, which is a comparatively lower switch count than other inverters of the same level, making it compact and lightweight for aviation applications. The study employs a carrier-based modulation strategy to ensure precise device switching and stable operation at high frequency. The system at first simulated in MATLAB/Simulink and then further verified through a hardware prototype implementation. To improve power quality, LC, LCL, and CLC passive filters are designed and analyzed, with stability confirmed through transfer function modeling and Bode plot analysis. Experimental Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) results confirm that the inverter achieves efficiency above 95% and a THD level below 1%, demonstrating high stability and reliability. Among the tested filters, the CLC filter provided the smoothest output waveform with the lowest harmonic distortion. The results validate that the proposed inverter system ensures compactness, high power quality, and reduced losses, thereby fulfilling the stringent requirements of aircraft power systems

title: Cross-Layer AI Optimization of Signaling and Media Planes in Cloud-Based Contact Center Architectures
authors: Balakrishnan Devaraj, Gokul Pandy, Naveen Komulwad, Rajesh Kumar Reddy Kallapari, Manikandan Manickavasagam, Karthik Raja Allimuthu
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The inefficiencies of cloud-based contact centers are caused by the lack of signaling and media plane alignment that causes high latency and inadequate quality of service during peak loads. The framework advanced in this paper suggests a crosslayer AI optimization framework based on multi-agent reinforcement learning to coordinate routing in the signaling plane and stream adaptation in the media plane. The method results in quantitative performance enhancement: the decrease of the average handling time by 30 percent, 25 percent rise in the first-contact resolution rates, 25 percent growth in the Mean Opinion Score, and a shift in the Mean Opinion Score to 3.7 with 1500 calls/hour. It is used on AWS Kubernetes with federated learning to ensure privacy to fill in the research gaps in unified plane optimization. NS-3 and JMeter simulations ensure scalability in omnichannel operation, which is part of sustainable AI-based contact centers.

title: Transformer-Based Approach for Accurately Identifying Saint and Common Forms of Bangla Language
authors: Samiul Karim Mazumder, Sabiha Jannat, Snaholata Mondal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bangla is known as one of the most widely spoken languages globally. This language exhibits significant stylistic diversity through its two main forms: Saint (Sadhu), a formal and classical style, and Common (Cholito), a modern and colloquial form. This study introduces a benchmarking analysis on the automatic classification of these two forms, applying the newly introduced BanglaBlend dataset, comprising 7,350 annotated sentences equally divided between Saint and Common forms. We evaluate five transformer-based language models, including Banglabert, Xlm-roberta, IndicBert v2, mbert, and DistilBER. All of these models are trained under the same experimental conditions to ensure a fair and consistent comparison. According to our results, transformer models significantly outperform the dataset's baseline results, with IndicBERTv2 achieving the highest performance with 95.44% accuracy, followed by XLM-RoBERTa and BanglaBERT with 94.63 % and 93.61 % accuracy, respectively. DistilBERT offers a balance of efficiency and accuracy, running faster and using less memory while maintaining nearly 91.9 % accuracy. The study's outcomes make it clear that the importance of language-specific pre-training and multilingual capabilities for effectively distinguishing between stylistically distinct Bangla forms, while creating opportunities for more advanced, linguistically sensitive NLP applications for classification of Bangla language forms by establishing new benchmarks.

title: PSO-Driven Actionable Counterfactual Explanations for Smart Building Energy Management
authors: Ögecan Muyanlı Kopan, İbrahim Kök, Feyza Yıldırım Okay, Suat Ödemir
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Counterfactual explanations have emerged as a practical concept for translating machine learning predictions into actionable recommendations, particularly in smart city applications. In this study, the primary focus is on generating highquality counterfactual explanations using single-objective and multi-objective Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) methods in order to provide actionable recommendations for reducing nexthour electricity consumption. The proposed approach formulates counterfactual generation as an optimization problem, where proximity, sparsity, and validity are explicitly considered. A Random Forest regression model is employed as the underlying forecasting model for next-hour electricity consumption. The generated counterfactual explanations are evaluated using standard counterfactual quality metrics, including validity, proximity, sparsity, diversity, and plausibility, and are compared against commonly used baseline methods. Experimental results demonstrate that PSO-based approaches consistently yield more realistic and actionable counterfactuals among competing methods. This study highlights the suitability of PSO-driven counterfactual generation as a robust recommendation mechanism for shortterm electricity consumption reduction in smart building environments.

title: Impact of IoT-Based Data Analytics on Decision-Making Performance of Bangladeshi SMEs
authors: Kh. Mustafizur Rahman, Mehedi Hasan Emon, Mowdud Ahmed, Avishek Nath, Meherun Nisa Nipa, Afsana Ferdous Emee
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The research examines how IoT-based data analytics is relevant to the decision-making process of Bangladesh-based SMEs, in terms of IDAC, DQ, KSC, ITR, and PU. The primary objective was to investigate how the combination of these organizational and technological factors has an effect on the speed, accuracy, quality and timeliness of the decision making in a highly dynamic business environment. The quantitative research design has been considered, and the structured questionnaires have been used to gather data about 152 managers and employees of the SMEs who are involved in the decision-making processes. SmartPLS 4 was used to perform the analysis of the relationship between the constructs using PLS-SEM. The results suggest that DAC, KSC, ITR, and PU have a significant positive impact on decision-making, whereas only DQ did not have a significant effect. This is where the application of the technological resources in combination with the organizational routine and human factors is crucial to attain any significant changes in the results of decision-making.
