In real-life, SMEs should improve IoT framework, inculcate a culture of knowledge exchange, and enhance the perception of the usefulness of analytics by employees to maximize the use of the IoT data. In the social context, the study offers information on how the emerging-market SMEs can be more responsive to competitive forces and become economic drivers. The study is unique because it integrates the KBV with TAM to determine organizational capabilities as well as individual perceptions together. The design is cross-sectional and the sampling is convenience-based, which implies that the limitation in the future research may involve the longitudinal design and larger and more heterogeneous samples.

title: Risk-Aware Static Var Compensator Placement on a Renewable-Dominated IEEE 14-Bus System: CVaR-Based Monte Carlo Analysis
authors: Sayba Kamal Orni, Soumik Saha, Adith Saha Dipro, Nittya Ananda Biswas
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Modern power systems are increasingly exposed to uncertainty due to high penetration of renewable energy, load fluctuations, and the risk of line outages. Static VAR Compensators (SVCs) are commonly used for voltage regulation, but optimally placing them under such uncertainty remains a challenging problem. This work presents a Monte Carlo based framework for evaluating SVC placement across a wide range of stochastic operating conditions and N-1 contingencies. Scenarios are simulated capturing variability in load, solar and wind generation, and single line outages. Each candidate SVC is placed at either a bus or midline location and is evaluated using a unified metric that combines real power loss and voltage deviation, with a tunable weight controlling their trade-off. Risk is measured using Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) to capture performance under worst case conditions. Unlike prior studies that assume conventional generator support, a deliberately stressed variant of the IEEE-14 bus test system is constructed by deactivating most synchronous generators and introducing renewable injections. This renewable-heavy, low-inertia grid increases voltage sensitivity and provides a challenging environment for evaluating optimal SVC placement under uncertainty. Our experiment demonstrates that the proposed CVaR driven approach achieves up to 78.4% reduction in tail risk, 7% reduction in average active power loss, 81% reduction in the mean of the unified score, and a 35.6% decrease in voltage violation probability over the baseline condition. The CVaR optimized placement also outperforms greedy loss minimizing placement in considered reliability and risk metrics.

title: Simulation and Control of a Robotic Arm to Intercept a Ping-Pong Ball Using Deep Reinforcement Learning and Computer Vision
authors: Said R. Hernández, Nain M. Ramos
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This article presents the simulation and control of a five-degree-of-freedom robotic arm designed to intercept a ping pong ball in real time. The system incorporates servo motors and a BLDC motor that provide the required torque and speed to perform dynamic and precise movements. For ball detection and tracking, a computer vision system based on 120 FPS cameras is employed, capable of estimating the ball's trajectory and generating a target interception point for the arm. Initially, a position control system will be implemented to enable the arm to follow predefined trajectories based on the predicted ball path. Subsequently, a deep reinforcement learning model will be integrated and trained in simulation to avoid physical environment errors. The system's performance will be validated by comparing its behavior in simulation and realworld conditions, focusing on responsiveness to fast and nonlinear trajectories.

title: Active Cooling System for Electric Vehicle Battery Using a Water-Glycol Liquid Loop
authors: Abdulaziz Alabbad, Meshari Almutlaq, Abdullah Aldawood, Mohammed Alghamdi, Mohammed Quraish, Hassan Quraish, Essam Jassem, Feroz Shaik
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Efficient thermal management is vital for the safety, performance and lifespan of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. This work presents the design and testing of an active water-glycol liquid-loop cooling system developed for a 6S10P battery module (22.2 V, 22 Ah). The system uses copper micro-coils placed around the cells through which the coolant circulates, maintaining uniform temperature and preventing thermal runaway during fast charging and high-load conditions. Analytical, computational and experimental methods were combined to assess performance. The battery module has generated about 210 W of heat during peak discharge. With coolant flow rate of 2 L min<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and laminar flow conditions (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Re} \approx$</tex> 2100), the system achieved an overall heat-transfer coefficient of nearly 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> Wm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup> K<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>. Pressure-drop analysis (~0.2 bar) confirmed low hydraulic losses that supports efficient operation using the compact DC pump. A laboratory prototype using an FL-800N chiller and a 70: 30 water-glycol mixture demonstrated stable thermal performance. The controller adjusted the pump speed and valve position in real time to keep the pack temperature within safe limits. Experimental results showed that more than 30% reduction in peak temperature compared to passive cooling which confirms effective heat removal with low energy consumption.

title: SwarmGaussian: Distributed 3D Gaussian Splatting for High Fidelity Aerial Scene Reconstruction
authors: Yao Yeboah, Joseph Teye Ignatius Buertey, Kwabena Agyapong-Kodua, Zeyad Farisi
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The ability to reconstruct high-fidelity 3D assets from video has long held promise for applications such as inspection, structural assessment, and surveying. While traditional photogrammetry techniques have demonstrated strong performance in controlled settings, they often lack the robustness required to handle complex scene characteristics, including non-Lambertian surfaces, reflective materials, and non-diffuse appearance. Recent neural scene representations have shown significant promise in addressing these challenges. In particular, 3D Gaussian Splatting achieves an effective balance between reconstruction quality and rendering efficiency. However, the explicit and memory-intensive nature of Gaussian primitives makes existing approaches prohibitive for large-scale reconstruction, especially when data is captured across multiple aerial platforms. In this work, we present SwarmGaussian, a framework for incremental and large-scale aerial scene reconstruction from multi-view video captured by swarms of aerial vehicles. The proposed approach integrates semantic scene clustering, depthguided Gaussian optimisation, and spatial partitioning with adaptive levels of detail to enable efficient distributed optimisation and scalable reconstruction. By decomposing complex scenes into semantically and spatially coherent subregions, SwarmGaussian supports parallel reconstruction across multiple agents while maintaining global consistency and high-fidelity visual quality. Experimental results demonstrate that SwarmGaussian enables efficient distributed 3D Gaussian-based reconstruction across aerial swarms, producing high-quality 3D assets suitable for downstream applications such as structural assessment, inspection, and large-scale surveying.

title: Two-DoF Oscillatory Test Bench for Low-Frequency Excitation Analysis
authors: Angela Vera, Jazmin Quispe, Styven Palomino, Elvis J. Alegria
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Mechanical oscillations affect small-device stability, making test-bench evaluation essential; the challenge is enabling reconfigurable mechanisms with independent amplitude and frequency control. This paper introduces a 2-DoF oscillatory test bench that employs two actuators: a rotational crank with fuzzy-PI velocity control and a translational reconfigurable link with PI position control. The dynamic orientation of the oscillating platform is estimated through inertial sensors combined with Kalman filtering. Furthermore, the mechanism's controllers are evaluated under varying payloads representing the device under test. The results confirm reproducible oscillations, enabling reliable testing of small devices under laboratory conditions.

title: Open Vocabulary Monocular 3D Object Detection
authors: Jin Yao, Hao Gu, Xuweiyi Chen, Jiayun Wang, Zezhou Cheng
abstract: We propose and study open-vocabulary monocular 3D detection, a novel task that aims to detect objects of any categories in metric 3D space from a single RGB image. Existing 3D object detectors either rely on costly sensors such as LiDAR or multi-view setups, or remain confined to closed vocabularies settings with limited categories, restricting their applicability. We identify two key challenges in this new setting. First, the scarcity of 3D bounding box annotations limits the ability to train generalizable models. To reduce dependence on 3D supervision, we propose a framework that effectively integrates pretrained 2D and 3D vision foundation models. Second, missing labels and semantic ambiguities (e.g., table vs. desk) in existing datasets hinder reliable evaluation. To address this, we design a novel metric that captures model performance while mitigating annotation issues. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results in zero-shot 3D detection of novel categories as well as in-domain detection on seen classes. We hope our method provides a strong baseline and our evaluation protocol establishes a reliable benchmark for future research.

title: InteriorAgent: LLM Agent for Interior Design-Aware 3D Layout Generation
authors: Kunal Gupta, Ishit Mehta, Kun Wang, Nicholas Chua, Abhimanyu Krishna, Yan Deng, Ravi Ramamoorthi, Manmohan Chandraker
abstract: Creating interior layout designs has numerous applications, including virtual reality, architectural visualization and real estate planning. Generating realistic and functional indoor scenes requires a nuanced understanding of spatial configurations and human-centered design principles. We propose InteriorAgent, an LLM-agent-driven framework for text-to-3D indoor scene generation that produces scenes with visual quality and functional utility that significantly surpass prior works. We achieve this through several key advantages of InteriorAgent: (1) encoding of interior design principles with a novel scene description language, (2) aesthetics and functionality through synthesis tools that satisfy design principles, (3) realism and prompt adherence with optimization tools that ensure ergonomics and iterative constraint satisfaction, (4) extensibility with a framework that allows incorporating even mature, complex tools like diffusion models, LLMs and 3D generation repositories. We evaluate InteriorAgent through a user study, where participants strongly favor its generated scenes over prior state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, we demonstrate novel applications uniquely enabled by InteriorAgent, including language-based scene editing and seamless tool integration for new tasks. Code and data will be publicly released.

title: Splatography: Sparse Multi-View Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Filmmaking Challenges
authors: Adrian Azzarelli, Nantheera Anantrasirichai, David R Bull
abstract: Deformable Gaussian Splatting (GS) accomplishes photorealistic dynamic 3-D reconstruction from dense multi-view video (MVV) by learning to deform a canonical GS representation. However, in filmmaking, tight budgets can result in sparse camera configurations, which limits state-of-the-art (SotA) methods when capturing complex dynamic features. To address this issue, we introduce an approach that splits the canonical Gaussians and deformation field into foreground and background components using a sparse set of masks for frames at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t=0$</tex>. Each representation is separately trained on different loss functions during canonical pre-training. Then, during dynamic training, different parameters are modeled for each deformation field following common filmmaking practices. The foreground stage contains diverse dynamic features so changes in color, position and rotation are learned. While, the background containing film-crew and equipment, is typically dimmer and less dynamic so only changes in point position are learned. Experiments on 3-D and 2.5-D entertainment datasets show that our method produces SotA qualitative and quantitative results; up to 3 PSNR higher with half the model size on 3-D scenes. Unlike the SotA and without the need for dense mask supervision, our method also produces segmented dynamic reconstructions including transparent and dynamic textures. Code and video comparisons are available online: http://bit.ly/4oqzZrO

title: M2SVid: End-to-End Inpainting and Refinement for Monocular-to-Stereo Video Conversion
authors: Nina Shvetsova, Goutam Bhat, Prune Truong, Hilde Kuehne, Federico Tombari
abstract: We tackle the problem of monocular-to-stereo video conversion and propose a novel architecture for inpainting and refinement of the warped right view obtained by depth-based reprojection of the input left view. We extend the Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) model to utilize the input left video, the warped right video, and the disocclusion masks as conditioning input to generate a high-quality right camera view. In order to effectively exploit information from neighboring frames for inpainting, we modify the attention layers in SVD to compute full attention for discoccluded pixels. Our model is trained to generate the right view video in an end-to-end manner without iterative diffusion steps by minimizing image space losses to ensure high-quality generation. Our approach outperforms previous state-of-theart methods, being ranked best <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.6 \times$</tex> more often than the second-place method in a user study, while being <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \times$</tex> faster.

title: SAT-SKYLINES: 3D Building Generation from Satellite Imagery and Coarse Geometric Priors
authors: Zhangyu Jin, Andrew Feng
abstract: We present SatSkylines, a 3D building generation approach that takes satellite imagery and coarse geometric priors. Without proper geometric guidance, existing imagebased 3D generation methods struggle to recover accurate building structures from the top-down views of satellite images alone. On the other hand, 3D detailization methods tend to rely heavily on highly detailed voxel inputs and fail to produce satisfying results from simple priors such as cuboids. To address these issues, our key idea is to model the transformation from interpolated noisy coarse priors to detailed geometries, enabling flexible geometric control without additional computational cost. We have further developed Skylines-50K, a large-scale dataset of over 50,000 unique and stylized 3D building assets in order to support the generations of detailed building models. Extensive evaluations indicate the effectiveness of our model and strong generalization ability. See the project page for more results: https://jinzhangyu.github.io/projects/SatSkylines/

title: Composite Inertia Weighted APSO for Autonomous Vehicle Path Planning
authors: Muhammad Haris, Yifang Shi, Haewoon Nam
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Efficient path planning is essential for autonomous vehicle navigation. This paper proposes a Composite Inertia Weighted Adaptive Particle Swarm Optimization (APSO) algorithm to improve convergence reliability and solution quality. The proposed method introduces a non linear inertia weight update mechanism that combines exponential decay with a quadratic distance based component to dynamically balance global exploration and local exploitation. This adaptive strategy enables the algorithm to effectively navigate complex environments and generate collision free, smooth, and efficient trajectories. The performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated using benchmark functions and autonomous path planning simulations. Results demonstrate that the composite approach significantly outperforms traditional metaheuristics, achieving a reduction in path length of up to 6.05% over standard PSO, 8.90% over ABC, and 2.48% over Dijkstra. Furthermore, the algorithm increases convergence speed by 34.9% and 20.8% compared to PSO and Dijkstra, respectively, proving its efficacy for high-dimensional, real-time optimization in complex environments.

title: Multi-band Carrier Phase Positioning toward 6G: Performance Bounds and Efficient Estimators
authors: Ehsan Shourezari, Ossi Kaltiokallio, Mehmet C. Ilter, Jukka Talvitie, Gonzalo Seco-Granados, Henk Wymeersch, Mikko Valkama
abstract: In addition to satellite systems, carrier phase positioning (CPP) is gaining attraction also in terrestrial mobile networks, particularly in 5G New Radio (NR) evolution toward 6G. One key challenge is to resolve the so-called integer ambiguity problem, as the carrier phase provides only relative position information. This work introduces and studies a multi-band CPP scenario with intra- and inter-band carrier aggregation (CA) opportunities across FR1, mmWave-FR2, and emerging 6G FR3 bands. Specifically, we derive multi-band CPP performance bounds, showcasing the superiority of multi-band CPP for high-precision localization in current and future mobile networks, while noting also practical imperfections such as clock offsets between the user equipment (UE) and the network as well as mutual clock imperfections between the network nodes. A wide collection of numerical results is provided, covering the impacts of the available carrier bandwidth, number of aggregated carriers, transmit power, and the number of network nodes or base stations. The offered results highlight that only two carriers suffice to substantially facilitate resolving the integer ambiguity problem while also largely enhancing the robustness of positioning against imperfections imposed by the network-side clocks and multi-path propagation. In addition, we also propose a two-stage practical estimator framework that achieves the derived bounds under all realistic bandwidth and transmit power conditions. Furthermore, we show that with an additional search-based refinement step, the proposed estimator becomes particularly suitable for narrowband Internet of Things (IoT) applications operating efficiently even under narrow carrier bandwidths. Finally, both the derived bounds and the proposed estimators are extended to scenarios where the bands assigned to each base station are nonuniform or fully disjoint, enhancing the practical deployment flexibility.

title: A Novel Approach to Temporal QoS Estimation via Extended Kalman Filter-Incorporated Latent Feature Analysis
authors: Ye Yuan, Song Wang, Hongxun Zhou, Ling Wang, Xin Luo
abstract: Predicting temporal Quality of Service (QoS) data is critical for optimizing network services and rationalizing resource allocation in cloud computing and service-oriented systems. Existing mainstream methods have achieved promising predictive performance. However, their purely data-driven manner limits their ability to capture non-stationary temporal patterns, thereby leading to accuracy degradation when temporal QoS data exhibits fluctuations. To tackle this limitation, we propose a novel <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">E</u>xtended <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">K</u>alman Filter-Enhanced <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</u>atent Feature Analysis (EKL) model to perform efficient and accurate temporal QoS prediction from the perspective of bidirectional model-data-driven. Its main idea is three-fold: a) designing a model-driven feature producer to obtain the temporal latent features to capture the intricate temporal pattern following the principle of an Extended Kalman Filter; b) building a data-driven feature producer based on the alternating least squares algorithm to identify time-invariant latent features describing intrinsic user-service characteristics; c) exploiting a density-oriented parallel strategy that achieves workload balancing by sorting users in accordance with their service invocation density, which effectively elevates computational efficiency. In addition, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis to formally prove the convergence of the proposed EKL. Experimental evaluations conducted on real-world temporal QoS datasets reveal that our proposed EKL surpasses existing state-of-the-art models with respect to both computational efficiency and prediction accuracy for missing temporal QoS data.

title: In Memoriam
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology

title: Design Method of Large-Field-of-View Baffle for Space Target Detection
authors: Shuaiwei Mu, Yuanhao Xie, Haolin Qi, Jiajian Guo, Lei Yan, Xin Zhang, Hongbo Wu, Shuanglong Tan
abstract: Stray light suppression is critical for ensuring the detection capability of wide-area space-target detection optical systems, and baffle design serves as the primary method for achieving this suppression. To effectively suppress stray light in wide-area detection optical systems for space targets, this paper proposes a design methodology for wide field of view (61°) baffles. Initially, we construct a detection range inversion model and a stray light suppression capability evaluation model for space target detection, deriving and calculating the point source transmittance (PST) value required to achieve the system's detection capability (6th magnitude star). Subsequently, we derive and summarize the calculation and design methodologies for baffles of different configurations, proposing a two-stage baffle design approach. Finally, we perform structural design and stray light suppression capability simulation verification for this two-stage baffle. The results indicate that the two-stage wide FOV baffle designed using this methodology can effectively suppress large-angle stray light, meeting the engineering application requirements of practical wide-area space target detection.

title: Large Scale Tunnel Air-Ground Collaboration With FLISP: Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner
authors: Fenghe Guo, Runjie Shen, Chenyang Sun, Junrui Zhang, Quanxi Zhan, Yongchun Wang, Junjie Zhang
abstract: Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), a mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) a unified architecture where a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) a hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in a 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves a 100% success rate with ∼7 ms latency, representing a 7-fold speedup over grid-based and three-order-of-magnitude improvement over sampling-based baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers a scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Vector-Symmetry-Based PWM Phase Shift Compensation for Current Reconstruction Using a Single DC-Link Current Sensor
authors: Lei Yang, William Cai, Qingbo Guo, Xinshuai Zhang, Yuchuan Lin, Huanwei Wang
abstract: To reduce the complexity and cost of permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drive systems, a single DC link current sensor scheme with phase-shifting is often adopted for three-phase current reconstruction. However, traditional phase-shifting methods tend to introduce current distortion and increase harmonic content. To address this issue, this paper proposes an improved Vector Symmetry Phase Shifting (VSPS) criterion, which synchronously adjusts the three-phase trigger waveforms under symmetric constraints in space vector modulation (SVM). Furthermore, three phase-shifting methods are proposed based on the VSPS criterion, and their integration with an existing saturation handling scheme is analyzed in terms of the linear modulation range. The proposed methods feature the following characteristics: 1) they enhance the symmetry of pulse width modulation (PWM) signals; 2) they reduce residual voltage vector distortion under saturation handling for two of the three variants; 3) they require no modifications to the standard SVM architecture and introduce no additional switching operations. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the VSPS-based phase-shifting methods not only reduce the total harmonic distortion (THD) of the phase current but also extent the linear modulation range, as compared to conventional phase-shifting methods.

title: Cross-Area Transfer Learning for VLC-based Indoor Localization with a Transfer Efficiency Score
authors: Masood Jan, Wafa Njima, Xun Zhang, Alexander Artemenko
abstract: Indoor localization systems are critical for Industry-4.0 applications.Visible Light Communication (VLC)-based systems offer advantages such as immunity to electromagnetic interference, high accuracy, and energy efficiency, but their performance is hindered by environmental variability across deployment areas. This paper presents a transfer learning (TL) framework for VLC-based indoor localization that enhances cross-area robustness while reducing data requirements. Using real-world measurements from four distinct areas of a Bosch manufacturing facility, we show that fine-tuning a deep neural network (DNN) pre-trained on source area data with only 40% of target-area measurements recovers 97% of full-data performance, thereby reducing the amount of target data needed. We further introduce the Transfer Efficiency Score (TES), a composite metric that identifies the most effective source models for transfer without exhaustive evaluation. Guided by TES, the framework selects the best source model, yielding localization errors as low as 40.9 cm with success rates above 86%. Validation on an unseen area confirms its generalization capability, with a 3.28 cm mean error achieved using the TES-selected source model. Overall, the proposed framework provides a scalable and data-efficient pathway for industrial VLC localization, substantially reducing deployment costs and computational burden while maintaining high accuracy in diverse and dynamic environments.

title: An Enhanced Tolles–Lawson Model with Temperature Compensation for Aeromagnetic Compensation of Triaxial Magnetometers
authors: Wenhua Song, Zhicheng Yang, Yan Ma, Bin Li, Songyun Xie, Chen Chen, Kankan Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: eromagnetic surveying requires compensation for the influence of aircraft dynamic interference magnetic fields.eromagnetic surveying requires compensation for the influence of aircraft dynamic interference magnetic fields.A However, the traditional Tolles-Lawson(T-L) model ignores the time-varying temperature effects, leading to dynamic measurement errors. To achieve better compensation performance under time-varying ambient temperature conditions, we propose an enhanced T-L aeromagnetic compensation model with temperature compensation. This model incorporates the influence of temperature factors on the triaxial magnetometer(TAM) into the T-L compensation model, thereby avoiding errors caused by time-varying ambient temperature and improving compensation accuracy. In dynamic experiments, when the temperature rises at a rate of 10°C/min, the traditional model uses fixed parameters and cannot track this drift, leading to compensation errors highly correlated with the temperature change trend. Experimental results show that the root mean square error (RMSE) of the total magnetic field prior to compensation was 29.97nT. Following compensation by the conventional T-L model, the RMSE was 8.15nT. Our proposed new model reduces the RMSE of the compensation error to 3.74nT and successfully detected a target magnetic anomaly signal of 13.6nT. Therefore, it can significantly reduce magnetic interference and improve the performance of TAM, which is of great importance for high-precision magnetic measurement applications.

title: Equilibrium Scheduling Strategy for MSOP via Bayesian Dynamic Segmentation of Complementarity among LVDNs
authors: Xuehan Chen, Lu Zhang, Wei Tang, Bo Tong, Bo Zhang, Jian Zhao
abstract: Mobile Soft Open Point (MSOP) technology improves the flexibility and utilization of interconnection resources by enabling mobile deployment across low-voltage distribution networks (LVDNs). However, efficient MSOP scheduling depends on accurately identifying the stage-wise complementarity among interconnectable LVDNs under time-varying source-load conditions. This paper proposes a bi-level equilibrium scheduling framework for MSOP by integrating stage-wise complementarity segmentation with coordinated scheduling optimization. First, a Gaussian mixture model is used to construct the complementary probability matrix among LVDNs, and Bayesian dynamic segmentation is employed to identify statistically stable complementarity stages from its temporal evolution. Then, by incorporating the segmented complementarity stages as stage-wise inputs, a bi-level MSOP scheduling model is developed to coordinate the trade-off between the net benefit of MSOP and the mobile scheduling cost. The scheduling problem is formulated as a Pareto-Nash-equilibrium-based multi-objective coordinated optimization problem. Simulation results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Automatic Synchronization of IMU Gait Data via Cross-Segment Angular Velocity Prediction
authors: Yi Chiew Han
abstract: Temporal misalignment between inertial measurement units (IMUs) can compromise the accuracy of gait analysis in wearable motion monitoring. This paper proposes a fully gyroscope-based method to synchronize multi-segment IMU data by leveraging cross-segment angular velocity prediction. Specifically, the angular velocity of the shank is predicted from the thigh angular velocity using multiple models, including feedforward neural network (NN), convolutional neural network (CNN), long short-term memory (LSTM), Bi-directional LSTM (BiLSTM), BiLSTM-Transformer, and CNN-BiLSTM-Transformer. The predicted shank signal is then aligned with the measured shank signal via cross-correlation to estimate temporal shifts, eliminating the need for hardware triggers or communication-based synchronization. A leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation scheme is employed to evaluate the generalization of the proposed method across subjects, ensuring that the models are tested on unseen participants. Experimental validation with six healthy adult subjects without gait impairments walking at a self-selected pace shows that all models achieve high correlation coefficients between predicted and measured shank angular velocity for synchronization. Absolute synchronization errors remain low, with mean absolute errors ranging from 0.99 to 1.6 samples (9.9 to 16 ms), even under intentional shifts of ±300 samples (±3 s). Results indicate that simpler models such as NN and LSTM offer lightweight, practical solutions suitable for real-time synchronization, while more complex architectures like CNN-BiLSTM-Transformer achieve slightly higher synchronization accuracy.

title: System-Level Fault-Tolerant Reconfiguration of 3-D VLSI Processor Arrays Under Multicomponent Failures
authors: Hao Ding, Xiangyong Wang, Yang Xu, Junyan Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Three-dimensional (3-D) VLSI processor arrays offer high integration density and scalability, but their increasing system and interconnect complexity pose significant challenges to reliable reconfiguration under permanent hardware failures. Most existing reconfiguration approaches primarily target processing element (PE) faults and provide limited support for interconnect-related failures, such as switch and link faults, which can severely constrain feasible reconfiguration solutions and the achievable size of fault-free subarrays at the system level. This article investigates system-level fault-tolerant reconfiguration of reconfigurable 3-D VLSI processor arrays under multicomponent failures, including PE, switch, and link faults. We first analyze the system-level impact of different fault types and show that interconnect-level faults, especially switch failures, impose more pronounced constraints on reconfiguration effectiveness than PE faults. Based on this observation, a general plane-exclusion mechanism is introduced that can be integrated with representative PE-only reconfiguration schemes to enhance tolerance to switch and link failures. Furthermore, a fault-transformation preprocessing mechanism is developed to model switch failures as equivalent link disconnections, enabling unified system-level handling of heterogeneous faults and improving structural robustness. To mitigate excessive interconnect overhead introduced during reconfiguration, a long-interconnect optimization strategy is incorporated. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed techniques significantly improve reconfiguration capability and scalability. In particular, the fault-transformation mechanism achieves over 90% of the theoretical upper bound in PE utilization under high switch fault densities, nearly doubling PE utilization compared with baseline methods, while the interconnect optimization reduces the number of long interconnects by more than 10% on average across all evaluated cases.

title: Reflections on Visualizing the COVID-19 Pandemic for the Public
authors: Tatiana Losev, Nicolas Médoc, Klaus Mueller, Nicolás Reinoso Schiller, Max Sondag, Yong Wang, Michael Wybrow, Hajo Zeeb
abstract: We are researchers from six different countries with diverse research backgrounds. We gathered at Schloss Dagstuhl to reflect on data visualizations for public health communication during the COVID-19 pandemic. There, we shared what we had learned and discussed our experiences visualizing COVID-19 data to gain useful insights into the role of data visualization in future pandemics. In this article, we highlight missed opportunities and considerations and provide recommendations. We learned that visualization practices should include cross-disciplinary collaborators with socio-technical expertise, consider personalization and audience more extensively, and use coordinated communication pathways to ensure effective, timely communication in future pandemics.

title: IEEE Recommended Practice for Functional Testing of a Communication-Based Trail Control (CBTC) System
authors: N/A
abstract: A preferred approach for functional testing of a communications-based train control (CBTC) system, based on the CBTC system design and functional allocations as defined in IEEE Std 1474.3™-2025, is established in this recommended practice.

title: Smokeseer: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Smoke Removal and Scene Reconstruction
authors: Neham Jain, Andrew Jong, Sebastian Scherer, Ioannis Gkioulekas
abstract: Smoke in real-world scenes can severely degrade image quality and hamper visibility. Recent image restoration methods either rely on data-driven priors that are susceptible to hallucinations, or are limited to static low-density smoke. We introduce SmokeSeer, a method for simultaneous 3D scene reconstruction and smoke removal from multi-view video sequences. Our method uses thermal and RGB images, leveraging the reduced scattering in thermal images to see through smoke. We build upon 3D Gaussian splatting to fuse information from the two image modalities, and decompose the scene into smoke and non-smoke components. Unlike prior work, SmokeSeer handles a broad range of smoke densities and adapts to temporally varying smoke. We validate our method on synthetic data and a new real-world smoke dataset with RGB and thermal images. We provide an opensource implementation and data on the project website.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://imaging.cs.cmu.edu/smokeseer

title: SNAP: Towards Segmenting Anything in Any Point Cloud
authors: Aniket Gupta, Hanhui Wang, Charles Saunders, Aruni RoyChowdhury, Hanumant Singh, Huaizu Jiang
abstract: Interactive 3D point cloud segmentation enables efficient annotation of complex 3D scenes through user-guided prompts. However, current approaches are typically restricted in scope to a single domain (indoor or outdoor), and to a single form of user interaction (either spatial clicks or textual prompts). Moreover, training on multiple datasets often leads to negative transfer, resulting in domain-specific tools that lack generalizability. To address these limitations, we present SNAP (Segment aNything in Any Point cloud), a unified model for interactive 3D segmentation that supports both point-based and text-based prompts across diverse domains. Our approach achieves cross-domain generalizability by training on 7 datasets spanning indoor, outdoor, and aerial environments, while employing domain-adaptive normalization to prevent negative transfer. For text-prompted segmentation, we automatically generate mask proposals without human intervention and match them against CLIP embeddings of textual queries, enabling both panoptic and open-vocabulary segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SNAP consistently delivers high-quality segmentation results. We achieve state-of-the-art performance on 8 out of 9 zero-shot benchmarks for spatial-prompted segmentation and demonstrate competitive results on all 5 text-prompted benchmarks. These results show that a unified model can match or exceed specialized domain-specific approaches, providing a practical tool for scalable 3D annotation. Project page is at https://neu-vi.github.io/SNAP/

title: Event-Based Multi-Range Radiance Separation and 3D Reconstruction via Line-Scan Pseudo-Square Illumination
authors: Ryuji Hashimoto, Yuta Asano, Shin Ishihara, Bohan Yu, Chu Zhou, Boxin Shi, Imari Sato
abstract: Decomposing scene radiance into physically meaningful components, including direct reflection, interreflection, and scattering, enables a deeper understanding of scene appearance. In this paper, we propose the first method to perform multi-range radiance component separation using only events captured by an event camera, without requiring any additional frame-based measurements. Our approach scans the scene by swiping line-shaped illumination across it, while exploiting the event camera's high temporal resolution and wide dynamic range to recover both direct and multiple global components corresponding to different light propagation distances. To address the noise inherent in event-integration-based radiance recovery, we present a pixel-wise calibration strategy that leverages the reproducibility of per-pixel noise patterns. We demonstrate that this calibration is highly effective in suppressing noise, enabling stable recovery from subtle signals. Moreover, we show that by detecting the timing at which the scanning line passes each pixel, the same line-scan event data can be exploited for coarse 3D reconstruction. Experimental results on real scenes show that our event-based approach achieves faster and finer component separation, while also enabling coarse depth estimation without the exposure control required by frame-based cameras.

title: Data-Efficient Inference of Neural Fluid Fields via SciML Foundation Model
authors: Yuqiu Liu, Jingxuan Xu, Mauricio Soroco, Yunchao Wei, Wuyang Chen
abstract: Recent developments in 3D vision have enabled significant progress in inferring neural fluid fields and realistic rendering of fluid dynamics. However, these methods require dense captures of real-world flow, which demand specialized lab setups, making the process costly and challenging. Scientific machine learning (SciML) foundation models, pretrained on extensive simulations of partial differential equations (PDEs), encode rich multiphysics knowledge and thus provide promising sources of domain priors for inferring fluid fields. Nevertheless, the transferability of these foundation models to real-world vision problems remains largely underexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that SciML foundation models can significantly reduce the data costs of inferring real-world 3D fluid dynamics with improved generalization. Our method leverages strong forecasting capabilities and meaningful representations of SciML foundation models. We equip neural fluid fields with a novel collaborative training that utilizes augmented frames, and fluid features extracted by our foundation model. We demonstrate significant advancements in both quantitative metrics and visual quality over previous methods, improving 9∼36% peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) in future prediction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \sim 50 \%$</tex> reduction in the number of training frames, thereby showcasing the practical applicability of SciML foundation models in real-world fluid dynamics. We release our code at: https://github.com/delta-lab-ai/SciML-HY.

title: Structure-Grounded Training Strategies Aid Generalization in Stereo Matching
authors: Liangxun Ou, Yuhui Liu, Zhenyang Li, Xiaoyang Bai, Yifan Peng
abstract: Stereo matching networks can suffer from generalization challenges when trained on synthetic data and deployed in real-world settings. While existing methods rely on fine-tuning or pre-trained vision foundation models for cross-domain robustness, we revisit this gap from a training perspective and explore a structure-grounded training design that directly improves generalization of RNN-based stereo matching models using only a limited amount of synthetic stereo data, without changing the network architecture or adding any inference overhead. Specifically, we target all three main modules of a typical stereo matching pipeline: in cost volume construction, we enhance geometric cues through data augmentation; in context encoding, we strengthen semantic guidance via auxiliary multitask context supervision; in recurrent disparity refinement, we regulate update dynamics with depth-update regularization. Experiments on multiple mainstream architectures and diverse real-world datasets suggest consistent gains in robustness, improving RAFT-Stereo by 6.6% on KITTI 2015, IGEV-Stereo by 13.7 % on Middlebury, and DLNR by 55.4 % on ETH3D. These insights reveal the previously overlooked importance of structure-grounded training design for achieving reliable stereo depth estimation under data-scarce, domain-shifted conditions.

title: Automated Detection and CNC Trajectory Generation for Pitting Repair in Aluminum Cylinder Heads Using YOLOv5
authors: Jose Flores, José Magallanes, Joel Figeroa
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: The maintenance of aluminum cylinder heads in internal combustion engines still relies on manual visual inspection and artisanal repair planning, leading to inconsistent results, long intervention times, and a high rate of rework. Although industry has made progress in inspection and machining systems, there are currently no integrated solutions capable of automatically detecting defects and generating CNC toolpaths for their repair, representing a significant technological gap. To address this limitation, this study proposes an automated methodology that combines image processing, pit detection using YOLOv5, and a geometric conversion module that transforms each detected defect into a CNC trajectory adapted to its morphology. This workflow enables, for the first time, an automated closing of the inspection-repair loop. Experimental results show 94.7% accuracy in defect detection, processing times below 10 s per image, and a mean error of 0.28 mm in trajectory generation, validated on 300 real images. The proposed solution demonstrates the feasibility of integrating automatic detection with assisted repair, reducing operator dependence and process variability, and providing a significant advancement toward automation in automotive remanufacturing. This integrated approach demonstrates a viable solution for closing the inspection-repair loop in automotive remanufacturing, reducing human dependency and process variability.

title: Learning Cooperative Task Allocation for Heterogeneous Multi-AUV Teams with QMIX, CommNet, and Task-Conditioned Cross-Agent Attention
authors: Hailin Wang, Shuo Li, Yiqun Wang, Tianyou Qiu, Yiping Li
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Heterogeneous multi-autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) missions require robust cooperative task allocation under partial observability and unreliable acoustic communication. This paper presents a value-based multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework that integrates centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) using QMIX, explicit interagent message passing via CommNet, and task-conditioned crossagent attention for selective information fusion. A 3D underwater task-allocation simulator is developed to model heterogeneous AUV capabilities, discrete motion primitives, and a configurable underwater communication interface, including range-limited adjacency, message corruption, and optional physical-layer constraints. To address training instability in such environments, a stabilization-oriented training strategy is employed, incorporating long-horizon exploration, conservative learning rates, soft target network updates, gradient clipping, and reward shaping. In an underwater search scenario with five heterogeneous AUVs and ten tasks, the learned policy consistently achieves full task completion, completing all tasks with a mean episodic return of 199.2 over 50 evaluation episodes.

title: Enhanced Linux Malware Detection Using Control Flow Graphs with Graph2Vec Embedding and Graph Attention Networks
authors: Sorifa Alam, Francis Rudra D Cruze, Rakibul Hasan Nirob, Md. Ruhul Kuddus
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Malware detection in Linux remains a challenging task to modern computing systems as existing techniques struggle to address the constant evolution of sophisticated threats and the advanced obfuscation techniques. Our research investigates graph-based techniques, efficiently examining the limitations in control flow feature extraction from Executable and Linkable Format (ELF) files by capturing their execution pattern and basic blocks information to improve malware detection accuracy, scalability and robustness against new obfuscation techniques. In this work, we propose a Linux malware detection framework that generates Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) from ELF files, embeds them using Graph2Vec and classifies them using Graph Attention Networks (GATs). We have conducted the experiment on recently released 1,385 Linux malware samples from VirusShare and 1,500 benign ELF samples from the Labeled-Elfs repository. Our experimental results illustrate that Graph2Vec with GAT achieves 97.05 % accuracy, 99.46 % area under the curve (AUC) with balanced Precision, Recall and F1-score of 97.05 %. These results demonstrate the framework's success to identify both known and unknown malware samples in ELF files by showing the importance of selecting appropriate feature extraction techniques and machine learning models, as well as the efficacy of graph-based techniques to identify newly sophisticated malware behaviors more efficiently.

title: Ensemble Deep Learning for Automated Multiclass Classification of Gastrointestinal Disorders in Endoscopic Images
authors: Shovan Samanta Turzo, Shafin Ibnul Mohasin, K.M. Arafat Islam, Towsifur Rahman, Md Jahidul Islam Moon, Hossain Mohammad Shuvo, Md Jahidul Islam Mozumdar, Maimun Nahar Rahi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The approach described in our work is novel in terms of identifying and categorizing aboard medical images of GI related conditions, which include Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), Esophagitis, Polyps, and Ulcerative Colitis. Chronic digestive disorders and diseases related to GERD include: Esophagitis - swelling of the esophagus, also due to persistent GERD. Four cutting-edge deep learning architectures namely: DeepLabV3+, U-Net, YOLOv4, and combination of all three modality were trained and assessed to address the constraints on their diagnosis of the diseases. This barrier was addressed by a set of 8000 augmented images. DeepLab V3+ was staked with the precision of classification of 94% and U-Net with 82% while the average precision of the YOLOv4 system is 95%. The ensemble model that incorporated the optimal components of all the models combined attained the very high level of 98% accuracy since it succeeded in enhancing the overall performance in diagnosing factors. In this research, ensemble learning has shown promise as a medical image classifier, especially of hard-to-detect gastrointestinal conditions like GERD and Esophagitis. We believe that our approach will open up the way towards more efficient systematized and automated early detection and classification processes which, on a long-run, will help in making clinical decision support systems in gastroenterological care.

title: CST-Logo Shaped Super Wideband Microstrip Antenna with ML-Driven Gain Prediction for Next-Generation Wireless Systems
authors: Anik Paul, Farhin Sultana, Md. Farhad Hossain, Md. Azad Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper introduces the design and performance evaluation of a CST-logo shaped super wideband (SWB) microstrip patch antenna optimized for next-generation wireless systems. The antenna is implemented on a Rogers RO4003C substrate (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon_{R}=3.55$</tex>, thickness 1.6 mm) with annealed copper metallization, achieving a broad impedance bandwidth of 38.6 GHz, spanning from 23.6 GHz to 62.2 GHz. Simulation results confirm excellent impedance matching, with over 85% of the operating frequencies exhibiting return loss below 15 dB, and a VSWR consistently maintained between 1 and 1.7. The proposed antenna delivers stable gain characteristics across the band, ranging from 6 dBi to a peak of 9.45 dBi, with a maximum radiation efficiency of 83%. To further improve performance evaluation, machine learning (ML) is employed to predict gain variation based on slot geometry parameters. Seven regression-based ML models were investigated, with Support Vector Machine (SVM) providing the most accurate prediction of gain trends. This work covers multiple mmWave communication windows, including the 5G FR2 band, Ka-band satellite links, and the 60 GHz ISM band, the proposed antenna demonstrates strong potential for integration into next-generation wireless and high-speed communication systems.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Driver Behavior Classification in Smart Transportation Systems: From Rule-Based Models to AI Agents
authors: Chaima Tayachi, Olfa Dridi, Mohamed Barkaoui, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Driver behavior classification has emerged as a critical component in intelligent transportation systems, advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and semi-autonomous vehicles. The accurate detection of abnormal driving behaviors such as distraction, drowsiness, aggression and impaired driving is essential for improving road safety and traffic efficiency. This paper presents a comprehensive and structured review of recent advancements in driver behavior analysis. It cover traditional computational algorithm, Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), hybrid ML/DL frameworks and emerging AI agent-based systems. We systematically analyze methodological trends, input modalities (visual, physiological, vehicle-based, environmental and smartphone sensors) and evaluation strategies. Particular emphasis is placed on multimodal fusion techniques and contextaware AI agents that extend conventional classifiers toward adaptive and decision-capable monitoring systems. This review further identifies critical research challenges, including privacy preservation, cross-driver generalization, real-time deployment constraints and optimal multimodal fusion strategies. Finally, future research directions are outlined, highlighting the role of explainable AI, lightweight embedded architectures and intelligent agent frameworks for next-generation driver monitoring systems.

title: A Lightweight Dual-Modality Biometric Classifier for Hand and Iris Images
authors: Refat-E-Jannat, Jeba Tasnia, Amimoy Kumar Biswas, Umme Rumman, Jakirul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Strengthening digital system security, especially by shielding consumers from online fraud and illegal account breaches, is the main goal of this thesis. Unauthorized access to private data has grown to be a major worry in today's connected society, and strong and dependable authentication systems are desperately needed to protect users.A complicated convolutional neural network (CNN) based multimodal biometric identification approach has been presented to overcome this difficulty. Convolutional, pooling, dense layers, and concatenation are used to conduct feature fusion. This method uses restricted and strategic data augmentation and minimum preprocessing to optimize accuracy and resilience by combining several biometric features. With a Test Accuracy of 98.94 %, Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR) of 97.87 %, Weighted F1 Score(test set) of 98.87 %, and most importantly an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.0000, the assessment demonstrates that the model produced extremely encouraging results. The False Acceptance Rate (FAR) and False Rejection Rate (FRR) of the system are identical, therefore EER is a crucial parameter in biometric security. The system's ability to nearly flawlessly differentiate between authentic users and imposters is demonstrated by its zero EER, which ensures far greater security than many current methods.The methodology is also appropriate for real-time biometric authentication because it takes an average of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 2 1}$</tex> milliseconds each step. These findings demonstrate that the suggested approach is a good option for high security applications as it not only improves accuracy but also offers more defense against unauthorized access and unethical intrusions.

title: Advanced Seed Classification and Defect Detection Using Convolutional Neural Networks and Unsupervised Learning Techniques
authors: Sirazzum Munira, Atiya Ibnat Lamia, MD. Sakhawat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid deep learning framework for automated seed classification and defect detection in agricultural quality control. The system integrates supervised learning through Custom Convolutional Neural Networks (CustomCNN) and Attention-based CNNs (AttentionCNN) with unsupervised techniques including clustering algorithms and autoencoders. Working with high-resolution microscopic soybean seed images, the supervised models achieve test accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 7. 6 5 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 7. 5 5 \%}$</tex> respectively for multi-class classification. The unsupervised autoencoder successfully identifies 312 anomalies through reconstruction error analysis. Clustering techniques partition RGB pixel data into three quality classes with silhouette scores up to 0.6810. This hybrid approach addresses key challenges in agricultural image analysis by enabling both accurate classification of labeled data and anomaly detection in unlabeled datasets, with processing times of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 0}-\text{1 2 0 m s}$</tex> per seed suitable for real-time deployment in seed sorting facilities.

title: Coherent 1.2–25 μm Supercontinuum in a 1 mm CdTe-on-CdS Rib Waveguide
authors: Jannatul Nayem Priya, Rakayet Rafi, Sagar Mutsuddi, M R Karim, Mohammad Istiaque Reja
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In this work, a CdTe-on-CdS rib waveguide is designed to generate a broadband mid-infrared supercontinuum (SC) ranging from about 1.2 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>. The waveguide is pumped with a 25-femtosecond pulse at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.90 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>, with around 2.5 kW of peak power. Mode simulations were done in COMSOL, and pulse propagation was modeled using the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (GNLSE). A 1 -mm-long waveguide, when pumped, generates a spectrum stretching from 1.2 to almost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2 5 ~} \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>. The broadening is driven first by self-phase modulation and then, after compression, by soliton fission with dispersive waves taking energy outward. Numerical modeling with the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation—accounting for loss, Raman response, and self-steepening—has shown that even a 1 mm device can produce a wide, flat spectrum. The coherence stays above 0.9 for wavelengths over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.4 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex>. Such a device is considered a plausible fit for spectroscopy and mid-IR imaging/sensing, provided that long-wavelength losses are managed during fabrication.

title: Subtype-Specific Biomarker Discovery in Leukemia: Bridging Expression Profiling and Machine Learning
authors: Anik Dev Nath, Md Al Amin, Sajib Acharjee Dip, Mahjebin Jerin, Bikash Kumar Paul, Touhid Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Leukemia is a common type of blood cancer that can occur in people of any age. Detecting it early and diagnosing it correctly are very important for successful treatment and better recovery. This study presents a simplified machine learning pipeline for analyzing leukemia cancer subtypes using high-dimensional gene expression data. Initially, variance-based filtering was applied to remove low-information genes, preserving discriminative features for better model performance. Univariate feature selection using ANOVA F-test identified the top 30 genes, followed by t-tests to detect differentially expressed genes (DEGs) for biomarker discovery. For leveraging balanced class weights to address subtype imbalance, machine learning models ware utilized to capture gene expression patterns. Experimental results showed strong performance, achieving 98% accuracy and 99% weighted F1-score for Support Vector Machine(SVM). This research project supports reliable leukemia subtype classification and biomarker discovery, advancing precision oncology.

title: Integrating Post-Quantum Cryptography and AI-Driven Security in Multi-Tier Network Architectures
authors: Mustafa El Bizri, Ahmad M. El-Hajj, Layth Sliman, Ali M. Haidar
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Recent studies quantify post-quantum cryptography (PQC) overhead in isolated TLS handshakes, while intrusion detection research focuses on legacy traffic patterns, leaving the intersection of PQC deployment and security monitoring unexplored. This work bridges this gap with the first systematic assessment of multi-hop PQC networks integrated with AI-based intrusion detection. Through systematic evaluation of four deployment configurations, we quantify that multi-hop PQC incurs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17-18 ~\text{ms}$</tex> latency overhead, exceeding single-hop predictions yet operationally feasible. Critically, Isolation Forest maintains 98.11 % detection accuracy despite PQC-modified traffic, significantly outperforming Random Forest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(94.75 \%, p<0.001)$</tex>. These findings establish that networks can adopt quantum-resistant cryptography without compromising threat detection, providing foundations for integrated security architectures. While results are simulation-based, they offer critical insights for guiding realworld PQC-IDS deployments.

title: Towards Real-Time Service Classification in Tor: A Supervised Machine Learning Approach
authors: Reem Khalife, Bassem Haidar, Carole Bassil
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The Tor network uses multiple layers of encryption to preserve users anonymity. However, this protection often attracts malicious actors as it reduces the effectiveness of traffic monitoring mechanisms. In this work, we assess the feasibility of using machine learning to perform service level classification of Tor traffic in real-time. We evaluate five supervised learning algorithms: LightGBM, CatBoost, Random Forest, C4.5 and C5.0 trained on a combined dataset consisting of the ISCXTor2016 dataset and a self-collected Tor traffic dataset. A systematic pipeline is developed to analyze model performance under varying preprocessing strategies and feature representation. Among the evaluated models, LightGBM achieved the highest predictive performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.994 when using identifierinclusive features and 0.885 under an identifier-exclusive setting. The top performing models were then deployed in a real-time classification framework. To measure how the system behaves in practice, we introduce two operational metrics, responsiveness and packet efficiency, to quantify real-time performance. Experimental results demonstrate that LightGBM and Random Forest maintain robust performance in live settings, with browsing, audio, and file-transfer services consistently classified with high accuracy.

title: Collaborative Service of On-Device Unimodal Model and Cloud-Based Multimodal Model for Mobile Livestreaming Content Understanding
authors: Chaoyue Niu, Yufei Zhu, Yutong Dai, Yikai Yan, Zhijie Cao, Hao Jiang, Chengfei Lv, Shaojie Tang, Fan Wu, Guihai Chen
abstract: To guide consumers to mobile livestreams that involve their interested products, it is necessary to have a good understanding of livestreaming contents, and the key task is to accurately recognize the products being promoted by streamers. The mainstream cloud-based service framework is challenged by the high concurrency of requests, the high overhead of multimodal recognition, and the requirement of low latency. To break these bottlenecks, we propose a new device-cloud collaborative service framework, where each streamer's mobile device holds unimodal model that can process most of video frames and also uploads the extracted unimodal features to facilitate the cloud-side multimodal recognition of the remaining few frames. On-device unimodal model is further incrementally trained over the samples constructed by leveraging the streamers' manual labeling behaviors, leading to personalized version that can adapt to the heterogeneous and dynamic livestreaming contents of different streamers. Nevertheless, the device-side personalized unimodal features are misaligned in feature space and cannot be directly fused into the cloud-side multimodal model. We thus design a pluggable prompt generation module to transform the personalized unimodal features into prompt embeddings and prepend them to the original inputs of the multimodal backbone network, instructing feature fusion in a streamer-specific manner. We extensively evaluate on the public Fashion-Gen dataset and an industrial dataset collected from Taobao Live. We also perform online testing and practical overhead testing in Taobao Live. Evaluation results reveal the effectiveness and efficiency of our design as well as the consistent advantage over existing baselines.

title: Parallel Active Microcantilevers for High-Throughput Atomic Force Microscopy
authors: Hazhir Mahmoodi Nasrabadi, K. S. Vikrant, S. O. Reza Moheimani
abstract: The limited throughput of single-cantilever Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) motivates parallel imaging using multiple cantilevers. However, parallel imaging with passive cantilever arrays requires multiple macroscale actuators and sensors, leading to a large system footprint, increased vibration sensitivity, and reduced imaging bandwidth per cantilever. We present an alternative approach based on active microcantilevers with integrated microscale actuators and sensors to address these limitations. We microfabricated a three-cantilever array, equipping each cantilever with a piezoelectric actuator (PA)– piezoelectric sensor (PS) pair for tapping-mode operation (free amplitude $\approx 100$ nm near 50 kHz) and an electrothermal Z-actuator (range 10 $\mu$m, closed-loop bandwidth 100 Hz) to regulate tip–sample separation. Operating all three cantilevers in parallel, each scanned a 3 $\mu$m × 3 $\mu$m region on two different calibration gratings at a 1 Hz line rate. The system achieved a lateral sampling of 12 nm per pixel and resolved 100 nm step features with $\leq \!\!10\%$ height error. These results demonstrate a threefold increase in effective imaging throughput relative to single-probe AFM, while maintaining high-resolution performance, highlighting the advantages of independently actuated and sensed microcantilevers for parallel AFM.

title: IEEE IAS Member Fang Zheng Peng Received the 2026 IEEE Medal in Power Engineering [Awards]
authors: Radu Bojoi
abstract: Presents the recipients of IEEE Medal in Power Engineering awards for 2026.

title: Offloading Optimization in Intent-driven Edge Computing: An LLM-based Distributed Framework
authors: Mingqi Han, Xinghua Sun, Chenyuan Feng, Wen Zhan, Xijun Wang, Tony Q. S. Quek
abstract: Meeting the demand for personalized, low-latency services in 5G networks requires the convergence of intent-driven edge computing. However, existing frameworks are limited: centralized approaches incur high overhead and privacy risks, while distributed solutions overlook the flexibility of natural-language intents and their impacts on offloading optimization. To solve this, we propose a distributed LLM-based joint intent comprehension and offloading optimization framework, in which each edge server utilizes an LLM agent to locally interpret natural-language intents and optimize offloading decisions in a distributed manner. To enable distributed coordination, we introduce a self-reasoning prompt to guide the LLM in predicting neighbors’ decisions, thus capturing correlations among UEs. Moreover, we propose the Weighted Reward-aligned DPO (WR-DPO) algorithm to enhance decision capability. High-quality samples are utilized to mitigate reward noise and incorporate reward-gap weighting and regularization for stable convergence. Simulation results demonstrate that WR-DPO significantly outperforms other baselines with a 30% average performance gain, and demonstrates strong generalization capability in inexperienced scenarios.

title: Aperiodic Sampled-Data $H_{\infty }$ Controller Design for Vehicle Active Suspension Systems: A Structure-Integrated Data-Driven Approach
authors: Yu-Long Fan, Chuan-Ke Zhang, Jing-Yue Song, Du Xiong, Yong He
abstract: This article presents a novel structure-integrated data-driven approach for designing <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_{\infty }$</tex-math></inline-formula> static output feedback controllers for vehicle active suspension systems with unknown dynamics and aperiodic sampling. Unlike traditional model-based approaches, the proposed framework synthesizes the controller directly from offline input-state measurements without requiring an explicit system model. First, the unknown system dynamics are parameterized from noisy data. Specifically, by integrating the structural characteristics of the suspension system, the unknown output and state constraint matrices are embedded into the constructed data-driven system representation. Building upon this, a static output-feedback controller design scheme formulated as a set of data-based linear matrix inequalities is derived using the looped-functional approach. Therefore, the proposed method provides theoretical guarantees for closed-loop stability under aperiodic sampling while ensuring <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_{\infty }$</tex-math></inline-formula> performance and safety constraints. Finally, numerical simulations and hardware-in-the-loop experiments validate that the proposed control strategy effectively improves ride comfort while ensuring the stability and safety of the suspension system.

title: Breaking the Depth Barrier in Motor Imagery Classification via a Residual Depthwise-Separable Network
authors: Ruiyu Zhao, Ian Daly, Xinjie He, Ruitian Xu, Chongfeng Wang, Xingyu Wang, Andrzej Cichocki, Jing Jin
abstract: Lightweight networks that include depthwise-separable convolution are widely used in motor imagery (MI) electroencephalogram (EEG) decoding of brain–computer interface (BCI). Many established MI classification networks are relatively shallow, preventing them from benefiting from the hierarchical feature extraction capabilities of deeper structures. Due to suboptimal residual connection structures, the mismatched residual baseline layer design, and the poor compatibility between data preprocessing and residual modules, the deepening of networks cannot be effectively combined with residual structures. This creates a depth barrier that hinders further performance improvements. To address these challenges, we propose a novel method, residual depthwise-separable deep neural network (ResDSNet), built upon an unraveled view-path analysis of residual connection structures. The analysis reveals that the residual mechanism achieves optimal performance when the layer distribution across different paths approximates a binomial distribution. Furthermore, we design a residual depthwise-separable convolution module and a tailored data-preprocessing module that effectively integrate with the residual structure, filtering noise and retaining MI task features. We evaluate ResDSNet on three publicly available datasets, including the BCI Competition IV Dataset IIa, the BCI Competition IV Dataset IIb, and the PhysioNet dataset, which collectively contain EEG signals recorded from 127 human subjects. ResDSNet achieves accuracies of 79.36%, 84.95%, and 64.13%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 3.16%, 1.59%, and 8.40% with statistical significance. Experimental results indicate that ResDSNet fully unlocks the hierarchical representation capabilities of deep networks for MI-EEG decoding, achieving robust performance and demonstrating substantial potential to overcome the inherent challenges in BCIs.

title: ALA-MARL: Multi-objective edge server placement based on artificial lemming algorithm and multi-agent reinforcement learning in mobile edge computing
authors: Zhou Zhou, Tingyu Zheng, Gang Liu
abstract: Mobile edge computing (MEC) has emerged as a critical paradigm for supporting latency-sensitive and computation-intensive applications by deploying edge servers close to end users. However, determining optimal edge server placement remains a challenging multi-objective optimization problem that involves trade-offs among latency, energy consumption, load balancing, and deployment cost. Existing approaches often struggle to balance exploration and exploitation in large-scale, dynamic network environments. To address this gap, this paper proposes an ALA-MARL algorithm that integrates an Artificial Lemming Algorithm (ALA) with Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) for multi-objective edge server placement in MEC. Given that ALA provides robust global search capabilities and an efficient population-based optimization mechanism, the ALA algorithm is initially employed to deploy edge servers in MEC. The MARL algorithm then adapts to real-time network dynamics through collaborative learning and further refines the initially deployed edge servers to achieve optimal performance. Experimental results show that ALA-MARL significantly outperforms benchmark methods in terms of latency reduction (up to 38%), load-balancing improvement (up to 42%), and energy consumption reduction (up to 20%). The proposed framework offers a scalable and adaptive solution for edge infrastructure planning in next-generation mobile networks.

title: AMPGLDA: Predicting LncRNA-Disease Associations Based on Adaptive Meta-Path Generation and Multi-Layer Perceptron_supp1-3696766.xlsx
authors: Dengju Yao
abstract: Increased evidence suggests that long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) hold a vital position in intricate human diseases. Nonetheless, the current pool of identified lncRNAs linked to diseases remains restricted. Hence, a reliable and cost-effective computational method is needed to predict probable correlations between lncRNAs and diseases, which could help reveal underlying mechanisms and foster the development of novel treatments. In this study, we propose a novel approach for predicting the associations between lncRNAs and diseases, which relies on the adaptive meta-path generation and multi-layer perceptron (AMPGLDA). Firstly, we integrate information about lncRNA, diseases, and miRNAs to construct a heterogeneous graph. Then, we utilize principal component analysis to extract global features from nodes. Based on this heterogeneous graph, AMPGLDA adaptively generates multiple meta-path graph structures and uses a graph convolutional neural network to learn the semantic feature representations of lncRNA and disease from the meta-path. Ultimately, AMPGLDA utilizes a deep neural network classifier to accurately predict the association between lncRNA and disease. Cross-validation experiments revealed that AMPGLDA outperformed 5 other cutting-edge prediction methods. Moreover, ablation studies and case studies confirmed its technical contributions and its capability to identify disease related lncRNAs.

title: Ray-Tracing-Based Multipath Classification and Adaptive Satellite Selection for Improved Tropospheric Estimation
authors: Saqib Mehdi, Mingda Ye, Guohao Zhang, Witold Rohm
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate estimation of the Zenith Tropospheric Delay (ZTD) is essential for high-precision GNSS positioning and atmospheric sensing, but in dense urban environments, severe multipath and Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) receptions introduce geometry-dependent biases that destabilize Precise Point Positioning (PPP) and limit the use of urban GNSS data for troposphere estimation. Conventional observation weighting approaches based on elevation or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C/N_{0}$</tex-math></inline-formula> lack physical awareness of the surrounding environment and perform poorly under complex urban propagation. This study introduces a ray-tracing–assisted PPP framework that explicitly models GNSS signal interactions with urban structures using detailed 3D city models. For each satellite–receiver epoch, signals are classified as line-of-sight, reflected, diffracted, mixed multipath, or NLOS, supported by electromagnetic reflection and diffraction coefficients, and are adaptively reweighted or excluded within the PPP processing. Experiments with real urban GNSS data show that unmitigated PPP suffers from code residuals of 50–100 m, horizontal position errors exceeding 10 m, and ZTD estimates with a standard deviation of 35.7 m and a bias of 10.85 m. After applying the proposed method, code and phase residuals are reduced to approximately 2 m and 0.02 m, horizontal positioning achieves sub-meter precision, and ZTD accuracy improves by more than two orders of magnitude, with a standard deviation of 0.238 m and a bias of -0.033 m. These results demonstrate that geometry-aware multipath modeling is essential for reliable urban tropospheric estimation and enables high-precision ZTD retrieval from dense, low-cost GNSS infrastructures, including smartphones.

title: Machine Learning Unveils System-Level Signatures of Integrated Motor–Non-Motor Dysfunction in Parkinson’s Disease during Telerehabilitation
authors: Lucia Pepa, Marianna Capecci, Antonia Antoniello, Luca Spalazzi, Nicolò Baldini, Elisa Andrenelli, Marialuisa Gandolfi, Giulia Bonardi, Michele Tinazzi, Maria Gabriella Ceravolo
