abstract: In-home monitoring and rehabilitation are promising approaches for efficient and effective care of people with Parkinson’s Disease (PwPD). However, their adoption is still minimal, few evidences come from real in-home studies, and the integration of telemonitoring and telerehabilitation functions is absent. This study presents a novel system with remote smartwatch-based monitoring and rehabilitation integrated functions. Furthermore, an original machine learning approach is proposed to predict the clinical improvement, stability, or worsening of motor symptoms severity after a 3-month at-home rehabilitation period. Ninety-five PwPD were enrolled. An exploratory data analysis based on linear regression was applied to the collected smartwatch data to drive the extraction of meaningful features about the trend of physiological variables. Then, a time-series forest classification approach was applied to predict motor outcome from extracted features. The performance was evaluated against the clinical evaluation of the Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale before and after the 3-month period. Performance metrics reached 63% for accuracy, F1-score and recall, and 62% precision in nested cross-validation. Shapley analysis revealed an interesting agreement between model classification strategy and known clinical markers of overall health conditions and physical activity. Features related to heart rate and stress emerged as the most discriminative, showing on average decreased values of stress and heart rate in improved participants and increased values of the same features in worsened ones. Overall, these findings support the potential of combining remote monitoring and machine learning approaches to better understand the complex interaction between physiological signals, physical activity, and clinical status in PwPD, but the identified limitations suggest it should be considered a starting point for further research and improvements.

title: Differential Phase Sensing of Glucose Concentrations Using Frequency Mixing
authors: Thamer S. Almoneef
abstract: This letter presents a compact differential phase sensor for dielectric characterization of liquid samples. The sensing structure consists of two microwave sensor paths, one serving as a reference and the other loaded with the material under test, enabling differential phase measurements with strong common-mode noise rejection. A triangular defected ground structure (DGS) enhances the phase sensitivity of each path. The phase difference induced by the sample is converted into a DC voltage using a homodyne zero intermediate-frequency (IF) mixer, eliminating the need for vector network analyzer measurements. The sensor is experimentally validated using glucose concentration measurements across two distinct regimes: a large-volume industrial range (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 10\rm{--}60 \rm{ g/100mL}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and a smaller differential regime (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 10\rm{--}50 \rm{ g/50mL}$</tex-math></inline-formula>). At a fixed operating frequency of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.65 \rm{ GHz}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, the system demonstrates a sensitive, monotonic voltage response. These results confirm the sensor's ability to provide accurate and repeatable detection of subtle dielectric variations, making it suitable for applications ranging from industrial bioprocessing to clinical diagnostics.

title: Unit-Load Dual-Command Cycles: Comparisons between Traditional and Non-traditional Cross-aisle Layouts
authors: Subir S. Rao
abstract: We study howcross-aisle orientation/position alters expected dual-command travel in low-level order picking systems in unit-load warehouses and give recommendations regarding which layout should be preferred in practice. We develop exact, closed-form pick-travel models for warehouses with a V cross-aisle and the expected dual-command distance (DC) into a single-command component (SC) and a travel-between (TB) component that captures pairing efficiency. The propositions proved in this research drastically reduce the number of possibilities in the mathematical model to compute the TB component, thus making it easy to perform a sensitivity analysis with typical warehouse parameters. The V cross-aisle warehouse considered in this paper is formulated to be closer to the real setting and to include the key features of a real facility like multiple parallel aisles, realistic cross-aisle width and geometry, single P/D point at the center, and provisions for rack space lost due to the cross-aisle. We benchmark the V cross-aisle warehouse against three alternative traditional layouts A (without middle cross-aisle) and B/C (with middle cross-aisle) using computational experiments over representative facility sizes. Across the reported results, V cross-aisle warehouses attains the lowest expected dual-command distance in most settings, with advantages that tend to grow as facilities widen; where the V cross-aisle layout always dominates. Traditional layouts are more competitive for facilities having lower number of aisles with longer aisle lengths. We conclude that the V cross-aisle offers a practical and better performing layout for dual-command order picking, and the closed-form pick-travel models enable rapid design comparisons and sensitivity checks.

title: Energy-Based Adaptive Cruise Control With Structurally Induced Self-Excited Driving for Car-Following
authors: Yuntae Kim, Byungjun Kim, Dasol Jeong, Hyuck-Kee Lee, Seibum B. Choi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a novel energy-based adaptive cruise control (ACC) algorithm for car-following that fundamentally redefines the control target. Conventional ACC, formulated as a spacing-error regulation problem, structurally leads to frequent actuator switching under realistic driving conditions, which can increase control effort and limit opportunities for fuel-efficient behavior. To address these limitations, the proposed controller regulates a desired energy level within the car-following system instead of minimizing tracking errors with respect to the preceding vehicle. This change induces structurally self-excited driving, forming a loop in the relative distance–velocity domain and enabling Pulse-and-Glide-like behavior without relying on rule-based switching or gain tuning. To ensure safety during self-excited driving, a tube-based control structure is introduced, within which the system remains bounded. Additionally, a parameter update mechanism is incorporated to further reduce the influence of the preceding vehicle. Together, these features enable smoother control inputs, improved road load utilization, and reduced control effort from the lower-level controller. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms conventional ACC and production-level Smart Cruise Control (SCC) systems in terms of control smoothness and energy efficiency, while maintaining a safe following distance. The algorithm also satisfies string stability, validating its applicability in multi-vehicle scenarios. Overall, the proposed approach offers a structurally interpretable and driver-personalizable framework for efficient and robust car-following.

title: Designing a 32-Channel DWDM RoF-FSO System for Next-Generation Communication
authors: Shanjida Islam, Md. Shamim Ahsan, Isha Das, Monjurul Islam, S. M. Mahmudur Rahman, Md. Towhidul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This research presents the design and performance evaluation of a 32-channel Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing (DWDM)-based Radio over Fiber integrated with Free Space Optics (RoF-FSO) system. The proposed architecture achieves an aggregate data rate of 1280 Gbps, with each channel operating at 40 Gbps. The transmission link incorporates singlemode fiber (SMF), dispersion compensating fiber (DCF), an erbium-doped fiber amplifier (EDFA), and a free-space optical (FSO) segment, and the complete hybrid system is modeled in OptiSystem. Performance metrics including Q-factor, bit error rate (BER), and eye-diagram analysis are evaluated using a 1024-bit sequence with 256 samples per bit, resulting in more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8}$</tex> million processed samples across all <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 2}$</tex> channels, ensuring statistically reliable system assessment. The system demonstrates consistently high Q-factors (all above 8), clear eye openings with eye heights on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-7</sup>, and minimum BER values ranging between 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-17</sup> and 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-20</sup>, confirming excellent signal integrity at 40 Gbps per channel. The system can propagate data over up to 60 km of optical fiber and up to 3.5 km of free-space distance while maintaining minimal BERs across all channels, enabling an extended end-to-end reach of approximately 63.5 km, exceeding the performance limits of any individual link. Uniform behavior across representative channels verifies the scalability and robustness of the 32-channel DWDM RoF-FSO architecture. The proposed design effectively combines the longreach advantages of fiber with the flexibility of a wireless optical hop, offering a high-capacity and long-range solution suitable for 5G backhaul, dense and rural area connectivity, military communication, and emerging high-speed photonics networks. Simulation results confirm the feasibility and reliability of the system under the evaluated conditions.

title: Ensemble Deep Learning for Accurate Detection of Fish Diseases: A Comparative Study Using ResNet50, EfficientNet-B3, and Vision Transformer
authors: Irfanul Huda, Tanjima Rahman Farny, Zakaria Ahmed Jim, Abdullah Al Noman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Aquaculture has a large contribution to the nutritional and economic sectors of Bangladesh. It faces significant challenges from fish diseases that threaten economic stability and food security. So, fish disease detection is an important factor for the agriculture sector in Bangladesh. This study proposes an ensemble deep learning model combining ResNet50, EfficientNetB3, and Vision Transformer (ViT) for accurate fish disease detection through image classification. We develop a clean, deduplicated dataset and evaluate our model on the dataset. While the ensemble achieves 95.71 % accuracy-lower than some reported SOTA results-we demonstrate that many prior works use duplicated or inflated datasets, making their comparisons unreliable. A comparative analysis with prior works, including CNNs, clustering-based methods, and transfer learning, highlights the model's performance. This approach offers a scalable solution for automated disease detection, supporting sustainable aquaculture in Bangladesh. Also, this ensemble leverages complementary strengths of convolutional and transformer architectures for enhanced feature extraction. Our research has been made opensource, so that anyone can use our models and instructions, which are availablexsat:https://github.com/Parjival/FishDiseaseDetection

title:
First-Principle Approach of Eco-Friendly K
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    RI
                    <sub>6</sub>
                    (R= Ga, in) Materials for Optoelectronic Applications: A DFT Study
authors: Mohammad Jahidul Islam, Naimur Rahaman Rifat, Md Rifat Hossain, Ruhul Amin, Umme Muslima, Ahmed Al Mansur
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Materials based on halide perovskites have garnered a lot of interest from scientists due to their various photovoltaic and optoelectronic advantages. In this work, the substantial differences in the physical characteristics of cubic halide perovskites <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{3} \text{RI}_{6}(\mathrm{R}=\text{Ga}$</tex>, In) were examined using density functional theory (DFT) to investigate their mechanical and optoelectronic parameters with special emphasis on their electronic behavior. At the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Gamma$</tex> point, the intrinsic structures of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{3} \text{GaI}_{6}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{3} \text{InI}_{6}$</tex> display direct electronic band gap values of 0.812 eV and 1.21 eV, respectively, which exceed the optical band gap requirement. The partial and total density of states, along with a noticeable absorption peak at both low and high energies, emerged along with the electronic band structure. The conduction and valence bands' contributions are modified by variations in the band gap because they change the density of states. The large orbital contribution of I-5p, K-3p, In-5p, In-5s and Ga-4p states at the fermi energy level are seen in the PDOS. Both materials exhibit ductility and stiffness in their mechanical parameters and elastic coefficients. With the highest spectra recorded in the UV region at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1. 7 8} \times \text{1 0}^{\text{5}} \text{c m}^{-\mathrm{1}}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{3} \text{GaI}_{6}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.7 \times \text{1 0}^{\text{5}} \text{~ c m}^{-\mathrm{1}}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{3}} \text{I n I}_{\mathrm{6}}$</tex>, the absorption coefficient values begin in the NIR region. These findings suggest that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{K}_{3} \text{RI}_{6}$</tex> is a potentially mechanically stable material suitable for optoelectronic applications.

title: Real-Time Multi-Modal Drone and Bird Tracking Using Modality-Aware Adaptation and Physics-Aware Filtering
authors: Fabliha Labiba, Mohammad Asif Hasan, Sudipto Sarkar, Ashik Shahriar, Nahian Tasnim, Shaikh Anowarul Fattah
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Distinguishing drones from birds in surveillance systems poses a critical security challenge due to their similar flight characteristics. This study presents a multi-modal tracking framework exploiting complementary IR and RGB video streams with physics-aware parameter optimization. Using the IEEE ICIP 2025 VIP Cup dataset, modality-specific preprocessing has been implemented: IR employs thermal-aware ByteTrack tuning, while RGB integrates multi-layer pre-filtering with static object detection, size-based cloud rejection, and temporal confidence smoothing. Our fusion approach uses lightweight EGDYOLOv8n (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 3 M}$</tex> parameters) for 4-channel early fusion. Without ground truth tracking labels, evaluation relies on detection accuracy and processing performance. Results demonstrate 97.87 FPS real-time processing with 50 % error reduction versus uniform configurations, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0. 1 \%} \mathbf{~ m A P}$</tex> (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3. 8 \%}$</tex> improvement) and eliminating <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$60-80 \%$</tex> of false positives through intelligent filtering. The system shows practical viability for security and infrastructure protection.

title: Microplastics Detection Using Deep Learning Ensemble with Vision Language Models
authors: S M Asif Hossain, Ruksat Khan Shayoni, Israt Jahan Mohona, Fateha Jannat Ayrin, Md Mizanur Rahman, Madhusodan Chakraborty, Md Maruf Hasan Khondaker, Vishwanath Akuthota, Sahal Bin Saad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Microplastics pollution poses a significant environmental threat requiring accurate and efficient detection methods. This paper presents a novel ensemble approach combining deep learning models with Vision Language Models (VLMs) and traditional machine learning algorithms for microplastics classification, achieving unprecedented accuracy rates across multiple datasets. Our methodology integrates state-of-the-art Vision Transformers (EVA02-Large, ViT-Large, Swin-Large, BEiTLarge), advanced CNNs (EfficientNet-B7, ConvNeXt-XLarge), and traditional ML baselines (SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost) with comprehensive data augmentation, test-time augmentation (TTA), and stratified cross-validation strategies. We evaluated our hybrid approach on three comprehensive datasets: holographic imaging dataset, microplastic imaging dataset, and PS-PMMA comprehensive dataset. The Vision Language Model ensemble consistently outperformed traditional approaches, the complete hybrid ensemble with TTA achieved a an accuracy of 99.21% across all datasets. Our results demonstrate the superiority of Vision Transformers over conventional CNNs for microplastics detection, establishing new benchmarks for automated environmental monitoring systems.

title: Impact of Temperature-Dependent Mechanical Behavior on Perovskite Solar Cell Performance
authors: Ihtesham Ibn Malek, Koushik Sarkar, Samia Subrina
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study investigates the mechanical behavior of methylammonium lead iodide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MAPbI}_{3}$</tex>) perovskite material to evaluate the stability of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). We perform molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to analyze the stress-strain response, Young's modulus, and fracture characteristics at different temperatures. We find that the strain at maximum stress decreases from 8.29% at 350 K to 5.09% at 700 K, while Young's modulus decreases from 18.68 GPa to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12.31 \text{GPa}$</tex>, indicating stress-dependent defect formation. We use drift-diffusion simulations to examine the effects of these defects on the photovoltaic performance of PSCs. When the defect density increases 50-fold, the open-circuit voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{O C}$</tex>) drops by 13.93%, reflecting a reduction in the effective bandgap due to defect-induced states, and the power conversion efficiency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta$</tex>) declines by 19.10%, while the short-circuit current (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$J_{\text{SC}}$</tex>) remains nearly constant. The efficiency reduction intensifies at higher temperatures when defect density is elevated, indicating that defect-related recombination becomes increasingly detrimental under thermal stress. These findings show how closely mechanical degradation and photovoltaic performance are connected, and they emphasize the need to manage defects and optimize the structure to build durable, high-efficiency PSCs.

title: Performance Evaluation of Tomcat Across Oracle Linux Versions in a Legacy Environment
authors: Elisa Benetti, Gianluca Mazzini
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Public healthcare ICT infrastructures frequently integrate legacy and modern components, resulting in layered software stacks and heterogeneous operational environments. Due to regulatory constraints, long procurement cycles, and strict service-availability requirements, system upgrades must be justified by clear performance benefits. This paper presents an empirical evaluation of Apache Tomcat performance across three generations of Oracle Linux (OL6, OL8, OL9) and two Java runtime families (JDK 7 and JDK 17) within LepidaScpA, the regional ICT provider of Emilia-Romagna. Controlled JMeter workloads were executed on Tomcat 7 and Tomcat 10 to isolate the contributions of OS evolution, kernel and glibc improvements, and JVM modernization. Results show that OL9, when combined with JDK 17 and Tomcat 10, achieves up to 30% throughput improvement and 40-50%latency reduction relative to legacy configurations. Even older runtimes benefit from updated kernels, albeit to a lesser extent. The findings underscore the operational value of systematic, evidence-based modernization in public healthcare ICT systems.

title: LoRA-Efficient BERT for Arabic Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
authors: Asma Shayea, M.A. Mangoud
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) provides fine-grained understanding of customer opinions by identifying aspect terms and the sentiment associated with each aspect. Although transformer-based models have significantly improved Arabic ABSA, most existing studies rely on full finetuning, which requires updating all parameters of large encoders and leads to high computational cost in resourceconstrained environments. This paper presents a LoRAefficient BERT framework for Arabic ABSA, integrating Low-Rank Adaptation into a BERT-based token classification architecture with a BiLSTM and Conditional Random Field (CRF) decoding layer. A joint labeling scheme is used so that each token is labeled with both its aspect boundary and sentiment polarity in a single tag. Experiments on the SemEval-2016 Arabic Hotels dataset show that LoRA reduces training cost while achieving 89.33 percent <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{F1}$</tex> for Aspect Term Extraction and 89.60 percent accuracy for Aspect Polarity Classification, outperforming previously reported fully finetuned transformer baselines. The results demonstrate that parameter-efficient tuning is highly effective for Arabic ABSA and supports scalable real-world sentiment intelligence systems.

title: Predicting 4D Hand Trajectory from Monocular Videos
authors: Yufei Ye, Yao Feng, Omid Taheri, Haiwen Feng, Shubham Tulsiani, Michael J. Black
abstract: We present HaPTIC, an approach that infers coherent 4D hand trajectories from monocular videos. Current videobased hand pose reconstruction methods primarily focus on improving frame-wise 3D pose using adjacent frames rather than studying consistent 4D hand trajectories in space. Despite the additional temporal cues, they generally underperform compared to image-based methods due to the scarcity of annotated video data. To address these issues, we repurpose a state-of-the-art image-based transformer to take in multiple frames and directly predict a coherent trajectory. We introduce two types of lightweight attention layers: cross-view self-attention to fuse temporal information, and global cross-attention to bring in larger spatial context. Our method infers 4D hand trajectories similar to the ground truth while maintaining strong 2 D reprojection alignment. We apply the method to both egocentric and allocentric videos. It significantly outperforms existing methods in global trajectory accuracy while being comparable to the state-of-the-art in single-image pose estimation.

title: Dense Motion Captioning
authors: Shiyao Xu, Benedetta Liberatori, Gül Varol, Paolo Rota
abstract: Recent advances in 3D human motion and language integration have primarily focused on text-to-motion generation, leaving the task of motion understanding relatively unexplored. We introduce Dense Motion Captioning, a novel task that aims to temporally localize and caption actions within 3D human motion sequences. Current datasets fall short in providing detailed temporal annotations and predominantly consist of short sequences featuring few actions. To overcome these limitations, we present the Complex Motion Dataset (CompMo), the first large-scale dataset featuring richly annotated, complex motion sequences with precise temporal boundaries. Built through a carefully designed data generation pipeline, CompMo includes 60,000 motion sequences, each composed of multiple actions ranging from at least two to ten, accurately annotated with their temporal extents. We further present DEMO, a model that integrates a large language model with a simple motion adapter, trained to generate dense, temporally grounded captions. Our experiments show that DEMO substantially outperforms existing methods on CompMo as well as on adapted benchmarks, establishing a robust baseline for future research in 3D motion understanding and captioning.

title: Fundamental Limit of Discrete Distribution Estimation under Utility-Optimized Local Differential Privacy_supp1-3696567.pdf
authors: Si-Hyeon Lee
abstract: We study the problem of discrete distribution estimation under utility-optimized local differential privacy (ULDP), which enforces local differential privacy (LDP) on sensitive data while allowing more accurate inference on non-sensitive data. In this setting, we completely characterize the fundamental privacy–utility trade-off. The converse proof builds on several key ideas, including a generalized uniform asymptotic Cramér–Rao lower bound, a reduction showing that it suffices to consider a newly defined class of extremal ULDP mechanisms, and a novel distribution decomposition technique tailored to ULDP constraints. For the achievability, we propose a class of utility-optimized block design (uBD) schemes, obtained as nontrivial modifications of the block design mechanism known to be optimal under standard LDP constraints, while incorporating the distribution decomposition idea used in the converse proof and a score-based linear estimator. These results provide a tight characterization of the estimation accuracy achievable under ULDP and reveal new insights into the structure of optimal mechanisms for privacy-preserving statistical inference.

title: Low-Profile Dual-Band Dual-Polarized Base Station Antenna Based on a Scaled Glide-Symmetric AMC
authors: S. Mirhadi, Z. Shaterian, R. Bahri
abstract: This paper presents a novel low-profile dual-band, dual-polarized base-station antenna for simultaneous 4G and 5G operation. The antenna integrates a low-band (LB) element covering 1.85–2.6 GHz and four high-band (HB) elements covering 3.3–3.8 GHz in an interleaved configuration. A scaled glide-symmetric artificial magnetic conductor (AMC) surface is introduced to provide a near-zero-phase response in the LB–reducing the antenna profile–while acting as a highly reflective surface for the HB. This configuration enables compact integration of both bands without radiation pattern degradation. A fabricated prototype demonstrates good agreement between simulations and measurements, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed design.

title: A High-Sensitivity Quadrupole Topological Circuit Backend for Capacitive Ethanol Sensing
authors: Yuxi Jin, Taha Sheheryar, Hongxin Qi, Chao Gao, Bo Lv, Qiang Cheng, Jinhui Shi
abstract: Capacitive sensors offer a highly promising solution for ethanol sensing due to their low cost and low power consumption. However, conventional electronic components are susceptible to external interference, leading to measurement inaccuracies, while there is a growing demand for higher sensor sensitivity. This study theoretically proposes and simulates a high-sensitivity circuit backend for capacitive ethanol sensing based on a quadrupole topological circuit, demonstrating the potential for label-free detection of trace ethanol concentrations through monitoring cornerstate frequency shifts. The sensor integrates a glass-substrate interdigitated capacitor (IDC) as the sensing element, connected in parallel to the grounding terminal at the upper-left node of the circuit.When ethanol flows over the IDC, the resulting capacitance change (ΔC) significantly modulates the impedance peak frequency (f0) corresponding to the topological corner state. Theoretical analysis and simulations demonstrate: A linear relationship between corner-state frequency shift and IDC capacitance (38 Hz/fF), with sensitivity further improvable through optimization of unit inductance and capacitance;it maintains < 0.2% frequency-shift error under 60% capacitance perturbations at non-(1,1) nodes, while remaining highly sensitive to perturbations at the sensing node (1,1).The system exhibits high target sensitivity while maintaining strong stability against disturbances. Introduction of negative resistance (-3 Ω) effectively compensates for inductor parasitic resistance, significantly enhancing the quality factor (Q) compared to uncompensated operation.This design establishes a novel approach for chemical detection, synergizing the interference immunity of topological systems with circuit-integration scalability.

title: FDSNet: Frequency-Decoupled Stack Fusion Network for Light Field All-in-Focus Image Generation
authors: Mingrui Sun, Yuxuan Liu, Li Zhang, Linjun Lu, Yushan Sun, Haibin Ai, Ioannis Brilakis
abstract: All-in-focus(AIF) images, which contain comprehensive scene information with global sharpness, play a crucial role in high-precision light field (LF) measurement and computational imaging. However, generating AIF images from LF data typically requires accurate depth priors, which are often unavailable or unreliable in practice. To overcome this limitation, directly fusing a series of LF refocused images provides an effective alternative that eliminates the dependency on explicit depth estimation. Nevertheless, existing multi-focus image fusion(MFIF) methods are primarily designed for fusing image pairs with complementary focus, performing poorly when applied to stacks due to the error accumulation that occurs during iterative fusion. To this end, we propose a Frequency-Decoupled Stack Fusion Network (FDSNet) for high-precision depth-free LF AIF image generation. FDSNet incorporates a spatial-frequency joint feature extraction module that captures multi-scale spatial details while decoupling high- and low- frequency components to model textures and contextual information separately, thereby alleviating edge blurring caused by subtle focal variations and weak textures in transition regions. Moreover, a dual-stage cross-attention fusion module, following a coarse-to-fine strategy, suppresses artifacts, enhances edge fidelity, and enables simultaneous fusion of arbitrary numbers of refocused images, thereby avoiding error accumulation and computational redundancy. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real LF datasets demonstrate that FDSNet achieves superior visual quality and quantitative performance. Additional experiments further demonstrate that FDSNet performs robustly under varying low-light and noisy conditions. These results validate that FDSNet delivers excellent fusion capability in terms of image clarity, detail preservation, noise resistance, and generalization, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

title: IEEE Feedback
authors: N/A

title: An AIoT-based Computer Vision System for Post-larval Shrimp Detection and Counting in Aquaculture
authors: Yo-Ping Huang, Irgi Surya
abstract: The development of the Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) has created new opportunities for improving aquaculture management, particularly in shrimp hatchery operations. By integrating computer vision techniques with connected monitoring systems, AIoT technologies enable automated observation of post-larval shrimp populations. In this study, an AIoT-based computer vision system is proposed for detecting and counting post-larval shrimp in aquaculture environments. The proposed framework adopts a YOLO-based detection architecture enhanced with DarknetConv2D to improve feature extraction capability for very small shrimp objects that often appear densely distributed in hatchery conditions. Different from conventional YOLO-based detection approaches, the proposed system emphasizes an integrated AIoT deployment framework that combines lightweight detection, automated counting, edge computing, IoT communication, and remote monitoring. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed system, experiments were conducted under two operational scenarios, namely a waterslide scenario representing dynamic water flow conditions and a dam scenario representing stationary water environments. Quantitative results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves 99.1% detection accuracy, 94.8% of precision, 88.1% of recall, and an F1-score of 91.3%, with an mAP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</sub> of 98.6%. In addition, the model maintains a lightweight architecture with 16.2 M parameters while achieving a high inference speed of 185 FPS, enabling efficient deployment for automated monitoring systems. The proposed system demonstrates strong potential for improving counting accuracy, reducing manual labor, and supporting the development of intelligent aquaculture management systems.

title: State of the Union: Towards Reproducible Performance Evaluations in Federated Learning
authors: Ningxin Su, Baochun Li, Bo Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-motivated paradigm for distributed training of deep learning models, as it allows a large number of clients to collaboratively train a shared global model without centralizing their private data. Since its debut with Federated Averaging as the first server aggregation algorithm, many FL algorithms have been proposed to improve performance. Yet, these algorithms are rarely benchmarked and compared under the same open-source framework and controlled configurations, and their performance claims can be difficult to substantiate in fair and reproducible studies. In this paper, we evaluate a curated and representative collection of FL algorithms in the same open-source benchmarking framework so that they can be compared fairly at scale in a reproducible fashion. To achieve this objective, we present Plato, an open-source FL research framework that we have designed and implemented from scratch. With Plato, we evaluate and compare algorithms spanning (1) server aggregation; (2) client training customization; (3) client selection, in both synchronous and asynchronous settings; (4) personalized federated learning; and (5) communication efficiency and payload processing. Across diverse experimental scenarios (tasks, client populations, and data distributions), we report findings and practical insights, including pitfalls and confounding factors that can lead to misleading conclusions if not reported carefully. Under our unified experimental settings and time model, Federated Averaging with random client selection remains a strong baseline and is often competitive with more complex alternatives.

title: Adaptive Control of Heterogeneous Platoons With Guaranteed Collision Avoidance
authors: Ashutosh Chandra Pandey, Sayan Basu Roy, Simone Baldi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This work proposes a guaranteed collision avoidance framework for Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control of a vehicular platoon characterized by unidirectional communication and heterogeneous parameters. In the proposed framework, the actual (heterogeneous) platoon is made to converge to a reference (homogeneous) platoon via adaptive laws designed using of set-theoretic model reference adaptive control. Yet, in contrast to the state-of-art that is based on ensuring collision avoidance on the reference platoon dynamics only, the approach we propose can ensure collision avoidance on the actual platoon dynamics. This result is possible thanks to the introduction of a novel concept of virtual platoon, only used for analysis, but that does not interact with the actual platoon. The stability and convergence properties of the proposed framework are established using Lyapunov-based analysis in conjunction with the aforementioned virtual platoon concept. Simulation results further demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

title: Just Noticeable Difference of Impedance Parameters while Walking in an Ankle Exoskeleton
authors: Axl Maberry, Anne E. Martin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Exoskeletons have been used to augment or enhance human performance in a variety of settings. For exoskeletons to be used most effectively, researchers need to know how changing the control parameters affects human perception. This has been investigated for impedance parameters while in static positions, but has not yet been investigated while walking. This study investigated the ability of users to detect small changes in stimuli in exoskeleton impedance control parameters during locomotion. The just noticeable difference (JND) was calculated using a psychometric function by having subjects report when they were able to detect a stimulus change across a range of stiffness and ankle angle control rate magnitudes. The JND was approximately 42% for ankle stiffness and 49% for ankle angle control rate. This is over 3 times larger than the JND for impedance parameters in a static position. It is also possible that the JND is higher than reported. It is unclear if the reference stimulus affected the JND. Any learning that may have occurred over the course of the experiment did not seem to affect the ability to fit the JND. The increased JND may be caused by a variety of factors including increased cognitive load due to dual tasking and tactile gating. This suggests that these exoskeleton control parameters could be varied significantly without users being able to determine a difference, potentially allowing for additional parameters, such as metabolic cost, to be included in an optimization procedure.

title: Real-Time Seat-Based Sitting Action Recognition Using Soft Pressure Sensors and Embedded Machine Learning_supp2-3693752.mp4
authors: Chi Cuong Vu
abstract: Seat-based sitting behavior monitoring is an important research area due to its impact on spinal health, work performance, and long-term comfort. However, challenges remain in accuracy, stability, cost, deployment flexibility, and data privacy. This paper proposes an integrated system combining soft pressure sensors, embedded machine learning, and a flexible communication architecture. The pressure sensor, fabricated from conductive textile and Au film, achieves a sensitivity of up to 0.46 kPa⁻¹ with response/recovery times of approximately 60/50 ms. The compact machine learning model (4.34 KB) achieves 99.7% accuracy in recognizing six sitting actions, including trunk tilting and sitting transitions. All processing and inference are performed locally on the embedded board, with only classification results transmitted to the cloud to enhance data security. The system focuses on trunk tilt and sitting transition recognition rather than full-body ergonomic posture assessment. These results demonstrate the feasibility of real-time, resource-efficient monitoring of sitting actions on edge IoT devices. This pilot study used data from one participant, with a fixed chair and sensor setup in a single recording session. Therefore, the results show feasibility under controlled conditions, not generalizability across subjects.

title: Sketching the Dynamics of Traffic: Data-Efficient Predictive Connected Cruise Control
authors: Minghao Shen, Jeff Scruggs, Gábor Orosz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication enables connected cruise control by providing upstream traffic information beyond the line of sight. However, practical V2X deployments face challenges including packet loss, limited training data, and domain shifts between different driving conditions. In this paper, we propose a data-driven prediction framework that addresses these challenges through memory-efficient online adaptation. The ego vehicle predicts the speed of the vehicle immediately ahead based on the speeds of connected vehicles in the upstream traffic. We derive an input-output model from state-space car-following dynamics and develop a Gaussian sketching algorithm that enables efficient online adaptation, reducing per-step update complexity and memory compared to the full-dimension recursive least squares (RLS) algorithm. We validate the framework using real-world traffic data under challenging scenarios: cross-domain transfer, limited warmstart data, and V2X packet loss. The proposed Gaussian sketch method achieves a 34.4% improvement under input distribution shift, outperforming both non-adaptive and full RLS baselines. It also reaches up to 92.1% improvement with limited warmstart data. Crucially, the method maintains a 30.7% improvement even under 50% packet loss, demonstrating robustness to realistic V2X communication impairments.

title: Point-Spread-Function Guided HRRP Recognition Network: A Framework for Robust Target Classification Under ISRJ
authors: Guozheng Sun, Lei Wang, Yanhao Wang, Jie Wang, Yimin Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Radar automatic target recognition (RATR) based on high-resolution range profile (HRRP) has received extensive attention in recent years. Due to its ability to capture fine-grained structural features of targets, the HRRP enables reliable target classification and exhibits well adaptability to deep learning-based recognition frameworks. However, despite the remarkable progress of HRRP recognition techniques, target recognition under electronic countermeasures (ECM), especially under the mainstream interrupted-sampling repeater jamming (ISRJ), remains a significant challenge, where the HRRP suffers from serious feature distortion. To overcome this limitation, this paper introduces a HRRP recognition framework assisted by prior jamming information. Specifically, we propose a prior knowledge representation method based on the point spread function (PSF) to model the HRRP distortion induced by jamming. Based on this, the recognition framework incorporates a multi-scale feature extraction module designed to thoroughly capture HRRP features, a feature interaction module using a cross-attention mechanism that integrates HRRP features with prior jamming information, and a hybrid loss function that enhances the model's representation capability. With the aid of prior information, the model can learn invariant features within distorted HRRP under different jamming parameters. Evaluations on both simulated and measured datasets show that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art ones in all metrics. Moreover, it exhibits stronger generalization capabilities when facing unseen jamming parameters.

title: IEEE Security & Privacy
authors: N/A

title: IEEE Standard for the Electronic Reporting of Distribution Transformer Test Data
authors: N/A
abstract: A basis for the electronic reporting of transformer test data on liquid-immersed distribution transformers, as defined in the IEEE C57.12.2X, IEEE C57.12.3X, and IEEE C57.12.4X standards series, is provided in this standard. The specific set of test data to be reported, and the report format are detailed along with an extended set of data as an option for the user.

title: Mastery Learning Improves Performance on Complex Tasks on PCP Literacy Test
authors: Chandana Srinivas, Elif E. Firat, Robert S. Laramee, Alark P. Joshi
abstract: Developing literacy with unfamiliar data visualization techniques, such as parallel coordinate plots (PCPs), can be a significant challenge for students. We adopted the Revised Bloom’s taxonomy to instruct students on PCPs using Mastery Learning in the classroom. To evaluate Mastery Learning’s impact, we conducted an intervention in a data visualization course to teach students about PCPs with and without Mastery Learning. Based on our intervention, we found that while students in both groups performed similarly on the first two (Remember, Understand) modules, the students in the Mastery Learning group performed better on modules that required more advanced thinking (Analyze, Evaluate) and demonstrated a better comprehension of PCPs. We provide all the materials developed including a new, six-module Bloom's Taxonomy PCP literacy (BTPL) test for full reproducibility on our website at https://vis-graphics.github.io/PCP-Literacy-Test/.

title: Matrix-Free Second-Order Optimization of Gaussian Splats with Residual Sampling
authors: Hamza Pehlivan, Andrea Boscolo Camiletto, Lin Geng Foo, Marc Habermann, Christian Theobalt
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is widely used for novel view synthesis due to its high rendering quality and fast inference time. However, 3DGS predominantly relies on firstorder optimizers such as Adam, which leads to long training times. To address this limitation, we propose a novel second-order optimization strategy based on LevenbergMarquardt (LM) and Conjugate Gradient (CG), specifically tailored towards Gaussian Splatting. Our key insight is that the Jacobian in 3DGS exhibits significant sparsity since each Gaussian affects only a limited number of pixels. We exploit this sparsity by proposing a matrix-free and GPU-parallelized LM optimization. To further improve its efficiency, we propose sampling strategies for both camera views and loss function and, consequently, the normal equation, significantly reducing the computational complexity. In addition, we increase the convergence rate of the second-order approximation by introducing an effective heuristic to determine the learning rate that avoids the expensive computation cost of line search methods. As a result, our method achieves a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times$</tex> speedup over standard LM and outperforms Adam by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times$</tex> when the Gaussian count is low while providing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 1.3 x$</tex> speed in moderate counts. In addition, our matrix-free implementation achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times$</tex> speedup over the concurrent second-order optimizer 3DGSLM, while using <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.5 \times$</tex> less memory. Project Page: https://vcai.mpi-inf.mpg.de/projects/LM-RS

title: Layered Quantum Architecture Search for 3D Point Cloud Classification
authors: Natacha Kuete Meli, Jovita Lukasik, Vladislav Golyanik, Michael Moeller
abstract: We introduce layered Quantum Architecture Search (layered-QAS), a strategy inspired by classical network morphism that designs Parametrised Quantum Circuit (PQC) architectures by progressively growing and adapting them. PQCs offer strong expressiveness with relatively few parameters, yet they lack standard architectural layers (e.g., convolution, attention) that encode inductive biases for a given learning task. To assess the effectiveness of our method, we focus on 3D point cloud classification as a challenging yet highly structured problem. Whereas prior work on this task has used PQCs only as feature extractors for classical classifiers, our approach uses the PQC as the main building block of the classification model. Simulations show that our layered-QAS mitigates barren plateau, outperforms quantum-adapted local and evolutionary QAS baselines, and achieves state-of-the-art results among PQC-based methods on the ModelNet dataset <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>1project page: https://4dqv.mpi-inf.mpg.de/LQAS/.

title: Topic Modeling Violence Against Women in Bangladesh News: A Comparative Approach with LDA, Top2Vec, and BERTopic
authors: Ashiqur Rahman Saron, Hasin Almas Sifat, Soumodip Madhu, Koushik Biswas Arko, Faiza Mahmood Aroni, Md. Mortuza Ahmmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In Bangladesh, violence against women is one of the most significant social issues that continues to be underreported and under-represented by institutions. The conversation surrounding the gendered violence has not been adequately captured by surveys and other policy frameworks. Therefore, this study builds on the current body of methodological literature by offering a more comprehensive view of Violence Against Women using topic modeling methods; specifically, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), Top2Vec and BERTopic. The analysis utilized a set of 2,000 news articles published in the media that cover the topic of Female Violence as the data source for this study. The three models were evaluated according to their ability to produce coherent, interpretable, meaningful and context sensitive topics. The LDA model yielded topic categories at a high level; however, political news items were often combined with those focused on gender. The Top2Vec model formed context-specific clusters, but the need for proper names limited its generalizability. In contrast, the BERTopic model produced semantically rich and thematically distinct topics, including Campus Harassment, Dowry-related Violence, and Workplace Discrimination, resulting from the use of transformer embedding techniques. The comparison of all three models indicates that the BERTopic Model has greater capabilities than the LDA and Top2Vec Models with respect to capturing the subtle semantics of the relationships between the three models, and thus provides more usable data. Therefore, the findings of this study provide a new way for NLP-based topic modeling methods to be effective resources for growing researcher and policy decision maker awareness of systemic and gendered violence as well as enhancing the use of data-driven approaches for implementing solutions.

title: MangoLeafDTR: ResCANet for Mango Leaf Disease Detection with Rule-based Treatment Recommendation
authors: Anika Afrin, Md. Nahiduzzaman, Natasha Tanzila Monalisa, Shinthi Tasnim Himi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Mango farming faces seasonal leaf diseases that affect the quality and quantity of production. Usually, manual examination is used to diagnose various kinds of disease. Sometimes, due to a lack of early prediction of these diseases causes a lot of harm to the mango farmers. We introduce an AIdriven system to detect Bangladeshi mango leaf diseases and recommend treatments. Our model ResCANet is built on a custom convolutional neural network with residual block and channel attention mechanisms. It is trained with a primary dataset to detect mango leaf diseases such as Anthracnose, Sooty Mold, White Scale, and Healthy leaves. A rule-based system recommends treatments for farmers. While collecting the dataset, we mainly focused on the pre-farming time when the mango trees are flowering, as many kinds of diseases occur at that time. We collected the dataset throughout the whole months of March and April. At this time farmer manually inspects each disease and sprays medicine so that no diseases can harm the flowers. All recommendations are made under the close supervision of an agricultural expert and farmers. Our system is efficient for real-world use in farms. It achieved an accuracy of 96 %. This framework detects the problem and recommends treatment.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Micro-credentials: A Hyperledger Besu Framework for Academic Trust and Portability
authors: Abrar Mahbub Tanim, Humira Saria, Md. Foysal Hossain, Nafees Mansoor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Traditional education struggles to provide the agile, skill-focused qualifications demanded by today's evolving workforce. Micro-credentials offer a promising solution, yet challenges with recognition, seamless portability, and robust security hinder their widespread adoption. This paper introduces and systematically evaluates a decentralized system utilizing Hyperledger Besu to integrate secure, credit-bearing micro-credentials into academic frameworks. Leveraging smart contracts, the system establishes a resilient, tamper-proof framework for issuing and verifying digital credentials, thereby addressing critical needs for a more flexible and trustworthy learning paradigm. Performance evaluation comprehensively confirms the system's efficiency, demonstrating an average block creation time of 2.64 seconds and a mean block retrieval time of merely 3.30 ms. While initial tests showed consistent performance under low transaction loads, significant scalability challenges emerged under high concurrency, indicating crucial areas for future development and optimization. By bridging the gap between formal education and evolving industry needs, this research validates a robust blockchain-based pathway for significantly enhancing credential portability and fostering trust, paving the way for a new era of continuous lifelong learning and skill validation.

title: A Multi-Agent Quantum Chain of Thought Reasoning and Accuracy Accelerators Framework
authors: Arpita Dass, Sadia Sultana, Sadia Anjum Farea, Vishwanath Akuthota, Nobanul Hasan, Madhusodan Chakraborty, Md Maruf Hasan Khondaker, Ahmed Selim Anwar, Prosenjit Roy, Md Tanzim Reza
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: We present the Quantum Chain of Thought (QCoT) Reasoning and Accuracy Accelerators Framework, a novel multiagent quantum-classical hybrid architecture that significantly outperforms existing machine learning and LLM-based approaches in complex reasoning tasks. While recent studies show that fine-tuning LLMs like Llama, Gemma, and Mistral achieve modest improvements (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10-32 \%$</tex> accuracy gains) in sentiment analysis tasks, and deep learning approaches for image classification reach up to 68 % accuracy with optimization techniques, our quantum multi-agent framework demonstrates superior performance with 20.90 % accuracy improvement over baseline quantum models and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{4 5 - 6 0 \%}$</tex> improvement over traditional ML approaches. Our framework integrates three specialized quantum agentsa Structured Chain-of-Thought Quantum Agent implementing 5-step cognitive reasoning, a Semantic Analyzer Agent with multi-pass inference capabilities, and a Quantum Error Correction Agent utilizing surface code simulation. Unlike conventional single-agent quantum approaches that struggle with complex reasoning, our multi-agent orchestration enables quantum systems to handle intricate cognitive processes more effectively.

title: Cross Topology Multi-Class Fault Analysis in Multilevel Inverters: Topology Driven ML Performance
authors: Al Hamza, Mehrab Hussain Zuhan, Fatimah Tasneem Samiha, MD. Milon Khan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents a unified, data-driven fault classifier for two 5-level, three-phase inverter topologies; Neutral-Point-Clamped (NPC) and Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB). In Simulink, labeled data are produced during phasedisposition PWM in 37 classes (36 open circuit faults and Healthy). Each signal window (200 ms between 1 kHz to 10 kHz) was transformed into <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 4}$</tex> features spanning time-domain, frequency, wavelet, and topology-aware indicators. The dataset comprises <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 7 0 0}$</tex> labeled windows across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 7}$</tex> classes. SVM with the RBF kernel, Random Forrest, KNN and XGBoost are benchmarked on four practical models. A stratified validation with macro-F1 used as primary measure. Random Forest is ranked first on NPC (macro-F1 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.9099$</tex>; accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.9200$</tex>). SVM-RBF is the best method in terms of macro-F1 (0.8036) and accuracy (0.8198) in CHB. Confusion matrices indicate that the vast majority of errors are recall-limited and focused in a few small clusters of look-alike classes (neutral-point related to NPC, adjacent-cell for CHB). In general, the model selection is topology-related, Random Forest may be applied to NPC, whereas SVM-RBF to CHB. This study investigates fault diagnostics in industrial and renewable-energy multilevel inverters, focusing on how their distinct topologies influence fault behavior. It further analyzes how fault classes differ between NPC-based industrial systems and CHB-based renewable systems due to their inherent structural and operational characteristics.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Classical Filters and Conditional GANs for MRI Image Denoising
authors: Nourhen Yahyaoui, Selma Batti, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Noise in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) degrades image quality and adversely affects both clinical interpretation and subsequent signal processing based analysis. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of classical image denoising filters and a learning-based approach for MRI noise reduction. Specifically, Gaussian, median, and bilateral filtering techniques are analyzed alongside a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN). Experiments are conducted on the Brain Tumor Segmentation dataset, comprising 3,064 T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI slices from 233 patients. Performance is quantitatively assessed using standard signal processing metrics, including peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index (SSIM), mean squared error (MSE), mean absolute error (MAE), entropy, and sharpness. Among the classical methods, bilateral filtering achieves the highest PSNR (36.36 dB), the lowest MSE (0.000231), and the highest sharpness value (0.1431), while Gaussian filtering attains the best SSIM score (0.9514). The cGAN-based approach demonstrates competitive performance, yielding a PSNR of approximately 33.0 dB, an SSIM of 0.92, the lowest MAE (0.0130), and the highest entropy (13.99), indicating improved perceptual quality and effective noise suppression. These results highlight that while classical signal processing filters particularly bilateral filtering provide strong numerical performance, learning-based denoising offers complementary advantages in perceptual fidelity and detail preservation, supporting advanced MRI preprocessing pipelines.

title: Design and Techno-Environmental Assessment of a Semi-Transparent BIPV System: a Case Study in Barisal, Bangladesh
authors: Anika Ramisha, Miss Al Ive, Isha Das, Abu Talha Haque Miah, Roby Mohajon, Md Shabbir, Sakib Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bangladesh's rapid urbanization and constrained rooftop space underscore the necessity for alternative renewable energy solutions in buildings. Building-Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) offer both electricity generation and architectural advantages; however, large-scale implementations in the country remain infrequent. This study examines a mixed-use case in Barisal, Bangladesh, utilizing semi-transparent Onyx Solar glass modules in both commercial (floors 1-4) and residential (floors 5-8) sections. A 3D model was developed in SketchUp and analyzed using PVsyst to assess annual energy yield, performance ratio, shading losses, levelized cost of electricity (LCOE), and avoided <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> emissions. The system incorporates <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 7}$</tex> modules (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 4 4 ~ k W p}, \mathbf{1 1} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> transparency) in the commercial building envelope and 384 modules (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30.3 \text{kWp}, 43 \%$</tex> transparency) in the residential elevations. Simulation results indicate annual outputs of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3 4. 3 6 ~ M W h}$</tex> for the commercial section (PR 69.3%, LCOE 0.023 USD/kWh) and 18.22 MWh for the residential section (PR 76.7%, LCOE 0.081 USD/kWh). Collectively, the system offsets approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2,227.7 \text{tCO}_{2}$</tex> annually. The findings illustrate the viability of semitransparent BIPV for institutional buildings, effectively balancing daylighting with energy generation, and offer a transferable framework for sustainable urban design in South Asia.

title: Pachychoroid Detection Based on Artificial Intelligence Algorithms
authors: Mariam Khedhira, Hela Mahersia, Fadi Kacem, Ines Hachicha, Sarra Zaibi, Rim Bouraoui, Rim Limaiem
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Pachychoroid spectrum diseases are characterized by abnormal choroidal thickening and dilated pachyvessels, which can be observed on optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. Accurate diagnosis remains challenging and highly dependent on expert interpretation, particularly when distinguishing between different pachychoroid subtypes. This study proposes an automated approach based on deep learning for the detection and multiclass classification of pachychoroidrelated pathologies using OCT B-scan images. A comprehensive pipeline was developed, including automatic region extraction, grayscale conversion, denoising, contrast enhancement using CLAHE, contour enhancement, and data augmentation. Several convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures pretrained on ImageNet-namely VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, Xception, and MobileNetV2-were evaluated for both binary (healthy vs. diseased) and multiclass classification. The dataset comprised OCT images labeled into six pachychoroid-related categories (PPE, PNV, PPS, CRSC, NAT1, and non-complicated pachychoroid) as well as healthy. Experimental results demonstrate that deep learning models, particularly VGG16, achieve high classification performance in both binary and multiclass settings, with robust accuracy between pachychoroid subtypes. These findings highlight the potential of deep learning-based analysis of OCT images as a reliable and objective tool to assist clinicians in diagnosing and characterizing pachychoroid spectrum diseases.

title: An Electrical Model for Software Defined Radio Sub-THz Systems
authors: Chady Geagea, Ali Al Hadi Tlais, Sara Abou Chakra, Gunes Karabulut Kurt
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Sub-Terahertz (Sub-THz) communication is a key candidate for future wireless systems due to the availability of large bandwidths. However, it suffers from severe propagation loss, and is highly sensitive to hardware and alignment impairments. This paper presents an end-to-end virtual Software-Defined Radio (SDR) testbed operating at 180 GHz, implemented in MATLAB/Simulink. The system integrates QPSK baseband framing, synchronization, and equalization with a hardware-aware RF chain based on an AD9361 intermediate-frequency transceiver and cascaded frequency extension to the sub-THz band. Directional horn antennas and a multipath fading channel are included to model realistic propagation effects. Simulation results validate correct frame recovery and show the impact of antennas misalignment as well as fading on the received spectrum. After digital compensation, the receiver achieves stable constellation recovery with low error vector magnitude and bit-error rate, demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed SDR-based sub-THz system model.

title: 3D Printed Microstrip Array Antenna Loaded with Dielectric Resonators for Frequency Agility Applications
authors: Layan T. AbuRayyan, Raneem F. Abdelrahim, Jana M. AlNatour, Yanal S. Faouri
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: In this paper, a 3D-printed frequency-reconfigurable dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) is proposed for wireless applications. The design consists of two substrates and a ground plane between them, with a power divider and two cylindrical DRAs elements. The frequency behavior of the designed antenna is controlled by utilizing two PIN diodes to achieve four different frequency agility states, which provide different bands with different resonant frequencies below 14 GHz and multiple bandwidths with fractional frequencies varying between 1.528 % and 6.7720 %. Consequently, this design is suitable for various wireless applications.

title: Noise Benchmarking and Denoising Evaluation on the ENID Medical Imaging Dataset
authors: Yosr Haddad, Hela Mahersia
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Medical image denoising remains a a critical step for reliable subsequent analyses, with its performance critically dependent on the noise characteristics specific to each imaging modality and acquisition system. This work provides a comprehensive evaluation of state of the art denoising methods classical filters, supervised, self-supervised and deep learning approaches on the ENID medical imaging dataset. We assess performance under both <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma$</tex>-specific and blind settings and report PSNR/SSIM as well as robustness across noise levels. Experimental results indicate that supervised denoising achieves the highest reconstruction quality (PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=37.96 \text{dB}$</tex>, SSIM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.973$</tex>), whereas self-supervised Noise2Void offers a viable alternative when clean reference images are unavailable. Among classical approaches, median filtering demonstrates the best overall averages (PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=32.49 \text{dB}$</tex>, SSIM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.815$</tex>). The study emphasizes the sensitivity of deep learning models to accurate noise-level estimation and underscores the necessity of modality-specific evaluation. This benchmark provides a comprehensive perspective on denoising performance in the ENID dataset and informs the selection of appropriate restoration strategies in medical imaging applications.

title: Real-Time Forest Fire Risk Assessment in Tunisia: Integrating LoRaWAN IoT with Machine Learning and FWI
authors: Rania Maaoui, Karim Fathallah, Olfa Dridi, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Forest fire risk prediction is a critical challenge in environmental monitoring, particularly in Mediterranean ecosystems increasingly affected by extreme climatic conditions. This paper presents an operational forest fire prediction framework based on machine learning for estimating the Fire Weather Index (FWI), a widely used indicator of fire danger derived from meteorological variables. Several regression models were trained and evaluated using a historical fire-weather dataset from the UCI Machine Learning Repository to identify the most accurate predictive approach. Following comprehensive preprocessing, feature selection, and hyperparameter optimization, tree-based ensemble models especially XGBoost demonstrated strong predictive performance and robust generalization capability. The trained model was subsequently integrated into a real time prediction system that combines a LoRaWAN-enabled IoT sensing infrastructure with a web-based deployment platform. Real-time environmental measurements collected by IoT sensors are processed using the same preprocessing pipeline and fed into the trained model to enable continuous online FWI estimation. The proposed framework demonstrates the practical feasibility of deploying optimized machine learning models for real-time forest fire risk monitoring and early warning applications.

title: Reliability Analysis of UAV-Assisted Urban FSO Backhaul Under Joint Weather, Turbulence, and Pointing Errors
authors: Amani Chouikhi, Maha Sliti
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Urban free-space optical (FSO) backhaul can provide fiber-like throughput with rapid deployment, but its reliability is limited by concurrent atmospheric and platform-induced impairments. This paper presents a unified evaluation framework for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-assisted urban FSO links under visibility-dependent extinction, Gamma-Gamma turbulence fading, and statistical pointing/misalignment loss due to residual tracking errors and jitter. The received optical power model combines geometric collection, ITU-R parameterized attenuation, and the Farid-Hranilovic pointing-error model, and an IM/DD receiver with thermal and shot noise is considered under representative background-light regimes. Reliability is quantified via received-power/SNR statistics, outage probability (with an SNR threshold consistent with the target BER), and BER, obtained through numerical evaluation and parameter sweeps over range, visibility, turbulence, and jitter. Results indicate that visibility largely governs the mean SNR decay with distance, whereas turbulence and pointing errors shape the low-SNR tail that drives outage and BER, reducing design margins when stabilization authority is limited. The framework enables consistent comparison across urban regimes and supports design-oriented reliability envelopes for UAV-enabled optical backhaul.

title: Machine Learning-Based Analysis of Academic Performance Factors Using Explainable AI
authors: Nasrin Sultana, Samia Tabassum Hrity, Ispak Jahan Ispa, Shah Tanzeem Afsar, Md Jalal Uddin Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Today's students are tomorrow's architects of tomorrow, the revolutionaries, innovators, and achievers of the future global shifts. Learning how to read the world's shifting tides is something that education teaches. Beyond instruction, education focuses on creating a transparent society based on equity, critical thinking, and ethical values among pupils, equipping them to handle the responsibilities of the future. Youth who receive an education are better equipped to question social mores and superstitions. As education plays a vital role for the future generation, the performance in it is also important. This paper explores the prediction of student performance using machine learning. By keeping that in mind we think the report result could be very useful for the students and their mentors about how they should lead their life to get the proper student performance we have focused on each of the students age, gender, group, educational background (academic history, learning behavior, study hour, attendance, and material interaction), assessment related performance, lifestyle, and attitude to study and sleep cycle, source of entertainment and if they are satisfied about the result according to their lifestyles. This study aims to predict whether lifestyle can change the academic performance of students. In order to obtain the results, we surveyed 300 students from middle school, undergraduate, and graduate levels. We also collected a dataset for the prediction from Kaggle with a record of 1000 students. In this study, we used different ML techniques. Among these, the SVM model demonstrated the highest performance, achieving an accuracy of 95.49 %, a test ROC-AUC of 0.992, and a CV mean ROC-AUC of 0.985. On the other hand, the Decision Tree model had the lowest performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 0. 2 8 \%}$</tex>. As we can see, the results are very positive, but in the future, if anybody works on the same topic, we would like to advise them to observe with a bigger dataset.

title: On Interpretation and Feature Extraction of Micro-Doppler Response in Reflected Global Navigation Satellite System Signals to Dynamic Sea Surface
authors: Feng Wang, Chuanrui Tan, Weichen Sun, Xiangchao Ma, Faping Lu, ZhiChao Xu, Linfeng Wang, Jie Li, Lei Yang, Bo Zhang, Dongkai Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Conventional coastal Global Navigation Satellite System-Reflectometry (GNSS-R) for sea state retrieval relies on the decorrelation of GNSS signals caused by rough sea surfaces, lacking the capacity to capture wave motion. This paper demonstrates the detectability of wave motion. Although specular reflection is insensitive to the horizontal component of wave motion, it remains modulated by the vertical wave motion, resulting in a weak time-varying oscillation in the Doppler frequency, referred to as micro-Doppler. A Doppler spectrum, which is related to wave height spectrum, can be derived from the micro-Doppler time series. Besides wave modulation, reflected GNSS signals contain certain low-frequency interferences. To mitigate such interference and reliably extract the micro-Doppler component, a novel method combining dynamic programming and empirical mode decomposition is proposed. Some characteristics of the micro-Doppler are defined to quantize its dependencies on wind speed, wind direction, wave direction, swell, and mean wave period. A significant finding lies in the existence of off-specular scattering in GNSS-R forward geometry, thereby establishing a physical basis for wind direction retrieval. The experimentally obtained relationships between these characteristics and ocean parameters agree well with theoretical predictions, confirming the modulation of wave motion on reflected GNSS signals. The wave height spectrum is derived from the Doppler spectrum. Wind speed, wind direction, significant wave height and mean wave period are retrieved, with root mean square errors (RMSEs) of 2.00 m/s, 32.0°, 0.50 m and 1.5 s, respectively.

title: An Improved Ultra-Local Model-Based for Model-Free Speed and Current Control of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Yifei Yang, Xiaodong Sun, Zhihui Huang, Teng Li
abstract: To enhance the performance of the speed loop and current loop of permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), this article proposes a model-free sliding mode speed controller (MFSMSC) and a model-free predictive current controller (MFPCC) based on an improved ultra-local model. First, the conventional MFPCC with an extended state observer (ESO) based on the ultra-local model significantly improves the robustness against motor parameter mismatches. However, in the conventional MFPCC, the estimated disturbance term includes both linear terms with system states and unknown nonlinear terms, which leads to inaccuracies in the observation results. Therefore, a new ultra-local model is proposed to improve the estimation accuracy of disturbances. In addition, an improved sliding mode observer (SMO) is used instead of the ESO to enhance the high-frequency noise suppression performance. On the other hand, to improve the dynamic performance and robustness of the speed loop, a MFSMSC is proposed to replace the conventional proportional-integral (PI) control. Comparative experiments are conducted on a surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM) test platform to verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed control method.

title: A Threat Assessment Method for Air Targets Considering Spatiotemporal Interactions Among Multiple Targets
authors: Zhouwei Lou, Weiyi Ge, Ke Xie, Fei Gong, Kanjian Zhang, Shuo Shan, Haikun Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Timely and accurate threat assessment of enemy targets is crucial for enhancing command and decision-making capabilities. In modern combat systems, multi-target cooperation and confrontation are widespread, leading to significant spatio-temporal interactions among targets. However, existing methods still focus primarily on assessing single enemy targets and fail to adequately capture the interdependencies among multiple targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multi-target-to- multi-target threat assessment framework. Within this framework, a graph neural network model considering spatiotemporal interactions among multiple targets (STIGNN) is proposed, which incorporates an innovatively designed multi-dimensional spatial feature fusion module and an improved temporal convolutional network, used to model complex spatial interaction relationships and temporal dynamics, respectively.
