Current models underrepresent Arabic-spoken by over 420 million people-with dialectal variants receiving less than 2% of typical training corpora. This paper proposes a comprehensive framework for building sovereign foundation models tailored to the MENA context. We address three interconnected challenges: (1) a federated data governance architecture enabling cross-border collaboration while maintaining national sovereignty over sensitive data; (2) efficient training strategies leveraging Chinchilla-optimal paradigms and state space model architectures that reduce computational costs by an estimated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 0 - 7 0 \%}$</tex> compared to standard Transformer-based approaches; and (3) MENA-Bench, a culturally-aligned evaluation framework spanning five major Arabic dialect groups, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2,000+$</tex> regional knowledge tasks, and bias assessment protocols. We demonstrate feasibility through a case study on cultural heritage preservation, showing that a 7B parameter model trained on 500B tokens can achieve promising performance at an estimated cost of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\$\mathbf{1 5 0 -}$</tex> 300 K -within reach of regional institutions. These projections are forward-looking estimates; empirical validation on deployed systems remains a critical next step. Our framework provides a strategic roadmap for MENA nations to achieve technological autonomy while fostering collaboration and protecting cultural identity.

title: Design and Deployment of a Minimal Federated Learning Prototype for IoT Devices
authors: Sajjad Abdulla, Mohab Mangoud
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed devices without requiring centralized data, making it ideal for Internet of Things (IoT) applications. However, deploying FL frameworks such as Flower on heterogeneous, low-power devices often leads to compatibility issues and heavy dependencies. This paper presents a lightweight, framework-free FL system implemented using raw socket communication between Raspberry Pi clients and a centralized server. The system features a custom length-prefixed protocol for transmitting model weights, supports warm-start training, and performs manual aggregation without external libraries. To demonstrate the system, we trained a binary motion detection classifier using temporal features (day and hour) across two Raspberry Pi devices. A centralized baseline model was trained using the combined datasets and the same total number of training epochs. Over 10 federated rounds, the model reached 65.1% accuracy with just 0.04 MB of communication, while the centralized model achieved 66.8% accuracy. The results confirm the feasibility of deploying FL on resource-constrained edge devices without sacrificing model performance. The proposed system offers a practical, reproducible foundation for future research in privacypreserving and decentralized machine learning on IoT hardware.

title: AI-Assisted Emergency Dispatch for Smart Cities: A Multi-Source Data Fusion Approach
authors: Christophe Guyeux
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Emergency call centers (112/911) remain isolated from Smart City ecosystems, despite the potential for urban sensors, traffic data, and IoT infrastructure to dramatically improve emergency response. This paper presents an integration architecture that fuses voice-based emergency calls with real-time urban data streams for enhanced dispatch decisions. We propose a bidirectional API framework connecting Next-Generation 112 (NG112) platforms with Smart City data hubs, enabling situational awareness enrichment and dynamic resource allocation. Our architecture incorporates a Fusion Engine that aggregates call transcriptions, caller geolocation, fleet GPS positions, traffic conditions, and environmental sensors into a unified operational picture. A Decision Support module recommends optimal resource deployment based on incident severity, proximity, and predicted travel times. Preliminary evaluation on simulated scenarios demonstrates reduced response times and improved resource utilization compared to traditional dispatch approaches.

title: Anomaly Detection and Exceedance Forecasting in IoT Sensor Networks: An MLOPS Pipeline Approach
authors: Vipin Kataria, Nitin Kumar, Vishal Bharadwaj Meruga
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Anomaly detection and exceedance forecasting in distributed IoT sensor networks remain open challenges: standalone threshold alerting produces excessive false alarms, deep learning detectors operate in isolation from forecasting modules, and existing MLOps frameworks do not account for the data quality heterogeneity, node dropout, and seasonal model drift that characterize long-running real-world deployments. This paper presents a six-layer end-to-end MLOps pipeline that unifies automated data quality gating, multi-sensor feature fusion with a pluggable domain-specific context feature, and a three-model ensemble of Isolation Forest, LSTM-Autoencoder, and XGBoost with a Population Stability Index (PSI) driven automated retraining loop. The architecture is sensor-agnostic and applicable to any IoT network with scalar measurement thresholds. We validate on a large-scale regional gas monitoring network using the EPA Air Quality System (AQS) public dataset, providing realworld heterogeneity, ground-truth exceedance labels, and an 18-month evaluation horizon. The full ensemble achieves anomaly detection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F} 1=0.91$</tex> and false alarm rate 3.1%, versus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F} 1=0.78$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F A R} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{1 4. 2 \%}$</tex> for the industry Z-score baseline, with sub200 ms end-to-end detection latency and accurate 30-minute exceedance forecasting. A full ablation study confirms the threemodel ensemble is jointly necessary to achieve optimal F1 and minimum false alarm rate simultaneously, and node-level analysis demonstrates graceful <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> degradation across heterogeneous network conditions, confirming robustness to real-world node imperfection.

title: Secure Coded Caching in Wireless Networks
authors: Batoul Zghaib, Yasser Fadlallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The growth of mobile data traffic intensifies challenges of congestion and latency. While coded caching enhances efficiency by enabling a single multicast transmission to satisfy multiple user requests, most existing schemes overlook critical data confidentiality. This research presents a secure coded caching framework that integrates multicast-aware encryption and per-user key distribution directly into the multicast process, maintaining efficiency while enabling precise access control. Evaluated under diverse network conditions, the framework demonstrates that strong confidentiality can be achieved with negligible impact on bandwidth efficiency and preserved multicast gains. This favorable trade-off, combined with compatibility for dynamic groups, makes the framework a practical, scalable solution for secure content delivery in 5G, IoT, and edge networks.

title: GazeD: Context-Aware Diffusion for Accurate 3D Gaze Estimation
authors: Riccardo Catalini, Davide Di Nucci, Guido Borghi, Davide Davoli, Lorenzo Garattoni, Giampiero Francesca, Yuki Kawana, Roberto Vezzani

title: Recovering 3D Shapes from Ultra-Fast Motion-Blurred Images
authors: Fei Yu, Shudan Guo, Shiqing Xin, Beibei Wang, Haisen Zhao, Wenzheng Chen
abstract: We consider the problem of 3D shape recovery from ultra-fast motion-blurred images. While 3D reconstruction from static images has been extensively studied, recovering geometry from extreme motion-blurred images remains challenging. Such scenarios frequently occur in both natural and industrial settings, such as fast-moving objects in sports (e.g., balls) or rotating machinery, where rapid motion distorts object appearance and makes traditional 3D reconstruction techniques like Multi-View Stereo (MVS) ineffective. In this paper, we propose a novel inverse rendering approach for shape recovery from ultra-fast motion-blurred images. While conventional rendering techniques typically synthesize blur by averaging across multiple frames, we identify a major computational bottleneck in the repeated computation of barycentric weights. To address this, we propose a fast barycentric coordinate solver, which significantly reduces computational overhead and achieves a speedup of up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.57 \times$</tex>, enabling efficient and photorealistic simulation of high-speed motion. Crucially, our method is fully differentiable, allowing gradients to propagate from rendered images to the underlying 3D shape, thereby facilitating shape recovery through inverse rendering. We validate our approach on two representative motion types: rapid translation and rotation. Experimental results demonstrate that our method enables efficient and realistic modeling of ultra-fast moving objects in the forward simulation. Moreover, it successfully recovers 3D shapes from 2D imagery of objects undergoing extreme translational and rotational motion, advancing the boundaries of vision-based 3D reconstruction.

title: Follow My Hold: Hand-Object Interaction Reconstruction Through Geometric Guidance
authors: Ayce Idil Aytekin, Helge Rhodin, Rishabh Dabral, Christian Theobalt
abstract: We propose a novel diffusion-based framework for reconstructing 3D geometry of hand-held objects from monocular RGB images by leveraging hand-object interaction as geometric guidance. Our method conditions a latent diffusion model on an inpainted object appearance and uses inference-time guidance to optimize the object reconstruction, while simultaneously ensuring plausible hand-object interactions. Unlike prior methods that rely on extensive post-processing or produce low-quality reconstructions, our approach directly generates high-quality object geometry during the diffusion process by introducing guidance with an optimization-in-the-loop design. Specifically, we guide the diffusion model by applying supervision to the velocity field while simultaneously optimizing the transformations of both the hand and the object being reconstructed. This optimization is driven by multi-modal geometric cues, including normal and depth alignment, silhouette consistency, and 2D keypoint reprojection. We further incorporate signed distance field supervision and enforce contact and non-intersection constraints to ensure physical plausibility of hand-object interaction. Our method yields accurate, robust and coherent reconstructions under occlusion while generalizing well to in-the-wild scenarios. The project page is at https://aidilayce.github.io/FollowMyHold-page/.

title: Broadening View Synthesis of Dynamic Scenes from Constrained Monocular Videos
authors: Le Jiang, Shaotong Zhu, Yedi Luo, Shayda Moezzi, Sarah Ostadabbas
abstract: In dynamic Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) systems, state-of-the-art novel view synthesis methods often fail under significant viewpoint deviations, producing unstable and unrealistic renderings. To address this, we introduce Expanded Dynamic NeRF (ExpanDyNeRF), a monocular NeRF framework that leverages Gaussian splatting priors and a pseudo-ground-truth generation strategy to enable realistic synthesis under large-angle rotations. ExpanDyNeRF optimizes density and color features to improve scene reconstruction from challenging perspectives. We also present the Synthetic Dynamic Multiview (SynDM) dataset, which is the first synthetic multiview dataset for dynamic scenes with explicit side-view supervision, created using a custom GTA V-based rendering pipeline. Quantitative and qualitative results on SynDM and real-world datasets demonstrate that ExpanDyNeRF significantly outperforms existing dynamic NeRF methods in rendering fidelity under extreme viewpoint shifts. Further details are provided in the supplementary materials. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/ExpanDyNeRF.

title: GIGA: Generalizable Sparse Image-Driven Gaussian Humans
authors: Anton Zubekhin, Heming Zhu, Paulo Gotardo, Thabo Beeler, Marc Habermann, Christian Theobalt
abstract: Driving a high-quality and photorealistic full-body virtual human from a few RGB cameras is a challenging problem that has become increasingly relevant with emerging virtual reality technologies. A promising solution to democratize such technology would be a generalizable method that takes sparse multi-view images of any person and then generates photoreal free-view renderings of them. However, the state-of-the-art approaches are not scalable to very large datasets and, thus, lack diversity and photorealism. To address this problem, we propose GIGA, a novel, generalizable full-body model for rendering photoreal humans in free viewpoint, driven by a single-view or sparse multi-view video. Notably, GIGA can scale training to a few thousand subjects while maintaining high photorealism and synthesizing dynamic appearance. At the core, we introduce a MultiHeadUNet architecture, which takes an approximate RGB texture accumulated from a single or multiple sparse views and predicts 3D Gaussian primitives represented as 2D texels on top of a human body mesh. At test time, our method performs novel view synthesis of a virtual 3D Gaussian-based human from 1 to 4 input views and a tracked body template for unseen identities. Our method excels over prior works by a significant margin in terms of identity generalization capability and photorealism.

title: Disturbance Estimation for the Control of Web Tension in Electrode Calendering
authors: Lisa Wünderlich, Andreas Rauh
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This study presents a control method for electrode web tension in the context of battery manufacturing, which integrates a state and disturbance observer. This optimization method is applied to the calendering process of electrodes, in order to estimate non-measurable state variables. Desired web tensions before and after calendering are defined as set values, and are obtained by controlling the respective velocity inputs of the calender rolls and nip rolls. For the controller design, the nonlinear dynamic system model is linearized. The results show that this approximation approach for the controller is able to estimate the disturbance of the electrode web tension due to the calendering force. The methodology described in this paper can serve as a baseline approach for state-feedback control and disturbance estimation in the framework of digital twin integration with the aim to optimize process workflows in various battery manufacturing stages.

title: One-Step Diffusion Model for Dark Burst Image Enhancement
authors: Mehmet Sarıgül, Levent Karacan, Mahir Atmış
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Robust visual perception in extreme low-light environments is a fundamental requirement for autonomous robotic systems operating in unstructured or nighttime conditions. However, conventional enhancement methods often introduce significant computational latency, hindering real-time decision-making and control. This paper presents a high-efficiency dark burst image enhancement framework leveraging the speed of one-step diffusion models. By adapting the Stable Diffusion Turbo architecture to process multi-frame sequences, we achieve rapid image restoration without the iterative overhead of standard diffusion processes. Validation on the Sony SID dataset demonstrates that our method provides a state-of-the-art LPIPS score of 0.221 while maintaining structural fidelity with a PSNR of 29.22 and an SSIM of 0.87. The proposed approach significantly improves perceptual clarity and detail preservation, offering a computationally viable solution for enhancing the situational awareness of robots in lowvisibility environments.

title: Comparative Analysis of Low-Cost Indoor Localization Methods for Swarm Robotics
authors: Van Cuong Chu, Hoang Anh Pham, Trung Hieu Nguyen, Thierry Soriano, Valentin Gies
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Knowing exactly where robots are indoors is very important for swarm robots, especially when GPS signals are unavailable. This paper compares three low-cost methods for indoor localization: 2D LiDAR sensors, RGB cameras, and UltraWideband (UWB) sensors. We tested each method in an indoor environment to understand how well they support swarm robot tasks. The 2D LiDAR sensors showed good accuracy when many obstacles were present, but performed poorly in areas without clear landmarks. RGB camera localization was inexpensive and allowed robots to create maps and locate themselves simultaneously (visual SLAM). However, this method had difficulties in poor lighting conditions or in places where visual features were unclear. UWB provided stable and precise localization independent of lighting conditions, although it needed careful setup and faced accuracy issues in areas with many signal reflections. We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each method regarding accuracy, reliability, and cost. This study provides practical guidance for choosing suitable localization methods for indoor swarm robotics applications.

title: Quaternion-Based Orientation Filter Benchmarking with 9-DOF MARG Sensors: Evaluation via Robot-Guided Motion and MoCAP
authors: Burak Tantay, Hakan Temeltaş
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Accurate orientation estimation using 9-DOF Magnetic, Angular Rate, and Gravity (MARG) sensors is essential for robotics, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and motion analysis. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of six quaternion-based orientation filters: Complementary filter, Madgwick filter, 6-DOF and 9-DOF Error-State Kalman Filters (ESKF), the proprietary XKF3i, and the Versatile Quaternion-Based Filter (VQF). Experiments were conducted on a UR3 collaborative robot carrying an Xsens MTi-300 sensor, with reference orientation provided by an OptiTrack motion capture system. Eight robot-guided scenarios, ranging from static drift to dynamic multi-axis motions, were executed to ensure repeatability and systematic benchmarking. Performance was assessed using Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) between estimated and ground-truth quaternions. Results show that VQF provides stable estimates across diverse conditions with minimal tuning, XKF3i achieves high accuracy due to proprietary real-time fusion, and Kalman-based filters perform reliably under moderate dynamics but require parameter adaptation. The study highlights the advantages of robot-guided benchmarking for filter evaluation and identifies VQF and Kalman-based approaches as particularly suited for robotics applications requiring robust and precise orientation tracking.

title: Call for Articles: IT Professional
authors: N/A

title: Reviewers
authors: N/A

title: Self-Expressive High-Order Tensor Unrolling Network for Unsupervised Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
authors: He Wang, Yang Xu, Zhihui Wei, Zebin Wu
abstract: Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HMF) enhances spatial-spectral quality by fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI). Although recent fusion methods have shown promise in preserving the multi-mode structure of high-dimensional data, existing fusion methods still face some challenges. For tensor-based approaches, conventional mode-wise decomposition, such as order-3 CP or Tucker decomposition, may disrupt intrinsic spatial consistency. Furthermore, although deep learning exhibits powerful feature representation ability, existing deep fusion methods either rely on ‘black-boxed’ deep fusion networks remain insufficiently interpretability with large training data. To address these issues, a novel Self-Expressive High-Order Tensor Unrolling Network (SHOTUN) is proposed for unsupervised HSI-MSI fusion. Within the sparse core tensor decomposition framework, we introduce the intrinsic self-expressive relationships among overlapping image patches as a form of high-order mode representation to preserve spatial structure of the fusion model. During optimization, we adopt an alternative optimizing strategy and design dedicated modules for each sub-problem, yielding an interpretable end-to-end training pipeline. Furthermore, to improve generalization across different sensors, we introduce a pre-training strategy into the unsupervised training for the more accurate estimation of unknown degraded parameters. Extensive experimental results on simulated and real datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Shawn-H-Wang/SHOTUN.

title: Complexity-Aware Comparison of Bounded-Domain Models for Quantum Noise Effects in Amplitude Encoding
authors: Ola Al-Ta’ani
abstract: This paper presents a complexity-aware comparison of bounded-domain statistical models for normalized perturbations induced by quantum noise in amplitude-encoded images. Because these perturbations lie on the unit interval and often exhibit non-Gaussian behavior, bounded-domain modeling provides a natural framework for their statistical characterization. We compare six candidate distributions: the one-parameter Power and Topp–Leone models, the classical two-parameter Beta and Kumaraswamy models, and the two-parameter Modified Kumaraswamy (MK) and Reflected Modified Kumaraswamy (RMK) models. The comparison is conducted under a matched experimental design involving eight benchmark images, three standard completely positive trace-preserving noise channels, and four noise levels. Model performance is evaluated using complementary distributional, likelihood-based, information-criterion, and runtime-based criteria. The results reveal a clear complexity–accuracy trade-off. The one-parameter Power and Topp–Leone models are computationally simple but generally too restrictive to capture the observed perturbation behavior, especially when the empirical distributions broaden. Among the two-parameter models, RMK achieves the strongest overall distributional agreement under the primary Wasserstein distance W1 and the supporting Kullback–Leibler divergence and mean absolute error criteria. Beta remains a consistently competitive and computationally efficient alternative, while Kumaraswamy is useful in selected condition-level regimes. The comparison also shows that likelihood-based improvements do not always translate into stronger global distributional agreement under W1. Mixed-effects and pooled channel-level analyses further show that model performance is strongly channel dependent and varies with noise level. Overall, the findings support bounded-domain statistical modeling as a useful framework for characterizing quantum-noise-induced perturbations and show that additional shape adaptability can be beneficial, although its value depends on the noise channel, noise level, model family, and computational cost.

title: Attenuation Correction for Flexible MR Hardware in PET/MR Using MLAA-Guided Template Registration_supp1-3693542.png
authors: Nicole Jurjew

title: Degradation and Interference Effects of High-Power Microwave Pulse Irradiation on the Navigation Receiver
authors: Zhao Zhang, Yang Zhou, Yang Zhang, Jun Zhang, Xingjun Ge, Zekang Huang, Yiqun Zhang
abstract: The operation of the navigation receiver may be affected in complex electromagnetic environments. To the best of our knowledge, most existing research focuses on the effects of in-band electromagnetic interference signals on the receiver sensitive components and signal links. There is a lack of system-level studies on the effects of electromagnetic interference signals on the navigation receiver, and the effects of out-of-band high-power electromagnetic signals on the receiver remain unclear. For this reason, this article investigates the effects of in-band and out-of-band high-power microwaves (HPMs) on the typical unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based navigation receiver. Irradiation tests reveal that in-band HPM can cause permanent damage to the receiver, while out-of-band HPM produces temporary interference effects. Furthermore, the system-level electromagnetic irradiation simulation of the navigation receiver is conducted to reveal the effect mechanism. The simulation results indicate that in-band HPM energy primarily enters the receiver system via antenna coupling, damaging radio frequency (RF) front-end circuits. In the case of out-of-band HPM irradiation, the HPM energy enters the system through coupling between the electromagnetic field and the circuit board conductors, thereby causing interference effects on the receiver.

title: TEg-STGNN: Learning Time-Evolving Heterogeneity in Spatial-Temporal Traffic Networks
authors: Lutao Zheng, Guanjun Liu, Ying Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Networks (STGNNs) have become a dominant paradigm for traffic modeling due to their ability to capture spatial and temporal dependencies in traffic networks. However, existing methods typically rely on a uniform representation for modeling temporal dependencies, which fails to capture the evolutionary heterogeneity of traffic dynamics under varying scenarios. Consequently, despite achieving competitive overall results, their performance is often compromised in critical scenarios. Specifically, traffic dynamics under different scenarios manifest as heterogeneous temporal patterns, which are generally categorized into short-term fluctuations with quick dissipation and long-term trends that evolve gradually. Effectively representing these temporal patterns while mitigating their interference is crucial for enhancing model generalization across diverse scenarios. To bridge this gap, we propose a Time-Evolving Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network (TEg-STGNN), a novel framework that incorporates the time-evolving heterogeneity of traffic dynamics. Our approach introduces a dynamic graph construction module that encodes scenario-specific patterns, along with a dual-pathway architecture designed to disentangle short-term disturbances from long-term evolutionary trends. A learnable gating network dynamically fuses these disentangled representations, ensuring robust prediction performance across diverse traffic scenarios. Comprehensive experiments on real-world datasets show that TEg-STGNN excels in predicting specific congestion scenarios and maintains strong generalization across a wide range of traffic conditions.

title: Power Fluctuation Suppression Electric-Drive-Integrated Onboard Converter for Emergency Energy Storage Vehicle Under Load Unbalance
authors: Shuo Liu, Guodong Yin, Yukun Wan, Yi Zhong, Yawen Wang, Zhigang Chen
abstract: The emergency energy storage vehicles (EESVs) provide stable power to critical loads during sudden events such as natural disasters or grid faults. To handle the abrupt load variations typical of emergency scenarios, an EESV must maintain continuous operation under unbalanced load conditions. This article proposes a power fluctuation suppression electric-drive-integrated onboard converter (PFS-EDIOC). The PFS-EDIOC integrates a power inverter and an unbalance suppression circuit by multiplexing the split-phase open-winding motor (SP-OWM), eliminating the need for additional power devices. The unbalance suppression circuit actively absorbs the negative-sequence current caused by load unbalance to reduce power fluctuations in the inverter. Finally, an experimental platform was built to verify the proposed topology and control strategy. The results show that the PFS-EDIOC effectively compensates for power fluctuations induced by load unbalance and significantly mitigates the second-harmonic current in the on-board energy storage system (ESS). Consequently, the power quality and operational reliability of the EESV are significantly enhanced under complex working conditions.

title: Physics-Informed Coupled Contrastive Learning for Fault Detection in Battery Packs Under Data-Scarce Conditions
authors: Yijing Wang, Qi Gao, Peng Li, Zhicheng Zhang, Zhiqiang Zuo
abstract: Fault detection for lithium-ion battery packs plays a crucial role in enhancing the safety of energy-storage systems. To address the challenges of limited data available for near-space vehicles, this article proposes a physics-informed coupled contrastive fault detection (PICCFD) framework for battery packs. First, a battery aging neural network (BANN) is developed to predict the battery terminal voltage under long-duration operation. The aging adaptability and prediction accuracy of the BANN are validated on two aging datasets corresponding to constant-current and constant-power conditions. Subsequently, a coupled contrastive fault detection strategy is proposed to effectively detect faults and quantify their severity by exploiting the relationship between current measurements and voltage prediction errors. Experimental validation on datasets covering three different fault types shows that the PICCFD framework can attain a detection accuracy of 98.25% even when training data are extremely limited. The results also demonstrate the superiority of the proposed strategy in accuracy, computational cost, and data demand.

title: The In-situ Electrical Measurement of Hybrid Bonding_supp1-3695170.docx
authors: Tri-Rung Yew
abstract: The three-dimensional integrated circuit (3DIC) technologies have attracted attention attributed to the advantages of smaller size, higher-speed digital performance, and low power consumption on integrated circuits. Hybrid bonding is a key technology for advanced 3D packaging that offers shorter signal transmission paths through bumpless connections. While most studies on the influence of different annealing conditions on bonding quality have focused on post-annealing evaluations, there is significant potential for further exploration into in-situ analyses. This study introduces a novel approach, in-situ electrical measurement, for the real-time monitoring of hybrid bonding processes. It aims to reduce manufacturing costs and enhance product quality by investigating critical heating conditions. Furthermore, the effects of temperature and ramping rate on bonding behavior are investigated. This study demonstrates the feasibility of achieving hybrid bonding at 235 °C with a brief dwell time of 1.5 min under this experimental design and fabrication processes.

title: Contact-Aware Soft Robot Design via Differentiable Simulation and Morphological Optimization
authors: Zenan Song, Guozhen Zhou, Feifei Chen
abstract: Contact frequently occurs in soft robots and significantly influences their deformation characteristics. However, the inherent nondifferentiability and the design-dependent nature of contact behaviors pose major challenges for gradient-based design optimization. Here, we propose an optimization-oriented static, frictionless contact model that acts as a smooth surrogate to approximate emergent contact behaviors across diverse design candidates during optimization, and more importantly remains continuously differentiable across contact state transitions with respect to both deformation states and geometric design parameters. This capability is achieved via a smooth penetration-penalty function and an automated contact region detection scheme. Building on this contact model, we develop a contact-aware morphological optimization framework for pneumatic soft robots, wherein gradients of contact-related objectives are efficiently evaluated using the adjoint method combined with automatic differentiation. Our results validate the model’s accuracy through benchmarking against Abaqus, demonstrate the necessity of contact-aware optimization for bidirectional bending actuators prone to self-contact, and showcase the framework’s capability to proactively exploit contact interactions for functional gains, as evidenced by a vacuum-driven actuator utilizing self-contact for enhanced torsional stiffness and a soft gripper featuring an expanded contact area with the target object. This work paves the way for programming contact-induced mechanical intelligence in soft robots.

title: Diffusion-Augmented Koopman Kalman Filter for Vehicle Sideslip Angle and Velocity Estimation Within and Beyond Stability Limits
authors: Guoying Chen, Xuanming Zhao, Min Hua, Jiaqi Wang, Zheng Gao, Zhenhai Gao
abstract: Sideslip angle and velocity are key parameters for assessing vehicle stability during typical cornering and drifting maneuvers, but accurate measurement under varying roads is prohibitively expensive. Therefore, a diffusion-augmented Koopman Kalman filter (Diff-AKKF) is proposed in this article, enabling high-precision estimation of sideslip angle and velocity within and beyond the stability limits across different road surfaces. First, a conditional diffusion model with physical constraint is established to directly predict parameters rather than noise, enabling inference of equivalent dynamics parameters across various maneuvers to provide prior information on dynamics characteristics and state evolution to augment estimation. Subsequently, the Koopman Kalman filter is developed based on Koopman operator theory combined with deep learning, embedding parameters inferred from the diffusion model to estimate sideslip angle and velocity. Finally, real-vehicle experiments involving typical cornering and drifting maneuvers are conducted on both wet and dry asphalt roads, thoroughly validating the effectiveness, estimation accuracy, and robustness of the Diff-AKKF algorithm within and beyond the stability limits scenarios.

title: Wisdom Is Knowledge Combined with Intellect: Knowledge-Embedded Hypergraph-of-Thought Reasoning for Visual Abductive Ratiocination
authors: Wenbo Zheng, Lan Yan, Long Chen, Qiang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: For a still image, humans can abductively ratiocinate various probable inferences and hypotheses beyond the given image from visual clues scattered throughout a pictorial scene. However, achieving such a task, i.e., visually abductive ratiocination, is challenging for models because of the limitations of observations, knowledge, and their synthesis process. Therefore, we propose a novel multimodal model: knowledge-embedded hypergraph-of-thought reasoning. First, it represents a physical scene as a semantic graph and a hypergraph to capture the underlying intra- and inter- visual -linguistic relationships, thereby overcoming the limitations of observations derived from the spatial domain. Then, a multimodal knowledge hypergraph is constructed to discriminate and associate scene-related multimodal multigranular concepts with a hierarchical structure, leveraging web resources. This approach overcomes the limitations of lacking world knowledge. Finally, a multimodal hypergraph-of-thought reasoning paradigm is devised based on the semantic and knowledge hypergraphs to model the higher-order relationships and interaction mechanisms between the graphs for conducting multihop reasoning, thus overcoming the limitations of semantics and knowledge being separate and independent. Our method reflects the philosophy of “wisdom is knowledge combined with intellect” in the sense that reasoning models including multimodal knowledge and semantic representations are more robust than those that do not synthesize them. The experimental results show that our model achieves significantly greater performance than state-of-the-art methods do.

title: ComputingEdge
authors: N/A

title: Sustainable Farming Management System: A Web-Based Agricultural Decision Support Platform for Bangladesh
authors: Al-Imam Uddin, Raiyan Islam, Rukaiya Jahan Mihi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Agricultural productivity in Bangladesh faces critical challenges including knowledge gaps between farmers and experts, inefficient resource utilization, and lack of data-driven decision support. This paper presents the Sustainable Farming Management System (SFMS), a comprehensive web-based platform that addresses these challenges through systematic digitalization of agricultural knowledge and expert consultation. Built using Spring Boot framework with MySQL database, SFMS implements a dual-actor architecture enabling administrators to populate agricultural databases while farmers access personalized recommendations. The platform integrates Hugging Face's GPT-2 API for conversational agricultural assistance, OAuth2 authentication for secure access, and implements NPK-based soil testing algorithms for precise fertilizer recommendations. Key solutions include real-time expert consultation systems, weatherintegrated irrigation scheduling, and seasonal crop recommendations based on local conditions. The system runs on port 2024 and provides RESTful APIs for seamless data exchange. Through features like plant suitability prediction, soil health monitoring, and pest control guidance, SFMS transforms traditional farming practices into data-driven operations, bridging the critical knowledge gap in Bangladesh's agricultural sector.

title: Skin Lesion Analysis Using Hybrid Machine Learning Techniques for Early Detection of Skin Cancer
authors: MD. Shahriar Rajib, Md. Diner Ahmed Nissan, Md. Kawsar Arafat, Md. Sabbir Hossain, Muntasir Hasan Porag, Leisa Armstrong, T. M. Shahriar Sazzad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Accurate classification of pigmented skin lesions is critical for the early detection of skin cancer but is complicated by visual heterogeneity, class imbalance, and overlapping clinical features. This study introduces a hybrid ensemble learning framework that integrates classical image preprocessing with deep learning to improve diagnostic accuracy. The preprocessing pipeline incorporates local histogram equalization, morphological operations, and mean-shift segmentation to enhance image contrast and isolate lesion regions. Deep features are extracted using a pretrained VGG16 network and refined through feature selection based on Random Forest and XGBoost importance scores. Final classification is performed using a stacking ensemble of Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and XGBoost classifiers. Evaluation on a seven-class dermoscopic dataset comprising <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{10,015}$</tex> images—including malignant (akiec, bcc, mel) and benign (bkl, df, nv, vasc) lesions—demonstrates consistently high performance. For malignant cases, the model achieved an accuracy of 98.2 %, precision of 98.5 %, recall of 98.1 %, and an F1-score of 98.3 %. For benign lesions, it recorded 98.7 % accuracy, 98.9 % precision, 98.6 % recall, and 98.7 % F1-score. These results underscore the effectiveness and clinical applicability of the proposed method for robust, automated skin cancer diagnosis.

title: A Formal Framework for Provable Security in AI-Native 6G Networks
authors: Nefzi Arij Salha, Faten Hasni, Akram Hakiri, Abderrazak JEMAI
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The transition toward AI-native 6G networks introduces unprecedented autonomy across communication layers but simultaneously expands the attack surface through adversarial machine learning, model manipulation, and cross-layer exploitation. Current heuristic and reactive security mechanisms fail to provide the formal guarantees required for safety-critical 6G applications. This paper introduces a formal framework for achieving provable security in AI-native 6G architectures. The proposed framework models the interaction between non-deterministic AI components and stochastic wireless environments, enabling the precise specification of adversarial capabilities and security properties. We define a structured taxonomy of threats targeting the interface between AI and 6G, and we formalize security objectives spanning integrity, robustness, availability, and policy compliance. A layered verification architecture is proposed to bridge high-level security requirements with low-level neural network behavior and protocol dynamics. We analyze the applicability of formal methods, including model checking and abstraction techniques, to enforce cross-layer guarantees under adaptive adversaries. The framework exposes key limitations in current 6G security research, particularly in compositional verification and scalability. By shifting from empirical defenses to mathematically grounded assurances, this work establishes a foundation for secure-by-design and verifiably resilient 6G systems.

title: A Survey on Foundation Models for Industrial Intelligence: Architectures, Training Paradigms, and Applications
authors: Nourhen Yahyaoui, Habib Fathallah, Bilel Elayeb, Mohamed Barkaoui
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The rapid digital transformation of modern industrial systems has led to the generation of massive volumes of heterogeneous data from sensors, industrial machines, manufacturing platforms, and enterprise information systems. Traditional machine learning approaches often struggle to effectively exploit such large-scale and complex datasets. Recent advances in foundation models, including large-scale transformer-based architectures, have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in learning generalizable representations from massive multimodal data. These models provide new opportunities for developing intelligent and scalable solutions in Industry 4.0 environments. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of foundation models for industrial intelligence. We review the evolution of artificial intelligence architectures from conventional machine learning to large-scale foundation models and analyze key architectural components that enable scalable industrial AI systems. In addition, we examine emerging training paradigms, including large-scale data curation, self-supervised learning, and distributed training infrastructures designed for industrial datasets. The survey further highlights major industrial applications such as predictive maintenance, automated quality inspection, and intelligent manufacturing systems. Finally, we discuss key challenges related to data availability, domain adaptation, realtime constraints, safety, and energy consumption, and outline promising research directions for future industrial AI systems.

title: Real-Time Visual Privacy Preservation in Mobile Mixed Reality
authors: Zaynab Al Haj, Salam Tabet, Ayman Kayssi, Imad H. Elhaj
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Mixed Reality (MR) applications rely on continuous camera capture to seamlessly integrate virtual and real-world content, raising significant privacy concerns due to the inadvertent exposure of sensitive visual information. Such information can be leaked not only through the direct visual appearance of private objects, but also through indirect visual cues. Addressing these cues is therefore as critical as direct object obfuscation, as side channels such as shadows and reflections may still reveal sensitive information about the object. Consequently, ignoring these side channels can result in inadvertent privacy leakage in a real-world MR scenario. Although several privacypreserving frameworks have been proposed, existing approaches focus on a single form of visual leakage, limiting their effectiveness in real-world MR scenarios. In this paper, we propose a lightweight privacy-preserving framework designed for real-time mobile MR deployment that jointly addresses shadow and reflection obfuscation within a unified pipeline. Experimental results demonstrate improved end-to-end performance compared to the existing frameworks, reducing the inference time of the reflection and shadow pipelines by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.1 \times$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times$</tex>, respectively.

title: BlindGate: A Gateway Architecture for Practical Privacy-Preserving Deep Packet Inspection on Encrypted Traffic
authors: Evan Gunawan, Yohan Muliono
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The widespread adoption of TLS has reduced the effectiveness of traditional deep packet inspection (DPI) by encrypting application payloads, forcing enterprises to choose between visibility and privacy. Prior work such as BlindBox demonstrates that searchable encryption can enable DPI over encrypted content, but requires endpoint participation and a modified protocol stack, limiting deployability in enterprise environments. This paper presents BlindGate, a gateway-based architecture that adapts BlindBox-inspired token matching to enable practical, privacy-preserving inspection with minimal client-side modification. BlindGate centralizes tokenization and deterministic token encryption within a trusted network gateway, and exports only encrypted token representations and minimal flow metadata to an untrusted detection engine for tokenized rule matching. A proof-of-concept prototype is implemented using an HTTP traffic instantiation while HTTPS support is treated as a deployment extension via gateway-side TLS termination. Experimental results from a multi-VM testbed show that the Custom-IDS can load encrypted rule token sets, receive encrypted token batches from live traffic, and generate alerts based solely on encrypted token intersections while plaintext remains confined within the trusted boundary of the gateway. Experimental evaluation shows that encrypted tokenization introduces an average gateway processing overhead of approximately 13 ms per request compared to baseline forwarding. These results demonstrate the feasibility of enforcing strict trust boundaries for content-aware inspection and provide a practical deployment path for privacy-preserving DPI in managed enterprise networks.

title: A Comprehensive Analysis of Feature Selection Techniques and Their Impact on Blood Cancer Detection
authors: Anika Ashraf, Tanjila Broti, Alisha Kabir, Shohana Chowdhury, T. M. Shahriar Sazzad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Leukemia, a potentially fatal blood and bone marrow cancer, is a major global health concern because of its quick spread and detrimental effects on the immune system. The need for quicker and less invasive alternatives is highlighted by the fact that traditional diagnostic techniques, like blood tests and bone marrow biopsies, are frequently invasive, expensive, and timeconsuming. Recent developments in machine learning, specifically the use of gene expression data, offer promising methods for precisely classifying leukemia subtypes, such as acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). Finding the most informative attributes in high-dimensional datasets through effective feature selection is essential for improving model performance. In this research, it has been comparing various feature selection methods across a number of machine learning classifiers, including Mutual Information-based Selection, Variance Thresholding, Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), and Univariate Selection. With an accuracy of 94 %, F1-score of 0.93, and ROC-AUC of 0.94, our results demonstrate that hybrid feature selection in conjunction with ensemble classifiers—specifically, Gradient Boosting—achieves superior performance. These results surpass current approaches and show how ensemble learning and optimized feature selection can be used to create leukemia diagnostic tools that are highaccuracy, non-invasive, and dependable. This will lead to better patient outcomes and clinical decision support.

title: Real-Time Facial Animation of Gaussian Head Avatars via Mocap-to-Parametric Expression Mapping
authors: I-Hsin Chen, Sheng-Yen Huang, Yi-Ping Hung
abstract: We present a real-time system for animating photorealistic 3D Gaussian head avatars driven by motion data from consumer facial mocap systems. At the core of our framework is an efficient regression model that maps mocapderived blendshape parameters to the expression space of parametric face models, enabling direct control over Gaussian avatars without iterative optimization. Our design incorporates a lightweight expression regularization mechanism that improves stability and expressiveness by encouraging semantically disentangled, identity-specific deformations. Through extensive evaluations-implemented using ARKit as the mocap source and FLAME as the target parametric model-we show that our method outperforms both fitting-based and regression-based baselines in animation quality and latency. The system supports both live streaming and offline reenactment, enabling efficient, real-time avatar control for virtual meetings and social telepresence.

title: Uncertainty-Aware 3D Edge Reconstruction with Difference of Gaussians
authors: Caixia Zhou, Jiahao Ma, Yaping Huang, Haibin Ling, Jing Zhang
abstract: 3D edge reconstruction from posed multi-view images remains a critical yet under-explored task. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based methods have recently achieved promising performance, they face two main challenges. First, edges exhibit clear discontinuities from the background, but the intrinsic smoothness of Gaussian kernels makes it challenging to model such discontinuities. Second, due to the absence of multi-view edge annotations, models are trained with pseudo labels instead. These pseudo labels extracted by pre-trained <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> edge detectors often exhibit cross-view inconsistencies, leading to degraded performance. To address these issues, we propose a novel uncertainty-aware 3D edge reconstruction using Difference of Gaussians (DoG) as kernels, called EdgeDoG. First, we incorporate DoG kernels to model edge discontinuities explicitly. Second, we design a dual-uncertainty strategy: primitive-level uncertainty is estimated via multi-view Fisher information to eliminate noisy 3D primitives, while pixel-level uncertainty is computed from gradients of rendered depth maps to reweight the training loss, thereby compensating for inconsistent 2D pseudo labels with robust 3D geometric cues. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to previous approaches.

title: DepthCanopyNet: Toward High-Precision Canopy Height Mapping via Gradient-Enhanced Learning Using Single UAV Optical Imagery
authors: Zaorui Song, Xijian Fan, Yu Shen, Tardi Tjahjadi, Yanjie Li, Qiaolin Ye, Liyong Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Tree height is a key indicator in both forestry ecosystems and tree breeding. Thus, accurately and rapidly monitoring the height distribution and dynamic changes of individual trees and forest stands is of vital importance. With the advancement of deep learning, there has been growing attention on canopy height mapping using a single remote sensing image. However, current research primarily relies on satellite or airborne laser scanning (ALS) data for wall-to-wall canopy estimation. In recent years, close-range remote sensing technologies, particularly unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based methods, have demonstrated significant potential in forest inventory, monitoring, and high-throughput phenotyping of trees. Thus, this work focuses on canopy height mapping using UAV-acquired imagery. To address challenges such as the loss of canopy details and the insufficient precision in crown height variation extraction, we propose a transformer-based method for canopy height mapping, named DepthCanopyNet, specifically designed for ultrahigh-resolution UAV imagery. The DepthCanopyNet integrates a bidirectional gradient-enhancement module into a conditional random field, leveraging gradient-based structural knowledge to emphasize height details, particularly the variations in the tree crown and the edges between the tree crown and the background. Furthermore, a lightweight global stepwise aggregation (GSA) module is employed for multilevel feature aggregation, progressively integrating low-level details with high-level global semantic features. This facilitates the flow of multiscale information across different layers, thereby enhancing the ability of the model to represent tree structures with varying sizes and spatial distributions. A two-stage training strategy is further introduced to alleviate the domain gap between the pretrained model and our depth mapping task. Comprehensive experiments conducted on two UAV datasets—one from a coniferous forest with moderate stand density and another from a broad-leaved forest with high stand density—demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art architectures. On the coniferous forest dataset, the absolute relative loss decreased by 0.0128, while on the broad-leaved forest dataset, it decreased by 0.0076. In addition, cross-dataset out-of-distribution transfer experiments validate the generalization capability of the proposed method. This work marks a significant advancement in applying monocular depth estimation to centimeter-level remote sensing imagery and highlights the potential of using a single image for extracting individual tree-level crown parameters.

title: Proxy-Free Gaussian Splats Deformation with Splat-Based Surface Estimation
authors: Jaeyeong Kim, Seungwoo Yoo, Minhyuk Sung
abstract: We introduce SpLap, a proxy-free deformation method for Gaussian splats (GS) based on a Laplacian operator computed from our novel surface-aware splat graph. Existing approaches to GS deformation typically rely on deformation proxies such as cages or meshes, but they suffer from dependency on proxy quality and additional computational overhead. An alternative is to directly apply Laplacianbased deformation techniques by treating splats as point clouds. However, this often fail to properly capture surface information due to lack of explicit structure. To address this, we propose a novel method that constructs a surface-aware splat graph, enabling the Laplacian operator derived from it to support more plausible deformations that preserve details and topology. Our key idea is to leverage the spatial arrangement encoded in splats, defining neighboring splats not merely by the distance between their centers, but by their intersections. Furthermore, we introduce a Gaussian kernel adaptation technique that preserves surface structure under deformation, thereby improving rendering quality after deformation. In our experiments, we demonstrate the superior performance of our method compared to both proxy-based and proxy-free baselines, evaluated on 50 challenging objects from the ShapeNet, Objaverse, and Sketchfab datasets, as well as the NeRF-Synthetic dataset. Code is available at https://github.com/kjae0/SpLap.

title: Symmetry Informative and Agnostic Feature Disentanglement for 3D Shapes
authors: Tobias Weißberg, Weikang Wang, Paul Roetzer, Nafie El Amrani, Florian Bernard
abstract: Shape descriptors, i.e., per-vertex features of 3D meshes or point clouds, are fundamental to shape analysis. Historically, various handcrafted geometry-aware descriptors and feature refinement techniques have been proposed. Recently, several studies have initiated a new research direction by leveraging features from image foundation models to create semantics-aware descriptors, demonstrating advantages across tasks like shape matching, editing, and segmentation. Symmetry, another key concept in shape analysis, has also attracted increasing attention. Consequently, constructing symmetry-aware shape descriptors is a natural progression. Although the recent method <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\chi$</tex> [53] successfully extracted symmetry-informative features from semantic-aware descriptors, its features are only one-dimensional, neglecting other valuable semantic information. Furthermore, the extracted symmetry-informative feature is usually noisy and yields small misclassified patches. To address these gaps, we propose a feature disentanglement approach which is simultaneously symmetry informative and symmetry agnostic. Further, we propose a feature refinement technique to improve the robustness of predicted symmetry informative features. Extensive experiments, including intrinsic symmetry detection, left/right classification, and shape matching, demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed framework compared to various state-of-the-art methods, both qualitatively and quantitatively. Project page: https://tweissberg.github.io/chirality/

title: DetailGen3D: Generative 3D Geometry Enhancement via Data-Dependent Flow
authors: Ken Deng, Yuan-Chen Guo, Jingxiang Sun, Zi-Xin Zou, Yangguang Li, Xin Cai, Yan-Pei Cao, Yebin Liu, Ding Liang
abstract: Modern 3D generation methods can rapidly create shapes from sparse or single views, but their outputs often lack geometric detail due to computational constraints. We present DetailGen3D, a generative approach specifically designed to enhance these generated 3D shapes. Our key insight is to model the coarse-to-fine transformation directly through data-dependent flows in latent space, avoiding the computational overhead of large-scale 3D generative models. We introduce a token matching strategy that ensures accurate spatial correspondence during refinement, enabling local detail synthesis while preserving global structure. By carefully designing our training data to match the characteristics of synthesized coarse shapes, our method can effectively enhance shapes produced by various 3D generation and reconstruction approaches, from single-view to sparse multi-view inputs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DetailGen3D achieves high-fidelity geometric detail synthesis while maintaining efficiency in training. Our project page is https://detailgen3d.github.io/DetailGen3D/

title: DFT and TD-DFT Study of Polyene Carotinoids for Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells
authors: Md. Arman Arafat, Jewel Hossen, Sumaiya Kabir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Carotenoids are promising natural sensitizers for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) due to their abundance, nontoxicity, and strong visible-light absorption. Using DFT and TDDFT, we have investigated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\alpha}$</tex>-carotene, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\beta}$</tex>-carotene, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\gamma}$</tex>-carotene, and lycopene to assess how structural differences influence their photovoltaic performance. Lycopene, with the longest conjugated chain, exhibits the smallest HOMO-LUMO gap <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(2.101 \text{eV})$</tex> and the most intense red-shifted absorption, indicating superior light-harvesting ability. All dyes show high light-harvesting efficiency (0.999–1.000), sufficiently negative electron-injection Gibbs free energies (−2.203 to −2.234 eV), and favorable open-circuit voltages <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(1.796-1.873 \mathrm{V})$</tex>. These results highlight the pivotal role of conjugation length and electronic structure in optimizing carotenoid-based DSSCs and identify lycopene as the most promising sensitizer among those studied.

title: No Excuses [Electrical Safety]
authors: Dennis J. Hill

title: A Low-parasitic Integrated CM EMI Filter Structure with Additional Terminals for SiC Power Modules: Analysis, Design, and Experimental Validation
authors: Yifan Zhang, Jianwei Lv, Yiyang Yan, Shuangxi Zhu, Wenzhe Xu, Jiaxin Liu, Xinmin Liu, Cai Chen, Yong Kang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Silicon carbide (SiC) devices operate at higher switching speeds and frequencies than traditional silicon (Si) devices, and also generate more significant common-mode (CM) electromagnetic interference (EMI). Integrated EMI filters within the power module offer better performance than external filters above 10 MHz. But the existing integrated design still suffers from insufficient filtering performance below 10 MHz and high core losses in the CM inductors due to the parasitic inductances and terminal placement. To address these problems, this paper presents a novel integrated EMI filter structure within the SiC power module. Featuring a low-leakage stripline inductor and additional terminals, it enhances EMI suppression over a wider frequency range and effectively reduces the core losses. A frequency-domain analysis model is developed to analyze the impact of key parasitic parameters from 150 kHz to 30 MHz, addressing gaps in current theoretical analysis. Based on this, the proposed EMI filter structure is introduced. A power module prototype employing this structure is fabricated, and an EMI test bench is constructed. Experimental results from 150 kHz to 30 MHz demonstrate that the proposed structure achieves superior filtering performance to the existing structure, and also achieves a 10 dB improvement compared to the external filters.

title: Bi-Temporal Benefits: Progressive Spectral-Spatial-Temporal Feature Extraction for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Wenming Liu, Shuang Li, Xinghua Li
abstract: Spectral-spatial feature extraction widely serves as the foundation for hyperspectral image classification (HSIC). However, its effectiveness decreases when applied to land covers with temporal variations. This limitation arises from lack of the temporal dimension in existing HSIC methods, hindering their ability to model real-world surface dynamics. To tackle these problems, Bi-tEmporal HyperspectrAL image classiFication network (BehalfNet) employs the dual-branch stacked architecture to process bi-temporal images, learning spectral-spatial-temporal features. Within each stacked block, features undergo the sequential feature processing pipeline. Specifically, the progressive adaptive fusion (PAF) module firstly extracts foundational spectral-spatial features for each temporal phase through long-short term fusion. These features are then refined at an intra-temporal level by the gated spectral-spatial attention (GSSA) module. Subsequently, the bi-temporal self-cross attention (BTSCA) module effectively captures the complex dynamic changes between the bi-temporal features using a novel closed-loop attention mechanism. Furthermore, the Anji dataset was introduced as the first publicly available dataset for bit-emporal HSIC. Comprehensive experiments on the Anji and public Viareggio datasets (originally used for anomaly change detection) demonstrate the competitiveness of the proposed BehalfNet over other state-of-the-art HSIC methods. The code and Anji dataset will be released at https://github.com/lixinghua5540/BehalfNet.

title: Passive Environment Reconstruction via Bistatic Single-Bounce Multipaths in Communication Channels
authors: Jiachi Zhang, Rongchen Sun, Dongmei Liu, Liu Liu, Kai Wang
abstract: Accurate environmental sensing is a promising and essential application for next-generation mobile communication systems. In this paper, we propose a novel passive three dimensional (3-D) environmental sensing method that leverages wideband multipath components (MPCs). The sensing task is performed by utilizing the propagation delay, elevation angle of arrival (EAoA), and azimuth angle of arrival (AAoA) of MPCs obtained from bistatic stations. The proposed method operates in two sequential phases. First, we derive a closed form solution for obstacle localization by formulating a quadratic equation constrained by the propagation parameters under ideal resolution conditions. Second, to address the practical resolution limitations imposed by finite signal bandwidth and a limited number of array elements, we develop a Bayesian filter-based grid mapping technique that enables robust environmental reconstruction in dynamic mobile scenarios. Comprehensive validation demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method: (1) practical evaluation using measured high-speed railway (HSR) channel data successfully identifies trackside metallic poles, with reconstructed MPC trajectories closely matching the measurements; and (2) ray-tracing simulations in the HSR cutting scenario reproduce similar MPC variation patterns induced by periodic trackside structures, and the use of single-bounce MPCs with perfect resolution enables accurate estimation of environmental features. These findings advance the capabilities of passive environmental sensing for wireless communication systems, particularly in infrastructure-rich mobile environments.

title: LLM-Enhanced Multi-Modal Perception Data Fusion and Cascaded Augmentation for Crop Growth Monitoring Based on UAV Remote Sensing
authors: Xihuizi Liang, Ruihong Jing, Yueye Yong, Xuelei Yuan, Guoping Feng, Liuxin Zhang, Zhiyuan Fu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: With the growing demand for high-precision monitoring of crop growth in precision agriculture, the heterogeneity of multimodal remote sensing data and the scarcity of high-quality annotated samples have become key bottlenecks hindering the practical implementation of related technologies. To address this, this paper proposes an LLM-enhanced multimodal data fusion and dual-space cascaded augmentation technique. First, we construct the TUFusion-GM network, which achieves adaptive feature weighting through parallel hybrid encoding and a composite attention mechanism, and introduces an LLM-semantically guided diffusion decoder to perform controllable high-fidelity image generation. Second, we design the SADA-CA algorithm to simultaneously expand data distributions in both the sample and feature spaces to alleviate class imbalance. Experiments demonstrate that this method significantly outperforms mainstream baselines in all performance metrics, achieving a peak signal-to-noise ratio of 62.88, a crop detection <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$F_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula> score of 91.9%, and a false alarm rate of 27.8%. This study provides an efficient technical approach for precise monitoring with limited data in complex agricultural environments and holds significant value for advancing the intelligent development of smart agriculture.

title: EVA: Editing for Versatile Alignment against Jailbreaks_supp1-3694189.pdf
authors: Wenjie Wang
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities but remain vulnerable to jailbreaking attacks, where adversaries exploit textual or visual triggers to bypass safety guardrails. Recent defenses typically rely on safety fine-tuning or external filters to reduce the model's likelihood of producing harmful content. While effective to some extent, these methods often incur significant computational overheads and suffer from the safety utility trade-off, degrading the model's performance on benign tasks. To address these challenges, we propose EVA (Editing for Versatile Alignment against Jailbreaks), a novel framework that pioneers the application of direct model editing for safety alignment. EVA reframes safety alignment as a precise knowledge correction task. Instead of retraining massive parameters, EVA identifies and surgically edits specific neurons responsible for the model's susceptibility to harmful instructions, while leaving the vast majority of the model unchanged. By localizing the updates, EVA effectively neutralizes harmful behaviors without compromising the model's general reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EVA outperforms baselines in mitigating jailbreaks across both LLMs and VLMs, offering a precise and efficient solution for post-deployment safety alignment.

title: IEEE Computer Society: Unlock the Benefits
authors: N/A

title: Cognition-Driven Sentiment Analysis: Leveraging Affective Knowledge in Graph Convolutional Networks for Teaching Evaluations
authors: Wenjun Zheng, Wenbin Zhu, Shoufei Han, Xiaofen Jia, Weiping Ding
abstract: With the proliferation of massive open online courses, teaching evaluation texts have become an important source of feedback, providing valuable affective and cognitive insights into students’ learning experiences. Although automated sentiment analysis techniques have been developed, existing methods still struggle to capture domain-specific affective knowledge and to systematically model the affective and cognitive processes inherent in educational contexts. To address these challenges, we propose an affective cognition-enhanced graph convolutional network (ACEGCN), which simulates a human-like sentiment reasoning process by integrating domain-specific affective cognition knowledge with syntactic information. Specifically, we construct a domain-specific affective cognition knowledge graph by aligning sentiment entities with a general knowledge base. The resulting knowledge is then integrated into the input sentence to form a knowledge-enriched sentence tree, from which semantically enhanced dependency graphs are constructed using a multigranularity syntactic masking mechanism. Finally, we employ a graph convolutional network to capture key sentiment paths and model complex semantic dependencies. Experimental results across three real-world teaching evaluation datasets demonstrate that ACEGCN significantly outperforms baseline models, suggesting its potential to enhance teaching feedback analysis and help improve teaching quality.

title: H ∞ Optimal Fault-Tolerant Load Frequency Control for Multi-Area Power Systems: A Dynamic Output Feedback Scheme via Hybrid Iteration
authors: Chaochen Song, Junmin Li, Hai Wang, Mengjie Ma, Chao He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper addresses the Load Frequency Control (LFC) problem in multi-area power systems by proposing an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H_{\infty } $</tex-math> </inline-formula> optimal fault-tolerant (FT) control scheme based on dynamic output feedback (DOF). In response to practical challenges such as unmeasurable full system states and frequent actuator failures, a DOF controller is designed using only input–output data. Furthermore, a novel model-free fault-tolerant hybrid iteration (FT-HI) algorithm is introduced, which estimates optimal control gains without prior knowledge of system dynamics. The algorithm ensures system stability, effective disturbance attenuation, and tolerance to actuator faults. In simulation studies, the effectiveness of the proposed DOF control scheme with FT is verified using a three-area power system as an example.

title: Dynamic Parameter Identification for Redundant Manipulator Using a Hierarchical Framework With Nonlinear Compensation
authors: Yufei Zhou, Wenjun Xu, Lingbo Liu, Ziying Zhao, Yuanbin Liu, Liang Lin
abstract: A high-precision dynamic model is crucial for enhancing the control performance and improving the motion accuracy of robots. However, traditional algorithms can only integrate a linear friction model, leading to limited identification accuracy and yielding dynamic parameters that are not physically feasible or lack rationality. To address these problems, this study proposed a four-step hierarchical identification framework. Initially, all dynamic parameters were identified by incorporating physical feasibility constraints and Riemannian geometry metrics. A hierarchical method was then developed to integrate and reidentify various nonlinear friction parameters. Subsequently, the inertial parameter reidentification refined the parameter distribution, which enhanced the overall accuracy. To compensate for uncertain identification errors, an extensive dataset was used to train a Gaussian process regression model, thereby further enhancing the identification accuracy. This methodology addressed three key limitations—that is, the incompatibility with nonlinear friction models, low identification accuracy, and failure to satisfy physical feasibility constraints. Extensive comparative experiments were conducted using eight distinct algorithms. Compared to the classical weighted least squares algorithm, the identification and validation accuracies improved by an average of 16.84% and 22.94%, respectively. The results indicate that the proposed method exhibited more reasonable parameter values, a more accurate friction model, and improved identification accuracy.

title: Optimizing Reinforcement Learning Training over Digital Twin Enabled Multi-fidelity Networks
authors: Hanzhi Yu, Hasan Farooq, Julien Forgeat, Shruti Bothe, Kristijonas Cyras, Md Moin Uddin Chowdhury, Mingzhe Chen
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a novel digital network twin (DNT) assisted deep learning (DL) model training framework. In particular, we consider a physical network where a base station (BS) uses several antennas to serve multiple mobile users, and a DNT that is a virtual representation of the physical network. The BS must adjust its antenna tilt angles to optimize the data rates of all users. Due to user mobility, the BS may not be able to accurately track network dynamics such as wireless channels and user mobilities. Hence, a reinforcement learning (RL) approach is used to dynamically adjust the antenna tilt angles. To train the RL, we can use data collected from the physical network and the DNT. The data collected from the physical network is more accurate but incurs more communication overhead compared to the data collected from the DNT. Therefore, it is necessary to determine the ratio of data collected from the physical network and the DNT to improve the training of the RL model. We formulate this problem as an optimization problem whose goal is to jointly optimize the tilt angle adjustment policy and the data collection strategy, aiming to maximize the data rates of all users while constraining the time delay introduced by collecting data from the physical network. To solve this problem, we propose a hierarchical RL framework that integrates robust adversarial loss and proximal policy optimization (PPO). The first level robust-RL dynamically adjusts the antenna tilt angles, and the second level PPO determines the ratio of data collected from the physical network to improve the first level robust-RL training performance. Compared to traditional single level RL algorithms such as deep Q network (DQN), the designed method optimizes the data collection ratio and the antenna tilt angles at diverse time intervals, allowing the second level PPO to adjust the data collection ratio with a large timescale using the training information provided by the first level robust-RL. Simulation results show that our proposed method reduces the physical network data collection delay by up to 28.01% and 1× compared to a hierarchical RL that uses vanilla PPO as the first level RL, and the baseline that uses robust-RL at the first level and selects the data collection ratio randomly.

title: A Unified Framework for Reliable Survival Analysis
authors: Armand Bandiang Massoua, Mohamed Bouguessa, Abdoulaye Baniré Diallo
abstract: Survival analysis (time-to-event prediction) focuses on predicting the time until an event of interest, such as disease relapse or death. It is a critical tool for clinical decisionmaking. An effective survival approach must address model understandability, quantifying uncertainty, and predictive robustness for practical adoption and trustworthiness in clinical settings. While progress has been made in these areas, existing methods often address these aspects separately, leading to limited performance and practical challenges. In this paper, we propose a principled framework that addresses these limitations in a unified approach. Our framework incorporates a stochastic, biologically inspired sparsity mechanism for feature selection and applies a Bayesian treatment of model weights, enabling double stochasticity through two distinct sampling processes. These mechanisms enhance model understandability and enable robust uncertainty quantification. Furthermore, we introduce an information-bound regularization inspired by information theory, which controls information flow by promoting the learning of essential information while filtering out irrelevant and redundant data. This regularization improves generalization and enhances predictive resilience to noise and adversarial perturbations. Through extensive experiments on five real-world survival datasets, our approach demonstrates competitive performance in both discrimination and calibration compared to state-of-the-art deep survival models. On a synthetic dataset, our approach effectively identifies relevant features, showcasing its ability to support reliable feature selection. Additionally, the robustness of the proposed framework to adversarial attacks highlights its predictive resilience, further reinforcing its trustworthiness for survival analysis applications.

title: Automated geometric compliance assessment of barrier-free ramps based on LiDAR data
authors: Keyu Chen, Linfeng Li, Hongzhe Yue, Maggie Y. GAO, Qian Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Barrier-free ramps in public facilities are still predominantly inspected through manual measurement, making compliance checking labor-intensive, inconsistent, and difficult to scale, especially for existing facilities with complex layouts or without BIM models.
