To address this practical gap, this study proposes a LiDAR-based automated framework for the geometric compliance assessment of barrier-free ramps using only point cloud data. The framework standardizes multi-station scans, semantically identifies platform surfaces, ramp surfaces, and handrails, extracts regulation-oriented geometric parameters, and directly converts them into clause-level compliance results and standardized inspection reports. In this way, the proposed method is intended not only for geometric measurement, but also for routine compliance auditing of existing facilities by non-specialist personnel (e.g. facility managers) without requiring specialist interpretation of barrier-free codes. Tests on twelve outdoor barrier-free ramps with diverse geometric layouts show robust performance under scanning noise and reliable handling of multi-segment ramps and different handrail configurations. Compared with manual inspection, the framework provides more standardized and repeatable compliance outputs and achieves a maximum inspection efficiency improvement of 27.32% for geometrically complex ramps. These results demonstrate the practical usefulness of the proposed method for large-scale barrier-free facility auditing, retrofit prioritization, and digital infrastructure management.

title: Privacy-preserving Online Federated Learning for Massive Infinite Streams
authors: Liang Shi, Xuebin Ren, Shusen Yang, Cong Zhao, Yijun Hao, Zongben Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Online federated learning (OFL) is essential for privacy-preserving collaborative online analytics over decentralized streams. Different from batch-based FL, OFL faces new challenges including longitudinal privacy leakage, and accumulated utility loss and communication costs, caused by the infinite data streams. This paper first extends the definition of traditional differential privacy (DP) to OFL, to provide window-based privacy protection with a tunable granularity for infinite streams. By analyzing baseline methods, a generic sampling-based solution framework is then proposed for designing a DP-enhanced OFL algorithm. We prove that despite the DP constraint, the sampling solution framework can achieve an asymptotic optimality when time tends to infinity. Finally, we present Sampling<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-OFL, an adaptive triple-sampling strategy driven by deep reinforcement learning, which can dynamically determine a near-optimal sampling strategy with significant gains in both utility and efficiency. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that Sampling<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-OFL can scale to millions of streams, and achieves utility improvements of 0.74%-15.84% and communication cost reductions of 33.33%-95.24% across these datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: Biogeographical Trends of Benthic Habitats in Guam from WorldView-2/3 Observations Using Advanced Deep Learning_supp1-3696586.pdf
authors: Bingqing Liu

title: Comparative Study of Stand-Alone and Hybrid Deep Learning Models for EEG Signal-Based Emotion Classification
authors: Faria Parvin, Md Foysal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: One of the fundamental parts of human communication and interaction is emotion. In this digital era, the ability to accurately detect and interpret human emotions is crucial in domains ranging from healthcare and education to human-computer interaction and entertainment. The Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) uses physiological signal such as Electroencephalography (EEG) to improve human-machine interactions through emotion. As machines increasingly influencing our lives, automatic emotion detection with high accuracy has become an impactful research area. This study compares deep learning methods for classifying emotions from EEG signals using the DEAP dataset, a benchmark in emotion analysis. Feature extraction of the dataset is done using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). Over the past years, most of the researches relied on conventional machine learning algorithms like Support Vector Machine (SVM) and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for classifying emotion from the signal. Here, we build and compare Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), (Gated Recurrent Unit) GRU, and a hybrid model combining CNN, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and GRU for classification. The models classify emotions into positive, negative, and neutral categories based on valence, arousal, dominance, and liking. Results show the CNN model achieved more than 93 % accuracy, while GRU has the lowest loss of 22 %, both GRU and the hybrid model exceeds 92 % Precision and F1 scores are over 92% across all categories, demonstrating the models' robustness. These findings highlight the effectiveness of deep learning in emotion classification and its potential in affective computing.

title: High-Gain 4×4 MIMO Antenna Array for Ku-Band Satellite Applications: Design and Performance Analysis
authors: Md. Suaibur Rahman, Nafiz Imtiaz Bin Hamid, Palash Kundu, Tajdid Ul Alam, Md. Hachhibur Rahman, Mohammad Atiqullah, Asif Ahmed Khan, Md Mahabub Alam, Sadman Al Farabe, Mst. Rokeya Khatun
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The growing demand for high data rates in densely populated urban areas makes compact, high-gain, and efficient antenna designs crucial for the advancement of Ku-band satellite communication systems. Array configurations are essential for achieving high gain, while MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) technology enhances both capacity and reliability by enabling the simultaneous handling of independent data streams. In this work, a compact 4-port <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 4$</tex> MIMO array antenna with 50Ω feed lines is proposed. The design emphasizes structural simplicity, high gain, and effective inter-element isolation. It is designed on a Rogers RT/Duroid 5880 substrate that is 0.79 mm thick, measuring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83.20 \times 80.00 ~\text{mm}^{2}$</tex> in size, with a dielectric constant of 2.2 and a loss tangent of 0.0009. Performance testing shows that the bandwidth is 290 MHz, the isolation is better than -17 dB, the peak gain is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3. 8 0 ~ d B i}$</tex>, and the radiation efficiency is 86.75 %. The antenna also has strong MIMO features, such as a diversity gain (DG) of more than 10 dB, a mean effective gain (MEG) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 3. 2 0 ~ d B}$</tex>, a total active reflection coefficient (TARC) of less than -7.50 dB, and a channel capacity loss (CCL) of less than 0.03 bit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$/ \mathrm{s} / \text{Hz}$</tex>. Full-wave simulations in CST Studio Suite confirm the design's compactness and efficiency. These detailed results demonstrate that the proposed antenna is a strong candidate for next-generation Ku-band satellite systems that need reliable MIMO performance and higher throughput.

title: PALM: A Dataset and Baseline for Learning Multi-Subject Hand Prior
authors: Zicong Fan, Edoardo Remelli, David Dimond, Fadime Sener, Liuhao Ge, Bugra Tekin, Cem Keskin, Shreyas Hampali
abstract: The ability to grasp objects, signal with gestures, and share emotion through touch all stem from the unique capabilities of human hands. Yet creating high-quality personalized hand avatars from images remains challenging due to complex geometry, appearance, and articulation, particularly under unconstrained lighting and limited views. Progress has also been limited by the lack of datasets that jointly provide accurate 3D geometry, high-resolution multiview imagery, and a diverse population of subjects. To address this, we present PALM, a large-scale dataset comprising 13k high-quality hand scans from 263 subjects and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$90 k$</tex> multi-view images, capturing rich variation in skin tone, age, and geometry. To show its utility, we present a baseline PALM-Net, a multi-subject prior over hand geometry and material properties learned via physically based inverse rendering, enabling realistic, relightable single-image hand avatar personalization. PALM's scale and diversity make it a valuable real-world resource for hand modeling and related research.

title: LLM-Based Advanced Persistent Threat Attribution: A Novel Framework for Enhanced Cyber Threat Intelligence
authors: Adel Alshamrani
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) represent one of the most sophisticated challenges in modern cybersecurity, requiring innovative approaches for accurate attribution. This paper introduces the LLM-Based Advanced Persistent Threat Attribution Framework (LLMAPT), a novel approach that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance APT attribution capabilities. The framework addresses critical limitations in existing attribution methods by implementing a multi-layered architecture that includes multi-source intelligence integration, LLM-powered semantic analysis, structured attribution reasoning, calibrated confidence quantification, and explainable attribution interfaces. Key innovations include chain-of-thought attribution reasoning, adversarial robustness mechanisms, calibrated uncertainty quantification, temporal evolution modeling, and multi-level explainability. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that LLMAPT significantly outperforms traditional attribution methods and earlier machine learning approaches across multiple performance metrics. The framework provides security analysts with more accurate, robust, and explainable attribution capabilities, enabling more effective response to sophisticated cyber threats.

title: Communication and Machine Learning Framework for Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions Targeting Eating Behavior
authors: Edward Sazonov, Delwar Hossain, Siavash Esfandiari Fard, Md Billal Hossain, Megan A. McCrory, Janine Higgins, J. Graham Thomas
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (JITAIs) are a promising tool in behavioral medicine, offering the potential to modify eating behaviors as they happen. Advances in wearable sensor technology enable accurate, objective measurement of ingestive behavior, and near real-time analysis of these data supports the development of individually tailored feedback delivered precisely when it is most effective-during eating episodes. In our prior work, we introduced the Automatic Ingestion Monitor v2 (AIM-2), a wearable device for passive detection of eating. In this paper, we present a communication and machine-learning framework designed to support JITAIs that target eating behavior using AIM-2 data. The framework integrates device-to-phone and phone-to-server communication pathways, server-side processing of sensor signals, and delivery of intervention messages back to the user's phone. It is designed to meet the latency constraints of JITAIs by leveraging split computing on the device and remote server to reduce the bandwidth and to drive JITAI decision logic. The framework was evaluated in both laboratory and free-living settings. The (mean ± standard deviation) latency, defined as the time between the onset of eating and the delivery of the first message to the participant, was <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$43.0 \pm 4.2$</tex> seconds in the laboratory setting, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$59.7 \pm 22.04$</tex> seconds in the free-living setting. These results suggest that the proposed framework provides a viable foundation for implementing JITAIs aimed at targeting eating behavior.

title: Protonic: An Interactive Interface for Protein Structure Prediction with Enhanced Usability and Interpretation
authors: Shaikh Prothomaa Binte Minhaz, Tasnia Haque Kheya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Understanding protein structure is vital for annotating molecular functions, guiding drug discovery, and elucidating disease mechanisms. Deep learning has advanced this field, yet many existing models are computationally expensive and focus on either structural or sequential modalities in isolation. This paper introduces Protonic, a unified framework that addresses protein classification and backbone torsion angle prediction through the integration of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and a hybrid CNN-BiLSTM architecture. The GNN module constructs residue-level graphs with sequential and spatial edges, employing residual graph convolutional blocks with normalization to capture topological and geometric features. In parallel, the CNN-BiLSTM branch predicts backbone angles (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\phi, \psi$</tex>) using sine-cosine encoding to ensure smooth regression. Experiments on the SidechainNet CASP12 dataset show that Protonic achieves competitive classification accuracy and reduced mean angular error compared to recent methods. Rigorous preprocessing-including coordinate normalization, residue masking, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> nearest neighbor graph construction-further improves stability. Protonic achieves up to 95 % validation accuracy. Importantly, the trained models are exported via TorchScript and deployed in an Android environment with GPU-accelerated inference and 3D visualization. Protonic thus contributes an efficient and deployable deep learning solution that jointly models structural and sequential information, bridging protein informatics with practical mobile applications.

title: Frequency-Dependent Performance Analysis of a Double-Slot Antenna for Microwave Ablation
authors: Taslima Jannat, Md. Rejvi Kaysir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Microwave ablation (MWA) has emerged as a promising minimally invasive technique for treating malignant tumors, though its performance strongly depends on antenna design and operating frequency. In this study, a double-slot coaxial antenna was evaluated using the finite element method implemented on COMSOL Multiphysics 6.2 to investigate frequency-dependent heating characteristics in a liver tissue model. Six microwave frequencies <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.915 \text{GHz}, 2.45 \text{GHz}, 5.8 \text{GHz}, 10 \text{GHz}, 14.5 \text{GHz}$</tex>, and 18 GHz, were analyzed to assess temperature distribution profile, specific absorption rate (SAR), reflection coefficient (S11), ablation zone geometry, and fractional damage. In general, the simulation result showed that higher frequencies lead to higher temperature and SAR. However, the findings also revealed that lower frequencies achieved deeper penetration with elongated ablation zone, while higher frequencies produced more compact and symmetric heating patterns. Among the tested cases, 14.5 GHz provided the most effective outcome, delivering the highest temperature rise and SAR with a nearly spherical ablation region. On the other hand, 2.45 GHz offered the most reliable antenna matching, and 18 GHz generated a compact but clinically less effective ablation zone due to poor impedance matching. These findings underscore the importance of frequency-aware optimization in microwave ablation, providing valuable insights for improving treatment precision and antenna design.

title: A Hybrid Ensemble Learning Framework for Early Detection of Ectopic Pregnancy from Ultrasound Images
authors: Sofia Zerin Tamanna Rahman, T. M. Shahriar Sazzad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Ectopic pregnancy (EP), a condition where the embryo implants outside the uterine cavity, poses significant risks to maternal health if not diagnosed early. Despite the widespread use of ultrasound imaging, early stage EP detection remains a diagnostic challenge due to image heterogeneity and the subtle visual features of abnormal implantation. This study proposes a hybrid ensemble learning framework that integrates classical image preprocessing with deep learning techniques to enhance diagnostic accuracy and robustness. The preprocessing pipeline includes local histogram equalization, morphological operations, and U-Net-based segmentation to improve image clarity and accurately isolate regions of interest. Deep features are extracted using a pre-trained VGG16 model and refined through feature selection using Random Forest and XGBoost algorithms. Classification is carried out using an ensemble of Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and XGBoost models combined through stacking, reducing bias and improving performance. Evaluated on 1,400 ultrasound images, the framework achieved 97.5% accuracy, 97.93% precision, 99.48% recall, and a 98.69% F1-score, demonstrating its potential as an effective tool for early EP detection in clinical settings.

title: Structurally Disentangled Feature Fields Distillation for 3D Understanding and Editing
authors: Yoel Levy, David Shavin, Itai Lang, Sagie Benaim
abstract: Recent work demonstrated the ability to leverage or distill pre-trained 2D features obtained using large pretrained 2D foundation models into 3D features, enabling impressive 3D editing and understanding capabilities with only 2D supervision. While powerful, such features contain significant view-dependent components, especially in scenes with complex materials and reflections. When distilled into a single 3D feature field, these inconsistencies are averaged, degrading feature quality and harming downstream tasks like segmentation. We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the physical causes of view-dependence is key to “cleaning” these features during distillation. To this end, we propose to decompose the 3D feature field into view-independent and view-dependent components, guided by a physically-based reflection model. Our core contribution is demonstrating that this structural disentanglement improves the quality and view-invariance of the distilled semantic features. This leads to improved 3D seg-mentation, particularly in challenging reflective regions,and enables higher-fidelity physically-grounded editing applications. Our project page is available at https://structurallydisentangled.github.io/.

title: ContA-HOI: Towards Physically Plausible Human-Object Interaction Generation via Contact-Aware Modeling
authors: Zhe Li, Jiakun Li, Mingqi Gao, Jinyu Yang, Wei Wang, Feng Zheng
abstract: In human-object interaction (HOI), physical contact between the body and objects is a primary determinant of realism and plausibility. Prior HOI methods typically encode relations via global joint-to-centroid or joint-to-boundary. Such strategies neglect contact anchors that are essential for defining joint-to-contact relations-where and how HOI occurs, thereby implicitly reducing the problem to nearest-distance optimization. Without explicit contact anchors and joint-to-contact dynamics, previous models drift toward artifacts: human-object penetration or unnatural object floating. We argue that modeling contact relationships by contact anchors is important for generating realistic HOIs, as it directly captures where and how humans physically interact with objects rather than merely minimizing spatial proximity. To address these limitations, we propose Contact-Aware HOI (ContA-HOI), a progressive framework that decomposes HOI generation into three synergistic stages: discovering where contact occurs, modeling how contact evolves, and guiding generation with contact constraints. First, a Contact Affordance Predictor (CAP) addresses the “where” by predicting precise object-surface contact anchors from text, human pose, and object geometry. Second, these anchors seed a Contact Relation Field (CRF) that captures “how” by modeling spatiotemporal dynamics of joint-to-contact relations throughout the interaction. Finally, a Contact Dynamics Model (CDM) learns a prior CRF evolution pattern and guides motion diffusion sampling by aligning the generated motion's CRF with this learned prior. On the FullBodyManipulation dataset, ContA-HOI yields more realistic and physically plausible HOIs, improving foot sliding and contact percentage over recent baselines.

title: SceneGen: Single-Image 3D Scene Generation in One Feedforward Pass
authors: Yanxu Meng, Haoning Wu, Ya Zhang, Weidi Xie
abstract: 3D content generation has recently attracted significant research interest, driven by its critical applications in VR/AR and embodied AI. In this work, we tackle the challenging task of synthesizing multiple 3D assets within a single scene image. Concretely, our contributions are fourfold: (i) we present SceneGen, a novel framework that takes a scene image and corresponding object masks as input, simultaneously producing multiple 3D assets with geometry and texture. Notably, SceneGen operates with no need for extra optimization or asset retrieval; (ii) we introduce a novel feature aggregation module that integrates local and global scene information from visual and geometric encoders within the feature extraction module. Coupled with a position head, this enables the generation of 3D assets and their relative spatial positions in a single feedforward pass; (iii) we demonstrate SceneGen's direct extensibility to multi-image input scenarios. Despite being trained solely on single-image inputs, our architecture yields improved generation performance when multiple images are provided; and (iv) extensive quantitative and qualitative evaluations confirm the efficiency and robustness of our approach. We believe this paradigm offers a novel solution for high-quality 3D content generation, potentially advancing its practical applications in downstream tasks. The code and model will be publicly available at: https://mengmouxu.github.io/SceneGen. “Everything you can imagine is real.” —Pablo Picasso

title: NVC-GS: Monocular Dynamic Scene Reconstruction via Normal-Regularized and Multi-View-Consistent 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Huiwen Xue, Kaixing Zhao, Tingcheng Li, Tao Xu, Zuheng Ming
abstract: High-quality, real-time dynamic scene reconstruction and rendering are essential for immersive applications. While techniques like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) succeed in static scenes, dynamic monocular scenarios still suffer from deformation, surface noise, and inconsistent view-dependent effects due to insufficient geometric constraints and inadequate multi-view consistency enforcement for monocular input. To address these challenges, we present Normal-Regularized and Multi-View-Consistent Gaussian Splatting (NVC-GS), a novel approach combining geometry-aware normal regularization with diffusion-based multi-view consistency. Our method explicitly preserves geometric consistency in dynamic objects through tailored normal constraints, while leveraging diffusion-driven latent space regularization to ensure cross-view rendering consistency, particularly for complex materials such as reflective surfaces. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach effectively improves geometric accuracy and visual quality in dynamic scenes while maintaining real-time capabilities, outperforming existing methods in terms of deformation handling, surface noise reduction, and rendering of reflective materials.

title: An Empirical Study of MapEx Under Localization Uncertainty: Effects on Ensemble Map Completion and Frontier Selection
authors: Niusha Khosravi, Rodrigo Ventura, Meysam Basiri
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Mobile robots exploring unknown environments must incrementally build maps while selecting actions that efficiently reduce unobserved space. While LiDAR-based Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) can provide accurate occupancy estimates in observed regions, exploration is inherently performed with incomplete maps and is vulnerable to accumulated pose drift. Recent learning-based inpainting models, such as Resolution-robust Large Mask Inpainting (LaMa), can provide plausible completions of unobserved map regions, and the MapEx framework [1] leverages this capability by using an ensemble of predictors to support uncertainty-aware frontier selection. This paper presents an empirical evaluation of the MapEx framework under controlled localization and sensing uncertainty. A “Twin-Pose” simulation methodology is introduced to decouple the robot's physical motion from the pose used for map integration and planning, enabling targeted analysis of how drift-induced misregistration affects (i) the structural consistency of ensemble map completions and (ii) the reliability of frontier selection based on predictive uncertainty. Two exploration strategies implemented in the MapEx pipeline are evaluated on a representative indoor map under multiple injected noise regimes, and mission-level outcomes, together with qualitative evidence of map degradation, are reported. Across the evaluated settings, uncertainty-aware scoring is most effective when pose errors remain small, whereas larger drift produces map aliasing artifacts that disrupt prediction consistency and planning in constrained geometries. These findings highlight the dependence of generative exploration performance on localization quality and motivate tighter coupling between learned completion, driftaware mapping, and active localization.

title: Collaborative Data Completing for Robust SSD Failure Prediction
authors: Yuze Jiang, Shuyue Zhou, Ruiming Lu, Qiao Li, Zhirong Shen, Yiming Zhang
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: Cut-Shortcut Whole-Program Pointer Analysis: Re-imagining Context-Sensitivity without Contexts
authors: Shengyuan Yang, Wenjie Ma, Thomas Reps, Tian Tan, Yue Li
publish: ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems
abstract: None

title: UAV-Assisted Intelligent Power Grid Inspection in Remote Areas: Challenges and Solutions
authors: Sijing Duan, Mingliu Liu, Jieyu Zhou, Lijuan He, Mushu Li, Zaixun Ling, Manjia Liu, Feng Lyu
abstract: Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAV)-assisted intelligent inspection offers a promising solution for maintaining power grids in remote areas, addressing the limitations of traditional methods in terms of safety and efficiency. However, challenges such as limited network coverage, complex trajectory design due to diverse pylon structures, and low defect detection efficiency due to constrained resources still exist. This paper presents a comprehensive framework consisting of three key technologies: UAV-mesh inspection network construction (IPLNet), 3-D point cloud-based trajectory design (PylonNet), and cloud-edge collaborative defect detection. Case studies demonstrate that IPLNet reduces mesh nodes by 30% and nest nodes by 20% compared to baselines. Meanwhile, PylonNet achieves an average F1-score of 0.85, improving performance by up to 54.5%.

title: Multi-Translator FL: Modality-Agnostic Knowledge Construction via Modality Translation in Federated Learning
authors: Sookyeong Kim, Hyungjune Lee
abstract: The paradigm of foundation models has shifted the technology trends through guiding a well-established model towards intelligence across a variety of modalities similar to human intelligence. At the same time, the growth of utilizing edge devices grows rapidly, acquiring multi-modal data at the client sides. However, despite this abundance of data and the unparalleled potential of development in artificial intelligence, the majority of current research has not taken into account effective multi-modal knowledge construction in the distributed resource-scarce device environments. This work aims to bridge the gap between the core ideas of multi-modal research (for yielding general knowledge from additional modalities) and the characteristics of distributed-settings (under limited devices with single modality). We propose a multi-translation federated learning architecture called MTFL, by making the server infuse modality-specific knowledge into a general modality-agnostic knowledge space via one-to-all translation as a bridge and reservoir, expanding the knowledge space across modalities. A single device is able to learn uncovered knowledge derived from a unified knowledge space at the server, while the server with a global model consisting of VAE encoderde-coders, performs modality-agnostic translation. Further, after constructing general modality-agnostic knowledge, MTFL translates the unified model back into modality-specific knowledge tailored to individual clients, facilitating the accommodation of various downstream tasks. The experimental results validate its effectiveness across multiple baselines using the CUB-200-2011, AwA2, Food-101, and MS-COCO datasets. Notably, on the CUB-200-2011, our MTFL performs 84.14% and 44.12% for image and text classification tasks, respectively; in the FL settings, with 82.42% and 42.77%.

title: Comparative Assessment of Graglia-Wilton-Peterson and Rao-Wilton-Glisson Basis Functions for Radiofrequency Coil Modeling in Magnetic Resonance Imaging
authors: Damiano Franzò, Ilias I Giannakopoulos, Adrien Merlini, Francesco Andriulli, Riccardo Lattanzi
publish: IEEE Journal on Multiscale and Multiphysics Computational Techniques
abstract: Accurate modeling of radiofrequency (RF) coils is essential for reliable electromagnetic (EM) field simulations in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). This study evaluates the numerical accuracy of higher order surface basis functions for RF coil simulation within the surface volume integral equation (SVIE) framework. Graglia–Wilton–Peterson (GWP) basis functions and quadratic curvilinear triangular discretization elements replace the conventional Rao–Wilton–Glisson (RWG) basis functions and flat triangular elements used to approximate the coil currents. SVIE simulations show that the solver based on GWP achieves up to two orders of magnitude lower error in the electric field distribution within the coil load for coils without open edges, while no significant performance improvements were observed for coils with open edges. These results demonstrate that higher-order formulations are needed for accurate RF coil modeling in certain scenarios.

title: Detecting Black Hole and Volcano Tidal Patterns in Free-Floating Bike-Sharing Systems: Balancing Computational Efficiency and Accuracy
authors: Wendong Chen, Jiemin Kang, Long Cheng, Mengqiu Cao, Lan Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In the case of free-floating bike-sharing (FFBS) systems, it is essential to be able to detect ‘black holes’, characterised by substantial flow convergence, and ‘volcanoes’, marked by pronounced flow divergence, in order to understand supply-demand imbalances driven by tidal patterns. However, the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) limits the effectiveness of traditional detection methods based on aggregated units such as grids or traffic analysis zones. To overcome this issue, we propose a road-segment-based, two-stage framework that integrates management subzone delineation with tidal pattern detection, thus balancing computational efficiency and identification accuracy. Using Nanjing, China, as a case study, we detect tidal patterns across holidays, weekdays, and weekends. The results show that black holes and volcanoes cluster in midday off-peak and evening peaks during holidays, dominate in the morning and evening peaks on weekdays, and are distributed more evenly across weekends. Land-use analysis indicates that weekday black holes are concentrated around metro stations and employment districts during morning peaks, and around metro stations and residential districts during evening peaks. This reflects the widespread use of FFBS for ‘first/last-mile’ connections between homes or workplaces and metro stations, causing concentrated bike accumulation. Geometric properties further reveal that tidal patterns at the road-segment level exhibit irregular shapes and multi-scale characteristics, which conventional aggregated units fail to capture due to the MAUP. These findings help to improve understanding of FFBS supply-demand interactions and provide detailed policy recommendations for designing targeted FFBS operational strategies to enhance efficiency and service quality.

title: Edge Deployable Weed Detection in UAV Imagery Using YOLOv12 With Scale and Motion Aware Data Augmentation
authors: Murtiza Ali, Paul Abhisek Mondal, Karan Nathwani, Liang Zhao
abstract: Accurate and scalable weed detection is crucial for reducing herbicide use and enhancing the sustainability of soybean production, where input costs are significantly influenced by weed presence. Although uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with red, green, and blue (RGB) cameras offer a practical and low-cost solution for large-area monitoring, the limited spectral richness of RGB imagery makes reliable weed–crop discrimination challenging. This study proposes an integrated agri-food electronic system for UAV-based weed detection in soybean fields, combining an enhanced you only look once (YOLOv12) detection framework, with a novel preprocessing and augmentation pipeline that introduces directional motion blur and altitude simulation via image segmentation and upsampling. These strategies enhance model robustness to scale variation, motion artifacts, and changes in UAV flight altitude, enabling accurate species-level weed identification under realistic field conditions. The modified YOLOv12 s model achieves 96.8% precision, 89.0% recall, and 94.8% mAP@50, while maintaining deployable inference capability on resource-constrained hardware. The trained model is deployed on a Raspberry Pi 4B edge platform through a custom Streamlit-based graphical user interface that performs fully on-device inference without cloud dependency, with general purpose input/output (GPIO)-driven light emitting diode (LED) and buzzer actuation providing real-time hardware-level feedback. On-device benchmarking confirms a 45.7% latency reduction via open neural network exchange (ONNX) Runtime (3282–1783 ms) with thermally stable operation (36 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>C–43 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>C) and power consumption within 5–7 W, validating field deployability. Detection outputs are converted into georeferenced shapefiles to support global positioning system-guided, site-specific herbicide application. The results demonstrate a compact, low-cost, and deployable sensing-to-actuation pipeline that enhances decision-making in precision agriculture while supporting sustainable soybean cultivation.

title: HyStream: A Hybrid System for Application Streaming via Predictive Delivery and Sequence-Linearized Caching
authors: Jun Lu, Sheng Yue, Xiang Liu, Jinrui Zhang, Yongjian Fu, Jialin Li
abstract: Traditional application delivery requires full local installation, introducing persistent security risks from outdated software and imposing significant download delays. While advances in network bandwidth and latency have made remote content delivery more viable, existing dynamic loading mechanisms, such as network filesystems, often remain constrained by performance bottlenecks. Worse still, these solutions degrade sharply under variable or weak connectivity, where untimely code delivery can stall execution altogether. We propose HyStream, a hybrid application streaming system that combines predictive remote delivery with local sequence-linearized caching to sustain responsive and robust execution without requiring installation. HyStream addresses three key challenges: (1) maintaining microsecond-level latency comparable to local storage; (2) bridging the semantic gap between stateless remote storage and stateful execution; and (3) mitigating the limitations of purely network-based solutions under degraded connectivity. To achieve this, HyStream integrates three core components: a dual-mode transmission mechanism that decouples synchronous demand-driven requests from asynchronous speculative prefetching; a thread-aware Markov-chain model that captures fine-grained, concurrent access patterns for accurate prediction; and a sequence-linearized cache that persists streamed blocks in predicted execution order to support deterministic fallback behavior. Together, these components transform irregular, latency-sensitive I/O into efficient structured access that masks network variability. Evaluation shows HyStream delivers near-native performance across diverse networks. On mobile devices, it achieves 16–29% better per-page access latency than local UFS3.1, even over variable Wi-Fi connectivity. On desktops, it typically sustains startup overheads below 30% relative to local NVMe. Under variable and degraded network conditions, the sequence-linearized cache increasingly serves execution-critical accesses, rendering application performance largely insensitive to network latency and jitter within intra-city and inter-city deployments.

title: To Compete or Expand? Offline Retailer's Social Commerce Entry via the Platform Under Consumer Heterogeneity_supp1-3696297.pdf
authors: Hongguang Bo
abstract: Social commerce is disrupting traditional offline retail, yet it also offers opportunities to reach broader consumer segments, thus facilitating new growth. This study develops a game-theoretic model featuring an offline retailer, a social commerce platform, and a market segmented into multichannel comparison (MC) and social-commerce-preferring (SP) consumers. To investigate the impacts of the retailer expanding into social commerce specifically to access SP consumers, we compare two scenarios: scenario C, where the retailer operates offline and competes with the platform, and scenario H, where the retailer expands into the social commerce channel via the platform, which acts as both a competitor and an agent. Our findings reveal that the platform commission, traditionally viewed as a retailer cost, can function as a market coordination mechanism, where a high commission can surprisingly increase the profits of both the retailer and the platform by softening price competition. Furthermore, although a more competitive market is not conducive to the profitability of the two entities, it can lower the commission threshold required for both entities to prefer channel expansion. Critically, while social recommendation boosts the social channel's profit, the resulting cannibalization of the offline channel can be so severe that it ultimately harms the retailer's overall profitability. Finally, we uncover a welfare paradox: while channel expansion offers consumers more choice and social recommendation brings positive utility to MC consumers, the resulting price increase may ultimately harm their surplus, suggesting that the competitive scenario is sometimes more beneficial for consumers.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Cybersecurity: Toward Smarter Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Mujeeb Ur Rehman, Kamran Rehan, Muhammad Abrar, Imran Rehan
abstract: The growing scale and complexity of contemporary cyber threats require intrusion detection systems (IDS) that can operate effectively in high-dimensional feature spaces while maintaining robustness against rare and evolving attack patterns. Classical machine learning techniques, such as support vector machine (SVM) can face increasing limitations in modeling nonlinear decision boundaries under such conditions. This work presents a systematic and reproducible benchmark comparing classical support vector machine (SVM) and quantum support vector machine (QSVM) for nine-class network intrusion detection, covering benign traffic and eight attack categories (DoS Hulk, DDoS, FTP Brute Force, SSH Brute Force, SQL Injection, XSS, PortScan, and Infiltration). The evaluation is conducted on the CICIDS2017 dataset, comprising over 2.8 million labeled network flows. To ensure compatibility with near-term quantum hardware, mutual information (MI) based feature selection is employed to construct a compact yet information-preserving representation, which is encoded into a quantum feature map with an optimized entanglement structure. Experimental evaluation is conducted across two complementary settings. In the binary setting (1,000 balanced samples), classical SVM achieves 99.2% accuracy and 99.1% F1-score against QSVM’s 96.9% accuracy and 96.7% F1-score, an accuracy gap of 2.3 percentage points and an F1-score gap of 2.4 percentage points, reflecting marginal differences in precision–recall balance across the balanced test set. In the primary nine-class multiclass setting (1,800 balanced samples, 200 per class), classical SVM achieves a macro-F1 of 97.3% compared to QSVM’s 95.5%, narrowing the F1 gap to 1.8 percentage points. Critically, per-class analysis reveals that the SVM–QSVM gap narrows to only 0.3% for SQL Injection and Infiltration, the highest-complexity minority classes, suggesting that quantum kernel geometry provides relatively stronger representational capacity precisely for the rare, complex attack patterns of greatest operational significance. The proposed Docker- and Qiskit-based benchmarking framework provides a reproducible and extensible foundation for systematic classical–quantum benchmarking in cybersecurity, establishing a transparent baseline that can guide the design of future hybrid quantum–classical intrusion detection architectures.

title: A Tri-Band Base Station Antenna Array with Wideband Radiation and Dual-Band Electromagnetic Transparency Characteristics
authors: Lin Feng, Jinghui Qiu, Ari Sihvola, Jiaran Qi
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a cross-band decoupled tri-band base station antenna array with wideband radiation and dual-band electromagnetic transparency characteristics. The low-band (LB) antenna is constructed by a miniaturized frequency selective surface (FSS) with a dual-band high transmission property, whose external contour is defined by the shape of a strip-dipole. Together with the dual-band radiating HB antenna elements, experiment results demonstrate that the tri-band antenna array achieves wideband electromagnetic radiation of 630-1250 MHz (66.0%), as well as dual-band electromagnetic transmission of 3.3-3.8 GHz (14.1%) and 4.8-5.0 GHz (4.1%), restoring the distorted radiation patterns of the high-band (HB) antenna elements caused by cross-band coupling in tri-band base station antenna array.

title: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
authors: N/A

title: Predicting Cerebral Aneurysms Rupture From Hemodynamics_supp1-3695167.pdf
authors: Samuel Castonguay
abstract: Cerebral aneurysms are relatively common vascular abnormalities with potentially severe consequences. Although most remain unruptured, a small number do rupture, sometimes leading to subarachnoid hemorrhage. Reliably identifying which aneurysms pose the greatest risk remains an unresolved challenge in clinical practice. In this work, we show that combining computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with statistical testing and basic classification models can help identify features linked to aneurysm rupture and assess rupture risk. Using simulations of 103 aneurysms, we extracted a wide set of hemodynamic metrics, combined with other available geometric, anatomical, and patient-specific information. Using PERMANOVA and simple classification models, we assessed the ability of different datasets to differentiate between ruptured and unruptured cases. Results show that monovariate analyses do not perform especially well, with a prediction accuracy of 65% at best, and that combining features from different categories improves rupture prediction significantly. More specifically, we showed that datasets using non-CFD data reached up to 67% accuracy, while adding flow-based features increased performance to nearly 79%. Some hemodynamic variables also showed stronger statistical relevancy during specific phases of the cardiac cycle, suggesting that transient flow effects may be key in uncovering rupture mechanisms. Overall, our findings demonstrate that combining hemodynamics with statistical techniques enables a robust assessment of rupture risk and offers insights into the underlying mechanisms of rupture. Given rapid advances in image-based hemodynamic analysis using CFD in clinical settings, we anticipate that our approach could be translated into clinical practice to support more informed and individualized rupture risk assessment.

title: 3D CoCa: Contrastive Learners are 3D Captioners
authors: Ting Huang, Zeyu Zhang, Yemin Wang, Hao Tang
abstract: 3D captioning, which aims to describe the content of 3D scenes in natural language, remains highly challenging due to the inherent sparsity of point clouds and weak cross-modal alignment in existing methods. To address these challenges, we propose 3D CoCa, a novel unified framework that seamlessly combines contrastive vision-language learning with 3D caption generation into a single architecture. We design a frozen CLIP vision-language backbone to provide rich semantic priors, a spatially-aware 3D scene encoder to capture geometric context, and a multi-modal decoder to generate descriptive captions. Unlike the prior two-stage methods that rely on explicit object proposals, 3D CoCa jointly optimizes contrastive and captioning objectives in a shared feature space, eliminating the need for external detectors or handcrafted proposals. This joint training paradigm yields stronger spatial reasoning and richer semantic grounding by aligning 3D and textual representations. Extensive experiments on the ScanRefer and Nr3D benchmarks demonstrate that 3D CoCa significantly outperforms current state-of-the-arts by 10.2% and 5.76% in CIDEr@0.5IoU, respectively. Code will be available at https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/3DCoCa.

title: MapAnything: Universal Feed-Forward Metric 3D Reconstruction; map-anything.github.io
authors: Nikhil Keetha, Norman Müller, Johannes Schönberger, Lorenzo Porzi, Yuchen Zhang, Tobias Fischer, Arno Knapitsch, Duncan Zauss, Ethan Weber, Nelson Antunes, Jonathon Luiten, Manue Lopez-Antequera, Samuel Rota Bulò, Christian Richardt, Deva Ramanan, Sebastian Scherer, Peter Kontschieder
abstract: We introduce MapAnything, a unified transformer-based feed-forward model that ingests one or more images along with optional geometric inputs such as camera intrinsics, poses, depth, or partial reconstructions, and directly regresses the metric 3D scene geometry and cameras. MapAnything lever-ages a factored representation of multi-view scene geome-try, i.e., a collection of depth maps, local raymaps, camera poses, and a metric scale factor that effectively upgrades local reconstructions into a globally consistent metric frame. Standardizing the supervision and training across diverse datasets, along with flexible input augmentation, enables MapAnything to address a broad range of 3D vision tasks in a single feed-forward pass, including uncalibrated structure-from-motion, calibrated multi-view stereo, monocular depth estimation, camera localization, depth completion, and more. We provide extensive experimental analyses and model ab-lations demonstrating that MapAnything We provide extensive experimental analyses and model ab-lations demonstrating that MapAnything outperforms or matches specialist feed-forward models while offering more efficient joint training behavior, thus paving the way toward a universal 3D reconstruction backbone.

title: Online Video Depth Anything: Temporally-Consistent Depth Prediction with Low Memory Consumption
authors: Johann-Friedrich Feiden, Tim Küchler, Denis Zavadski, Bogdan Savchynskyy, Carsten Rother
abstract: Depth estimation from monocular video has become a key component of many real-world computer vision systems. Recently, Video Depth Anything (VDA) has demonstrated strong performance on long video sequences. However, it relies on batch-processing which prohibits its use in an online setting. In this work, we overcome this limitation and introduce online VDA (oVDA). The key innovation is to employ techniques from Large Language Models (LLMs), namely, caching latent features during inference and masking frames at training. Our oVDA method outperforms all competing online video depth estimation methods in both accuracy and VRAM usage. Low VRAM usage is particularly important for deployment on edge devices. We demonstrate that oVDA runs at 42 FPS on an NVIDIA A100 and at 20 FPS on an NVIDIA Jetson edge device. We will release both, code and compilation scripts, making oVDA easy to deploy on low-power hardware.

title: SH-SAS: An Implicit Neural Representation for Complex Spherical-Harmonic Scattering Fields for 3D Synthetic Aperture Sonar
authors: Omkar Shailendra Vengurlekar, Adithya Pediredla, Suren Jayasuriya
abstract: Synthetic aperture sonar (SAS) reconstruction requires recovering both the spatial distribution of acoustic scatterers and their direction-dependent response. Time-domain backprojection is the most common 3D SAS reconstruction algorithm, but it does not model directionality and can suffer from sampling limitations, aliasing and occlusion. Prior neural volumetric methods applied to synthetic aperture sonar, e.g. Reed et al. [43], treat each voxel as an isotropic scattering density, not modeling anisotropic returns. We introduce SH-SAS, an implicit neural representation that expresses the complex acoustic scattering field as a set of spherical harmonic (SH) coefficients. A multi-resolution hash encoder feeds a lightweight MLP that outputs complex SH coefficients up to a specified degree L. The zerothorder coefficient acts as an isotropic scattering field, which also serves as the density term, while higher orders compactly capture directional scattering with minimal parameter overhead. Because the model predicts the complex amplitude for any transmit-receive baseline, training is performed directly from 1-D time-of-flight (ToF) signals without the need to beamform intermediate images for supervision. Across synthetic and real SAS (both in-air and underwater) benchmarks, results show that SH-SAS performs better in terms of 3D reconstruction quality and geometric metrics than previous methods such as time-domain backprojection and Reed et al. [43].

title: DyTact: Capturing Dynamic Contacts in Hand-Object Manipulation
authors: Xiaoyan Cong, Angela Xing, Chandradeep Pokhariya, Rao Fu, Srinath Sridhar
abstract: Section 1 introduces the details of our capture procedure for capturing new sequences for DyTact-21 benchmark. Section 2 discusses how we visualize the contact map. Section 3 describes the implementation details of DyTact. Section 4 shows more qualitative comparisons on novel view synthesis. Section 5 provides a detailed related work. Section 6 discusses limitations and future work. We kindly refer readers to our supplementary video for more results.

title: Displacement Measurements on Hyperelastic Materials Using Multiple Object Tracking: a Case Study
authors: Robinson Guachi, Vincent Boon, Flavio Napoleoni, Gian Paolo Currà, Nathaly Chanatasig, Diego H. Peluffo-Ordóñez, Marco Controzzi
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Soft robotics applications increasingly demand accurate measurement techniques for capturing detailed displacement fields in highly deformable, hyperelastic materials. Conventional methods, including strain gauge sensors, are limited in providing full-field strain data and may alter material stiffness, highlighting the need for alternative measurement approaches. This paper presents a methodology based on Multiple Object Tracking that employs paraboloid markers embedded in the surface of soft specimens to enable precise, contactless tracking of displacement fields. The methodology was tested on Dragon Skin<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TM</sup> specimens, mimicking artificial skin for robotic hands, and evaluated through compression experiments using an Instron Universal Testing Machine. Results show that the MOT algorithm yields an average standard deviation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma u=2.23 \times 10^{-2} ~\text{mm}$</tex> in the horizontal direction and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sigma v=1.78 \times 10^{-2} ~\text{mm}$</tex> in the vertical direction, demonstrating promising accuracy relative to traditional techniques, with no significant impact on specimen stiffness from marker integration. This methodology overcomes the limitations of strain gauges by enabling reliable displacement measurements without altering specimen mechanical properties. It provides a valuable tool for validating finite element models, facilitating the refinement of constitutive models, mesh quality, and boundary conditions. Overall, the MOT-based methodology could enhance soft actuator characterization, providing highfidelity data essential for predicting mechanical responses in complex robotic applications.

title: Ambient Analytics: Calm Technology for Immersive Visualization and Sensemaking
authors: Sebastian Hubenschmid, Arvind Srinivasan, Niklas Elmqvist, Dieter Schmalstieg, Michael Sedlmair
abstract: Augmented reality has great potential for embedding data visualizations in the world around the user. While this can enhance users’ understanding of their surroundings, it also bears the risk of overwhelming their senses with a barrage of information. In contrast, calm technologies aim to place information in the user’s attentional periphery, minimizing cognitive load instead of demanding focused engagement. In this article, we explore how visualizations can be harmoniously integrated into our everyday life through augmented reality, progressing from visual analytics to ambient analytics.

title: Design of a MEMS Heating Film with Octagonal Multipole Magnetic Moment for Miniaturized SERF Magnetometers
authors: Linwei Zhang, Xiangyang Zhou, Chuanming Yin, Weiqian Wang
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Spin-Exchange Relaxation-Free (SERF) atomic magnetometers exhibit exceptionally high sensitivity. However, their performance is seriously influenced by magnetic interference and non-uniformity temperature induced by electrical heating of the vapor cell. Consequently, designing a heating device with minimized magnetic interference and thermal uniformity is critical for enhancing sensor sensitivity. To achieve a trade-off between magnetic suppression and thermal homogeneity, this paper proposes a micro-electro-mechanical system (MEMS) heating coil design method with an octagonal multipole magnetic moment for miniaturized SERF magnetometers. First, a magnetic field model of the coil is established, alongside constraints for thermal distribution uniformity. Subsequently, a multi-objective nonlinear optimization framework based on the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA- II) algorithm is employed to optimize coil geometric parameters. Finite Element Analysis (FEA) validates the effectiveness of the proposed structure. The results indicate that, compared to the traditional equid-width square coil, the optimized octagonal coil design reduces the average temperature deviation of the working surface by 16.4 % to 0.188° C, while simultaneously reducing the average magnetic flux density by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2. 7} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 2. 7 ~ p T} / \mathbf{m A}$</tex>.

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Multi-Class Global Weather Classification with Mutual-Information-Driven Features
authors: Sidratul Muntaha, Ashim Dey
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Weather condition classification is still challenging because atmospheric processes are nonlinear and stochastic, whereas traditional numerical weather prediction models require high computational costs and less accuracy for short-term local forecasting. In this work, the challenges were overcome by constructing a leakage-safe, imbalance-aware ML pipeline for multi-class weather classification based on the World Weather Repository dataset containing more than 90,000 entries over global capital cities with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 0 +}$</tex> meteorological features. Systematic preprocessing, redundancy reduction, feature engineering, and mutual information-driven feature choice are followed by both conventional classifiers (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Naïve Bayes, SVM, k-NN) as well as ensemble learners (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Bagging, XGBoost, CatBoost) evaluation along with hard and soft voting meta-ensemble. Experiments show ensemble methodologies significantly beating traditional models, where the best performance is attained using XGBoost, achieving an accuracy of 92.8 % and an F 1 -score of 92.6 %. It was verified through the experiment that the ensemble-based ML models not only exhibit higher accuracy but also provide more robust probability estimates that turn out crucial for risksensitive applications like disaster management and aviation. Although minority classes continue challenging prospects because such classes significantly outnumber the majority ones due to extreme imbalance, the experiment contributes a reproducible benchmark pipeline applicable for extension using resampling measures as well as physics-informed ML hybrids.

title: MonoSISTR: Monocular 3D Object Detection via Staged Iterative Structure and Target Refinement
authors: Genlin Zhou, Cheng Feng, Feng Lu, Zige Wang, Zhen Chen, Congxuan Zhang
abstract: Monocular 3D object detection aims to predict object category, position, size, and orientation from a single RGB image. Existing DETR-based monocular 3D detectors suffer from maintaining consistent high-confidence responses due to weak or incomplete target features, resulting in information loss for distant and occluded objects during encoding-decoding. Firstly, to address the core challenge of insufficient target feature perception in complex scenes, we propose a staged iterative monocular 3D detector that progressively refines targets from coarse to fine through multiple paired encoding-decoding stages, significantly improving both feature utilization and network convergence. Furthermore, each stage integrates a dynamic target iteration module that continuously enhances query representation by focusing on high-confidence regional features, thereby enhancing the model's perception of potential targets. Finally, we design a dual-branch depth estimator with parallel global and local processing for a comprehensive representation of the depth feature. Experimental results show that our method achieves superior performance over prior approaches on challenging scenarios (e.g., distant and occluded objects) in the KITTI dataset without auxiliary data, while maintaining competitive accuracy on the nuScenes benchmark under frontal-view settings.

title: Governance Framework for Resilient Critical Infrastructure
authors: Taiwo Peter Akinremi, Jacques Bou Abdo, Jacques Demerjian
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Water and wastewater infrastructure and systems continue to be targeted by threat actors amid rapid digital transformation, yet governance mechanisms lag behind technical security measures. To address this shortcoming, this paper proposes a vulnerability-informed governance framework that bridges technical vulnerability analysis with regulatory and management functions for cyber-resilient water and wastewater infrastructure. Based on the existing water governance literature and system dynamics modeling of cyber-physical vulnerabilities from four water and wastewater architectures: SWRO, SWAT, Wastewater, and CENTER, this study developed a five-step approach mapping vulnerability to governance functions and interventions. The framework integrates regulation functions with management arrangements while incorporating adaptive governance attributes essential for resilience. This study presents a vulnerability-to-policy mapping methodology and implementation pathways through first-order (enabling conditions), secondorder (behavioral change), and third and fourth-order (measurable resilience improvements) outcomes. This work contributes a systematic approach for translating cybersecurity vulnerability findings into actionable governance and cybersecurity governance instruments for cyber-physical systems of a water infrastructure.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of Power-Efficient Compact BCD to Seven Segment Decoder Using Pass Transistor Logic
authors: Saquib Farhan, Tanjida Bintee Jafar, Md Tawfiq Amin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The BCD to Seven Segment Decoder is an essential component in digital systems for converting binary coded decimal data into signals for seven segment displays. This paper proposes a power efficient decoder design using Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) to minimize power consumption and transistor count which makes it suitable for low power and small area applications. The proposed design demonstrates significant improvements by achieving power consumption of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16.87 \mu ~\mathrm{W}$</tex> with buffers and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.723 \mu ~\mathrm{W}$</tex> without, with a transistor count of 88 and 60, respectively. The layout occupies only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$286 \mu ~\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> which demonstrates high area efficiency. Compared with a transmission-gate-based approach, the proposed PTL implementation achieves an area reduction of approximately 29.6 %. The decoder design and simulation were validated using Cadence Virtuoso with the GPDK90 process technology. The proposed PTL decoder outperforms conventional CMOS and other logic designs, offering a cost effective compact solution for embedded systems and Internet of Things (IoT) applications.

title: Design and Forecast Optimization of a Household Solar Pv and Energy Storage System with Machine Learning Methods
authors: Riad Mollik babu, Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, Md Hasibur Rahman, Md Shahidul Islam, Md. Shofiqul Islam, Atika Farhana
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study presents an automatic framework that assists to select the best size for solar PV panels and a battery energy storage system (BESS) so that a home can operate without using the grid. The data in this study were collected from the Dhaka Electric Supply Company Ltd (DESCO), the onsite recordings by watt meters, and the POWER database from NASA. We have obtained hourly forecasts for both the PV system and the load using the available data. Observing the statistical parameter <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> for all curves, it was found that random forest regression did better than linear regression, leading to 91 % accuracy in modeling power generation and 90 % in predicting power usage. After reviewing the forecasted load-generation profiles, the energy shortfall analysis showed that the BESS should be able to provide 299 kWh. Since the home requires more energy, the PV array was expanded from 11 kW to 150 kW. The framework is designed to create secure and grid-independent home.

title: Robust Blood Cell Detection and Counting Using Efficient Channel Attention-Integrated YOLOv10
authors: Rubaiya Reza Sohana, Shakil Ahmed, Sumaiya Binte Shahid, Rashidul Hasan Hridoy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Automated blood cell analysis plays a crucial role in early disease diagnosis. Traditional microscopic examination remains labor-intensive and prone to subjective variability. In this study, we have proposed an enhanced YOLOv10-based detection framework integrated with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) to classify and count red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets from peripheral blood smear images. The ECA mechanism improves channel-wise feature selection without introducing computational overhead, enabling more accurate discrimination of morphologically similar cell types. Additionally, we have introduced an improved preprocessing pipeline, Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) that enhances local contrast in an image along with other techniques. The final model achieves an accuracy of approximately 97.00 % across all classes, demonstrating superior robustness in both detection and categorization. These results highlight the potential of attention-enhanced lightweight architectures for clinical hematological analysis and pave the way for reliable and rapid diagnostic support.

title: Tea Leaf Disease Classification and Out-of-Distribution Detection Using Deep Learning
authors: Anika Islam, Md. Ahasanul Arafath, Abhijit Kumar Ghosh, Tawsif Mustasin Rafin, Turjoy Das, Simantha Saha, Fateha Jannat Ayrin, Mizanur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Tea production plays a vital role in the agricultural economy of Bangladesh, where the quality and yield of tea leaves are significantly affected by various leaf diseases. Manual inspection for disease identification is often time-consuming, labor-intensive, and prone to human error, necessitating an automated and reliable detection system. This study proposes a deep learning-based framework for multi-class tea leaf disease classification and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) recognition using the EfficientNet-B0 backbone augmented with a prototype-based classification head (ProtoHead). The model was trained and evaluated on the publicly available TeaLeafBD dataset containing 5,278 images across seven classes, including one OOD category. The proposed system integrates extensive data augmentation, stratified sampling, and energy-based OOD scoring to enhance generalization and interpretability. Experimental results demonstrate that the EfficientNet-B2 variant achieved the highest balanced accuracy of 97.87 %, outperforming contemporary architectures such as MobileNet-V3 and SqueezeNetV3. Comparative analysis further indicates superior performance over prior CNN and YOLO-based models in similar domains. Visualization through prototype activation maps and t-SNE feature embeddings confirms the model's interpretability and robustness. Overall, the proposed framework provides an accurate, explainable, and computationally efficient solution for automated tea leaf disease detection and OOD recognition in real-world agricultural settings.

title: Comparative Study of Hyperparameter Optimization Techniques for Malware Detection
authors: Sumaiya Kabir, Nabiha Parvez, Sumaiya Yeasmin, Nusrat Sharmin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In recent times, the use of malware obfuscation techniques has increased, which threatens traditional signature based detection systems. These techniques hide their presence to avoid being identified and put security of the system at risk. This paper offers a machine learning based method for detecting obfuscated malware through memory dump analysis with improved hyperparameter tuning. The study uses a balanced dataset of 58,596 memory dump records from the CIC-MalMem2022 collection. This dataset has an equal number of benign and malicious processes. It includes 55 extracted features and uses two labeling systems to classify processes as malicious or benign. Binary classification was performed to distinguish between benign and malware processes. Six machine learning algorithms were evaluated: Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, AdaBoost, CatBoost, and LightGBM. To improve model performance, five techniques for tuning hyperparameters were used: Grid Search, Random Search, Bayesian Optimization, Simulated Annealing and Particle Swarm Optimization(PSO). Each algorithm was chosen for its effectiveness in handling complex classification tasks and its ability to learn intricate patterns from hidden malware data. Memory analysis offers better detection capabilities than traditional methods by revealing run-time behaviors, process interactions and system changes that obfuscated malware cannot easily hide. The results show that using machine learning based memory dump analysis with hyperparameters tuning provides a strong and efficient way to identify complex hidden malware variants that often slip past traditional detection methods.

title: Demand-Aware Multi-Area Multi-UAV Empowered Mobile Edge Computing: A Joint Energy and Delay Optimization
authors: Chaoda Peng, Yanglin Chen, Xumin Huang, Zexiong Wu, Yueting Xu, Yuan Wu
abstract: Multi-UAV-assisted mobile edge computing (MEC) has emerged as a promising paradigm for smart city infrastructures, where multiple uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) collaboratively provide airborne computing services by bringing computational resources closer to end users, thereby reducing both communication costs and service latency. Existing multi-UAV-assisted MEC approaches typically employ fixed allocation schemes that predetermine the number of UAVs for a service area, failing to adapt to heterogeneous regional demands and leaving substantial UAV fleet capacity underutilized. To address this issue, we consider a multi-area multi-UAV-assisted MEC scenario where the control center jointly optimizes UAV deployment, user association, and resource allocation for each service area based on actual regional demands. This optimization enables multiple UAVs to be allocated to high-demand areas while assigning fewer UAVs to low-demand areas, thereby maximizing the service capacity of the entire UAV fleet. We formulate the optimization problem as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem (CMOP) that simultaneously minimizes the total system energy consumption and average user task completion delay, subject to deployment, user association, resource capacity, and task completion delay constraints. To effectively solve this CMOP, we propose a constrained multi-objective evolutionary algorithm that systematically reconstructs infeasible solutions into feasible ones through a constraint-guided solution reconstruction mechanism, thereby accelerating convergence toward feasible regions. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms five state-of-the-art baseline methods in solution diversity and convergence performance. The results validate the effectiveness of flexible UAV allocation strategies, establishing a foundation for demand-aware resource provisioning in multi-UAV-assisted MEC systems.

title: Regional Output-Feedback Control for T–S Fuzzy Systems Using Lyapunov Functions With Polynomial Dependency on States and Membership Functions
authors: Yara Quilles-Marinho, Donghwan Lee, Ricardo C. L. F. Oliveira, Pedro L. D. Peres
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article addresses the problem of local output-feedback control design for continuous-time Takagi–Sugeno fuzzy systems using the parallel distributed compensation (PDC) approach. The main contribution is the introduction of a novel class of Lyapunov functions with polynomial dependence on both the system states and the membership functions (MFs), providing less conservative stability and synthesis conditions. To compute the PDC gains, a locally convergent iterative algorithm based on linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) is proposed. Unlike many existing methods, the approach eliminates the need for bounds on the time derivatives of MFs by explicitly incorporating their dynamics into the stability conditions. The algorithm operates in two phases: first, optimizing the decay rate of the closed-loop trajectories, andsecond, maximizing the estimated domain of attraction (DOA) under a fixed decay rate. Comparative numerical examples based on benchmark systems demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method over existing techniques, particularly as the polynomial degree of the Lyapunov function increases.

title: Agentic AI for 6G Satellite Networks: Achieving Scalable and Low-Latency Resource Scheduling With the AERS Framework
authors: Jiacheng Shen, Zhi Lin, Kefeng Guo, Ruiqian Ma, Chen Han, Yifu Sun, Kang An, Mohammad Tabrez Quasim, Shakila Basheer, Yuanzhi He
abstract: Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite networks are essential for enabling global 6G connectivity, but their resource scheduling faces fundamental challenges from limited visibility windows, constrained onboard power, and a scarcity of beams relative to diverse service demands. Existing schemes struggle to simultaneously meet real-time processing requirements with computational constraints while ensuring user quality-of-service (QoS). To address these issues, this paper introduces AERS (Agentic Energy-Aware Resource Scheduling), a decentralized framework grounded in Agentic AI principles, including autonomy, adaptability, proactivity, and coordination. In AERS, each beam operates as an independent agent that collaboratively optimizes beam hopping, user scheduling, and power allocation based on local observations. Coordination is achieved implicitly through shared rewards and environmental coupling, such as co-channel interference and power constraints, without requiring continuous interaction between agents. Case studies show that AERS achieves superior tradeoffs between delay and packet loss, while achieving a 2.98× inference speedup compared with centralized methods, making it highly suitable for satellite onboard deployment. This work establishes a foundational paradigm for employing Agentic AI in satellite resource management, offering a scalable and efficient solution for future 6G satellite networks.

title: DST-CR: A Dual Sparse Transformer Network for Cross-Modality Cloud Removal via SAR-Optical Fusion
authors: Zhanghui Li, Jun Pan, Yumin Chen, Jiangong Xu, Fei Zeng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The presence of cloud contamination can vary significantly across different bands and regions of the optical image, affecting the usability of the data. The presence of speckle noise in SAR data, in conjunction with a substantial number of cloud-contaminated pixels in cloudy data, can result in blurring, artifacts, and other undesirable effects on the reconstruction results. To tackle these challenges, this paper proposes an innovative dual sparse transformer network for cross-modality cloud removal (DST-CR), which effectively captures spatial and channel dimensional features from both optical and SAR data and deeply fuses them while suppressing the interference caused by clouds and their shadows in optical images, as well as the speckle noise in SAR data, to reconstruct cloud-free optical imagery. In DST-CR, a spatial–channel collaborative framework based on cross-domain feature selection is proposed to enable joint feature selection and interaction modeling in both spatial and channel domains. Specifically, the sparse spatial transformer (SST) and sparse channel transformer (SCT) are serially integrated for progressive feature modeling and fusion. Furthermore, to achieve adaptive selection of cross-domain features, the gated local feature extraction module (GLFE), the sparse global attention mechanism, and the dual selective feed-forward network (DSFN) are introduced to effectively suppress cloud noise in cloudy regions as well as speckle noise in SAR images. Furthermore, a novel multi-perceptual loss function is developed to preserve two key aspects of the reconstructed imagery: spectral characteristics and structural details. The experimental results on two publicly available datasets—SEN12MS-CR and LuojiaSET-OSFCR—demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits superior performance in comparison to existing state-of-the-art cloud removal techniques. Excellent results and stability are demonstrated especially in high cloud coverage images.

title: A 0.05 Hz∼150 Hz bandwidth, 0.215 μVrms Input-Referred-Noise Three-Opamp Instrumentation Amplifier for Consumer Bio-Signal Acquisition
authors: Penghai Li, Xudong Li, Zhi Li, Yihang Luo, Quanzhen Duan
abstract: This paper proposes a chopper-stabilized three-opamp IA with an active high-pass filter (HPF) for biological signal acquisition. An equivalent resistance amplification technique is employed to construct a second-order high-pass filtering network, which significantly reduces the area on the chip occupied by resistors and capacitors and improves the level of circuit integration. In addition, an improved chopper modulation architecture is adopted to realize low-frequency noise and offset suppression, thus enabling high-precision signal acquisition. The proposed IA is fabricated in a standard 180 nm CMOS process. Under a 3.3 V supply voltage, the power consumption is only 1.281 mW.
