abstract: Moodify is an IoT-powered wearable system for comprehensive health and mood monitoring using a sensor-equipped Smart Mask and Android application. The platform integrates heart rate, SpO2, temperature, and humidity sensing, processed by an ESP32 microcontroller that supports both BLE and Wi-Fi connectivity modes for seamless data transmission. Real-time sensor data is analyzed by a personalized, rule-based mood detection algorithm, providing immediate visual feedback, mood-specific notifications, and triggering therapeutic music recommendations through a mobile interface. Data synchronization with Firebase cloud ensures remote accessibility and continuous user history tracking. By combining multi-modal physiology monitoring, intelligent mood inference, and actionable wellness interventions, Moodify advances personalized healthcare and mental well-being with intuitive, real-time guidance.

title: Sex- and Age-Stratified Structural MRI Classification for Autism Spectrum Disorder Using Pretrained ResNet-50
authors: Nusrat Jahan Jerin, Rayna Islam Rayna, Farjana Parvin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This research focuses on classifying autism spectrum disorder (ASD) by analyzing structural magnetic resonance imaging (sMRI) data stratified by sex and age groups, including children, adolescents, and adults. A pretrained Residual Neural Network (ResNet-50) processes middle 2D slices of sMRI volumes, enabling subgroup-specific model evaluation on the publicly available ABIDE dataset. Stratified 5-fold cross-validation reveals significant classification performance differences among subgroups, with female children demonstrating the highest average area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), reaching a value of 0.782. These findings highlight the critical role of biological heterogeneity in ASD neuroimaging classification and motivate further exploration of advanced modeling techniques tailored to demographic-specific characteristics.

title: Turbulence Blur Text Extraction with Large Reasoning Models Ensemble Attention & Context Engineering
authors: Fateha Jannat Ayrin, Muhammad Anwarul Azim, Mohammad Khairul Islam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Atmospheric turbulence severely degrades text visibility in long-range optical imaging, rendering traditional optical character recognition (OCR) nearly ineffective with accuracy rates of only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6}-\mathbf{1 2} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex>. Standard vision-language models (VLMs) show marginal improvement at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 - 1 5 \%}$</tex> accuracy, revealing fundamental limitations of pattern-matching approaches for severely distorted images. This paper introduces a novel framework that leverages Large Reasoning MultiModals (LRMs) through an attention-guided ensemble architecture with context engineering and chain-of-thought prompting. Our approach integrates seven state-of-the-art reasoning models Gemini 2.5 Flash, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, DeepSeek-V3.1 (671 B hybrid reasoning model), GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GLM-4.1V-Thinking, and Kimi-VL-combining their complementary reasoning capabilities through weighted consensus aggregation. The system employs a multi-scale spatial attention mechanism to identify text regions (achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16-34 \%$</tex> coverage) and comprehensive context engineering to guide systematic text reconstruction. our ensemble achieves 84.7 % character accuracy, representing a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9.8 \times$</tex> improvement over traditional OCR and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.2 \times$</tex> improvement over standard VLMs. Among individual models, DeepSeekV3.1's hybrid reasoning architecture achieves the highest singlemodel accuracy (61.8 %), demonstrating the effectiveness of large-scale mixture-of-experts architectures with thinking modes. This work establishes that reasoning capabilities, rather than pattern matching, are essential for text extraction under severe atmospheric distortion, opening new directions for robust visual understanding in challenging imaging conditions.

title: Mental Health Detection Using Late Fusion Transformer Models With Llm Chat Integration
authors: Riad Rahman, Md Saifullah, Natasha Bose, Rubaiya Hafiz
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Mental health issues like depression, anxiety, and stress require screening systems that are reliable, easy to understand, and able to function effectively in real-world settings. In this study, we present a multilingual, security-conscious textbased framework built on a dual-encoder late-fusion architecture. This system combines the DistilBERT and XLM-RoBERTa models and merges their outputs using a learnable softmax-based weighting mechanism at the logit level. We fine-tune only the final layers of the encoders, using AdamW optimization, scheduled learning-rate adjustments, and gradient-stability controls, allowing the system to run efficiently on CPUs or lightweight GPUs. The framework also includes an interpretability pipeline that reveals the behavior of each component, the logic behind fusion, and overall performance metrics. Additionally, a complementary large language model (LLM) module provides nonclinical guidance after detecting key linguistic features. The fused model achieves an accuracy of 76.68 % (weighted <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F} 1: 76.54 \%$</tex>), demonstrating the advantages of combining late-stage representations. In summary, these results highlight a transparent, responsive approach to integrating AI-based tools into mental health screening processes.

title: Performance Analysis of MIMO-NOMA Systems with MRT Beamforming and Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces
authors: Mahmuda Khatun, Akash Biswas, Md. Farhad Hossain, Md. Torikul Islam, Md. Hazrat Ali, Rubab Ahmmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper focuses on the performance analysis of downlink MIMO - NOMA systems combining Maximum Ratio Transmission (MRT) beamforming and Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS) assistance. We suggest a single system model, in which direct and cascaded BS-RIS-users, residual interference consequences brought by an imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC), and practical power-domain NOMA power allocation are modelled. From this model we obtain the closed-form SINR expressions unequivocally including RIS phase shifts and imperfect SIC which we use to achieve analytical outage characterizations. Analytical results are validated by Monte-Carlo simulations with 4 -users case (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Nt} = \mathrm{4}$</tex>), 32 elements RIS, and show in a perceptible trend that, MRT has concentrated array gain effects and significant outage reduction for the beam-targeted (near) user, while RIS+MRT will yield the best system wide protection and fairness, by harmoniously grouping the reflected and direct paths- resulting in dramatic reduction of outage to cell edge users. We also quantify practical effects as, for example, we see different relative outage reductions from RIS+MRT (31 % on our sample results for the strongest user with 20 dB) compared to the perfect SIC due to the error floor effect. The paper concludes with some design recommendations for RIS and research directions into joint RIS/beamforming/power optimization, robust CSI estimation and experimental validation to unleash the full potential of RIS-aided MIMO-NOMA in 6G networks of the future.

title: Automated Knowledge Graph Construction From Unstructured Medical Data
authors: Fatma Safi, Fadi Kacem, Walid Jallouli, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: In the era of rapidly advancing Artificial Intelligence (AI) and big data, there is an acute need to structure and represent the vast volume of available knowledge in a clear, efficient, and meaningful manner. Knowledge graphs (KGs), which encode entities and the relationships among them, have emerged as a powerful and rapidly expanding area of research. Medical KGs have shown significant potential across a wide range of healthcare applications, including clinical decision-support systems and drug recommendation. In this paper, we develop a coherent system that is able to extract and manage medical knowledge from unstructured medical data. Based on our system, we build a high quality medical KG with 663 entity and 861 relation instances. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of using compact multimodal models for clinical knowledge extraction, paving the way for scalable and intelligent medical data management.

title: Exploring Open-Weight Foundation Models: A Systematic Review from Transformers to Multimodal AI
authors: Chaima Tayachi, Olfa Dridi, Mohamed Barkaoui, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Foundation models have transformed artificial intelligence by enabling large-scale pretraining on diverse datasets, which supports a wide range of subsequent tasks. While many of the most capable models remain closed-access, the emergency of open-weight foundation model where trained parameters are publicly released has facilitated research reproducibility, domain adaptation and community driven innovation. In this survey, we provide a systematic overview of open-weight models across language, vision and multimodal modalities. We introduce a taxonomy based on architecture, modality and openness, examine transformer-based foundations and modern training pipelines and provide a comparative analysis of major model families including LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, BLOOM, Stable Diffusion and LLaVA-style architectures. We also review standard and multimodal evaluation benchmarks, discuss performance gaps versus closed models and highlight emerging challenges related to security, misuse, compute inequality and AI sovereignty. By synthesizing current developments and technical foundations, this survey offers researchers, practitioners and policymakers a structured framework for understanding the capabilities, limitations and strategic implications of open-weight foundation models.

title: ELAP: End-to-End Latency Prediction for Edge Computing Applications
authors: Akash Cuntur Shrinivasmurthy, Ali Kadhum Idrees, Jaime Sebastian Burbano, Rolf Schuster
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: End-to-end (E2E) latency is a vital performance indicator for edge computing applications, especially for latencysensitive services like autonomous systems, real-time video analytics, and interactive IoT apps. Precise forecasting of E2E latency, involving sensing, data transmission, computing at edge nodes, and response delivery, is crucial for efficient utilisation of resources, placement of service, and service quality guarantee. This paper proposes an E2E latency prediction (ELaP) for edge computing applications. ELaP employs a hybrid CNN-LSTM deep learning model to predict the E2E latency incurred when transmitting a video frame from the client to the edge server and receiving the corresponding notification. ELaP will consider metrics from the edge computing network, such as client, edge server, network, and application, including CPU and network load conditions. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that ELaP reduced the MAE by approximately 86 % compared with the optimal classical baseline and by about 39.5 % compared to the most efficient standalone deep learning model (LSTM) using the YOLOv8 application.

title: A Speech-Aware Edge System for Adaptive Acoustic Masking in Iot Devices
authors: Saeed Serwan Abdulsattar, Mohab A. Mangoud, Fayzeh Abdulkareem Jaber
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Noise pollution remains a persistent environmental and public-health challenge, while most existing mitigation solutions rely on static playback strategies or threshold-based alerts that fail to adapt to dynamic acoustic conditions. This paper presents a fully on-device, privacy-preserving acoustic masking system that operates entirely at the network edge and explicitly protects human speech. The proposed framework integrates perceptually grounded feature extraction, lightweight neural classification, and probabilistic control to enable contextaware noise mitigation in real time. Audio is processed locally using a compact convolutional neural network operating on log-Mel representations derived from short-time analysis windows. The system employs a rolling buffer of 2.0 s at a sampling rate of 16 kHz and executes inference at fixed intervals to balance responsiveness and computational efficiency. Mask activation is governed by temporally smoothed posterior probabilities and a speech-protection mechanism with hysteresis, ensuring that speech is never masked, transmitted, or reconstructed. The classifier is trained on the MUSAN dataset and evaluated on an independent benchmark derived from LibriSpeech, comprising 4034 audio segments. Experimental results demonstrate an overall accuracy of 96.48 % and a macro-F1 score of 0.9613, with a false speech masking rate below 3 %. All processing runs in real time on IoT-class hardware, specifically a Raspberry Pi 5, without cloud connectivity. These results confirm that adaptive, perceptually aligned noise mitigation can be achieved with low latency and strong privacy guarantees. The proposed architecture offers a scalable and deployable solution for smart buildings, healthcare environments, and residential spaces.

title: iTACO: Interactable Digital Twins of Articulated Objects from Casually Captured RGBD Videos
authors: Weikun Peng, Jun Lv, Cewu Lu, Manolis Savva
abstract: Articulated objects are prevalent in daily life. Interactable digital twins of such objects have numerous applications in embodied AI and robotics. Unfortunately, current methods to digitize articulated real-world objects require carefully captured data, preventing practical, scalable, and generalizable acquisition. We focus on motion analysis and part-level segmentation of an articulated object from a casually captured RGBD video shot with a hand-held camera. A casually captured video of an interaction with an articulated object is easy to obtain at scale using smartphones. However, this setting is challenging due to simultaneous object and camera motion and significant occlusions as the person interacts with the object. To tackle these challenges, we introduce iTACO: a coarse-to-fine framework that infers joint parameters and segments movable parts of the object from a dynamic RGBD video. To evaluate our method under this new setting, we build a dataset of 784 videos containing 284 objects across 11 categories that is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \times$</tex> larger than available in prior work. We then compare our approach with existing methods that also take video as input. Our experiments show that iTACO outperforms existing articulated object digital twin methods on both synthetic and real casually captured RGBD videos.

title: DeltaVisionX: A Low-Cost Vision-Guided Automation System Using YOLOv11 and PLC-Based Actuation
authors: Lara Sobh, Marc Nahhas, Francisco Katach El Halabi, Michel Owayjan, Fadi Elias Sleiman
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents DeltaVisionX, a low-cost industrial automation system that integrates real-time computer vision with PLC-based actuation for object classification and sorting. The system uses the YOLOv11 (You Only Look Once) deep learning model to detect objects on an operating conveyor belt and communicates detection results to a Delta DVP-SE PLC, which actuates pneumatic pistons for sorting. A custom dataset was created, labeled, and augmented to train the YOLOv11 model, achieving a mean average precision of 0.97 with real-time inference of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 0 ~ m s}$</tex> per frame. Encoder-based tracking ensures reliable synchronization between detection and actuation, supporting a throughput of 80 objects per minute. Results show that affordable, locally developed automation can reduce labor dependency and improve manufacturing efficiency. The novelty of the proposed system lies in its encoder-synchronized vision-PLC integration rather than in the detection model itself. Future work includes integrating a high-speed Delta robot with EtherCAT-based servo control for advanced pick-and-place operations.

title: ISAC-Empowered Air–Sea Collaborative System: A UAV–USV Joint Inspection Framework
authors: Rui Zhang, Fuwang Dong, Wei Wang
abstract: In this paper, we construct an air-sea collaborative system framework based on the integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) techniques, where the uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) and uncrewed surface vehicle (USV) jointly inspect targets of interest while keeping communication with each other simultaneously. We first demonstrate the unique challenges encountered in this collaborative system, i.e., the coupling and heterogeneity of the UAV/USV’s trajectories. By applying the hover-and-fly strategy, we formulate a total energy minimization problem to jointly optimize the trajectories, time durations, target scheduling, and beamforming, subject to the constraints of motion states, sensing quality, and communication rate requirements. To handle the strong coupling among variables, the problem is decomposed into two subproblems: hover-point selection and joint trajectory planning with beamforming design. The first subproblem is formulated as a novel bi-traveling salesman problem with neighborhoods (Bi-TSPN). To solve this NP-hard problem, we develop a three-step hierarchical method to successively determine hover-point location and target scheduling, optimize the visiting order of hover-points, and allocate the time duration. For second subproblem, the remaining trajectory planning and beamforming design are addressed in each hover-and-fly stage using semidefinite relaxation (SDR) and successive convex approximation (SCA) methods. Finally, we conduct a series of simulations to demonstrate the superiority of the proposed scheme over existing sequential access, leader–follower, and fly-and-sense strategies.

title: IEEE Recommended Practice for Voltage-Endurance Testing of Form-Wound Bars and Coils
authors: N/A
abstract: A comprehensive framework for the voltage-endurance testing of form-wound bars and coils used in large rotating machine stator windings is provided in this recommended practice. The procedures for conducting voltage endurance tests on bars/coils with nominal voltage up to 30 000 V, and operating frequencies of 50 Hz or 60 Hz, are outlined. Power supply and voltage requirements, test set-up, specimen preparation, pre-test evaluation methods, as well as temperature and voltage application and measurement methods are specified. Guidelines for failure investigations, suggested test records, and test report requirements are provided.

title: Air Spring for Precision Manufacturing Considering Gas-Solid Coupling Effects in Metal Bellows
authors: Mingkai Wu, Jiulin Wu, Xiaoqing Li, Wei Jiang
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Air springs are widely used in precision instrumentation due to their excellent low-frequency vibration isolation performance, enabling the isolation of harmful vibrations. Common air springs consist of a gas chamber and an elastic rubber diaphragm. When paired with flexible hinge mechanisms and magnetic negative stiffness mechanisms, they can form six-degree-of-freedom ultra-low-frequency vibration isolation systems. However, in extreme environments such as vacuum conditions, rubber diaphragm-based air springs become unsuitable due to contamination risks. To address this issue, this paper proposes an air spring incorporating a metal bellows. The metal bellows air spring comprises a gas chamber and multiple welded corrugated plates. A stiffness model for the proposed air spring is constructed based on the gas-solid coupling effect between the metal corrugated plates and high-pressure gas. Finally, simulation results validate the accuracy of the stiffness model and confirm its applicability in the ultra-low-frequency domain.

title: An Integer Linear Programming Approach to Geometrically Consistent Partial-Partial Shape Matching
authors: Viktoria Ehm, Paul Roetzer, Florian Bernard, Daniel Cremers
abstract: The task of establishing correspondences between two 3D shapes is a long-standing challenge in computer vision. While numerous studies address full-full and partial-full 3D shape matching, only a limited number of works have explored the partial-partial setting, very likely due to its unique challenges: we must compute accurate correspondences while at the same time find the unknown overlapping region. Nevertheless, partial-partial 3D shape matching reflects the most realistic setting, as in many real-world cases, such as 3D scanning, shapes are only partially observable. In this work, we introduce the first integer linear programming approach specifically designed to address the distinctive challenges of partial-partial shape matching. Our method leverages geometric consistency as a strong prior, enabling both robust estimation of the overlapping region and computation of neighbourhood-preserving correspondences. We empirically demonstrate that our approach achieves high-quality matching results both in terms of matching error and smoothness. Moreover, we show that our method is more scalable than previous formalisms. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/vikiehm/partial-geco.

title: Seeing Through Clutter Structured 3D Scene Reconstruction via Iterative Object Removal
authors: Rio Aguina-Kang, Kevin James Blackburn-Matzen, Thibault Groueix, Vladimir Kim, Matheus Gadelha
abstract: We present SeeingThroughClutter, a method for reconstructing structured 3D representations from single images by segmenting and modeling objects individually. Prior approaches rely on intermediate tasks such as semantic segmentation and depth estimation, which often underperform in complex scenes, particularly in the presence of occlusion and clutter. We address this by introducing an iterative object removal and reconstruction pipeline that decomposes complex scenes into a sequence of simpler subtasks. Using VLMs as orchestrators, foreground objects are removed one at a time via detection, segmentation, object removal, and 3D fitting. We show that removing objects allows for cleaner segmentations of subsequent objects, even in highly occluded scenes. Our method requires no task-specific training and benefits directly from ongoing advances in foundation models. We demonstrate state-of-the-art robustness on 3D-Front and ADE20K datasets. Project Page: https://rioak.github.io/seeingthroughclutter/

title: I2BD: A Dual-Perspective Deep Learning Architecture for Bengali Cyberbullying Detection
authors: Ashraful Goni, Farjana Akter, Md Umor Faruk Jahangir, MD. Abdul Awal Ansary
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The growing popularity of the social media has escalated exposure to implicit and increasingly developing forms of online harassment that are difficult to detect using traditional methods. Emerging machine learning (ML) and transformer models such as BERT and XLM-RoBERTa have demonstrated shortcomings in recognizing context-specific aggression in a context confined to individual comments. This paper has presented Incremental and Implicit Bullying Detector (I2BD) a dual perspective deep learning (DL) architecture that integrates micro-level implicit feature encoding with Bidirectional LSTM and macro-level sequential modelling with LSTM-based temporal aggregation. The combination of semantic and behavioural representations has been achieved through synergistic integration of multiplicative interaction of a hybrid fusion mechanism. I2BD was trained on a corpus of 44, 001 annotated Bengali remarks which has been reported to have 93.39 % accuracy and is superior than the state-of-the-art hybrid and transformer baselines. The architecture has shown better discriminative power to identify subtle and dynamic patterns of bullying, which provides a solid basis of smart moderation in the context of low-resource language conditions.

title: Enhancing Brain Tumor MRI Classification with Dual Attention Vision Transformers (DaViT)
authors: Anamika Rani, Rajkumar Biswas, Chinmoy Modak Turjo, Tonmoy Ghosh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Medical image analysis for brain tumor identification encounters substantial obstacles due to the heterogeneous nature of tumor appearances in MRI imaging. Contemporary deep learning methodologies show potential, yet many investigations face data quality challenges, particularly image duplication that artificially inflates model performance and creates unreliable validation outcomes. Our approach tackles these concerns by implementing a systematic data pre-processing pipeline on a public Brain Tumor MRI Dataset, utilizing cryptographic hash verification to maintain dataset consistency during duplicate removal procedures. The processed collection contains 6,854 MRI samples distributed across four diagnostic categories: glioma, meningioma, healthy tissue, and pituitary adenoma. Five vision transformer models (DaViT, BEiT, DeiT, SwinV2, and ViT) are fine-tuned to classify those four diagnostic categories. Performance evaluation reveals DaViT as the best classifier, achieving 99.01 % classification accuracy with individual class metrics spanning <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98-100 \%$</tex> for precision, recall, and F1-measure. This study demonstrates the effectiveness of vision transformer methodologies, specifically DaViT architecture, for dependable automated brain tumor diagnosis in medical imaging workflows.

title: Half Title Page
authors: N/A

title: A Gateway-Assisted Fine-Grained Data Sharing Scheme for Cross-Domain IIoT
authors: Chuanda Cai, Yue Cao, Changbing Bi, Changgen Peng, Ali Shoker, Chao Fu
abstract: Cross-domain data sharing is essential for industrial 4.0, envisioning for intelligent manufacturing, equipment collaboration and industrial automation. Inherently, gearing by the interconnection of devices and systems across heterogeneous domains, how to ensure efficient and secure multi-party data sharing remains a critical challenge in the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). Many broadcast encryption and signature encryption schemes have been proposed to ensure the privacy and rapid sharing of cross-domain data in recent years . However, existing schemes still have shortcomings in supporting fine-grained access control and adapting to resource-constrained edge node deployments. To address this issue, this paper proposes a ciphertext policy attribute-based broadcast matching encryption scheme (CP-ABBME). This scheme integrates a ciphertext policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) mechanism to achieve one-to-many secure ciphertext transmission in a fine-grained manner. Furthermore, considering the limited computation and storage resources of terminal devices, the proposed scheme builds an encapsulation–decapsulation chain architecture. It introduces bidirectional access control for mutual authentication between the sender and receiver, while offloading deployment and partial decryption to the receiving gateway. This design effectively reduces terminal-side computation and storage overhead during decryption and authentication. Formal security analysis shows that the proposed scheme meets stringent security requirements. Experimental results show that CP-ABBME improves the decryption performance by 39.3% to 99.6%, and reduces the terminal storage resource consumption by up to 9.35 KB.

title: WINning Against Audio Deepfakes_supp1-3695000.pdf
authors: Arth Juhul Shah
abstract: In this work, we propose the Wavelet Integrated Network (WIN), a novel neural architecture and employ it for Audio Deepfake Detection (ADD). Unlike standard transformer-based models that mix information across tokens via global self-attention, WIN replaces these attention blocks with intra-token wavelet atoms to enhance intra-token understanding of the network. These atoms perform localized time-frequency analysis inside each token, capturing spectral patterns that emphasize local structure rather than the global context. We further propose Multi-Head Wavelet (MHW), and demonstrate that the proposed network has a lower number of parameters as compared to the existing baseline systems. In addition, we conduct ablation studies on various real and analytic wavelets to further optimize the network. We compared our work with baseline, and demonstrate $\approx$ 2 % decrease in pooled EER over 9 competitive datasets for ADD research. All the codes are publicly available at https://github.com/Arth-Shah/WIN.

title: A Novel Distributed Optimization Algorithm With Linear Convergence for Economic Dispatch Under Compressed Communication
authors: Wei Chen, Zidong Wang, Jimmy Chih-Hsien Peng, Guo-Ping Liu
abstract: This article addresses the distributed economic dispatch (ED) issue of microgrids. The primary objective of this study is to derive a distributed optimization algorithm with a compressed communication scheme over directed networks. Specifically, the algorithm aims to solve the ED problem, where the total power generation of distributed energy resources (DERs) is dispatched to meet the overall demand at the minimum operational cost under DER capacity constraints. To improve communication efficiency, a novel data compressed transmission mechanism is introduced into the consensus-based distributed algorithm by constructing estimator-like equations. Furthermore, by resorting to the property of matrix norms and system theory, a sufficient condition is derived to ensure that the proposed algorithm linearly converge to the optimal solution under arbitrary compression rate. This condition explicitly depends on the communication topologies and the algorithm parameters but is independent of the compression rate. Finally, simulated examples are provided to validate the theoretical claims and demonstrate the performance of the proposed algorithm.

title: Approximating Dispatchable Regions in Three-Phase Radial Networks with Conditions for Exact SDP Relaxation
authors: Bohang Fang, Yue Chen, Changhong Zhao
abstract: The concept of dispatchable region plays a pivotal role in quantifying the capacity of power systems to accommodate renewable generation. In this paper, we extend the previous approximations of the dispatchable regions based on direct current (DC), linearized, and nonlinear single-phase alternating current (AC) models to unbalanced three-phase radial (tree) networks and provide improved outer and inner approximations of dispatchable regions. Based on the nonlinear bus injection model (BIM), we relax the non-convex problem that defines the dispatchable region to a solvable semidefinite program (SDP) and derive its strong dual problem (which is also an SDP). Utilizing the special mathematical structure of the dual problem, an SDP-based projection algorithm is developed to construct a convex polytopic outer approximation to the SDP-relaxed dispatchable region. Moreover, we provide sufficient conditions to guarantee the exact SDP relaxation by adding the power loss as a penalty term, thereby providing a theoretical guarantee for determining an inner approximation of the dispatchable region. Through numerical simulations, we validate the accuracy of our approximation of the dispatchable region and verify the conditions for exact SDP relaxation.

title: Discrimination of Tree-Contact High-Impedance and Flame Faults in Distribution Networks via Zero-Sequence Voltage Monitoring
authors: Ze Yang, Jiangling Tan, Kang Yuan, Tianxiang Chen, Zhuofeng Jiang, Hong Gu, Yankai Xing, Dongsheng Cai
abstract: Tree-contact high-impedance faults (THIF) and flame faults are common fault types in the forest area. Their mitigation and prevention are crucial to the management of distribution network faults. However, the signals of these faults are so weak that existing zero-sequence protection methods cannot ensure effectiveness and accuracy during the early fault stage. To address this issue, this paper investigates the electro-thermal coupling mechanism during the fault evolution process. Fault resistance models are established for different fault types. Experiments on 10 kV and 35 kV distribution systems verify that the proposed models can effectively capture the time-varying characteristics of the faults. Then electrical characteristics are investigated during discharges by applying Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to the zero-sequence voltage. Feature parameters are obtained including Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), Power Spectral Entropy (PSE), Skewness (SKE) and Kurtosis (KUR) of the sub-fundamental spectral components. Statistical analysis is employed to mitigate the influence of individual sample variability. From the experimental results, the frequency domain characteristics of both THIF and flame faults are independent of voltage levels. THD of flame fault is significantly higher than that of THIF due to the unstable discharge path in the flame. Conversely, THIF shows higher SKE and KUR because of low-frequency turbulence in the flame. The combined unstable discharge channel and turbulence cause PSE to be significantly higher in flame fault than in THIF. This research provides a methodological basis for fault classification and prevention for both 10 kV and 35 kV distribution lines.

title: Assessing competences in Sustainability, Ethics, and Gender Perspectives in Final Degree Projects_supp1-3697049.pdf
authors: Adriana Dapena
abstract: The Final Degree Project (FDP) represents the culmination of higher education studies and marks a key transition from academic training to professional practice. This paper aims to analyze the integration of sustainability, ethics, and gender perspectives in the design, development, and evaluation of FDPs, in alignment with the principles of the European Higher Education Area (EHEA). These dimensions are essential for fostering transversal competences that prepare students to address complex social and professional challenges. We propose a general framework for addessing these cross-cutting aspects throughout the different stages of the FDP process. This involves embedding them in course guides, applying them during project development, and integrating them into the final evaluation criteria. Additionally, we present two case studies that illustrate the current state of integration of these dimensions in real FDP contexts. The analysis reveals that, while some sustainability, ethical, and gender-related aspects are already considered, their implementation remains partial and inconsistent. This highlights the need for clearer guidelines, structured methodologies, and greater institutional support to ensure their effective incorporation.

title: Towards Trustworthy IIoT: A Reinforcement Learning Framework with Federated Unlearning
authors: Daniela Annunziata, Pian Qi, Kenli Li, Francesco Piccialli
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things enhances automation and efficiency but faces challenges from data poisoning, device failures, and unstable networks. To address these issues, we propose a unified framework, FU-MARL, that integrates federated unlearning with multi-agent reinforcement learning to improve system resilience and adaptability. The framework comprises intelligent agents, a scheduling server, and a secure communication network. Each agent performs local model training, threat detection, and unlearning through a federated learning module and a reinforcement learning module for adaptive control. The server coordinates global aggregation, verifies unlearning results, and ensures integrity, while encrypted communication safeguards data privacy. The unlearning process can be triggered by opt-out requests, attacks, or administrative commands, enabling the system to remove targeted data contributions without full retraining. Experiments on AWE and CWRU demonstrate that FU-MARL improves accuracy and reduces attack success rate, and achieves stable convergence with practical efficiency.

title: Security and Hardening of Kubernetes in Public Clouds: A Comparative Study of EKS, AKS, and GKE
authors: Andrzej Mycek, Maryna Łukaczyk
abstract: Kubernetes has become the foundation and a critical tool of modern cloud infrastructure; however, its complex architecture brings significant security challenges. This study presents a comparative analysis of the three most popular managed Kubernetes services—Amazon EKS, Azure AKS, and Google GKE—focusing on their security architecture, hardening mechanisms, and compliance with the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark. The work combines theoretical assessment with empirical testing using the kube-bench tool on clusters deployed in each cloud. The analysis covers authentication, authorization, data encryption, auditing, and real-time protection. The results indicate that all platforms offer a comparable level of security, providing end users with advanced security features. The study also considers recent vulnerabilities, such as IngressNightmare, demonstrating how their presence impacts production environments’ security and highlighting the critical importance of Kubernetes hardening. The findings provide practical guidance for DevSecOps teams and infrastructure architects in selecting, hardening, and securingKubernetes deployments according to organizational priorities.

title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Time-Series Foundation Models for Electric Vehicle Charging Load Forecasting
authors: Ying Li, Peng Gao, Xiya Li
abstract: Time-series foundation models pretrained on massive corpora have emerged as general-purpose forecasters capable of zero-shot prediction across diverse domains. However, whether such models can be effectively adapted to the highly stochastic and spatially heterogeneous domain of electric vehicle (EV) charging load forecasting remains underexplored. This paper investigates parameter-efficient fine-tuning of Time-MoE, a decoder-only time-series foundation model with sparse mixture-of-experts architecture, for EV charging load prediction across data-rich, few-shot, and zero-shot scenarios. The proposed approach freezes all backbone parameters and applies low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to selected attention projection matrices, reducing the number of trainable parameters to only 0.26% of the total model size. A direct multi-horizon prediction strategy is employed that leverages the multi-resolution output heads inherent to the Time-MoE architecture, enabling simultaneous multi-step-ahead forecasting from the final input position without autoregressive error accumulation. Instance normalization with a robust minimum standard deviation threshold addresses the zero-variance input sequences prevalent in sparse EV charging data. Comprehensive experiments conducted using real-world EV charging data collected from 275 charging stations in Shenzhen, China show that the fine-tuned Time-MoE achieves the lowest weighted mean absolute percentage error (WMAPE) among all methods under data-rich conditions, reducing the pretrained baseline error by 21.3%. The fine-tuned model also attains the best zero-shot spatial transfer performance across 138 unseen stations. Under the few-shot setting with severely limited training data, the fine-tuned Time-MoE still obtains the best WMAPE among all methods despite a 103× reduction in training data, highlighting the data efficiency of parameter-efficient fine-tuning with pretrained temporal representations. These results, together with a strict 137/138 station split evaluation for zero-shot spatial transfer, demonstrate the generalization capability of time-series foundation models for multi-station EV charging load forecasting.

title: Attenuation Correction for Flexible MR Hardware in PET/MR Using MLAA-Guided Template Registration_supp2-3693542.png
authors: Nicole Jurjew

title: The Shape of Wings of Hydrogenic Spectral Lines Under Linearly Polarized Monochromatic Waves in Plasmas
authors: E. Oks, E. Dalimier, P. Angelo
abstract: There is a garden variety of waves in plasmas. Some of these waves can be monochromatic (or quasimonochromatic) and linearly polarized. Examples are Langmuir waves, lower hybrid waves (in magnetized plasmas), and the electromagnetic radiation penetrating into plasma from the outside (such as the laser radiation). In the present article, we focus at the practically important situation of the strong modulation of the atomic oscillator by such waves. For hydrogenic spectral lines, this leads to numerous satellites, such that in practice only the envelope of the satellites is observed. We study the shape of the wings of hydrogenic spectral lines in this situation. We show that in this situation, the logarithm of the observed intensity versus the detuning $\Delta \lambda $ from the unperturbed wavelength of the spectral line should exhibit a quasilinear dependence. In the case where there is no laser radiation in a plasma, finding such a quasilinear dependence for the observed low-excited (i.e., the most intense) hydrogenic spectral lines would be the indication of the development of the lower hybrid oscillations in the plasma. This is the first spectroscopic method for detecting the lower hybrid oscillations in plasmas, to the best of our knowledge. Furthermore, we show that the experimental value of the frequency of the lower hybrid oscillations can be estimated from the tangent of such quasilinear dependence. Then, one can estimate either the electron density, if the magnetic field is known, or the magnetic field, if the electron density is known.

title: Compact ASIC Design of a Secure Hexadecimal Digital Lock for Consumer Electronics
authors: Bhaskar Lal Das, Weiwei Jiang, Alak Majumder
abstract: This paper presents HexLock, a compact and energy-efficient ASIC-based digital locking system that employs a 4-bit hexadecimal key architecture for secure hardware-level access control in embedded and consumer electronic applications. Unlike traditional microcontroller-based locks, it eliminates firmware dependencies and operates using fully synchronous, power gated digital logic, significantly reducing power and area overhead. The system accepts parallel 16-bit binary key input or 4-bit sequential hexadecimal key inputs, and verifies them through a pipelined XOR-OR logic against a configurable stored code, enforcing tamper resistance via an on-chip false attempt counter and early mismatch termination logic. Designed and verified on SCL 180nm CMOS node, HexLock records a core layout area of 0.35mm2 and post-layout delay of 4.5 ns along with dormant and active power consumption of 4.76 μW and 0.15 mW respectively under typical operating conditions. Functional validation across full PVT corners (-20°C to 120°C) confirms reliable operation up to 85 MHz. As summarized in Table IV, HexLock achieves substantially lower active power and a smaller silicon footprint compared to standard MCU and FPGA based locking alternatives, thereby eliminating firmware dependencies through hardware-only authentication. These properties make HexLock a practical candidate for integration in smart locks, IoT platforms, and safety-critical consumer systems.

title: A Cryogenic Superconducting Quantum Computing Unit Interface Chipset With Phase-Detection-Based Readout and Phase-Shifter-Based Controller
authors: Yanshu Guo, Yaoyu Li, Wenqiang Huang, Tian Tian, Nan Wu, Siqi Zhang, Qichun Liu, Tiefu Li, Zhihua Wang, Ning Deng, Yuanjin Zheng, Hanjun Jiang
abstract: This article presents a cryogenic quantum interface chipset at 3.5 K for superconducting transmon qubit operations. The chipset comprises a phase-detection readout and a phase-shifter-based polar-modulation controller with flexible scalability. With the proposed phase-detection readout scheme, a 9-bit time-to-digital converter (TDC)-based state detector is used to read out the qubit state by detecting the phase difference between the stimulus signal and the reflected signal. A low-IF N-path filter-based receiver and a multi-tone transmitter are co-designed to enable frequency-division multiplexing (FDM) dispersive readout of multiple qubits. A phase-shifter-based polar-modulation XY-driver is developed with support for fast phase quantum gate operation and derivative-removal-by-adiabatic-gate (DRAG) pulse generation. The Z-driver is realized using a static digital-to-analog converter (DAC) array and a 10-bit dynamic DAC for power efficiency. Fabricated in a 28-nm bulk CMOS process, the chipset occupies a die area of 4.6 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The readout RX achieves a noise temperature of 40 K with a gain of 45–83 dB and a bandwidth of 1.2 GHz. The readout RX consumes 32% less power than state-of-the-art designs. The measured total readout power for simultaneous detection of two qubits is 12.2 mW, which is the lowest among readouts with a similar integration level. In addition, the controller achieves a spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR) of 49 dB for a 1-GS/s Z-driver and an SNR of 42 dB for a 1-GS/s XY-driver. The XY-driver consumes 4.3 mW per qubit under active control. Verified with practical transmon qubits, the readout fidelity exceeds 92.6%, approaching that of commercial instruments. A Rabi experiment at 5.61/6.36 MHz, a Ramsey experiment at 4.1 MHz, and a T1 experiment at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$9.98~{\mu }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>s are also successfully observed using the proposed chipset at 3.5 K.

title: ENHANCING PUBLIC SAFETY IN INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS: Energy-Efficient Task Orchestration in 5G Vehicular Edge Networks
authors: Nawaz Ali, Giancarlo Fortino, Gianluca Aloi, Marco Loaiza, Raffaele Gravina, Ali Hassan Sodhro, Magnus Johnsson
abstract: Ensuring public safety in intelligent transportation systems (ITS) requires reliable and energy-efficient execution of vehicular applications under highly dynamic conditions. However, the inherent mobility of vehicles, fluctuating network quality, and constrained energy budgets often lead to task failures in 5G-enabled vehicular edge computing (VEC) environments. This article proposes an energy-aware task orchestration framework designed to minimize failure rates and optimize execution paths across mobile, edge, and cloud tiers. The framework integrates two adaptive mechanisms, transmission power control (TPC) and dynamic power scaling (DPS), to respond to real-time variations in signal strength, task complexity, and energy availability. A classification model predicts task feasibility across tiers, while a regression model estimates service responsiveness. The system selects the optimal execution path based on predicted success likelihood, energy profiles, and network conditions. Evaluated in a SUMO-integrated EdgeCloudSim environment with more than 1,000 vehicles, the proposed approach demonstrates more than 40% reduction in task failures under high-load scenarios. The framework supports diverse ITS applications, including danger assessment, traffic management, and infotainment, and offers a scalable solution for enhancing the reliability and safety of 5G vehicular networks.

title: Post-Silicon Deception: Evasive Hardware Trojan Through Adversarial Power Trace_supp1-3696769.pdf
authors: Behnam Omidi
abstract: The globalization of the Integrated Circuit (IC) supply chain, driven by time-to-market and cost considerations, has made ICs vulnerable to hardware Trojans (HTs). Against this threat, a promising approach is to use Machine Learning (ML)-based side-channel analysis, which has the advantage of being a non-intrusive method, along with efficiently detecting HTs under golden chip-free settings. In this paper, we question the trustworthiness of ML-based HT detection via side-channel analysis. We introduce an HT obfuscation (HTO) approach to allow HTs to bypass this detection method. Rather than theoretically misleading the model by simulated adversarial traces, a key aspect of our approach is the design and implementation of adversarial noise as part of the circuitry, alongside the HT. We detail HTO methodologies for ASICs and FPGAs, and evaluate our approach using the TrustHub benchmark. Interestingly, we found that HTO can be implemented with one single transistor for ASIC designs to generate adversarial power traces that can fool the defense with 100% efficiency. We also efficiently implemented our approach on a Spartan 6 Xilinx FPGA using 2 different variants: (i) DSP slices-based, and (ii) ring-oscillator-based design. Additionally, we assess the efficiency of countermeasures like spectral domain analysis, and we show that an adaptive attacker can still design evasive HTOs by constraining the design with a spectral noise budget. In addition, while adversarial training (AT) offers higher protection against evasive HTs, AT models suffer a considerable utility loss, potentially rendering them unsuitable for such a security application. We believe that this research represents a significant step in understanding and exploiting ML vulnerabilities in a hardware security context, and we make all resources and designs openly available online: https://github.com/beomidi/HW-Trojan-UAP-Generation

title: Distributed Battery-Oriented Active Power Dispatch for Parallel-Connected Household Energy Storage Inverters Using Virtual Common DC Bus Regulation
authors: Jiahao Zhang, Qi Zhang, Fujin Deng, Rongwu Zhu, Biying Ren, Xiangdong Sun
abstract: Parallel operation of multiple household energy storage inverters (HESIs) enables flexible system capacity expansion. However, the absence of a DC point of common coupling (PCC) poses prominent challenges in real-time active power allocation, state-of-charge (SOC) balancing, and communication coordination. This paper establishes the system's impedance model under DC bus signal control, analyzes its decoupling mechanism in a single-unit system and AC-DC coupling characteristics in parallel configurations. To address these issues, a virtual common DC bus balancing control strategy is proposed. By constructing virtual interconnections among all units' DC buses and a virtual DC PCC, AC-DC decoupling of the parallel HESIs system is achieved. Each unit autonomously adjusts output power per unified benchmarks and realizes dynamic inter-unit compensation based on its own characteristics, achieving autonomous active power dispatch with sparse communication. Further, a SOC balancing strategy for distributed energy storage is developed using the virtual DC PCC. The proposed scheme maintains low inter-unit coupling, enhances system efficiency and economy, and reduces control complexity. Simulation and experimental results validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the presented method.

title: Structure-Aware Deep Unfolding Network for Face Super-Resolution with Global-Local Modeling
authors: Chenyang Wang, Junjun Jiang, Zhiwei Zhong, Kui Jiang, Shiqi Wang, Xianming Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Face super-resolution aims to reconstruct high-resolution face images from the given low-resolution input, which has long been a research hotspot due to its wide-ranging applications. While deep learning has driven remarkable advances in this domain, existing approaches still face two fundamental limitations. First, existing methods often function as black-box systems with limited interpretability and transparency in their internal representations and feature learning behavior. This limits the ability to understand and improve the model’s decision making process, then weakening the model’s trustworthiness. Second, both global and local information are essential for high-fidelity face reconstruction, existing methods still struggle to effectively model these complementary dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose an optimization-inspired structure-aware deep unfolding framework for face super-resolution. Specifically, we formulate the face reconstruction task as an explicit optimization problem and unfold its iterative solution into a deep neural network, where each stage corresponds to a well-defined optimization step with clear physical interpretation, enhancing model’s trustworthiness. To enable efficient global-local feature modeling within our unfolding framework, we propose a structure-aware Receptance Weighted Key-Value (RWKV)-based proximal operator, which leverages the linear-complexity architecture of RWKV for global context modeling. Furthermore, we design a structure-aware deformable shift mechanism to enhance its ability for preserving fine-grained facial geometry, which can dynamically adjust spatial aggregation patterns based on the facial structure to effectively capture local information. Extensive experiments conducted on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both quantitative metrics and visual quality. The code will be released at https://github.com/wcy-cs/SANet.

title: VoxHammer: Training-Free Precise and Coherent 3D Editing in Native 3D Space
authors: Lin Li, Zehuan Huang, Haoran Feng, Gengxiong Zhuang, Rui Chen, Chunchao Guo, Lu Sheng
abstract: 3D local editing of specified regions is crucial for the game industry and robot interaction. Recent methods typically edit rendered multi-view images and then reconstruct 3D models, but they face challenges in precisely preserving unedited regions and overall coherence. Inspired by structured 3D generative models, we propose VoxHammer, a novel training-free approach that performs precise and coherent editing in 3D latent space. Given a 3D model, VoxHammer first predicts its inversion trajectory and obtains its inverted latents and key-value tokens at each timestep. Subsequently, in the denoising and editing phase, we replace the denoising features of preserved regions with the corresponding inverted latents and cached key-value tokens. By retaining these contextual features, this approach ensures consistent reconstruction of preserved areas and coherent integration of edited parts. To evaluate the consistency of preserved regions, we constructed Edit3D-Bench, a human-annotated dataset comprising hundreds of samples, each with carefully labeled 3D editing regions. Experiments demonstrate that VoxHammer significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of both 3D consistency of preserved regions and overall quality. Our method holds promise for synthesizing high-quality edited paired data, thereby laying the data foundation for in-context 3D generation.

title: GarmentCrafter: Progressive Novel View Synthesis for Single-View 3D Garment Reconstruction and Editing
authors: Yuanhao Wang, Cheng Zhang, Gonçalo Frazão, Jinlong Yang, Alexandru-Eugen Ichim, Thabo Beeler, Fernando De la Torre
abstract: We introduce GarmentCrafter, a new approach to enable non-professional users to create and modify 3D garments from a single-view image. While recent advances in image generation have facilitated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> garment design, creating and editing 3D garments remains challenging for nonprofessional users. Existing methods for single-view 3D reconstruction often rely on pre-trained generative models to hallucinate novel views conditioning on the reference image and camera pose, yet they lack cross-view consistency, failing to capture the internal relationships across different views. In this paper, we tackle this challenge through progressive depth prediction and image warping to approximate novel views. Subsequently, we train a multi-view diffusion model to complete occluded and unknown clothing regions, informed by the evolving camera pose. By jointly inferring RGB and depth, GarmentCrafter enforces inter-view coherence and reconstructs precise geometries and fine details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior visual fidelity and inter-view coherence compared to state-of-the-art single-view 3D garment reconstruction methods. Our code will be publicly available.

title: Calibration of Depth Sensors for SLAM
authors: Martin Lučan, Kostiantyn Zamolotniev
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Autonomous robotics is currently receiving significant attention in both research and industry. Quadruped robots, such as the Artaban platform, represent a rapidly developing field due to their ability to operate in complex and uneven terrains. The main requirement to enable autonomous navigation of such robots is reliable perception of the environment, which is usually provided by multiple vision sensors. Efficient use of these sensors requires accurate calibration to provide consistent spatial information for simultaneous localisation and mapping (SLAM). This work focuses on the calibration of a multi-camera system with non-overlapping fields of view integrated into an Artaban robot to improve perception and navigation accuracy in complex real-world scenarios. Several calibration methods were tested on the Artaban robot, including Luxonis DepthAI, Multical, and Kalibr, each with specific advantages and limitations. After analysing their accuracy, robustness, and applicability to non-overlapping camera configurations, MC-Calib was chosen as the most suitable approach. This method proved capable of handling the challenging geometry of Artaban's sensor layout and consistently provided high-quality calibration results, outperforming the alternative techniques.

title: Dual Fuel Knock Mitigation Technique Through Liquid State Injection
authors: Andrew Fenech, Anthony Theodore Saliba, Mario Farrugia
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Dual-fuel engines allow the use of alternative fuels such as Liquified Natural Gas (LNG). Using LNG as the main energy source and a smaller quantity of diesel (to initiate combustion) offers the benefit of decreased emissions. The reduction of emissions is mostly due to the gaseous fuel's better ability to burn more effectively. The gaseous fuels (e.g. methane <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{4}$</tex>) has a lower carbon to hydrogen ratio than diesel and therefore less <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> is produced. Particulate matter typically generated with diesel combustion is also greatly reduced. The use of dual fuel is however impacted by an operational phenomenon referred to as engine knock which limits the operational window of the engine. This knocking problem is accentuated during transients. The objective of this paper is to explore liquid state injection of LNG/propane. A small quantity of pressurized LNG/propane is injected into the airstream to lower the temperature of the charge air through the latent heat of evaporation of the LNG/propane. This liquid state injection is intended to lower the knock propensity especially during load increase transients where fuel is increased before the mass air flow has picked up (due to turbo lag).

title: Intelligent CAD in the Age of AI
authors: Yang Liu, Hao Pan, Changjian Li, David Kasik
abstract: This Guest Editorial introduces the special issue Intelligent CAD in the Age of AI, which examines how artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping the foundations of computer-aided design (CAD). As CAD evolves beyond geometry-centric modeling toward intelligent, context-aware design partners, AI enables new capabilities for understanding design intent, generating controllable alternatives, reconstructing parametric histories, and supporting richer modes of human–AI interaction. At the same time, CAD provides a uniquely structured and semantically grounded testbed that exposes open challenges for AI—including data efficiency, constraint handling, and multimodal geometric reasoning. The four articles featured in this issue reflect the field’s expanding scope: advances in generative modeling for engineering CAD, new insights into dataset adequacy and augmentation, methods for recovering design logic from boundary-representation geometry, and hybrid workflows that bridge scanned data and editable representations. Together, these contributions illustrate a rapidly advancing research frontier and point toward a future in which intelligent CAD systems integrate automation, semantic understanding, and human expertise more seamlessly than ever.

title: Look Around and Pay Attention: Multi-Camera Point Tracking Reimagined with Transformers
authors: Bishoy Galoaa, Xiangyu Bai, Shayda Moezzi, Utsav Nandi, Sai Siddhartha Vivek Dhir Rangoju, Somaieh Amraee, Sarah Ostadabbas
abstract: This paper presents LAPA (Look Around and Pay Attention), a novel end-to-end transformer-based architecture for multi-camera point tracking that integrates appearancebased matching with geometric constraints. Traditional pipelines decouple detection, association, and tracking, leading to error propagation and temporal inconsistency in challenging scenarios. LAPA addresses these limitations by leveraging attention mechanisms to jointly reason across views and time, establishing soft correspondences through a cross-view attention mechanism enhanced with geometric priors. Instead of relying on classical triangulation, we construct 3D point representations via attention-weighted aggregation, inherently accommodating uncertainty and partial observations. Temporal consistency is further maintained through a transformer decoder that models long-range dependencies, preserving identities through extended occlusions. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets, including our newly created multi-camera (MC) versions of TAPVid-3D panoptic and PointOdyssey, demonstrate that our unified approach significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving 37.5% APD on TAPVid-3D-MC and 90.3% APD on PointOdyssey-MC, particularly excelling in scenarios with complex motions and occlusions. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/Look-Around-and-Pay-Attention-LAPA-.

title: Improving PCE of Lead Free, Organic, Inorganic, Metal Halide Bilayer Perovskite Solar Cells Through Optimization of Bandgap, Defect Density and Electron Affinity with Different ETLS
authors: Parijat Mohajan, Brinta Dey, M.R. Foysal, Laboni Rani Keya, Shaikat Das, Md Emon Mian
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study conducts a numerical analysis of a novel bilayer perovskite solar cell architecture, integrating an organic FACsSnI<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> absorber with an inorganic Cs<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> PtI<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf> absorber. The simulated device follows the architecture Glass/FTO/ETL/FACsSnI<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/Cs<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> PtI<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>/ PEDOT:PSS/Au. A systematic investigation was carried out to evaluate the impact of bandgap, defect density, and electron affinity on power conversion efficiency (PCE) using three separate electron transport layers (ETLs): PCBM, LBSO, and SnS. Key performance parameters, including PCE, fill factor (FF), open-circuit voltage (Voc), and short-circuit current density (Jsc), were quantified using the SCAPS-1D simulation environment under standard AM 1.5 G illumination (1000 W/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) at 300 K. PEDOT:PSS was selected as the hole transport layer due to its favorable processing characteristics, tunable conductivity, and cost-effectiveness. Comparative analysis of ETL materials revealed that the SnS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-based configuration yielded optimal performance, attaining a peak PCE of 47.27%, with an FF of 82.64%, a Voc of 1.1863 V, and a Jsc of 48.22 mA /cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Conversely, the structure incorporating LBSO as the ETL exhibited the lowest efficiency of 34.22%, along with an FF of 82.25%, a Voc of 1.1819 V, and a Jsc of 35.20 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Toward Reliable Tea Leaf Disease Diagnosis Using Deep Learning Model: Enhancing Robustness with Explainable AI and Adversarial Training
authors: Samanta Ghosh, Jannatul Adan Mahi, Shayan Abrar, Md Parvez Mia, Asaduzzaman Rayhan, Addul Awal Yasir, Asaduzzaman Hridoy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Tea is one of the most demandable products in Bangladesh. A lot of people around the world start their day with a cup of tea and that's why its demand is so high. Consequently, it boosts the economy as Bangladesh is one of the most tea producers of the world. But, tea plants are vulnerable to many diseases for which the production and quality of the tea could be hampered. Manual disease detection or traditional way of diseases detection is not quite easy and accurate. Automated deep learning model can be useful to detect the diseases in an automated way which can make the process easy and accurate. From this point of view, we decided to develop an automated deep learning model for classifying tea leaf diseases. For this, we used a rich dataset titled 'teaLeafBD'. The dataset consists of 5,278 high-resolution images. There are seven categories of the images; six of them contain tea leaf images with different diseases and one class is for healthy leaves. This study introduces an appropriate workflow including data preprocessing, data splitting, adversarial training, augmentation, model training, evaluation, and comprehension made possible with Explainable AI techniques. DenseNet201 and EfficientNetB3 are used to do the categorization method. For making the architecture more efficient, we used adversarial training which may help the model to work with noisy or disturbed inputs. For analyze the model's predictions, we used Grad-CAM visualization. The output of our experiment shows high accuracy. Across the models, EfficientNetB3 obtained the best classification accuracy of 93%, and DenseNet201 achieved an accuracy of 91 %. This outcome suggests that the used approach is able to detect tea leaf diseases accurately. Therefore, this approach can be considered as an effective asset in the field of tech-based agricultural management.

title: Splat-based Gradient-Domain Fusion for Seamless View Transition
authors: Dongyoung Choi, Jaemin Cho, Woohyun Kang, Hyunho Ha, James Tompkin, Min H. Kim
abstract: In sparse novel view synthesis with few input views and wide baselines, existing methods often fail due to weak geometric correspondences and view-dependent color inconsistencies. Splatting-based approaches can produce plausible results near training views, but they frequently overfit and struggle to maintain smooth, realistic appearance transitions in novel viewpoints. We introduce a splat-based gradient-domain fusion method that addresses these limitations. Our approach first establishes reliable dense geometry via two-view stereo for stable initialization. We then generate intermediate virtual views by reprojecting input images, which provide reference gradient fields for gradient-domain fusion. By blending these gradients, our method transfers low-frequency, view-dependent colors to the rendered Gaussians, producing seamless appearance transitions across views. Extensive experiments show that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sparse Gaussian splatting methods, delivering robust and perceptually plausible view synthesis. A comprehensive user study further confirms that our results are perceptually preferred, with significantly smoother and more realistic color transitions than existing methods.

title: Is Clustering Enough for LiDAR Instance Segmentation? A State-of-the-Art Training-Free Baseline
authors: Corentin Sautier, Gilles Puy, Alexandre Boulch, Renaud Marlet, Vincent Lepetit
abstract: Panoptic segmentation of LiDAR point clouds is fundamental to outdoor scene understanding, with autonomous driving being a primary application. While state-of-the-art approaches typically rely on end-to-end deep learning architectures and extensive manual annotations of instances, the significant cost and time investment required for labeling large-scale point cloud datasets remains a major bottleneck in this field. In this work, we demonstrate that competitive panoptic segmentation can be achieved using only semantic labels, with instances predicted without any training or annotations. Our method outperforms most state-of-theart supervised methods on standard benchmarks including SemanticKITTI and nuScenes, and outperforms every publicly available method on SemanticKITTI as a drop-in instance head replacement, while running in real-time on a single-threaded CPU and requiring no instance labels. It is fully explainable, and requires no learning or parameter tuning. ALPINE combined with state-of-the-art semantic segmentation ranks first on the official panoptic segmentation leaderboard of SemanticKITTI. ps

title: Improved Convex Decomposition with Ensembling and Negative Primitives
authors: Vaibhav Vavilala, Florian Kluger, Seemandhar Jain, Bodo Rosenhahn, Anand Bhattad, David Forsyth
abstract: Describing a scene in terms of primitives - geometrically simple shapes that offer a parsimonious but accurate abstraction of structure - is an established and difficult fitting problem. Different scenes require different numbers of primitives, and these primitives interact strongly. Existing methods are evaluated by comparing predicted depth, normals, and segmentation against ground truth. The state of the art method involves a learned regression procedure to predict a start point consisting of a fixed number of primitives, followed by a descent method to refine the geometry and remove redundant primitives. CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) representations are significantly enhanced by a set-differencing operation. Our representation incorporates negative primitives, which are differenced from the positive primitives. These notably enrich the geometry that the model can encode, while complicating the fitting problem. This paper presents a method that can (a) incorporate these negative primitives and (b) choose the overall number of positive and negative primitives by ensembling. Extensive experiments on the standard NYUv2 dataset confirm that (a) this approach results in substantial improvements in depth representation and segmentation over SOTA and (b) negative primitives improve fitting accuracy. Our method is robustly applicable across datasets: in a first, we evaluate primitive prediction for LAION images.

title: SHAP-Based Explainable AI Model for Early Detection of Cardiovascular Disease
authors: Md. Abu Rayhan Imran, Tawhid Hasan, Nahida Akter, Md. Solaiman Mia, Tanpia Tasnim
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) continue to rank among the leading contributors to global mortality. Although significant progress has been made, existing CVD prediction models still face many barriers including imbalanced datasets, limited data sizes, inefficient feature selection and the inability to provide fully effective solutions. This paper introduces an ensemble machine learning framework for the early prediction of CVD, using a publicly available heart disease dataset containing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4,45,132$</tex> samples. Feature selection was performed using hybrid Mutual Information scores and Random Forest (RF) importance rankings, and the class imbalance problem was addressed using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Our proposed stacking ensemble classifier integrates XGBoost, LightGBM and CatBoost as base learners, with a RF meta-learner combining their outputs. Traditional models including AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, Logistic Regression, and Linear Support Vector Classifier, were also trained on the same dataset for the comparison. The experimental evaluation shows that our proposed model has achieved an accuracy of 96.6 % and an area under the curve of approximately 0.99, which significantly outperforming individual classifiers. To improve interpretability, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values were employed, offering clear insights into how features influenced the model. These results demonstrate that the stacking ensemble framework is a robust, explainable and generalizable tool for CVD prediction.

title: Quantum Resilient Routing and Beam Assignment for Secure and Intelligent 6G LEO Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Lamia Enam, Sadia Enam, Sinthia Islam, Md. Nahid Hasan Sifat, Mostafa Zaman Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The rapid advancement of sixth generation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(6 \mathrm{G})$</tex> non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) is leading to the widespread deployment of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations to deliver global broadband connectivity with ultra-low latency. The stability and security of these networks are, however, threatened by two new issues: (i) the dynamic topology of LEO constellations and (ii) the growing susceptibility of classical encryption to quantum attacks. A post quantum cryptography (PQC) aware routing and beam assignment system. That guarantees quantum resilient, latency efficient communication over LEO satellite networks is proposed in this paper to address these challenges. The proposed approach combines a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) model for adaptive real time decision making, a low complexity heuristic algorithm for scalable routing, and mixed integer linear programming (MILP) for optimal offline benchmarking. Satellite mobility, PQC induced processing delays, and dynamic traffic changes are all incorporated into the development of a comprehensive simulation environment. According to the results, PQC integration introduces a mild <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0} - \text{1 5 \%}$</tex> latency overhead, but all methods maintain end-to-end delay within the 6G NTN threshold of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0 0 ~ m s}$</tex>. While the heuristic and DRL strategies provide near optimal performance with over 98 % pocket delivery ratio and consistency under high mobility, the MILP based approach achieves low latency. Thus, the proposed PQC aware framework offers a strong balance between security, scalability, and quality of service. Thus, paving the way for intelligent and secure 6G oriented LEO communication architecture.

title: Enhanced Facial Emotion Recognition Through Vision Transformer Architecture with Adaptive Training Strategies
authors: Md. Imrul Kayes, Sumaiya Amin Prova, Raida Chakma, Nishat Tasnim, Mamun Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Achieving facial expression recognition (FER) is an exceptionally difficult endeavour. CNNs have shown promising results thus far; however, they continue to overlook certain subtle emotional cues and are susceptible to imbalanced classes. Currently, the apex of attention-based image classification has been reached. A self-attention mechanism is present in Vision Transformers (ViTs), which conduct a more comprehensive analysis of features, capturing details that prior models may have overlooked. We introduce a modified ViT model in this paper that is capable of linking facial features at various scales with autonomously balanced track class distributions. This model utilises attention to concentrate on the most substantial aspects of facial images for the objective of identify emotion. FER2013 and RAF-DB datasets are utilised to conduct all experiments. The model has demonstrated an accuracy of 85.19%, surpassing the effectiveness of preceding state of the art models. Our proposed model's motivations and efficacy are illustrated by the qualitative results.

title: ConeGS: Error-Guided Densification Using Pixel Cones for Improved Reconstruction with Fewer Primitives
authors: Bartlomiej Baranowski, Stefano Esposito, Patricia Gschoßmann, Anpei Chen, Andreas Geiger
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves state-of-the-art image quality and real-time performance in novel view synthesis but often suffers from a suboptimal spatial distribution of primitives. This issue stems from cloning-based densification, which propagates Gaussians along existing geometry, limiting exploration and requiring many primitives to adequately cover the scene. We present ConeGS, an image-space-informed densification framework that is independent of existing scene geometry state.
