ConeGS first creates a fast Instant Neural Graphics Primitives (iNGP) reconstruction as a geometric proxy to estimate per-pixel depth. During the subsequent 3DGS optimization, it identifies high-error pixels and inserts new Gaussians along the corresponding viewing cones at the predicted depth values, initializing their size according to the cone diameter. A preactivation opacity penalty rapidly removes redundant Gaussians, while a primitive budgeting strategy controls the total number of primitives, either by a fixed budget or by adapting to scene complexity, ensuring high reconstruction quality. Experiments show that ConeGS consistently enhances reconstruction quality and rendering performance across Gaussian budgets, with especially strong gains under tight primitive constraints where efficient placement is crucial.

title: Towards Physically-Based Sky-Modeling for Image Based Lighting
authors: Ian J. Maquignaz
abstract: Accurate environment maps are a key component for rendering photorealistic outdoor scenes with coherent illumination. They enable captivating visual arts, immersive virtual reality, and a wide range of engineering and scientific applications. Recent works have extended sky-models to be more comprehensive and inclusive of cloud formations but, as we demonstrate, existing methods fall short in faithfully recreating natural skies. Though in recent years the visual quality of DNN-generated High Dynamic Range Imagery (HDRI) has greatly improved, the environment maps generated by DNN sky-models do not re-light scenes with the same tones, shadows, and illumination as physically captured HDR imagery. In this work, we demonstrate progress in HDR literature to be tangential to sky-modelling as current works cannot support both photorealism and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$22 f$</tex> stops required for the Full Dynamic Range (FDR) of outdoor illumination. We achieve this by proposing AllSky, a flexible all-weather sky-model learned directly from physically captured HDRI which we leverage to study the input modalities, tonemapping, conditioning, and evaluation of sky-models. Per user-controlled positioning of the sun and cloud formations, AllSky expands on current functionality by allowing for intuitive user control over environment maps and achieves state-of-the-art sky-model performance. Through our proposed evaluation, we demonstrate existing DNN sky-models are not interchangeable with physically captured HDRI or parametric sky-models, with current limitations being prohibitive of scalability and accurate illumination in downstream applications.

title: Energy-Efficient HBF Antenna Array for multi beams through Simplified Connection Architecture
authors: Ji-Jin Lu, Shi-Gang Zhou, Song Li, Lun Cui, Lian-Wei Zhu
abstract: This paper proposes an energy-efficient hybrid beamforming (HBF) antenna array architecture characterized by a simplified connection architecture. To optimize the connection architecture, a differential evolution (DE) algorithm is employed, integrating active element patterns, digital phase shifters (PS) and specific beam functions. The proposed architecture is validated through the design and measurement of a 6×6 HBF antenna array capable of simultaneous service and broadcast beamforming. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed architecture achieves a 59% reduction in digital-analog links compared to the full-connection (FC) architecture, while maintaining a 12 dBi main-lobe gain for service beam and a minimum gain of -1 dBi for broadcast beams. Furthermore, system evaluations using an extended cross-entropy QR-based HBF algorithm indicate that the energy efficiency (EE) of the optimized array is enhanced by up to 13% relative to the PC baseline.

title: SimMTC: Simple Multi-view Tensor Clustering
authors: Haonan Xin, Zhezheng Hao, Zhe Cao, Zihua Zhao, Rong Wang, Feiping Nie
abstract: Tensor-based multi-view clustering algorithms have attracted considerable attention due to their superior clustering performance. However, these algorithms typically treat each view independently, failing to utilize the complementary information across all views, thus lacking globality. Additionally, employing low-rank tensor constraints to extract consistent information among views may result in the loss of important information due to weak consistency constraints. These limitations significantly hinder the clustering performance. To address these issues, we propose Simple Multi-view Tensor Clustering (SimMTC), which achieves globality and strong consistency. SimMTC first applies Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to the bipartite graphs to obtain high-frequency and low-frequency information, which encode similarities between samples and anchors from all views, thereby capturing global information. Orthogonal tensor factorization is then conducted in the frequency domain. Moreover, a novel strong consistency constraint based on FFT is introduced, which enhances the extraction of consistent information in the frequency domain. What’s more, an efficient alternative optimization algorithm is designed to solve the optimization problem in SimMTC. Finally, extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SimMTC achieves state-of-the-art clustering performance.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)

title: Temperature-Rise Characteristics of Cable Tray Structures Under Stray Electromagnetic Fields in Fusion Facilities and Optimization Design
authors: Ling Zhang, Qiangjian Chen, Li Jiang, Hong Lei, Ge Gao
abstract: Metal cable trays installed around fusion power-supply systems are exposed to stray magnetic fields generated by high-current feeders, AC busbars, and ripple-current components. Under such conditions, conductive tray structures may experience eddy current and, for ferromagnetic materials, hysteresis losses, which can produce nonnegligible local heating and compromise thermal safety. This article establishes an electromagnetic–thermal coupled analysis method for cable tray structures in fusion facilities. First, a site-specific 3-D engineering model is built for a fusion-facility power-supply layout under design, and the temperature-rise risk of two representative tray locations is evaluated. Second, a representative parametric model is used to compare the effects of material, geometric structure, installation spacing, and grounding method. The engineering case results show that the reactor-side tray reaches about <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$33~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, while the tray located close to the AC busbar reaches about <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$51.4~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C under the studied loading condition. The parametric study further shows that low-permeability materials such as 316L316 L stainless steel and 6063 aluminum alloy reduce heating significantly compared with Q235 carbon steel; perforated or mesh-type structures weaken large closed current loops; increasing the tray-feeder spacing from 100 to 200 mm reduces the peak temperature rise by about 40%; and single-point or segmented grounding lowers local hot-spot severity compared with multipoint grounding. Based on these results, a practical optimization route covering material selection, structural form, spacing, and grounding arrangement is proposed for fusion-facility cable trays.

title: A 36 V Current-Balancing Instrumentation Amplifier With ±24 V Input Range, 5.6 MHz BW, and 140 dB CMRR at All Gain Settings
authors: Shen Ye, Minge Jiang, Jiahao Wang, Peng Cao, Zhiliang Hong, Jiawei Xu
abstract: This article presents a 36V high-voltage current-balancing instrumentation amplifier (HV-CBIA), providing a new solution for industrial signal-acquisition front ends, including PLC input modules and motor drivers, where large input range, wide bandwidth, and high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) are simultaneously required. The proposed HV-CBIA addresses these requirements through three innovations: 1) a class-AB transconductance (TC) stage with adaptive biasing is proposed to extend the differential-mode (DM) input range while minimizing quiescent current; 2) a source-input buffer is employed to enhance the driving capability of transimpedance (TI) stage, while a negative capacitance (NC) technique is further incorporated to nullify parasitic capacitance, thereby improving the bandwidth–efficiency trade-off; and 3) an HV chopper employing area-efficient HV switches is adopted to maintain high CMRR and low offset under significant common-mode (CM) and DM swings. Fabricated in a 0.18-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m BCD process, the proposed HV-CBIA achieves a ±24V DM input signal range, 5.6MHz bandwidth, and 140dB CMRR across all gain settings (0.4-50). It delivers a 280MHz gain–bandwidth product (GBW) at a gain of 50 while consuming only 0.7mA from an 8–36V supply, corresponding to a power efficiency of 400MHz/mA.

title: Frequency-Guided Acceleration of Diffusion-based Interactive Denoising in Mobile Edge Networks
authors: Yuxin Liang, Peng Yang, Ziqi Zhou, Conghao Zhou, Feng Lyu
abstract: Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content is reshaping interactive content creation. However, provisioning diffusion-based interactive denoising services in mobile edge networks remains challenging, due to the demanding computation and communication resources. To address these challenges, in this paper, we investigate the innate features of latent diffusion models, which have been widely used for image generation. We first observe that, 1) latent states are compact in data size compared to generated images, and 2) image attributes can be differentiated in frequency domain across the denoising stages. Motivated by these insights, we propose an end-edge collaborative denoising system to accelerate progressive image generation through hybrid latent state reuse and denoising tasks offloading. At the core of this system design are two synergistic algorithms configuring generation quality and service latency. The Adaptive Reuse Configuration Selection algorithm properly configures the synthesis of latent states by identifying reusable latent states, while preserving consistent image attributes between progressive tasks, thus minimizing computational redundancy with guaranteed generation quality. Subsequently, the Distributed Denoising Management algorithm optimizes the offloading ratio between the edge server and mobile users based on their computing resource availability. This paradigm enables the transmission of lightweight latent tensors instead of high-resolution images, thereby alleviating transmission burden. Extensive experimental comparisons against baselines demonstrate that, the proposed system can reduce service latency by up to 66.9% and enhance generation quality by up to 62.3%.

title: IEEE Computer Society
authors: N/A

title: All-Weather Land Cover and Building Damage Mapping: Outcome of the 2025 IEEE GRSS Data Fusion Contest
authors: Wang Liu, Zhiyu Wang, Xin Guo, Puhong Duan, Xudong Kang, Shutao Li, Zhe Wang, Jiarui Hu, Yuxuan Guo, Jiepan Li, He Huang, Yu Sheng, Yujun Guo, Wei He, Xiangqiang Zeng, Ying Qu, Claudio Persello, Ujjwal Verma, Saurabh Prasad, Gemine Vivone, Hongruixuan Chen, Junshi Xia, Jian Song, Clifford Broni-Bediako, Kohei Kurihara, Olivier Dietrich, Konrad Schindler, Naoto Yokoya
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This article presents the scientific results of the 2025 Data Fusion Contest organized by the Image Analysis and Data Fusion Technical Committee, the University of Tokyo, RIKEN, and ETH Zurich. The focus of the contest was to develop innovative solutions for all-weather land-cover and building damage mapping using multimodal SAR and optical EO data at submeter resolution. The contest is organized into two distinct tracks. Track 1 focuses on land-cover mapping and Track 2 is about building damage mapping. The competition presented two primary technical challenges: the effective integration of multimodal data and the development of robust models capable of handling noisy labels. The contest saw significant global engagement, with Track 1 receiving 507 registrations and 3,859 successful submissions, while Track 2 followed a similar trend with 423 registered teams and 5,008 successful entries. This paper provides the methodologies and results achieved by the first- and secondranked teams from each track. To promote transparency and ensure the reproducibility of results, all participating teams in this year's contest have publicly released their code. Furthermore, the dataset used in this competition has been made publicly available to the community to support and encourage further research in the field.

title: ETF-SwinNet: A Lightweight Aircraft Runway Segmentation Network With Edge-Texture Dual-Branch Fusion for Assisted Landing
authors: Zhujun Wang, Kai Wang, Tianhe Sun, Meizhi Liu, Xuhai Xiong
abstract: Ensuring landing safety constitutes a critical challenge in general aviation, especially in complex low-altitude environments. The severe performance degradation of semantic segmentation, caused by dynamic scale variations and blurred runway boundaries, severely restricts the reliability of aircraft assisted landing systems. To tackle this problem, this article proposes edge-texture feature fusion SwinNet network (ETF-SwinNet), a lightweight semantic segmentation network dedicated to runway and marking recognition. Unlike existing lightweight networks, which often suffer from edge blurring or inefficient feature fusion, this network adopts a dual-branch architecture combining convolutional neural network (CNN) and Transformer. It simultaneously captures hierarchical texture semantics and integrates enhanced edge details. These features are adaptively fused via a gated fusion mechanism, overcoming the difficulty in balancing texture semantics and edge precision. Furthermore, this article constructed a dedicated dataset named CHAR-2025, which contains 4330 aerial images. Experimental results demonstrate that ETF-SwinNet achieves a mean intersection-over-union of 97.7% and an accuracy of 99.8% with only 3.4 M parameters. Quantitatively, compared to SegFormer-B0, our network reduces parameters by 8.1% while improving mean intersection over union (mIoU) by 1.5%. It also outperforms SegNext and CSLFormer with fewer parameters. Furthermore, it achieves 97 frames per second on the embedded Jetson AGX Orin platform. This synergy of lightweight efficiency and real-time precision establishes a robust perception foundation for ensuring landing safety.

title: Multi-Agent Opportunistic Routing for UAV Swarms: A Deployment-Aligned Robust Learning Framework_supp1-3696749.pdf
authors: lei lei
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms are essential for mission-critical aerial Internet of Things (IoT) applications. However, reliable multi-hop routing in these swarms is severely challenged by highly dynamic topologies and partial, asynchronous observations. Furthermore, conventional training data poorly represent rare but decisive disruptions, such as terrain occlusion, no-fly-zone detours, and congestion bursts. To address these issues, this paper proposes a deployment-aligned robust learning framework organized into three functional layers. At the policy layer, we propose the Multi-Agent Opportunistic (MAO) routing method, learned via a Belief Graph-based MAPPO (BG-MAPPO) algorithm. To overcome partial and stale observations, BG-MAPPO constructs a local belief graph encoding the dynamic neighborhood state, which is then used to jointly determine the optimal forwarder-set size and a diversity-aware routing distribution. At the training layer, the policy is optimized inside a terrain-aware, physics-calibrated Digital Twin Network (DTN). To prevent overfitting to limited training distributions, a diffusion-based generator (FD3M) enriches the training data with trajectory-and-flow samples covering critical corner cases. At the validation layer, large-scale simulations show that MAO reduces end-to-end delay by 27–34% compared to representative baselines. The policy also incurs modest operational overhead, maintaining millisecond-level inference and bounded signaling. Finally, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) experiments confirm that the DTN accurately predicts physical execution, with relative gaps of only 4% in packet delivery ratio and 7% in delay, effectively bridging the sim-to-real gap for robust UAV-swarm networking.

title: Self-calibration framework for near-field radio frequency sensing
authors: Anusha Muralidharan, Edwin C.Kan
abstract: Near-field radio frequency (NFRF) sensing enables non-contact physiological monitoring of internal organs and tissues. When a transmitter (Tx) antenna is placed near a moving dielectric boundary, the motion modulates both magnitude and phase of the propagating electromagnetic (EM) energy in proximity. This motion can be retrieved and analyzed by post-processing the complex signal recorded at the receiver (Rx). Previous studies have shown feasibility of capturing physiological motion signals of organs and tissues using NFRF sensing in vital signs and muscle actuation. However, occasionally signal fidelity in NFRF sensing can be degraded by channel nonideality and transceiver nonlinearity, which can result in artifacts like phase reversal, biphasic twisting and nonlinear distortion. We propose a novel self-calibration framework where we inject a known calibration signal in the Tx quadrature baseband for channel evaluation. The calibration signal was designed such that any signal infidelity will distort the calibration signal the same way it would distort the motion signal. Such artifacts can be detected in real-time or during post-processing and then be corrected by ensuring the processed calibration signal at Rx matches the injected Tx waveform morphology. We employed two strategies: (1) complex vector injection (CVI) to correct for phase-domain morphological nonidealities, and (2) a lookup-table (LUT) to compensate for nonlinear gain distortion. We chose a tuning fork and synchronized high-speed camera to experimentally validate the proposed calibration scheme for accurate motion waveform recovery.

title: PVCA: Privacy-Preserving and Verifiable Cross-System Authorization for Platoon Communications in VANETs
authors: Hang Liu, Yang Ming, Chenhao Wang, Yi Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Vehicle platoon, an increasingly significant technology in vehicular ad hoc networks (VANETs), effectively reduces energy consumption and environmental pollution, mitigates traffic congestion, and enhances road capacity and traffic safety. Collaborative communication between platoons is available to promote the reliability of data dissemination, ameliorate driving strategies, and further strengthen traffic efficiency. Nevertheless, existing efforts for secure multi-platoon data dissemination still face the following issues: (i) lack of a direct authorization strategy for platoons working on different systems; (ii) no effective mechanism to protect vehicle privacy during platoon communications; (iii) absence of a practical verification approach for cross-system ciphertext transformation. This paper builds a privacy-preserving and verifiable cross-system authorization (PVCA) scheme to mitigate the above issues. Specifically, we first put forth the optimized anonymous identity-based broadcast encryption (IBBE) and policy-hiding attribute-based encryption (ABE) protocols to adapt platoon communications while protecting identity and attribute privacy. After that, the authorized token bridging the proposed protocols is designed to enable ciphertexts of IBBE format to be transformed into new ciphertexts of ABE format, enabling flexible authorization. Furthermore, inspired by the Fujisaki-Okamoto transformation, we devise an efficient zero knowledge proof of knowledge protocol, making our PVCA achieve verifiability and fairness. Rigorous security proof and analysis demonstrate that our PVCA is not only secure against chosen plaintext attacks and collusion attacks, but also satisfies the necessary security requirements. We implement a prototype of PVCA (on a desktop computer and Raspberry Pi) and provide a simulation utilizing NS-2 to validate its feasibility for platoon communications in VANETs. The results indicate that, compared to the state-of-the-art ciphertext transformation solutions, PVCA reduces the running time for original ciphertext generation, transformation, and decryption by at least 91.57%, 49.87%, and 50%, respectively, while compressing the original ciphertext and authorization token sizes by over 78.07% and 15.20%.

title: Perspectives on Routing in LEO/NTN Space Networks: Classical, Agentic, and Quantum
authors: Charbel Mattar, Jacques Bou Abdo, Abdallah Makhoul, Benoˆt Piranda, Jacques Demerjian
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) mega-constellations and 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTN) create large-scale, time-varying environments where routing must adapt to dynamic inter-satellite links (ISLs) and gateways. This PRISMA-inspired review examines network-layer routing in LEO/NTN networks, addressing (i) prevailing routing approaches and assumptions (RQ1), and (ii) reported gains of agentic and quantum methods over classical baselines (RQ2). From IEEE Xplore and Scopus, complemented by snowballing, 70 studies (2021-2026) were analyzed. Findings show that performance claims depend heavily on control-plane design: centralized/SDN models can enable broader optimization when sufficiently fresh global state and timely rule dissemination are available, while distributed learning can reduce signaling under partial observability yet raises training and deployment concerns. The evidence base remains predominantly simulation-driven, often under simplified workloads, with limited support from testbed, emulation, or mixed deployment studies. Recurring gaps include limited control-plane realism, missing threat modeling, weak scalability validation, and small-scale quantum demonstrations constrained by current hardware limits.

title: Leveraging Identity and Access Management to Mitigate Insider Threats
authors: Zlatan Morić, Vedran Dakić, Chinecherem Kalu Kalu
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper explores the essential function of Identity and Access Management (IAM) in reducing insider threats and enhancing the cybersecurity resilience of organisations. Insider threats, whether deliberate or unintentional, pose significant challenges to data integrity, operational stability, and regulatory compliance. With the growing adoption of cloud-based infrastructure and remote work models, identity and access management has transformed from a static access-control mechanism into a dynamic, intelligence-driven framework. The research employs a qualitative interpretive methodology, integrating contemporary academic and industry literature from 2022 to 2025 to examine how emerging technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA), and blockchain enhance the effectiveness of IAM. The findings indicate that AI-enhanced IAM frameworks significantly improve anomaly detection and privilege management, whereas blockchain provides transparency and immutability for access governance. Moreover, following international standards such as NIST SP 800-207 and ISO/IEC 27001 ensures compliance and facilitates interoperability across sectors. The results highlight that successful IAM implementation involves not only technology but also a blend of human, procedural, and regulatory factors. The paper concludes that future advancements in IAM will rely on integrating explainable AI, decentralised identity models, and quantum-resilient architectures to ensure adaptive, ethical, and secure identity governance within evolving digital ecosystems. As this study employs a qualitative, interpretive methodology based exclusively on secondary sources, the findings are conceptual. Empirical validation through longitudinal case studies and controlled deployments constitutes an important direction for future research.

title: HybridVesselNet: A Hybrid Approach for Retinal Vessel Segmentation and Biomarker Extraction for Alzheimer's Disease
authors: Arka Chakraborty, Rifa Nawal, Md. Arik Mubashir, Purba Barua, Santu Mallick, Aditta Chowdhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is caused by cognitive decline and cerebral microvascular dysfunction, which is often reflected in alterations of retinal vascular structure. This study proposes HybridVesselNet, a scratch trained hybrid deep learning framework developed to achieve precise retinal vessel segmentation and extract morphological biomarkers that may aid early AD detection. By combining residual blocks with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA), a Swin Transformer-based self-attention bottleneck, and a Parallel Atrous Pyramid (PAP) module, the model effectively captures both local and global vessel structures. A tailored preprocessing pipeline incorporating Contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), gamma correction, and brightness-contrast enhancementwas applied to improve image quality and vessel visibility. The model was trained on the DRIVE dataset and evaluated on CHASE DB1, attaining satisfactory segmentation performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 6. 6 0 \%}$</tex> accuracy, 80.56% Dice coefficient, and 67.45% Intersection-over-Union (IoU), outperforming several state-of-the-art(SOTA) approaches. To extract reliable vascular traits, different morphological feature analyses, including tortuosity, vessel width, density, and fractal dimension, are done, which may later serve as interpretable, noninvasive indicators of AD. These results underscore the potential of combining retinal imaging with deep learning to establish, accessible framework for early neurovascular assessment in neurodegenerative disease diagnostics.

title: J-Neus: Joint Field Optimization for Neural Surface Reconstruction in Urban Scenes With Limited Image Overlap
authors: Fusang Wang, Hala Djeghim, Fabien Moutarde, Désiré Sidibé
abstract: Reconstructing the surrounding surface geometry from recorded driving sequences poses a significant challenge due to the limited image overlap and complex topology of urban environments. SoTA neural implicit surface reconstruction methods often struggle in such setting, either failing due to small vision overlap or exhibiting suboptimal performance in accurately reconstructing both the surface and fine structures. To address these limitations, we introduce J-NeuS, a novel hybrid implicit surface reconstruction method for large driving sequences with outward facing camera poses. J-NeuS leverages cross-representation uncertainty estimation to tackle ambiguous geometry caused by limited observations. Our method performs joint optimization of two radiance fields in addition to guided sampling achieving accurate reconstruction of large areas along with fine structures in complex urban scenarios. Extensive evaluation on major driving datasets demonstrates the superiority of our approach in reconstructing large driving sequences with limited image overlap, outperforming concurrent SoTA methods.

title: Design and Development of a Cost-Effective and Portable BiPAP Device for Resource-Limited Healthcare Settings
authors: Mst Tasnim Fariha Khanom, Nafisa Ferdous, Jesia Briti, Md. Ibtesham Rashid, Avijeet Debnath, Prosanta Bhowmik, Md. Abdullah Al Mamun, Samiul Based Shuvo
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP) therapy is widely used for patients suffering from respiratory illnesses such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and sleep apnea, but commercial devices remain prohibitively expensive and inaccessible in many lowresource environments. This work presents the development of a low-cost, semi-automatic portable BiPAP system developed using an AC blower controlled by an Arduino and TRIAC-based dimmer for pressure regulation, with a hybrid keypad-TFT interface for parameter input and monitoring. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0}$</tex>-second initialization phase allows automatic estimation of the patient's inspiratory: expiratory (I: E) ratio, while allowing manual clinical adjustments. Bench testing demonstrated stable delivery of inspiratory positive airway pressure (IPAP) of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 9} \mathbf{~ c m H}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}$</tex>, expiratory positive airway pressure (EPAP) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 - 5} \text{cmH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$</tex>, and flow rates up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 7 ~ L} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{m i n}$</tex>. With a build cost of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{9, 0 0 0 ~ B D T} (\sim \text{USD} 74)$</tex>, nearly ten times cheaper than commercial alternatives, the system offers a scalable and affordable solution for respiratory support in resource-limited environments.

title: An Explainable Transformer-Based Framework for Bangla Health Misinformation Detection on Social Media
authors: Maimuna Akter Shawon, Farzana Tasnim, Ayesha Juleka, Mohammad Saeed Hasan Chowdhury, Tanjim Mahmud, Sindor Sapaev, Shahnoza Tursunova, Abubokor Hanip, Mohammad Shahadat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The widespread circulation of health misinformation on Bangla social media poses a serious public health concern. To address this issue, an explainable transformer-based framework is proposed for Bangla health misinformation detection. A dataset of 5,038 Bangla health-related statements has been developed from various online platforms such as social media, blog and annotated as REAL or FAKE based on verification by medical and food safety specialist. The dataset covers misinformation on major diseases including cancer, heart attack, stroke, dengue, malaria, seasonal flu, and COVID-19. Four transformer modelsBERT, mBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, and BanglaBERThave been fine-tuned and evaluated on this dataset. BanglaBERT has achieved the best performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 6 \%}$</tex> accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, outperforming other multilingual and cross-lingual models. The proposed framework has demonstrated its effectiveness and reliability for automated Bangla health misinformation detection on social media platforms.

title: Thermal and Optical Properties of Doped Germanene Nanoribbons: A Molecular Dynamics and First-Principles Study
authors: Md Jahidul Hoq Emon, Md. Rafiqul Islam Rafi, Samia Subrina
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Germanene nanoribbons (GeNRs), as emerging twodimensional materials, exhibit unique structural, thermal and optical properties which make them attractive candidates for future nanoelectronic and optoelectronic applications. In this work, we systematically investigate the thermal transport and optical responses of pristine and doped GeNRs using density functional theory (DFT) combined with molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Pristine GeNRs are analyzed to establish baseline phonon transport, while substitutional doping with Group IV elements is employed to tune their properties. Our results reveal that doping alters thermal conductivity thereby enabling thermal management control at the nanoscale. Furthermore, optical absorption spectra demonstrate notable red and blueshifts depending on the dopant species, indicating tunability for optoelectronic device integration. This research implies how adding doping can control the way GeNRs manage heat and light, which is key to creating custom-designed components for thermal management, light sensors and energy devices.

title: Benchmarking ROS2 Nav2 for Autonomous Indoor Navigation in a Simulated Institutional Environment
authors: Jihene Rezgui, Noah Favreau, Ilian Djorf
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: We present a ROS2 Nav2 indoor navigation stack deployed on a custom differential-drive robot with wheel encoders, IMU, and 360° LiDAR. We combine EKF sensor fusion with AMCL for localization, and adopt SmacPlanner2D with Savitzky-Golay smoothing and a Regulated Pure Pursuit controller for path tracking. We evaluate the system in a highfidelity Gazebo simulation of an educational facility across three scenarios: straight corridor, sharp 90° turn, and pedestrian-dense dynamic obstacles. Over three runs per scenario, Nav2 achieves a 100% terminal success rate. Tracking error is near-ideal in the straight corridor (RMS 1.6 cm) and bounded in the sharp turn (RMS 41.5 cm), while the dynamic obstacle scenario exhibits a heavy-tailed error distribution (mean 109 cm; max 384 cm) with an average of 2.3 AMCL pose jumps per run. We show that goal-reaching alone is insufficient as a deployment-grade success criterion without continuous clearance constraints, and advocate for jointly reporting terminal outcomes and continuouspath metrics when benchmarking Nav2 stacks in dynamic indoor environments.

title: Machine Learning-Based Gender Estimation from Hand Dimensions in Bangladeshi Population
authors: Md. Asadujjaman, S M Arif, Udoy Ahmed, Shamsunnahar Khanam, Dewan Hafiz Nabil
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study aims to estimate human gender from different hand anthropometric measurements using machine learning techniques. The study sample comprises a total of 292 individuals (145 men and 147 women), aged between 18 and 60 years old. A total of nine linear hand anthropometric measurements were collected from each hand such as hand length (HL), hand breadth (HB), maximum hand breadth (MHB), palm length (PL), thumb finger length (TFL), index finger length (IFL), middle finger length (MFL), ring finger length (RFL), and little finger length (LFL). Support Vector Machine (SVM) and random Forest (RF) algorithms were applied to classify gender, using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation approach. For left-hand measurements, SVM achieved the highest accuracy of 93.22%, while <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R F}$</tex> achieved a maximum accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1. 5 3 \%}$</tex> for right-hand measurements. The PL revealed as the best anthropometric predictor to estimate the gender, followed by the HL. The findings demonstrates that both the SVM and RF are effective to estimate the gender from hand anthropometric measurements that provides practical application in biomedical engineering, forensic science and human factors engineering.

title: The Role of Community Capitals in Shaping Resilience to Climate-Induced Drought: Lessons From the Barind Tract, Bangladesh
authors: Md. Shakil Ar Salan, Jahid Hasan, Md. Abdul Wakil, Sadia Anjum Khan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Barind Tract region of Bangladesh experiences frequent droughts, which have a substantial effect on the local communities residing in this area. Evaluating the community's ability to withstand and rebound from drought is essential for comprehending how these communities might enhance their capacity to manage and recuperate from the impacts of drought. The study aims to evaluate the resilience of the Barind Tract region to drought by using a community capitals viewpoint. The study will analyze several types of capitals present in the community in order to gain a thorough understanding of the community's capacity to endure and recover from drought occurrences. The community capitals framework, which includes social, human, natural, physical and financial provides a holistic perspective on community resilience. A mixedmethods research design and a range of data gathering strategies, such as focus groups, key informant interviews, household questionnaire surveys using the KOBO toolkit. Each upazila held one focus group discussion, nine important informants were interviewed, and their perceptive perspectives were evaluated. The study revealed that financial capital (path coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.35, \mathrm{p}<0.05$</tex>), natural capital (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.20, \mathrm{p}<0.05$</tex>), and social capital (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.18, \mathrm{p}<0.05$</tex>) had significant positive effects on community resilience, while human and physical capital had no significant effects. Descriptive indicators reflect strong physical capital (public market index value 0.968; transportation 0.900) and human capital (education and awareness index 0.725), while weaknesses in financial capital are observed in investment (0.221), savings (0.258) and insurance (0.252). While social capital shows strong bonding and bridging relationships, weaknesses in institutional linkages (linking capital 0.208) are observed. Qualitative results suggest that water-related infrastructure, technological innovation and coordinated government support are crucial in addressing drought. The research findings reflect the multidimensionality and interdependence of resilience, and policy recommendations emphasize increasing financial security, strengthening institutional linkages, investing in sustainable infrastructure, and developing capacity, which will advance sustainable adaptation in drought-prone agro-ecological systems.

title: TextureSplat: Per-Primitive Texture Mapping for Reflective Gaussian Splatting
authors: Mae Younes, Adnane Boukhayma
abstract: Gaussian Splatting have demonstrated remarkable novel view synthesis performance at high rendering frame rates. Optimization-based inverse rendering within complex capture scenarios remains however a challenging problem. A particular case is modelling complex surface light interactions for highly reflective scenes, which results in intricate high frequency specular radiance components. We hypothesize that such challenging settings can benefit from increased representation power. We hence propose a method that tackles this issue through a geometrically and physically grounded Gaussian Splatting borne radiance field, where normals and material properties are spatially variable in the primitive's local space. Using per-primitive texture maps for this purpose, we also propose to harness the GPU hardware to accelerate rendering at test time via unified material texture atlas. Code will be available at TextureSplat.

title: SPATIALGEN: Layout-Guided 3D Indoor Scene Generation
authors: Chuan Fang, Heng Li, Yixun Liang, Jia Zheng, Yongsen Mao, Yuan Liu, Rui Tang, Zihan Zhou, Ping Tan
abstract: Creating high-fidelity 3D models of indoor environments is essential for applications in design, virtual reality, and robotics. However, manual 3D modeling remains timeconsuming and labor-intensive. While recent advances in generative AI have enabled automated scene synthesis, existing methods often face challenges in balancing visual quality, diversity, semantic consistency, and user control. A major bottleneck is the lack of a large-scale, high-quality dataset tailored to this task. To address this gap, we introduce a comprehensive synthetic dataset, featuring 12,328 structured annotated scenes with 57,431 rooms, and 4.7 M photorealistic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> renderings. Leveraging this dataset, we present SpatialGen, a novel multi-view multi-modal diffusion model that generates realistic and semantically consistent 3D indoor scenes. Given a 3D layout and a reference image (derived from a text prompt), our model synthesizes appearance (color image), geometry (scene coordinate map), and semantic (semantic segmentation map) from arbitrary viewpoints, while preserving spatial consistency across modalities. SpatialGen consistently generates supe-rior results to previous methods in our experiments. We are open-sourcing our data and models to empower the community and advance the field of indoor scene understanding and generation.

title: Revisiting Depth Representations for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Duochao Shi, Weijie Wang, Donny Y. Chen, Zeyu Zhang, Jia-Wang Bian, Bohan Zhuang
abstract: Depth maps are widely used in feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) pipelines by unprojecting them into 3D point clouds for novel view synthesis. This approach offers advantages such as efficient training, the use of known camera poses, and accurate geometry estimation. However, depth discontinuities, which are particularly problematic at the boundaries of the reconstructed geometry, often lead to fragmented or sparse point clouds, degrading rendering quality-a well-known limitation of depth-based representations. To tackle this issue, we introduce PM-Loss, a novel regularization loss based on a pointmap predicted by a pre-trained transformer. Although the pointmap itself may be less accurate than the depth map, it provides a powerful prior for geometric coherence and structural completeness, especially at the very edges where depth prediction falters. With the improved depth map, our method significantly improves the feed-forward 3DGS across various architectures and scenes, delivering consistently better rendering results. Project page: https://aimuofa.github.io/PMLoss

title: Design Criteria for DC GIL Insulation: Permissible Electric Field Strength
authors: Chuanyang Li, Zuodong Liang, Bin Zhou, Lingling Tang, Zehao Zhang, Changhong Zhang, Geng Chen, Jinliang He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Gas-insulated metal-enclosed transmission lines (GIL) are critical for addressing high-capacity power transmission challenges in unique geographical environments. Mature design criteria for electric field strength exist for AC GIL insulation systems. However, the insulation polarization behavior under DC voltage differs significantly from that under AC, and no comprehensive international design criteria for permissible electric field strengths in DC GIL insulation systems have been established, nor is any DC GIL equipment put into operation. This paper addresses this gap by focusing on three critical regions: the conductor surface, the gas-solid interface, and the metal-insulator interface. The coupled effects of multiple factors—including steady-state DC, superimposed LI and SIL voltage, temperature gradients, surface roughness, micro-gaps, and material aging—are considered. Through experimental and simulation analysis, a permissible electric field strength criterion on HVDC GIL insulator design is established. Based on this criterion, a ±500 kV DC GIL insulation system is developed.

title: QuantSR+: Pushing the Limit of Quantized Image Super-Resolution Networks
authors: Haotong Qin, Xudong Ma, Xianglong Liu, Jie Luo, Jinyang Guo, Michele Magno, Yulun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Low-bit quantization is widely used to compress super-resolution (SR) models and reduce storage and computation costs for deployment on resource-limited devices. However, when SR models are pushed to ultra-low precision (2-4 bits), performance can drop sharply due to diminished representational capacity and the detail-sensitive nature of SR. To address these issues, we propose QuantSR+, a unified framework that improves quantization operators, network design, and training optimization, achieving better trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency than prior low-bit SR methods. QuantSR+ mainly relies on three technical contributions: (1) Redistribution-driven Bit Determination (RBD), which reshapes quantization distributions in both forward and backward passes to preserve representation fidelity; (2) Quantized Slimmable Architecture (QSA), which begins with an over-parameterized model and progressively prunes less critical blocks to meet efficiency budgets while pushing the accuracy performance; and (3) Slimming-guided Function-localized Distillation (SFD), which enforces block-aware feature alignment via a direct loss and a progressive, function-local training schedule to capture quantization effects better and speed up convergence. Extensive experiments show that QuantSR+ achieves state-of-the-art performance against both specialized quantized SR methods and generic quantization approaches. For SwinIR-S on Urban100 (×4), it improves PSNR by 0.29 dB over the 2-bit SOTA baseline. Meanwhile, it delivers strong efficiency gains at 2-bit, reducing operations by up to 87.9% and storage by 89.4%. QuantSR+ is effective for both convolutional and transformer-based SR models, indicating broad applicability.

title: A Resilient Edge Data Integrity Verification Approach Via Robust Merkle Trees and Steganographic Transmission_supp1-3697006.pdf
authors: Md Palash Uddin
abstract: Edge Data Integrity Verification (EDIV) plays a crucial role in maintaining the authenticity of cached data replicas in decentralized and resource-constrained edge computing environments. However, existing EDIV methods prioritize generating cryptographic proofs while overlooking the need to ensure robustness against localized data tampering and neglecting the inherent security risks involved in transmitting these proofs over untrusted backhaul communication networks. These gaps leave the EDIV process vulnerable to both the manipulation of integrity proofs and their interception during transmission, ultimately undermining the reliability of EDIV. To address these gaps, we propose RAMTStego-EDIV, a novel framework that ensures both the robustness of integrity proofs and their secure transmission. In particular, RAMTStego-EDIV first introduces the Robust Aggregated Merkle Tree (RAMT)—a ternary hash tree that combines real and auxiliary nodes to produce tamper-evident cryptographic proofs, ensuring that even partial manipulation of the data can be reliably detected. Then, it develops a dual-parity transmission control protocol (TCP) steganography technique to protect the transmission of the RAMT-driven cryptographic proofs that covertly embed the proof bits into two TCP header fields: the parity of the payload length and the least significant bit of the TCP window size. This embedding allows proofs to travel invisibly within normal traffic, avoiding detection and interference by adversaries monitoring the network. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate that RAMTStego-EDIV prevents proof tampering and interception, confirming its resilience and suitability for deployment in adversarial mobile edge computing environments.

title: Enhanced Dynamic Response and Robustness of Grid-Forming Control by Incorporating Internal Dynamics of MMC
authors: Prabhat Kumar Singh, Nallamatti Poornachandra Rao, Anshuman Shukla
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In this paper, an improved grid-forming (GFM) control is proposed for modular multilevel converter (MMC) in order to achieve better dynamic performance. This is accomplished due to two factors. The first one is due to the proposed modification in the impedance function of MMC, which now includes a component corresponding to the internal dynamics of submodule capacitor voltage occurring due to a transient change in output current. The second one is due to the design of the submodule capacitor, considering the transient performance of GFM control. Both these factors contribute to the leftward movement of closed-loop poles of the GFM control transfer function, enabling better damping and response times. Furthermore, it is shown that the proposed modifications preserve the small-signal stability and damping robustness of the MMC-based grid-forming power–frequency control against variations in grid strength measured in terms of short-circuit ratio (SCR). This paper includes a detailed analytical study of conventional as well as proposed methods. The enhancements obtained through proposed modifications are verified using PSCAD simulation studies and also on a scaled-down laboratory experimental prototype.

title: Leveraging Vehicle-Side Detectors With Graph Priors for Roadside Domain Adaptive 3-D Object Detection
authors: Wei Gong, Bowen Wang, Yafei Wang, Chin Long Ng, Jiaming Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The deployment of LiDAR-based 3D roadside measurements and detections in new scenarios involves a labor-intensive process of data collection and annotation. With the rapid development of the high-quality labeled vehicle-side autonomous driving dataset in last few years, it has potential to directly transfer the knowledge and enhance the robustness of roadside LiDAR-based 3D perception model. However, since the domain gaps between different perspectives, it is still suffering a performance degradation when directly applying the training model to unknown roadside scenarios. To this end, we propose a graph-aware self-supervised domain adaptation (GA-RP3D) method to enhance the roadside 3D perception using vehicle-side 3D object detection models. Firstly, we develop a graph-aware knowledge distillation framework to bridge the domain gap, where the learnable regions of interest (ROIs) with spatiotemporal motion states are used to construct global graph nodes and edges. In the adaptive fine-tuning stage, a decoupled graph consistency alignment loss function with a robust kernel is designed to enhance the quality of graph consistency representation and improve training stability. On popular 3D roadside benchmarks and self-collected roadside data, the proposed method demonstrates significant competitiveness compared with the state-of-the-art in the evaluated settings.

title: 3D SLAM and Deep Learning for Real-Time Fire Localization in Ship Engine Rooms_supp1-3696438.docx
authors: SEON DEOK KIM

title: Ground-Station-Free Multi-UAV Formation with an Airborne Wi-Fi Hub and Distributed Avionics
authors: Kazi Tauhid Mokbul Hussain, Farhan Rahman Atul, Md. Al Irfan Talukder, Md Rafid Rahman, Anbar Shamim Nabeeha, Md. Musfiqur Rahaman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Fieldable multirotor swarms often depend on a ground link or high-bandwidth perception, which limits robustness and range. We report a lightweight system that removes the ground station and still keeps formation tight by pairing an airborne master-slave network with ENU-referenced control and onboard person counting. The master serves as a Wi-Fi access point and timing anchor; followers receive compact formation cues in the local ENU frame and track them. We validate with two followers in AirSim and outdoor flights. On a 5 m circular formation, followers hold sub-meter radial RMSE and show bounded step responses for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{3} \mathrm{m}$</tex> formation perturbations with small overshoot. In-flight networking yields round-trip delay of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 200 \text{ms}$</tex>; latency-injection trials quantify sensitivity, with orbit RMSE increasing by 0.06 m per additional 100 ms of round-trip time (RTT), explaining part of the sim-to-field gap. The onboard perception stack transmits only integer counts yet attains F1 up to 0.81 across different scenes. Together, these results indicate that a bandwidth-efficient, airborne hub can deliver reliable, low-latency multi-UAV coordination without a ground station. Limitations include scaling beyond two followers, operation in stronger winds and longer missions.

title: Hybrid Transformer-Based Approaches for Pharmacovigilance: Detecting Adverse Drug Reaction from Patient-Generated Drug Reviews
authors: Reduanul Bari Shovon, Md. Anamul Haque Sabbir, Tanvir Chowdhury, Md. Akib Alam, Md. Rakibul Hasan, Safia Sultana Urmi, Md. Ahsan Arif
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Adverse drug reactions (ADRs), which result in major health problems and fatalities worldwide, remain a major concern in pharmacovigilance. For accurate ADR identification, automated techniques are needed because traditional spontaneous reporting under-reports. This research presents an end to end system for modeling transformer-based methodology for ADR detection using patient-generated drug evaluations. A dataset of 4,143 drug reviews from Drugs.lib was curated and preprocessed utilizing a conventional natural language processing pipeline, encompassing badline handle, emptry string filling with NaN values, data clearing, three types of patient review combining, lower-case converting and binary label coloumn creating. Five hybrid transformer based models—RoBERTa+XLNet, BERT+Softmax, BiLSTM+Glove, PubmedBERT, and a hybrid BioBERT+RoBERTa—were assessed. Among the models, Hybrid RoBERTa+BioBERT attained the highest classification accuracy of 88.9 %, signifying a robust balance between performance and computational expense. The findings indicate that RoBERTa+BioBERT is highly effective for precise ADR detection for this dataset. Moreover, all the other performance metrics of the proposed model are compared with each other, and it is shown that the proposed model is computationally faster than other applied hybrid models.

title: A Security Architecture for Hybrid Paas Mitigating Identity, Network, and Application Risks
authors: Afnan Alsaleh, Nesreen Alotaibi, Shahad Alaskar, Kashif Saleem, Mourad Benmalek, Kamel-Dine Haouam
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper proposes a security architecture for Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) in hybrid cloud environments, addressing identity, network, and application risks through an integrated, compliance-aware framework. The design adopts core security principles Zero Trust, Defense in Depth, and Least Privilege and implements controls such as MFA, RBAC, encryption, and secure CI/CD pipelines to mitigate threats modeled via STRIDE. The framework is explicitly aligned with regulatory standards including ISO 27001, GDPR, and PCI DSS, employing private endpoints, Hardware Security Modules (HSM), and hybrid-aware policies to manage data residency, identity synchronization, and cross-border compliance challenges.

title: Quantum-Enhanced Tax Revenue via A-Challan: ML, LLMs, and QML Approaches
authors: Md Jawadul Tashick, Arpita Dass, Swapnil Saha, Niloy Ahsan, Abid Mashrafi, Vishwanath Akuthota, Md Sadi Ashraf, Ahmed Selim Anwar, Sahal Bin Saad, Md Tanzim Reza
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: In an era of digital transformation, governments around the world are exploring cutting-edge technologies to improve the transparency and efficiency of revenue generation. This paper presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of three quantum-enhanced approaches for government revenue system analysis: Quantum Machine Learning (QML), Quantum Large Language Models (QLLM), and Quantum Advanced Models (QAM). Through empirical evaluation on 44 real Bangladesh government A-Challan documents totaling <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5,071,301$</tex> in revenue, we demonstrate that the Quantum Advanced Model achieves superior performance with a 100 % data processing success rate, 28.05 % validated ROI, and 95.2 % collection efficiency. In contrast, both QML and QLLM approaches exhibit critical limitations including 22.7 % failure rates, negative neural network performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{- 2 9 2. 9 3 4}$</tex>), and data reliability concerns. Our findings establish the Quantum Advanced Model as the optimal solution for real-world government financial analysis applications, offering immediate deployment capability with concrete financial benefits of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,422,499.93$</tex> annually. This research represents the first successful quantum analysis of real government financial data, positioning Bangladesh as a pioneer in quantum government technology.

title: An Efficient Miniaturized Tri-Band THz Antenna for Future 6G Systems and Beyond
authors: Sayed Shifat Ahmed, Shah Md Toufik Ahmed, Sadia Tabassum, Md Arafath Hossain Rafi, Md. Mehedi Hassan, Md. Saffat Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This manuscript delineates the design and performance of a wideband microstrip patch antenna, envisioned for utilization in the imminent 6G era and beyond, specifically tailored for terahertz communication systems. The antenna is meticulously crafted with a star-shaped structure, positioned on a Rogers RT5880 substrate, accompanied by a partial slotted ground plane. The antenna exhibits operation in three distinct bands accomplishing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 1 0} \mathbf{ d B}$</tex> impedance bandwidths of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$500 \text{GHz}, 730 \text{GHz}$</tex>, and 1280 GHz, respectively. Furthermore, the antenna demonstrates peak gains (6.95 dB) and high radiation efficiency (95%). The antenna boasts a commendable voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) of approximately 1.1 indicative of excellent impedance matching with the load. The proposed antenna possesses the merits of extensive bandwidth, high efficiency, and miniaturized size, rendering it apt for terahertz wireless communication networks such as wearables and Internet of Things (IoT) sensors, making it well-suited for a wide range of applications in the 6G and beyond ecosystem.

title: Improved Cinematic-Guided Camera Language Transfer in 3D Scene
authors: Yuan Wang, Zhuoling Jiang, Bailin Deng, Yipeng Qin
abstract: Directors and cinematographers often recreate iconic scenes by replicating the underlying camera language to evoke shared aesthetic and narrative meaning. In this work, we refer to this as the task of Cinematic-Guided Camera Language Transfer, where the goal is to reproduce the cinematic camera language of a reference video clip in a new 3D scene. The pioneer work, Jaws [62], tackles this problem by adapting generic computer vision methods but fails to model the essential principles of cinematography, often leading to inaccurate framing, motion mismatches, and loss of expressive intent. To overcome these limitations, we systematically define the objectives of camera language transfer, grounding them in professional cinematography literature. Specifically, we conduct an in-depth review of cinematography literature to identify eight key cinematic features and encode them into five novel camera language losses. These losses not only guide optimization of camera parameters for effective transfer, but also serve as quantitative metrics for evaluating cinematographic fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method.

title: Hybrid Algorithms for Suppression of Sidelobes
authors: Suraj K, Dhruva, Animesh Rawat, Dilip Kumar Choudhary
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Antenna array performance is significantly influenced by the presence of sidelobes, which cause unwanted radiation and interference in undesired directions. Traditional sidelobe suppression methods such as amplitude tapering using Dolph-Chebyshev distributions offer controlled sidelobe levels but suffer from limited adaptability and a trade-off between sidelobe reduction and main lobe width. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a hybrid optimization algorithm that combines the Invasive Weed Optimization (IWO) and Intelligent Water Drops (IWD) techniques for improved sidelobe suppression in linear antenna arrays. The hybrid approach leverages the global exploration ability of IWO and the local refinement efficiency of IWD to optimize excitation weights dynamically. A multi-objective fitness function is designed to minimize sidelobe levels while maintaining main beam direction and pattern smoothness. Simulation results demonstrate that the hybrid IWO-IWD algorithm achieves superior sidelobe suppression, faster convergence, and better radiation pattern uniformity compared to conventional tapering and single-algorithm methods. The proposed technique offers a computationally efficient and robust solution suitable for modern adaptive beamforming and phased array applications.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Learning for Online Bond Wire Lift-Off Monitoring in IGBTs via Acoustic Emission_supp1-3672753.pdf
authors: Yuan Yang
abstract: Bond wire lift-off is one of the dominant failure mechanisms in insulated gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) power modules. This article presents a physics-guided acoustic emission (AE) monitoring framework that couples mechanism modeling with a lightweight deep network (AE–STFT–R18-Lite-SE) for real-time bond wire health assessment. A coupled electromagnetic-force and constrained-thermal-expansion analysis identifies two degradation-sensitive AE frequency bands and derives closed-form amplitude scaling laws versus the number of lifted wires. These physics priors are systematically embedded into short-time Fourier transform (STFT) parameterization, squeeze-and-excitation attention allocation, channel pruning, and 8-bit integer (INT8) quantization, yielding a 1.9 M-parameter model with 3.2 ms inference latency on an edge multiprocessor system-on-chip (MPSoC). Comprehensive experimental validation on a multimodule platform covering eight degradation states grouped into four health levels achieves 98.6% classification accuracy. Accuracy above 95.9% is retained under noise, sensor placement variation, solder voiding, cross-package transfer, ambient temperature, H-bridge PWM drive, and full-lifecycle power cycling to natural failure. These results demonstrate that physics-informed AE monitoring offers an early, noninvasive, operating-point-robust approach to power module condition assessment.

title: Automated Classification of EQ-5D Literature in PubMed Using Multi-Phase Learning and LLM-Assisted Co-Training
authors: Zhyar Rzgar K Rostam, Márta Péntek, János Tibor Czere, Zsombor Zrubka, László Gulácsi, Gábor Kertész
abstract: The EQ-5D is a widely used tool for measuring health-related quality of life (HRQoL) to support clinical, economic, and policy decision making. Manually classifying the growing volume of literature reporting EQ-5D data for systematic literature reviews is a challenging, inefficient, and labor-intensive task. To address this, we propose a comprehensive classification framework utilizing pre-trained language models (PLMs), including BERT, SciBERT, BioBERT, PubMedBERT, and BioLinkBERT, to categorize PubMed records based on whether the article reports EQ-5D data using article metadata (titles, abstracts, and keywords). We examine three learning approaches: supervised learning, semi-supervised learning with pseudo-labeling, and a co-training strategy with and without Large Language Model (LLM) assistance (GPT and Claude) in pseudo-label generation. We introduce a confidence-based ensembling within the co-training framework to improve classification reliability and robustness. This study provides a systematic multi-phase evaluation of supervised, semi-supervised, and co-training paradigms on PubMed records using different input configurations and investigates model performance in stages with 200 labeled samples and an expanded unlabeled dataset through iterative pseudo-labeling, while benchmarking across models. The results show that the co-training approach achieved the highest performance, with an F1-score of up to 0.85. Performance is reported using multi-seed evaluation with mean ± standard deviation and 95% confidence intervals. LLM-assisted co-training improves weaker model pairs but may degrade performance for already strong model combinations and reduce the number of high-confidence pseudo-labeled samples due to confidence thresholds. LLMs used with ensemble and semi-supervised approaches provide an effective framework for EQ-5D literature screening under limited labeled data.
