title: Design and Additive Manufacturing of a Right-Hand Prosthesis for Phalangeal Amputations
authors: Segundo Manuel Espín-Lagos, Karla Abigail Guerra-Chiquito, Emily Anahí Coba-Ronquillo, Diego Rafael Freire-Romero, Valeria Isabel Espín-López, Enrique Mauricio Barreno-Avila
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Phalangeal amputations present significant clinical and socioeconomic challenges, as conventional prostheses are often costly and functionally limited. This study addresses this gap by presenting an innovative, additively manufactured right-hand prosthesis tailored for partial hand loss, focusing on optimizing biomechanical fidelity, structural durability, and economic accessibility. The design integrates a dual four-bar linkage mechanism for coordinated digital motion, requiring only a single-degree-of-freedom actuation. The prototype was fabricated using Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol (PETG) filament, selected for its superior strength and biocompatibility, achieving a total production cost of just $ 136. Rigorously validated through kinematic, static force, and Finite Element Analysis (FEA), the prosthesis successfully replicates natural finger trajectories with less than 8.2% deviation. Crucially, the mechanical advantage provided by the linkages generated grip forces 194-211% above WHO minimum standards, enabling the manipulation of objects up to 1.5 kg. FEA confirmed clinical-grade robustness, with minimum safety factors exceeding 1.78. Functional testing demonstrated 92-100% efficiency across five essential grip patterns, from precision pinch to power grasp. This solution represents a 90% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives, establishing a scalable, affordable, and highly functional framework for patient-specific prosthetic development, thereby offering a viable pathway to transform global prosthetic care in resource-limited settings.

title: Bio-Inspired Optic Flow-Based Navigation Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Praveena W. Dewapura, Matthew A. Garratt, Asanka Perera, Mandyam V. Srinivasan, Sridhar Ravi
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Autonomous navigation systems conventionally rely on multi-sensor fusion, incorporating GPS, LiDAR, radar, and cameras, which face limitations in GPS-denied and computationally constrained environments. In contrast, flying insects demonstrate remarkable navigation capabilities using only optic flow derived from simple visual systems, suggesting alternative strategies for autonomous platforms. This bio-inspired approach offers a computationally efficient solution particularly suitable for resource-constrained aerial vehicles. While recent advances in deep reinforcement learning (DRL) have yielded substantial progress in autonomous navigation, existing vision-based methods predominantly utilize raw imagery and depth information, incurring significant computational overhead. Our framework diverges from this paradigm by employing optic flow, acquired through dedicated optic flow sensors, as the sole sensory input. This vector field representing apparent motion significantly reduces processing requirements while maintaining robust performance. We introduce a dual-agent DRL architecture controlling pitch and roll independently through continuous action spaces, enabling precise maneuvering and enhanced training stability. Validated in simulated tunnel environments, the simulated bee successfully learns obstacle avoidance and stable navigation via end-to-end training. Comparative analysis demonstrates superiority over baseline approaches in computational time and memory demands. This work bridges bio-inspired sensing with DRL-based control for efficient autonomous navigation in challenging environments.

title: An Investigation of an Anchor for Vibratory Penetration Powered by Thrusters in Underwater Saturated Soft Soil
authors: Akira Ofuchi, Kojiro Iizuka
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Scallop fishing in Japan requires higher efficiency as the workforce ages. Small underwater robots using smart fishing technologies have been introduced to support scallop distribution surveys from visual data. However, visual data alone limits survey accuracy. More precise information requires direct contact with scallops, which in turn requires a stable posture on the seabed. This study proposes an anchoring method using a small anchor to hold the robot steady. The method assumes that the anchor penetrates the seabed through downward thrust and added vibration. However, penetration using thrust alone has not been tested. Thus, penetration tests using only thrust were performed, followed by tests comparing penetration depth with and without vibration. The results show that the anchor can penetrate soft seabed soil using thrust alone, and that vibration further increases penetration depth. These results support the development of stable and efficient underwater operation systems for small robots and advance smart fishing applications.

title: A Modular and Scalable System Architecture for Heterogeneous UAV Swarms Using ROS 2 and PX4-Autopilot
authors: Robert Pommeranz, Kevin Tebbe, Ralf Heynicke, Gerd Scholl
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: In this paper a modular and scalable architecture for heterogeneous swarm-based Counter Unmanned Aerial Systems (C-UASs) built on PX4-Autopilot and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) framework is presented. The proposed architecture emphasizes seamless integration of hardware components by introducing independent ROS 2 nodes for each component of a Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV). Communication between swarm participants is abstracted in software, allowing the use of various technologies without architectural changes. Key functionalities are supported, e.g. leader following and formation flight to maneuver the swarm. The system also allows computer vision algorithms to be integrated for the detection and tracking of UAVs. Additionally, a ground station control is integrated for the coordination of swarm operations.

title: Object Detection During Driving Using Hemispherical LiDAR for Autonomous Driving
authors: Shinji Kajiwara
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This research addresses the challenges in achieving fully autonomous driving, which is highly anticipated for its potential to prevent accidents by mitigating human error-the cause of over 90% of traffic incidents. Realizing this requires advancements in machine learning (AI), sensor accuracy, and especially the high-precision recognition of dynamic objects utilizing map information. Current object recognition relies heavily on cameras, but this approach is compromised by environmental factors such as night, rain, and glare, which severely reduce accuracy and compromise safety. While LiDAR offers superior reliability by generating accurate 3D point cloud data even under adverse conditions, the resulting large data volumes create high computational load and time constraints that challenge the real-time processing requirements of autonomous systems. To overcome the latency challenge while maintaining accuracy, this study proposes an approach that projects 3D point cloud data obtained from LiDAR onto a two-dimensional plane. This method aims to achieve both high recognition performance and high-speed processing. The successful implementation of this technique enables accurate and rapid dynamic object recognition using 3D LiDAR, which we believe will significantly enhance the overall safety and reliability of autonomous driving.

title: Hierarchical Space Partition for Surface Reconstruction
authors: Minjie Tang, Xiangfei Li
abstract: Generating compact polygonal models from point clouds is a key problem in 3D vision and computer graphics. However, due to inherent limitations of LiDAR scanning (e.g. range constraints and occlusions), critical scene information is often missing, leading to degraded reconstruction accuracy. To address this, we propose a plane assembling strategy that effectively recovers missing details while maintaining model compactness. We classify all the planes extracted from the scene into three categories: highly visible, barely visible, and invisible. The invisible planes, which are recovered by scene structure analysis, indicate the missing details. The three types of planes correspond to the three growth priorities. Each plane grows according to the priority level, and the space is partitioned progressively, that is, the hierarchical partition. Subsequently, we generate a watertight polygonal mesh from the partition via a min-cut-based optimization. Finally, comparisons on public datasets show the effectiveness and superiority of our method against mainstream approaches.

title: NURBSFit: Robust Fitting of NURBS Surfaces to Point Clouds
authors: Lizeth J. Fuentes Perez, Florent Lafarge, Renato Pajarola
abstract: NURBS surfaces are compact parametric representations widely used in Computer-Aided Design (CAD) modeling. Decomposing raw 3D data measurements into a set of such elements is a challenging problem that existing methods approach by learning from CAD databases to both segment synthetic data and fit parametric shapes on each segment. Unfortunately, these methods generalize poorly to raw data measurements, with low robustness to imperfect data and complex objects and low scalability. To address this issue, we propose NURBSFIT, an algorithm that fits NURBS surfaces to unorganized 3D point clouds, such as those generated by laser and photogrammetry acquisition systems. Starting with a fine configuration of planar patches that approximate the object geometry, our algorithm performs merging operations that progressively regroup pairs of adjacent patches into fewer, more expressive NURBS surfaces. This process is designed to be both robust and performant with a series of technical ingredients that include an energy that controls the global quality of a configuration of NURBS surfaces and an efficient ordering of the merging operations based on a cost-efficient quadric surface fitting analysis. We show the potential of our algorithm on both synthetic and real-world data and its efficiency against existing primitive fitting methods with results both simpler and geometrically more accurate. Our implementation is available at: https://github.com/lizOnly/nurbsfit

title: Semantic-Free Procedural 3D Shapes are Surprisingly Good Teachers
authors: Xuweiyi Chen, Zezhou Cheng
abstract: Self-supervised learning has emerged as a promising approach for acquiring transferable 3D representations from unlabeled 3D point clouds. Unlike 2D images, which are widely accessible, acquiring 3D assets requires specialized expertise or professional 3D scanning equipment, making it difficult to scale and raising copyright concerns. To address these challenges, we propose learning 3D representations from procedural 3D programs that automatically generate 3D shapes using simple 3D primitives and augmentations. Remarkably, despite lacking semantic content, the 3D representations learned from the procedurally generated 3D shapes perform on par with state-of-the-art representations learned from semantically recognizable 3D models (e.g., airplanes) across various downstream 3D tasks, such as shape classification, part segmentation, masked point cloud completion, and both scene semantic and instance segmentation. We provide a detailed analysis on factors that make a good 3D procedural programs. Extensive experiments further suggest that current 3D self-supervised learning methods on point clouds do not rely on semantics of 3D shapes, shedding light on the nature of 3D representations learned.

title: Reflective Metalenses for Near-Infrared Wavelengths Based on Silicon Nanorods
authors: Iftekhar Ahmed, K. B. M. Sharif Mahmood, Tanvin Tamanna
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This paper presents the design and analysis of reflective metalenses optimized for a 900 nm wavelength, using silicon nanorods as the primary components. The metalens consists of unit cells, each containing a thin silicon rod. Lumerical FDTD software is used to create a detailed library that connects the dimensions of these nanorods with their respective phase shifts and reflectance properties. The nanorods are placed on a 70 nm thick silicon dioxide <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{SiO}_{2}\right)$</tex> layer, with a 50 nm thick gold (Au) reflective layer underneath. In contrast to conventional transmissive metalenses, our design achieves complete <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \pi$</tex> phase control via geometry-optimized silicon nanorods, facilitating compact reflective optics. By carefully examining the impact of nanorod dimensions on optical performance, the main goal is to optimize the metalens design for enhanced light focusing and manipulation, which could benefit imaging systems, optical communications, and sensor technology.

title: BNFF-WaveGraph: A BiDirectional Node Fusion-Fission Hybrid Deep Learning Architecture for Android Malware Detection
authors: Fuad Hasan, Ashraful Goni, Farjana Akter, Md Umor Faruk Jahangir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Due to the fast development of the Android devices, there has been an increase in the vulnerability to complex malware in that even the traditional signature based detection mechanisms fail to find the obfuscated and polymorphic threats. Whereas several machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models have been used on malware classification, they have demonstrated significant shortcomings in multi-scale temporal captivity and hierarchy feature abstractions simultaneously. Traditional ensemble approaches have been affected by computational inefficiency, and standalone deep architecture systems have been affected by gradient instability and do not generalise to imbalanced datasets. This paper has over solved these shortcomings by proposing the BiDirectional Node Fusion Fission WaveGraph (BNFF- WaveGraph), a novel hybrid deep learning model that incorporates multi scale temporal convolutions, bidirectional recurrent processing, multi head self-attention and a novel node fusion fission transformation mechanism of compressing and expanding features dynamically. The proposed model with the help of the six-metric ensemble feature selection module has been reported to have reached 98.36 % accuracy on the CICMalDroid 2020 dataset and shows significant discriminative performance and architectural stability. This paper has made BNFF-WaveGraph an effective and scalable platform to detect Android malware with high levels of intelligence in a variety of complex and dynamic threat environments.

title: Sensor Antennas for Early Warning Systems: Requirements, Challenges and Future Directions
authors: Rula Alrawashdeh
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: This paper surveys and reviews sensor antennas designed for early detection systems. It discusses the main design requirements, challenges, and key evaluation parameters. The survey compares various sensor antenna types to highlight their specific features and limitations. Notably, the analysis reveals that critical parameters such as link budget and polarization have been rarely considered in previous evaluations. These parameters, however, are essential for accurate performance assessment in real-world scenarios and should be given careful attention in future designs. The review also identifies patch antennas as the most popular structure for sensor applications. However, other structures, such as loop antennas, can achieve comparable performance if designed with careful control of the coupling area. Additionally, split-ring structures are highlighted as promising candidates for future sensor antenna designs due to their potential advantages. This paper also aims at emphasizing the wireless power transfer and energy harvesting as appealing features for the sensor antenna system.

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting Using Stacked Ensemble Model and Feature Engineering
authors: Md Reazul Hoque, Farzana Akter, Shadia Selim, Fakir Sharif Hossain
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Efficient load forecasting is important for low cost operation of power system. This work introduces a new method for short term electricity demand forecasting in Bangladesh, highlighting the necessity for precise predictions. We utilize historical electricity and weather data from Bangladesh to perform efficient hourly load forecasting. In this paper, we integrated lag and rolling features with regional weather data to enhance the accuracy of the predictions. The proposed approach utilizes a stacked ensemble of tree based models, specifically Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Regression (GBR), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB), and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), augmented by feature engineering. Historical data from the Power Grid Company of Bangladesh undergoes pre-processing to address missing values and outliers. Features including time lags, rolling means, date and time variables are engineered to enhance model performance. Hyperparameter optimization reduces mean absolute error (MAE). The findings indicate that the stacked ensemble model surpasses individual models, with a MAE of 229.50 MW, a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 353.78 MW, and a mean absolute percentage Error (MAPE) of 2.64 %. Incorporating the previous hourly demand and rolling means in feature engineering markedly improves forecast accuracy.

title: Enhancing Computer Vision-Based Chest X-Ray Analysis: A CNN-Transformer Hybrid for Pulmonary Disease Diagnosis
authors: Maftun Ahommed, M. M Rafsanjani Showrav, Saiful Islam, Md. Yusuf, Takia Nawrin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The use of computer vision and deep learning has given us a new beginning of automated medical imaging, and there are still significant issues in architecture in the diagnosis of pulmonary disease using chest X-rays (CXRs). The usefulness of Traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in the local feature representation, but the receptive field usually is small, and it becomes hard to identify the long-range interactions as well as the contextual interactions of the diffuse pathologies across the globe. Nevertheless, the data-efficiency and cost of implementation of pure Transformer-based models, though capable of modelling global context, is notoriously large and thus impractical in clinical settings. In essence to address this critical gap, the article will introduce a new and efficient hybrid, ConvoFormer, which is an opportunistic fusion of the synergistic advantage of CNNs and Transformers. The simpler pipeline is a combination of multistage image preprocessing and augmentation, followed by the lightweight CNN backbone, which is an efficient local feature extractor. Such attention-seeking spatial features maps are encoded, shot through with positional encodings and inputted into a multi-layer Transformer encoder, which is efficient at encoding in complex, global interaction across the entire picture. This synergistic plan gives an overall view of the anatomical context of the fine-grained and holistic view. The model was strictly trained with a large multi-class, curated CXR dataset of 11,023 images in four diagnostic categories, namely COVID-19, Emphysema, Tuberculosis and Normal. ConvoFormer produced a state of the art overall accuracy of 97 % with a perfect F1-score of 1.00 in detection of Tuberculosis making it more precise than very robust baseline models. The article presents a viable tool in clinical decision-support systems by enhancing interpretability and strength and introducing smarter, more accessible medical imaging diagnostics.

title: Genetic Algorithm-Based Privacy Budget Optimization for Differentially Private Gradient Boosting Decision Trees
authors: Marianne Abi Kanaan, Elie Chicha, Jean-François Couchot, Maroun Abi Assaf
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Differentially private gradient boosting decision trees typically rely on uniform heuristics for allocating privacy budget across split decisions and leaf values, often leading to unnecessary loss in predictive performance. We propose a genetic-algorithm-based method for optimizing internal budget allocation under a fixed global privacy constraint, implemented in a lightweight GBDT framework using the exponential mechanism for private splits, Laplace noise for leaves, and Renyi Differential Privacy accounting with subsampling amplification. Experiments on the Adult and Credit-G datasets show consistent improvements in ROC-AUC and, in many regimes, accuracy over equal-budget baselines, while revealing dataset-dependent allocation patterns.

title: Digitizing Conventional Water Meter Readings in Bangladesh Using a Scalable OCR-Based Extension Module
authors: Rajit Patil Atri, Md Inteshar Ishrak, Rajia Sultana, Liza Akter, Abir Ahmed, Md. Rifat Hazari, Md. Saniat Rahman Zishan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Manual meter reading in Bangladesh consumes significant man-month resources, and it makes the process both costly and inefficient. This study presents an image processing-based water meter reading system that automates data collection and reduces the dependence on manual inspections to address the issue. The framework integrates Optical Character Recognition (OCR) with Internet of Things (IoT) functionalities. A Raspberry Pi 3B+ and a high-resolution Pi Camera Module with Wi-Fi connectivity are used to capture and process images of analog water meters. The system was first validated through simulation in the PROTEUS Design Suite, and a prototype was implemented to check operational reliability under varying lighting and installation conditions. Experimental results showed that high-resolution imaging modules improved recognition performance by achieving up to 97 % accuracy in controlled environments, while 90 % in low-light conditions and 40 % in dark settings. The proposed system provides a cost-effective and scalable pathway for modernizing utility management without costly infrastructure replacement by ensuring compatibility with existing analog meters widely deployed in Dhaka city.

title: Code IM-Assisted RIS for D2D Communications with Hardware Imperfections and Phase Noise
authors: Widad Belaoura, Saud Althunibat, Mazen Hasna, Khalid Qaraqe
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: In practical communication systems, residual hardware imperfections often impose distortion noise that compromises the performance of the system. This paper investigates the effect of hardware limitations on the performance of coded index modulation (IM)-assisted reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) for device-to-device (D2D) communication systems. We show that the concept of coded IM can enhance both the spectrum efficiency and error performance of D2D communications in the presence of hardware imperfections, including hardware impairment at the D2D transceivers and phase noise at the RIS. In this system, spread code selection is applied to convey additional information and achieve a higher diversity gain. The D2D receiver performs detection employing a two-stage match correlation approach to reduce the high complexity of the maximum-likelihood (ML) detector. Our simulation results demonstrate the superiority of the coded IM-assisted RIS solution in improving spectral efficiency and error performance compared to the conventional RIS-assisted D2D system, while reducing the impact of hardware imperfections.

title: EventNeuS: 3D Mesh Reconstruction From a Single Event Camera
authors: Shreyas Sachan, Viktor Rudnev, Mohamed Elgharib, Christian Theobalt, Vladislav Golyanik
abstract: Event cameras offer a considerable alternative to RGB cameras in many scenarios. While there are recent works on event-based novel-view synthesis, dense 3D mesh reconstruction remains scarcely explored and existing event-based techniques are severely limited in their 3D reconstruction accuracy. To address this limitation, we present EventNeuS, a self-supervised neural model for learning 3D representations from monocular colour event streams. Our approach, for the first time, combines 3D signed distance function and density field learning with event-based supervision. Furthermore, we introduce spherical harmonics encodings into our model for enhanced handling of view-dependent effects. EventNeuS outperforms existing approaches by a significant margin, achieving 34% lower Chamfer distance and 31% lower mean absolute error on average compared to the best previous method.

title: AI-Driven Recovery Prediction Using Wearable Sensors for Musculoskeletal Injury
authors: Joseph Mouhayar, Youssef Keryakos, Youssef Bou Issa, Christian Salim, Hala Mansour
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) ruptures and shoulder pathologies provide significant challenges for accurate prediction and tracking of recovery, owing to clinical practice's reliance on subjective evaluations and heterogeneous, fragmented patient data. Current artificial intelligence systems are further aggravated by a lack of comprehensive longitudinal datasets, which reduces their prediction power. To address these limitations, this study introduces the Accident Recovery Time Tracker, a multimodal AI platform that combines physiological data from EMG and FSR sensors with clinical assessments and patient demographic information. The platform uses a ResNetbased backbone to extract features from ultrasound and MRI images, as well as a U-Net architecture to accurately delineate damage regions. The system is designed for continual learning and data integration while maintaining anonymity, allowing for more reliable monitoring throughout the various stages of recovery. This dual-model technique improves recovery time forecast accuracy by up to 80 %, enabling faster clinical decisionmaking and individualized rehabilitation procedures.

title: AI Agent-Based Sound Dataset Generation and Neural Network Classification
authors: Zahraa Almokaddem, Rami Tawil, Reza Thani
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Environmental sound classification is essential for applications such as smart city monitoring, autonomous systems, and multimedia interaction, yet progress remains constrained by the limited availability of large, diverse, and high-quality datasets. This paper presents an AI agent-based framework for automated sound dataset generation and hybrid neural network classification. The proposed agent autonomously collects and filters audio from Freesound, YouTube, and TikTok using a multi-stage pipeline that combines signal-level analysis, semantic validation through CLAP (Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining), and visual verification using a Vision-Language Model (LLaVA), while rejecting music and speech contamination. All accepted clips are standardized to 3-second mono 22.05 kHz WAV files with full metadata logging. The resulting dataset comprises over 12,000 samples across 12 environmental and urban classes, refined to 10 for training. A CNN-RNNAttention classifier achieved 84.8 % validation accuracy and 83.4 % test accuracy, demonstrating that AI agents can autonomously construct high-quality sound datasets while enabling robust environmental sound recognition.

title: Transient Power System Analysis using an Adaptive Multi-Stage Holomorphic Embedding with Padé-Based Stability Indicators
authors: Igor D. Melo, Leticia L. S. De Sousa
abstract: Conventional power-system dynamic simulation methods rely predominantly on numerical integration schemes whose efficiency may deteriorate under stiff post-contingency conditions, while existing dynamic holomorphic embedding approaches remain constrained by convergence-radius limitations and the absence of adaptive mechanisms guided by analytical stability information. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an adaptive dynamic simulation framework based on the Multi-Stage Holomorphic Embedding Method (MS-HELM) combined with Padé approximation analysis. In the proposed approach, system dynamic variables are represented through Maclaurin series expansions whose effective convergence region is extended via Padé approximants, enabling explicit analytical trajectory representations through local series propagation. The framework incorporates detailed dynamic models, including synchronous-machine swing equations, excitation systems, turbine governors, and primary and secondary frequency control mechanisms. Two main contributions are introduced: first, an MS-HELM formulation for transient simulation that preserves the holomorphic structure while reducing computational effort relative to conventional dynamic HELM implementations; second, an online stability indicator based on the distance to the nearest Padé pole, augmented with temporal consistency filtering and controller-response awareness for adaptive time-step selection. Validation on IEEE benchmark systems and the Iceland power system demonstrates that the proposed method accurately reproduces post-contingency dynamic behavior while simultaneously improving robustness and reducing computational time by up to 90% relative to reference methods. Additionally, the proposed formulation preserves numerical accuracy comparable to that of the fourth-order Runge–Kutta method, yielding percentage differences below 0.1%.

title: Distance-Sensitive Task Allocation for Mobile Crowdsensing: A Reinforcement Learning-Based Mechanism_supp1-3691000.pdf
authors: Weidong Li
abstract: Mobile crowdsensing services (MCSs) have long been a popular research topic in service computing. An MCS provider recruits users to complete data collection tasks, with an incentive mechanism employed to achieve greater utility. However, most existing studies have assumed that data collected by different users in the same area have the same value, which is not consistent with reality and reduces the quality of service. For example, noise or air quality data collected from different locations within the same area may differ considerably. In this paper, a distance factor is innovatively integrated into the MCS task allocation problem to more accurately represent real situations. Specifically, we transform the distance-sensitive online MCS task allocation problem into an integer programming model with budget constraints and ordered submodular characteristics. Additionally, we design a reverse auction mechanism based on reinforcement learning (RL) to solve the winner determination and payment problems. Unlike existing machine learning-based mechanisms, the proposed algorithms can strictly guarantee the economic characteristics of the auction mechanism, such as its individual rationality, truthfulness, and budget feasibility, through weight replacement and equilibrium pricing strategies. In experimental evaluations, we compare our algorithm with the offline optimal (OPT) algorithm, the advanced monotonic discrete online TDMC algorithm and the FULL SP-RL algorithm as benchmarks. The results indicate that our approach can enable service providers to obtain higher values with lower payments.

title: A Trustworthy Hybrid Multi-branch Architecture for Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Classification under Data Scarcity
authors: Li Wang, Yikai Cai, Xinyu Zhao, Huihua Kuang, Xin Liu, Zhenquan Wu, Jie Chen, Guoming Zhang, Jianqiang Li
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide, making early and accurate screening essential. However, automated DR grading from fundus images remains difficult due to insufficient expert annotations and severe class imbalance. To address these challenges, we propose a hybrid multi-branch deep learning framework that integrates a convolution–attention Hybrid Feature Extraction Block (HFEB) and a Multi-Branch Feature Fusion (MBFF) block. A pre-trained Vision Transformer (ViT) is used to adaptively weight branch features for dynamic integration, and a Parallel Feature Fusion module and composite loss function are applied to enhance lesion feature learning and mitigate class imbalance. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art accuracies of 88.5%, 86.8%, and 73.4% on the APTOS2019, DDR, and IDRiD datasets, with strong generalization ability on a private ROP dataset. Grad-CAM visualization verifies that the model focuses on clinically important lesions. Furthermore, the framework is designed for edge deployment on IoT medical terminals. The lightweight version achieves real-time inference of 33.2ms on Jetson AGX Orin 64G. Even with ViT guidance, the inference latency is still within the acceptable range for hospital AI detection equipment, meeting the low-latency and low-cost demands of portable screening devices and supporting scalable telemedicine IoT applications.

title: Modeling and Design of an Integrated Machine With Decoupled Multirotors Based on Multiphase Orthogonal Subspaces
authors: Yiyang Xu, Hao Chen, Dong Yan, Tingna Shi
abstract: Independent multirotor operation within a single machine is increasingly demanded for applications such as counter-rotating propellers/wind turbines. However, decoupled dual-rotor control usually requires multiple sets of stators or windings, leading to complex machine structures. To address this challenge, current space vectors from multiphase orthogonal subspaces are utilized to independently control multiple rotors in this article. By quantitatively revealing the multipole armature magnetic field and decoupled average torque, the relationship between one single orthogonal subspace and the associated rotor pole-pair number is established. On this basis, a five-phase dual-rotor (FPDR) machine featuring a single 12-slot stator and two axially-arranged rotors (with four and eight pole-pair numbers) is proposed. Full decoupling of torques and speeds between dual rotors is achieved through this compact architecture. Thus, the proposed machine balances the operational performance and manufacturing complexity, while maintaining a torque density of 5.41 Nm/L. Moreover, the generalized topology and universal design principles for machines with more than two rotors are presented, providing a new pathway for integrated multiaxis drive systems. The feasibility of the proposed machine is verified by finite element analysis and experiments on a FPDR prototype.

title: Physics-Informed Meta-Feature–Guided Sequence Learning for Magnetic Core Loss Prediction
authors: Mengfan Wang, Tao Liu, Ping Han, Fucheng Tan, Zening Xiang, Xiaohui Ye
abstract: Magnetic core loss significantly impacts the efficiency and power density of modern power electronics, necessitating accurate modeling under multidimensional operating conditions. Classical analytical formulations exhibit systematic residuals under non-sinusoidal excitations, whereas purely data-driven models often fail to disentangle static nonlinearities from dynamic hysteresis, leading to physically inconsistent extrapolations. To address these challenges, this work proposes a physics-informed meta-feature– guided sequence-learning framework. A tree-based learner first extracts a compact structural prior from static variables via leakage-safe out-of-fold predictions to capture high-order nonlinear interactions. This meta-feature then guides a parameter-efficient FDHT-LSTM encoder to model excitation-history-dependent dynamics from flux-density waveforms. Meanwhile, the classical Steinmetz equation is incorporated as a physical anchor through residual learning with Steinmetz-consistency regularization, enforcing physically plausible predictions. Experiments on the MagNet benchmark demonstrate superior predictive accuracy over representative baselines and improved cross-material robustness within the investigated ferrite materials under leave-one-material-out validation.

title: DCS-AMTD: Attention-Based Deep Compressed Sensing with Multi-Loss Optimization for IIoT Vibration Data
authors: Anying Chai, Maolong Guo, Qiang He, Zhaobo Fang, Chi Xu, Xiaokang Zhou, Ammar Hawbani, Kaifa Zheng
abstract: IIoT sensors collect large volumes of vibration time-series data at high sampling rates. These data are nonstationary and multi-scale and are essential for condition monitoring across different devices and operating conditions. Deep compression sensing reduces data volume while preserving critical information, enabling efficient and low-cost data processing in IIoT systems. However, existing methods for industrial time-series data processing struggle to preserve features effectively under stringent bandwidth and storage constraints. Moreover, most deep compression sensing models overlook computational limitations and robustness demands in noisy industrial settings. Therefore, we propose an Attention-Based Deep Compressed Sensing with Multi-Loss Optimization for IIoT Vibration Data (DCS-AMTD). The model achieves efficient compression and high-quality reconstruction while improving both resource efficiency and noise robustness. Specifically, we design a dual-path convolutional module that incorporates dilated convolutions to capture multi-scale local and global features. We design a one-dimensional convolutional block attention module (CBAM1D) for industrial vibration signals to dynamically reweight multi-scale features and enhance discriminative representations. Furthermore, we design a joint time-frequency loss with multi-domain constraints to improve reconstruction quality under strict bandwidth and computational constraints. Experiments on the Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) and Paderborn (PB) datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior reconstruction performance across various compression ratios, outperforming existing approaches.

title: Ego-R1: Agentic Chain-of-Tool-Thought for Ultra-Long Egocentric Video Reasoning
authors: Shulin Tian, Ruiqi Wang, Hongming Guo, Penghao Wu, Yuhao Dong, Xiuying Wang, Jingkang Yang, Hao Zhang, Hongyuan Zhu, Ziwei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Egocentric videos are inherently long-form, as they provide a continuous, first-person perspective of daily life, capturing complex social interactions and routines that naturally span days or weeks. Understanding and reasoning over egocentric videos that span hours or even days poses significant challenges due to their length, multimodal nature, and complex temporal dependencies over long time horizons. To this end, we introduce Ego-R1, a novel framework for reasoning over ultra-long (i.e., days and weeks) egocentric videos. Ego-R1 leverages a structured Chain-of-Tool-Thought (CoTT) process, orchestrated by an Ego-R1 Agent trained via reinforcement learning (RL). Inspired by human problem-solving strategies, CoTT decomposes complex reasoning into modular steps, empowering the agent to act as a high-level controller that dynamically invokes specialized tools–such as hierarchical memory retrievers and multimodal perceptors–to iteratively and collaboratively answer sub-questions. This approach enables effective temporal abstraction, long-horizon dependency tracking, and step-by-step multimodal reasoning. The framework is built upon a flexible toolkit designed for efficient temporal retrieval and granular visual analysis: Hierarchical RAG (H-RAG), a text-based module that performs efficient top-down temporal localization by aggregating video logs from day-level summaries down to 10-minute intervals; Video-LLM, a short-horizon perception module that analyzes local temporal windows to interpret dynamic interactions; and VLM, a fine-grained vision-language model used to extract high-resolution details, such as text or object attributes, from specific frames. We design a two-stage training paradigm involving supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of a pretrained language model using CoTT data, to enable dynamic tool proposal for long-range reasoning; followed by RL, to enhance the performance of plan smartly with tools. To facilitate training, we construct Ego-R1 Data, which consists of Ego-CoTT-25 K for SFT and Ego-QA-4.4 K for RL. Furthermore, we evaluate Ego-R1 on a newly curated week-long video QA benchmark, Ego-R1 Bench, which contains hybrid-source, human-verified QA pairs. Extensive experiments show that our 3B-parameter Ego-R1 Agent achieves the strongest performance among open-weight and tool-agent baselines, while offering interpretable tool-grounded reasoning trajectories. On Ego-R1 Bench, Ego-R1 achieves 46.0% accuracy, substantially outperforming Gemini-1.5-Pro (38.3%) and LLaVA-Video (29.0%); we further report Gemini-3.1-Pro as a stronger closed-source reference at 53.7%. Moreover, the framework exhibits strong generalization to standard exocentric video benchmarks; by leveraging the long-video nature of egocentric data to train the orchestrator's planning capabilities rather than overfitting the perceptors to a specific view, our modular design remains robust across domains. Ego-R1 Agent achieves 64.9% accuracy on Video-MME (long), surpassing leading open-weight models. These results validate that dynamic, tool-augmented reasoning effectively bridges the gap between limited context windows and the demands of understanding both week-long first-person experiences and general long-form video content.

title: Hybrid EEG Feature Fusion Framework for Accurate Autism Spectrum Disorder Diagnosis Using Ensemble Learning
authors: Zaid Abdi Alkareem Alyasseri, Hajir Ammar Hatim, Riyadh Rahef Nuiaa Alogaili, Nor'ashikin Ali, Norziana Jamil
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a complex neurodevelopmental condition with increasing global prevalence and no standardized biological test for early detection. Current diagnosis methods rely heavily on behavioral assessments, which are subjective, time-consuming, and prone to variability. This study proposes a hybrid feature fusion framework for non-invasive ASD diagnosis using electroencephalogram (EEG) signals, specifically event-related potentials (ERPs) such as P300 components obtained from the BCIAUT-P300 dataset. EEG recordings were captured using a g.Nautilus wireless system with eight scalp electrodes, and preprocessed using 0.5-30 Hz bandpass filtering and baseline subtraction to enhance signal quality. Twenty-two EEG features were extracted across time, frequency, and time-frequency domains using methods such as Wavelet Transform, power spectral density, higher-order statistics, and principal component analysis. Five optimal methods, PCA, HOS, PSD, FDA, and CWT, were selected based on their classification potential and fused using both feature-level and decision-level strategies. Ensemble classifiers including SVM, XGBoost, LDA, and Random Forest were trained and evaluated on the fused feature set. The proposed hybrid fusion framework achieved a classification accuracy of 97.7%, sensitivity of 96.8%, and specificity of 98.5%, outperforming traditional single feature or single classifier approaches. The integration of multi-domain feature descriptors with ensemble learning contributes to increased robustness, generalizability, and diagnostic precision. Our work demonstrates the feasibility of combining EEG-based biomarkers with machine learning to support early ASD diagnosis. The framework offers a scalable approach that is aligned with biomedical informatics objectives, with potential for clinical deployment and integration into portable EEG-based screening systems.

title: AI-Enhanced High-Precision Segmentation and Perfusion Analysis in Myocardial Contrast Echocardiography
authors: Yuxiang Duan, Jili Long, Shunyi Zhao, Hao Wang, Yuriy S. Shmaliy
abstract: Myocardial contrast echocardiography (MCE) facilitates the quantification of myocardial perfusion and aids in diagnosing coronary artery disease (CAD). However, noise and artifacts in MCE data often hinder accurate perfusion analysis. This study presents an artificial intelligence-enhanced method for addressing these challenges. The method divides perfusion quantification into three steps: segmentation, segmental division, and parameter extraction. This method includes an AI model specifically designed for myocardial segmentation in MCE data. Evaluated on a custom dataset from Fuwai Hospital, the AI model achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.88 and an intersection over union (IoU) of 0.78 for the apical four-chamber (A4C) view, outperforming existing models. The segmented myocardium was divided into seven regions to extract perfusion parameters, with cardiac pose features across multiple cardiaccycles incorporated to enhance accuracy. By automating and streamlining perfusion assessment, the proposed method demonstrates potential to improve the clinical application of MCE in computer-aided diagnosis.

title: RIDE: Boosting 3-D Object Detection for LiDAR Point Clouds via Rotation-Invariant Analysis
authors: Zhaoxuan Wang, Xu Han, Hongxin Liu, Xianzhi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The rotation robustness property has drawn much attention to point cloud analysis, whereas it still poses a critical challenge in 3D object detection. When subjected to arbitrary rotation, most existing detectors fail to produce expected outputs due to the poor rotation robustness. In this paper, we present RIDE, a pioneering exploration of Rotation-Invariance for the 3D LiDAR-point-based object DEtector, with the key idea of designing rotation-invariant features from LiDAR scenes and then effectively incorporating them into existing 3D detectors. Specifically, we design a bi-feature extractor that extracts 1) object-aware features that are sensitive to rotation but preserve geometry well, and 2) rotation-invariant features, which lose geometric information to a certain extent but are robust to rotation. These two kinds of features complement each other to decode 3D proposals that are robust to arbitrary rotations. Particularly, our RIDE is compatible and easy to plug into the existing one-stage and two-stage 3D detectors, and boosts both detection performance and rotation robustness. Extensive experiments on the standard benchmarks showcase that the mean average precision (mAP) and rotation robustness can be significantly boosted by integrating with our RIDE, with +5.6% mAP and 111% rotation robustness improvement on KITTI, +5.1% and 34% improvement correspondingly on nuScenes. The code is available at https://github.com/CentauriStar/RIDE

title: A Large Language Model-Based Game Equilibrium Selection Approach for Human-Machine Shared Driving
authors: Yi Yang, Chunyue Song, Huang Li, Jun Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Human-machine shared driving (HMSD) has emerged as a crucial transitional paradigm before the widespread adoption of fully autonomous vehicles. However, existing research typically only considers either human-dominated or human-machine equal relationships, neglecting the fact that these two relationships alternate during driving, which leads to a gap between theory and reality. To address this issue, this study proposes a large language model (LLM)-based game equilibrium selection approach for human-machine shared driving authority allocation. Firstly, a game equilibrium selection model is developed to seamlessly transition between Stackelberg equilibrium and Nash equilibrium, addressing human-dominated and human-machine equal relationships, respectively. The selection process is implemented using an LLM, which bases its decisions on scenario understanding. To enhance the LLM’s scenario understanding performance, a set of indicators capturing human-machine conflicts, driver involvement, and collision risks is introduced as prior knowledge. Furthermore, an LLM-based scenario-understanding module is designed to embed knowledge into the LLM and enable it to function effectively within the HMSD system. Finally, a human-in-the-loop experiment is conducted to validate the proposed strategy. The results show that LLMs can understand the provided knowledge, flexibly adapt to different scenarios, and accurately grasp human-machine interactions. Moreover, the proposed strategy effectively reduces human-machine conflicts, better satisfies driver intentions, and reduces driver workload, showcasing the potential of LLM-based decision-making in human-machine interaction.

title: Evaluating the Impact of MRI Guidance on Plug-and-Play PET Reconstruction
authors: Marion Savanier, Noel Jeffrey Pinton, Valentin Gautier, Claude Comtat, Florent Sureau
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: This paper proposes a new approach for PET reconstruction using learned regularization operators guided by MRI. It extends the Plug-and-Play framework we previously proposed for standalone PET reconstruction. In contrast to existing AI-based reconstruction methods, our method can be proved to converge to a unique fixed point, thus bridging the gap between practical performance and theoretical guarantees. It relies on a preconditioned ADMM algorithm and leverages a learned, firmly nonexpansive neural network, defined with respect to a metric derived from the Fisher Information Matrix that reflects the statistical uncertainty of the Poisson model. The network is trained to satisfy the fixed-point equation associated with the reconstruction problem. In particular, we plugged various MRI-conditioned operators into our algorithm, from multibranch and multichannel U-Nets to a learned proximity operator as a deep equilibrium model. We investigated the quantitative performance of the method trained on a dataset of clinical [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F]-FDG PET and MR images acquired on a hybrid PET-MR system for three dose-reduction factors, comparing it with anatomical guidance using the Bowsher and Deep Image Prior methods. Our results illustrate that our multibranch U-Net without skip connections at higher decomposition levels achieved better bias-standard deviation trade-offs than the other U-Nets and the considered baselines. When synthetic lesions were simulated in the PET image alone, the deep equilibrium model outperformed the multibranch U-Net, making it a prime candidate for robust low-count MR-guided PET reconstruction.

title: Fuzzy Multiple Linear Least Squares Regression Analysis in the Hilbert Space of A-Linearly Correlated Fuzzy Numbers
authors: Laécio Carvalho Barros, Kadu Vinicius Toledo Paulino, Estevão Esmi, Francielle Santo Pedro Simões, Otavio Akira de Barros, Vinícius Francisco Wasques
abstract: This paper presents a fuzzy least squares method for multiple linear regression models formulated in the space of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$A$</tex-math></inline-formula>-linearly correlated fuzzy numbers, denoted by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The algebraic operations in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula> are defined through an isometric isomorphism with the complex field <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {C}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, guaranteeing that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula> is a field and that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}^{p}$</tex-math></inline-formula> is a Hilbert space over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. As a consequence, all the tools of differential and integral calculus for functions with fuzzy input and output become available, as well as the full framework of orthogonal projection theory in this class of fuzzy numbers. Based on these fundamentals, we propose a least squares method for fuzzy multiple linear regression models, where the coefficients can be either real or fuzzy numbers. Numerical results show that the proposed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-based formulation yields lower Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) values in comparison with existing approaches, lower Mean Absolute Error (MAE) values under the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbb {R}\_{\mathcal {F}(A)}$</tex-math></inline-formula> norm, and higher agreement with the observed fuzzy outputs, as measured by similarity.

title: Evaluating the Capability of InSAR-Derived Surface Deformation to Characterize the Spatio-temporal variability of Permafrost Active Layer Thickness in Svalbard_supp1-3696961.docx
authors: Yonghong Yi

title: Personalized Privacy-preserving Task Allocation in Spatial Crowdsourcing_supp1-3696510.pdf
authors: Jianhao Wei
abstract: As a popular service management system, the spatial crowdsourcing (SC) server is responsible for allocating nearby workers to perform tasks based on outsourced locations. However, protecting the sensitive information contained in these outsourced locations is crucial. Traditional differential privacy (DP) methods suffer from two limitations: 1) they usually rely on a trusted third party, failing to protect both worker and task location privacy simultaneously, thus risking privacy breaches; 2) they ignore the personalized privacy demands of different users. In this paper, we propose a personalized local DP-based location obfuscation (PLDPLO) scheme, thereby providing personalized privacy-preserving both worker and task locations locally while allocating high-quality tasks. To achieve this, we introduce a personalized location indistinguishability (PLI) model, a new personalized Laplace mechanism achieving local DP, to jointly provide the protection of worker locations and different privacy levels for different workers. To address task privacy, we present a spatial mapping indistinguishability (SMI) algorithm to obfuscate task locations based on a random response mechanism, thereby ensuring data utility. Additionally, we propose a Zipf-Poisson model-based task allocation graph (ZPTAG) algorithm to perform one-task-multiple-workers allocation and achieve a high competitive ratio, which reduces the move distance of workers. Our PLDPLO scheme guarantees ϵ-LDP. Extensive experiments over real datasets demonstrate that our scheme achieves over 89% data utility for task allocation and outperforms state-of-the-art methods while providing personalized privacy levels.

title: Physically Informed Driving Style Recognition for Intelligent Vehicle Assistance
authors: Congcong Bai, Saijun Zhong, Mengdi Chen, Xi Gao, Wenbin Yao, Sheng Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Recognizing driving styles is essential for enhancing advanced driver-assistance systems (ADASs), as it enables the provision of personalized driving experiences while improving road safety. Although trajectory-based driving style recognition has been widely studied, existing approaches often rely on behavioral statistical features, single-model parameters, or latent representations, which may compromise interpretability and cross-scenario generalizability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes a physically informed framework for driving style recognition that integrates multiple physical car-following models with data-driven clustering and classification techniques. The framework extracts driving preferences from vehicle trajectory data by calibrating parameters across multiple physical car-following models and identifying key style-discriminative features. This approach enhances the interpretability and robustness of trajectory-based representation while reducing reliance on sparse in-vehicle sensor data. Experimental results reveal three behaviorally distinct driving styles—cautious efficient, aggressive efficient, and conservative safe—each reflecting distinct tradeoffs among efficiency, safety, and stability. The extracted rules exhibit high classification accuracy and generalizability across datasets, forming a solid basis for style-aware ADAS strategies. The proposed framework offers an interpretable and transferable trajectory-based solution for scalable driving style analysis, offering a solid foundation for intelligent vehicle assistance.

title: Robust Vehicle Detection in Complex Traffic Scenes Using an Enhanced Faster R-CNN Architecture for Traffic Monitoring
authors: Sopheap Yoeurn, Wekin Piyarat
abstract: This paper presents an enhanced Faster R-CNN framework for robust vehicle detection in complex highway environments. Vehicle detection in real-world traffic scenes remains challenging due to scale variation, partial occlusion, illumination changes, and background clutter. To address these issues, a new Thai Highway Vehicle Dataset (THVD) is introduced, and an improved detection architecture is proposed by integrating a ResNet-101 backbone with a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), RoI Align, CIoU loss, and Soft-NMS. These components improve multi-scale feature representation and enhance localization accuracy, particularly for partially occluded and densely packed vehicles. Extensive experiments conducted on COCO, UA-DETRAC, KITTI, and the proposed THVD dataset demonstrate consistent performance improvements and strong generalization capability. The proposed model achieves 93.8% mAP@50, representing a 6.6% improvement over the baseline Faster R-CNN, while maintaining efficient inference speed suitable for real-time deployment in intelligent transportation systems and highway surveillance applications.

title: Design and Analysis of a Solar-Integrated LT Distribution Pole for Sustainable Urban and Rural Electrification
authors: Abu Talha Haque Miah, MISS AL IVE, ZAHROUL HAQUE MIAH
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The rapid expansion of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems in urban and rural areas has fueled land-use and distribution loss concerns along with system integration. The traditional ground-mounted PV systems consume large areas of land that are not available in congested urban areas and are highly contentious in rural areas. The research presents and analyzes the new concept of a solar-integrated low-tension (LT) distribution pole that uses the dead area on the surface of standard 400 V distribution poles to capture distributed PV power. The design model in SketchUp identified viable panel deployment at the pole head and at the sides, considering electrical clearances, mechanical support, and shading interactions on conductors and nearby buildings. The performance analysis in PVsyst calculated annual energy yield, performance ratio, and system loss for representative climatic patterns. Each pole supports <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.8-1.5 \text{kWp} \text{PV}$</tex> modules providing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,100-1,400 \text{kWh} /$</tex> annum output for a given configuration and site condition, translating to a cumulative net <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> reduction of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 77.4 \text{tCO}_{2}$</tex> for a 5.55 kWp system over 25 years. The proposed scheme saves land resources and uses the available distribution network to reduce the costs of deployment when compared to the conventional rooftop or ground-mounted systems. The research concludes by presenting the design guidance on the large-scale deployment basis, identifying the obstacles to safety and regulatory acceptance, and presenting the promise of the pole-mounted systems for grid support in microgrids and energy hybrids in new economies.
